topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Acoustics, Sound and Noise, Generation of Sound, Acoustic Pressure, Pure Tone and RMS Values, Speed of Sound, Frequency, Wavelength, Period, Relation Between Them

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Acoustics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Acoustics* is the interdisciplinary branch of physics and engineering that deals with the study of all mechanical waves in gases, liquids, and solids, including their generation, propagation, reception, and effects. It covers topics ranging from vibration, sound, ultrasound, and infrasound to noise control and architectural design of spaces.

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of acoustics as the "science of hearing and vibration." Just as optics is the science of light, acoustics is the science of sound. When you speak in a room, the room's acoustics decide whether your friend hears you clearly (concert hall) or as a muddy echo (empty bathroom). Acoustics governs the *entire journey* of a sound wave: from the lips of the speaker, through the air, bouncing off walls, into the listener's ear, and the brain's interpretation.

**Standard Metric in SI:** Sound speed $c$ in air at **20 °C ≈ 343 m/s**; reference acoustic pressure $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$.

---

## 1.2 Sound

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Sound* is a mechanical longitudinal wave that propagates through an elastic medium (solid, liquid, or gas) by means of compression and rarefaction of particles, and whose frequency lies within the audible range of human hearing (approximately **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz**).

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine pushing one end of a stretched spring back and forth quickly. The coils bunch up (compression) and spread apart (rarefaction), and this disturbance travels along the spring. Sound behaves identically, except the "spring" is the air, and your eardrum acts as the receiving end. If you push too slowly (below 20 Hz), you feel vibration but hear nothing; if you push too fast (above 20,000 Hz), again nothing is heard.

**Key Elements of Sound:**
- Requires a **source** (vibrating body).
- Requires a **medium** (cannot travel in vacuum).
- Requires a **receiver** (ear, microphone).
- Transfers **energy**, not matter.

---

## 1.3 Noise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Noise* is defined as any unwanted, undesirable, or unpleasant sound that may interfere with communication, hearing, work efficiency, or cause physiological/psychological harm. From a physics standpoint, it is often aperiodic, broadband, and statistically random in nature.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A school bell is *sound*; the same school bell ringing while you are writing an exam is *noise*. The physics of the wave is identical — the difference is purely **subjective and contextual**. A classical song pleasing to one listener is noise to another.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight — Difference between Sound and Noise:**
> *Sound is desired and meaningful (music, speech, alarm). Noise is unwanted, disturbing, or harmful. There is no physical difference in the wave itself; only perception and context define the boundary.*

---

## 1.4 Generation of Sound

Sound is **always** generated by a vibrating source. The mechanism involves three stages:

1. **Mechanical Vibration:** A solid body (e.g., vocal cord, guitar string, speaker cone) is set into oscillation.
2. **Energy Transfer to Medium:** The vibrating surface imparts kinetic energy to adjacent air molecules.
3. **Wave Propagation:** These molecules collide with neighbors, transmitting the disturbance outward as a longitudinal pressure wave.

**Mathematical Source Model:** A point source radiating spherical waves produces a volume velocity $q(t)$ that drives the surrounding fluid. For a harmonically oscillating piston of area $S$ and velocity $u(t) = u_0 \cos(\omega t)$, the volume velocity is $q(t) = S \cdot u(t)$.

---

## 1.5 Acoustic Pressure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Acoustic pressure* (or sound pressure) $p$ is the local deviation from ambient (atmospheric) pressure caused by a sound wave. It is a scalar quantity, measured in Pascals (Pa).

$$p(t) = p_{\text{total}}(t) - p_{\text{atm}}$$

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Atmospheric pressure at sea level is about **101,325 Pa**. A loud sound only changes this by a tiny amount — say **2 Pa**. Yet your eardrum is so sensitive that it can detect pressure variations as small as **20 μPa** (the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz). Acoustic pressure is the *whisper* added on top of the *shout* of the atmosphere.

**Reference Pressure:** $p_{\text{ref}} = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa}$

---

## 1.6 Pure Tone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A *pure tone* is a single-frequency sound wave whose acoustic pressure varies sinusoidally with time. It contains only one frequency component and no harmonics.

$$p(t) = p_{\text{peak}} \cos(2\pi f t + \phi)$$

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A pure tone is the "pure color" of sound. A tuning fork struck gently produces almost a pure tone at 440 Hz. Real-world sounds (speech, music) are *sums* of many pure tones. A pure tone is to acoustics what a single wavelength of red light is to optics.

---

## 1.7 RMS Value of Acoustic Pressure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** The *Root Mean Square (RMS)* value of acoustic pressure is the square root of the time-averaged square of the instantaneous pressure over one complete cycle. It represents the equivalent DC pressure that delivers the same energy.

$$p_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T p^2(t) \, dt}$$

**For a pure tone:** $p_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{p_{\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \, p_{\text{peak}}$

**Intuitive Analogy:**
RMS is the "effective" pressure. A sinusoid swings between $+p_{\text{peak}}$ and $-p_{\text{peak}}$, but most of the time it is at smaller values. RMS is the steady value that would do the same energetic work. It is used in sound level meters because the human ear responds to **energy**, not to instantaneous peaks.

---

## 1.8 Speed of Sound

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** The *speed of sound* $c$ is the distance travelled by a sound wave per unit time in a given medium. In air at 20 °C, $c \approx 343 \, \text{m/s}$.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Sound is comparatively slow. Light reaches you almost instantly, but thunder lags behind lightning by seconds. The "3-second rule" (count seconds between lightning flash and thunder, divide by 3 to get distance in km) is a direct consequence of $c \approx 343 \, \text{m/s}$ in air.

**Temperature Dependence in Air:**

$$c = 331 + 0.6 \, T \quad (\text{m/s, with } T \text{ in } ^\circ\text{C})$$

---

## 1.9 Frequency, Wavelength, Period

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definitions:**
> - *Period ($T$):* Time taken for one complete cycle of a wave, in **seconds (s)**.
> - *Frequency ($f$):* Number of complete cycles per second, in **Hertz (Hz)**.
> - *Wavelength ($\lambda$):* Spatial length of one complete cycle, in **metres (m)**.

**Intuitive Analogy (Ocean Waves):**
Stand on a pier and watch waves roll in:
- **Period** = time between two successive crests reaching you.
- **Frequency** = number of crests passing you every second.
- **Wavelength** = distance between two consecutive crests on the water surface.

For a sound wave, replace "crests" with "compressions" of air.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Acoustic Pressure Wave — Mathematical Framework

A one-dimensional plane acoustic wave propagating in the $+x$ direction through a lossless medium can be expressed as:

$$p(x,t) = p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

where:
- $p_0$ → **peak acoustic pressure amplitude** (Pa)
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ → **angular frequency** (rad/s)
- $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ → **angular wave number** (rad/m)
- $t$ → time (s)
- $x$ → position (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of the Phase Term $(\omega t - kx)$:**
> The term $\omega t$ captures *temporal oscillation* at a fixed location; the term $kx$ captures *spatial oscillation* at a fixed instant. Together, $(\omega t - kx)$ describes a sinusoid travelling in the $+x$ direction with phase velocity $c = \omega / k$.

---

## 2.2 Why These Quantities Matter in Engineering

- **Frequency ($f$)** determines pitch and is critical in filter design, equalizers, and noise rating curves (NR curves).
- **Wavelength ($\lambda$)** determines diffraction behaviour. A wall blocks high-frequency (small $\lambda$) sound but lets low-frequency (large $\lambda$) sound diffract around it — the principle behind highway noise barriers failing for low frequencies.
- **Period ($T$)** sets the sampling rate in digital audio (Nyquist criterion: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$).
- **Speed of sound ($c$)** governs delay-line calculations in noise surveys, time-delay spectrometry, and architectural acoustics.
- **Acoustic pressure** is the fundamental measurand of every sound level meter and microphone.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula a KTU student must memorize for Module 1 derivations and numerical problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / When to Use |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | s | Always used in time-domain problems |
| 2 | Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hz | Inverse of period |
| 3 | Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | rad/s | Used in $\sin/\cos$ arguments |
| 4 | Speed of sound in air | $c = 331 + 0.6\,T$ | m/s | $T$ in °C, valid $\pm$30 °C |
| 5 | Speed of sound in general | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma P_0}{\rho_0}}$ | m/s | $P_0$ = ambient pressure, $\rho_0$ = density, $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats |
| 6 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = cT$ | m | Fundamental relation; one of the most tested in KTU |
| 7 | Wavenumber | $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \dfrac{\omega}{c}$ | rad/m | Used in spatial phase |
| 8 | Acoustic pressure (pure tone) | $p(t) = p_0 \cos(2\pi f t + \phi)$ | Pa | Single-frequency signal |
| 9 | RMS pressure (pure tone) | $p_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{p_0}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Pa | For sinusoidal signal only |
| 10 | RMS pressure (general) | $p_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T p^2(t)\,dt}$ | Pa | Valid for any periodic signal |
| 11 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | dB | $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ |
| 12 | Intensity relation | $I = \dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 c}$ | W/m² | Plane progressive wave |
| 13 | Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | dB | $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}$ W/m² |
| 14 | Sound power of point source | $W = 4\pi r^2 I$ | W | Spherical spreading |
| 15 | Power Level | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | dB | $W_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}$ W |

---

## 2.4 Logic Flow: From Generation to Reception

The complete acoustic chain in any engineering system follows this order:

1. **Vibrating source** (mechanical input).
2. **Volume velocity** $q(t)$ couples source to medium.
3. **Acoustic pressure field** $p(\vec{r},t)$ propagates via wave equation.
4. **Particle velocity** $u(\vec{r},t)$ accompanies pressure.
5. **Receiver** (microphone/eardrum) converts $p$ into electrical/neural signal.
6. **Perception** → brain interprets frequency, intensity, and timbre.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The product $p \cdot u$ (pressure × particle velocity) gives **acoustic intensity** $I$ (W/m²). This is the energy flux — the most useful quantity for noise control because energy, not amplitude, determines human physiological response.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are written **step-by-step** with every algebraic transition shown. No step is skipped, as required by the KTU Board Examiner rubric for full marks.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Frequency ($f$) and Period ($T$)

**Statement:** Prove that $f \cdot T = 1$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Period:**
By definition, the period $T$ is the smallest time interval after which a periodic function repeats itself:

$$p(t + T) = p(t) \quad \text{for all } t$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Frequency:**
Frequency is defined as the *number of complete cycles* the wave executes in **one second**.

**Step 3 — Counting Cycles in One Second:**
If one cycle occupies $T$ seconds, then in 1 second the number of cycles completed is:

$$\text{Number of cycles in 1 s} = \frac{1 \text{ s}}{T \text{ s/cycle}} = \frac{1}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Equating to Frequency Definition:**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} \quad \implies \quad f \cdot T = 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Speed ($c$), Wavelength ($\lambda$), and Frequency ($f$)

**Statement:** Prove that $c = f \lambda$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Wavelength:**
Wavelength $\lambda$ is the *spatial distance* over which one complete cycle of the wave exists at a given instant.

**Step 2 — Definition of Speed:**
Speed is the *distance travelled per unit time* by a point of constant phase (e.g., a wave crest).

**Step 3 — Express Distance Travelled in One Period:**
In one time period $T$, the wave travels exactly **one wavelength** $\lambda$ (since the pattern repeats every $\lambda$ in space and every $T$ in time).

$$\text{Distance in time } T = \lambda$$

**Step 4 — Apply Speed Formula:**

$$c = \frac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}} = \frac{\lambda}{T}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $T = 1/f$:**

$$c = \frac{\lambda}{1/f} = f \lambda \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 6 — Equivalent Forms (very useful for KTU problems):**

$$f = \frac{c}{\lambda}, \quad \lambda = \frac{c}{f}, \quad T = \frac{\lambda}{c} = \frac{1}{f}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Speed of Sound in Air as a Function of Temperature

**Statement:** Show that $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T_K / M}$ and derive the linear approximation $c \approx 331 + 0.6\,T$.

**Step 1 — Thermodynamic Starting Point:**
For an ideal gas undergoing adiabatic (isentropic) compressions and rarefactions, the speed of sound is:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{\rho}}$$

where $K_s$ is the **adiabatic bulk modulus** and $\rho$ is the gas density.

**Step 2 — Express $K_s$ and $\rho$ in Terms of Gas Properties:**

$$K_s = \gamma P, \quad \rho = \frac{PM}{R T_K}$$

Here:
- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats ($1.4$ for air)
- $P$ = absolute pressure (Pa)
- $M$ = molar mass of air ($\approx 0.029$ kg/mol)
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) = universal gas constant
- $T_K$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{PM/(R T_K)}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T_K}{M}}$$

**Step 4 — Insert Numerical Values for Air ($\gamma=1.4$, $R=8.314$, $M=0.029$):**

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{1.4 \times 8.314 \times T_K}{0.029}} = 20.05 \sqrt{T_K} \;\; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Celsius ($T_K = T + 273.15$):**
Let $T$ be temperature in °C.

$$c = 20.05 \sqrt{T + 273.15}$$

**Step 6 — Linearize About $T_0 = 0\,^\circ$C:**
At $T_0 = 0\,^\circ$C, $c_0 = 20.05 \sqrt{273.15} \approx 331.4$ m/s.

Expand $\sqrt{T + 273.15} = \sqrt{273.15}\,\sqrt{1 + T/273.15}$ and use $\sqrt{1+x} \approx 1 + x/2$ for small $x$:

$$c \approx 20.05 \sqrt{273.15}\left(1 + \frac{T}{2 \times 273.15}\right) = 331.4 + 0.606\,T$$

**Step 7 — Final Linear Approximation:**

$$\boxed{c \approx 331 + 0.6\,T \;\; \text{m/s}, \quad T \text{ in }^\circ\text{C}} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Speed of sound in air increases by **0.6 m/s for every 1 °C rise** in temperature.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: RMS Pressure of a Pure Tone

**Statement:** Show that for $p(t) = p_0 \cos(\omega t)$, the RMS pressure is $p_{\text{rms}} = p_0 / \sqrt{2}$.

**Step 1 — Square the Instantaneous Pressure:**

$$p^2(t) = p_0^2 \cos^2(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Trigonometric Identity:**

$$\cos^2(\omega t) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$$

So:

$$p^2(t) = p_0^2 \cdot \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega t)}{2} = \frac{p_0^2}{2} + \frac{p_0^2}{2}\cos(2\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Time Average Over One Period $T = 2\pi/\omega$:**

$$\langle p^2 \rangle = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T p^2(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \left[ \frac{p_0^2}{2} + \frac{p_0^2}{2}\cos(2\omega t) \right] dt$$

**Step 4 — Split the Integral:**

$$\langle p^2 \rangle = \frac{p_0^2}{2T} \int_0^T dt \; + \; \frac{p_0^2}{2T} \int_0^T \cos(2\omega t) \, dt$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the First Integral:**

$$\frac{p_0^2}{2T} \int_0^T dt = \frac{p_0^2}{2T} \cdot T = \frac{p_0^2}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the Second Integral:**
The integral of cosine over an integer number of its half-periods is zero:

$$\int_0^T \cos(2\omega t) \, dt = \left[ \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2\omega} \right]_0^T = \frac{\sin(4\pi) - \sin(0)}{2\omega} = 0$$

(since $\omega T = 2\pi$ and $2\omega T = 4\pi$).

**Step 7 — Combine:**

$$\langle p^2 \rangle = \frac{p_0^2}{2} + 0 = \frac{p_0^2}{2}$$

**Step 8 — Take the Square Root:**

$$p_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\langle p^2 \rangle} = \sqrt{\frac{p_0^2}{2}} = \frac{p_0}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Numerical Value:**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; p_{\text{rms}} \approx 0.7071\, p_0$$

> [!TIP]
> **Energy Reasoning:** Since $p_{\text{rms}}^2 = p_0^2/2$, exactly **half** the energy of a sinusoid lies below $p_0$ and half above. This is why the *crest factor* (ratio of peak to RMS) for a pure tone is fixed at $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Order of Magnitude Quick-Reference

| Frequency $f$ | Wavelength in Air (20 °C) | Real-World Example |
|---------------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| 20 Hz | 17.15 m | Lowest audible rumble (large pipe organ) |
| 100 Hz | 3.43 m | Bass guitar fundamental |
| 1 kHz | 0.343 m | Human voice emphasis region |
| 10 kHz | 0.0343 m | Cymbal hiss, microphone resonance |
| 20 kHz | 0.01715 m | Upper audible limit |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sound Generation to Reception

The Mermaid diagram below maps the complete functional chain of an acoustic system, showing how vibration becomes a sound wave, propagates, and is finally perceived.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SOURCE VIBRATION] --> B[VOLUME VELOCITY q of t]
    B --> C[ACOUSTIC PRESSURE p at x t]
    C --> D[PARTICLE VELOCITY u at x t]
    D --> E[INTENSITY I equals p times u]
    E --> F[PROPAGATION MEDIUM]
    F --> G[RECEIVER TRANSDUCER]
    G --> H[PERCEPTION BRAIN OR METER]

    I[INPUT MECHANICAL ENERGY] --> A
    J[TEMPERATURE AND HUMIDITY] --> F
    K[REFLECTION AND ABSORPTION] --> F

    style A fill:#FFD580
    style C fill:#A0E0A0
    style F fill:#80B0FF
    style H fill:#FFA0A0
```

**Interpretation of Each Block:**

- **SOURCE VIBRATION** — Mechanical oscillator (string, membrane, speaker cone).
- **VOLUME VELOCITY** — Rate of air displacement; couples source to medium.
- **ACOUSTIC PRESSURE** — Local pressure deviation (the measurand of microphones).
- **PARTICLE VELOCITY** — Oscillatory velocity of air parcels (not the wave speed!).
- **INTENSITY** — Energy flux vector; basis for noise criteria.
- **PROPAGATION MEDIUM** — Air, water, or solid; determines $c$.
- **RECEIVER TRANSDUCER** — Microphone, eardrum, hydrophone.
- **PERCEPTION** — Human auditory system or sound level meter.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Conversion of Pure Tone to RMS Value

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Pure Tone Signal p of t equals p0 cos omega t] --> A2[Square the Signal p squared]
    A2 --> A3[Time Average Over One Period T]
    A3 --> A4[Apply Identity cos squared theta equals half one plus cos 2 theta]
    A4 --> A5[Cross Term Integrates to Zero]
    A5 --> A6[Mean Square equals p0 squared over 2]
    A6 --> A7[RMS equals p0 over sqrt 2 equals 0.7071 p0]

    style A1 fill:#FFE0B0
    style A7 fill:#B0FFC0
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Relationship Between $c$, $f$, $\lambda$, and $T$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Speed of Sound c in m per s] --> R2[Wavelength lambda equals c times T]
    R3[Period T in s] --> R2
    R2 --> R4[Frequency f equals c over lambda]
    R4 --> R5[Angular frequency omega equals 2 pi f]
    R5 --> R6[Wavenumber k equals omega over c equals 2 pi over lambda]
    R1 --> R6

    style R1 fill:#B0D0FF
    style R3 fill:#FFB0D0
    style R4 fill:#B0FFD0
    style R6 fill:#FFD0B0
```

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Block — Distinguishing Sound vs Noise

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1[Any Acoustic Wave] --> D2{Is it Desired by Receiver?}
    D2 -- YES --> D3[SOUND Information Speech Music Alarm]
    D2 -- NO --> D4[NOISE Disturbance Harm]
    D3 --> D5[Often Periodic Tonal Predictable]
    D4 --> D6[Often Random Broadband Aperiodic]

    style D1 fill:#E0E0FF
    style D3 fill:#90EE90
    style D4 fill:#FF9090
    style D5 fill:#C0FFC0
    style D6 fill:#FFC0C0
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below are modeled precisely on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern, where Part A carries 3 marks each and Part B carries 14 marks each (with internal choice).

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1]`

**Define the following terms with proper SI units:**
*(a) Acoustic pressure*
*(b) Period of a sound wave*
*(c) Wavelength*

**Model Answer:**

*(a) **Acoustic Pressure (Pa):** It is the local pressure deviation from the ambient atmospheric pressure produced by a sound wave. Mathematically, $p(t) = p_{\text{total}}(t) - p_{\text{atm}}$. SI unit is the pascal (Pa).*

*(b) **Period (s):** The time required for one complete cycle of a periodic sound wave. If the pressure variation is $p(t) = p_0 \cos(\omega t)$, then $T = 1/f = 2\pi/\omega$. SI unit: second (s).*

*(c) **Wavelength (m):** The spatial distance over which a sound wave repeats itself at a given instant — equivalently, the distance between two successive compressions or rarefactions. SI unit: metre (m). It is related to speed and frequency by $\lambda = c/f$.*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q2]`

**Distinguish between Sound and Noise. Why is the distinction subjective rather than physical?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Sound | Noise |
|--------|-------|-------|
| Definition | Desired, meaningful acoustic signal | Unwanted, undesirable acoustic signal |
| Examples | Speech, music, alarm | Traffic roar, machinery hum, neighbour's TV |
| Nature | Often periodic / tonal | Often random / broadband |
| Subjective | Pleasing/intended | Annoying/harmful |

*The distinction is **subjective** because the physical wave — pressure variation, frequency, intensity — is identical in both cases. Whether a particular sound is judged "noise" depends on the listener's context, expectation, culture, and current activity. The same loud music is "sound" to a dancer and "noise" to a sleeping neighbour. Hence, no physical measurement can universally classify a wave as noise.*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question below is presented in the **standard KTU choice pattern**: students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** Derive the relation between speed of sound, frequency, and wavelength in a medium. Starting from the wave equation, show that the speed of sound in air depends on temperature as $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T_K / M}$ and reduce it to the linear form $c = 331 + 0.6\,T$ (m/s). **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A pure tone has a peak acoustic pressure of $0.4$ Pa at 1 kHz in air at 20 °C. Calculate:
*(i) the RMS acoustic pressure,*
*(ii) the wavelength,*
*(iii) the period,*
*(iv) the sound pressure level in dB re 20 μPa.*
**\[7 Marks\]**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** For a one-dimensional plane wave, the displacement $\xi(x,t)$ of a particle obeys the wave equation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 2:** A sinusoidal solution is $\xi(x,t) = \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$. Substituting into the wave equation:

$$-\omega^2 \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx) = -c^2 k^2 \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

**Step 3:** Equating the coefficients of $\cos(\omega t - kx)$:

$$\omega^2 = c^2 k^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = \frac{\omega}{k}$$

**Step 4:** Since $\omega = 2\pi f$ and $k = 2\pi/\lambda$:

$$c = \frac{2\pi f}{2\pi/\lambda} = f\lambda$$

This is the required relation. *(2 marks for full derivation)*

**Step 5:** For an ideal gas, $c = \sqrt{\gamma P / \rho}$. Substituting $P = \rho R T_K / M$:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \cdot \rho R T_K / M}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T_K}{M}}$$

**Step 6:** For air, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $M = 0.029$ kg/mol:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{1.4 \times 8.314 \times T_K}{0.029}} = 20.05 \sqrt{T_K} \;\; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 7:** Convert $T_K = T + 273.15$ and linearize about $T = 0\,^\circ$C. At $T=0$:

$$c_0 = 20.05 \times \sqrt{273.15} = 331.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 8:** Differentiating $c = 20.05 \sqrt{T_K}$:

$$\frac{dc}{dT_K} = \frac{20.05}{2\sqrt{T_K}} = \frac{10.025}{\sqrt{273.15}} = 0.606 \text{ m/s/°C}$$

**Step 9:** Hence the linear approximation:

$$c = 331 + 0.6\,T \;\; \text{m/s} \quad (T \text{ in }^\circ\text{C}) \quad \blacksquare$$

*(3 marks for full derivation including numerical substitution)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $p_0 = 0.4$ Pa, $f = 1$ kHz $= 1000$ Hz, $T = 20$ °C, $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa.

**(i) RMS acoustic pressure:**

$$p_{\text{rms}} = \frac{p_0}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{0.4}{1.4142} = 0.2828 \text{ Pa} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Wavelength:**
First compute $c$ at 20 °C:

$$c = 331 + 0.6 \times 20 = 343 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{343}{1000} = 0.343 \text{ m} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**(iv) Sound Pressure Level:**

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2828}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(14140)$$

$$L_p = 20 \times 4.1505 = 83.0 \text{ dB} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Alt Choice]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the generation of sound from a vibrating source. Define acoustic pressure, ambient pressure, and instantaneous acoustic pressure, and derive the relation between them. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A speaker radiates a pure tone of frequency 500 Hz. The peak acoustic pressure measured at 3 m is 0.8 Pa. Calculate:
*(i) the speed of sound at 25 °C,*
*(ii) the wavelength,*
*(iii) the RMS pressure,*
*(iv) the intensity (assuming plane wave with $\rho_0 c = 410$ rayl),*
*(v) the intensity level in dB re $10^{-12}$ W/m².*
**\[7 Marks\]**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Generation of Sound — Sketch Description (Block Diagram):**

```
  Mechanical Force  -->  Vibrating Diaphragm  -->  Air Compression-Rarefaction  -->  Longitudinal Pressure Wave  -->  Receiver
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation (3 marks):**
1. An external agency (hand, electrical signal) energises a mechanical surface (e.g., speaker cone, vocal cord, tuning fork prong).
2. The surface oscillates sinusoidally with frequency $f$ and amplitude $a$.
3. On moving forward, the surface compresses the adjacent air layer → **compression**.
4. On moving backward, it leaves a low-pressure region → **rarefaction**.
5. This alternating pattern propagates outward as a **longitudinal wave** at speed $c$.

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Ambient pressure $p_{\text{atm}}$:** Static atmospheric pressure at the location (≈ 101,325 Pa at sea level).
- **Total instantaneous pressure $p_{\text{total}}(t)$:** Actual pressure at a point and instant, including both ambient and wave-induced components.
- **Acoustic pressure $p(t)$:** Deviation of total pressure from ambient:

$$p(t) = p_{\text{total}}(t) - p_{\text{atm}}$$

**Derivation of the relation (2 marks):**
Rearranging:

$$p_{\text{total}}(t) = p_{\text{atm}} + p(t)$$

For a pure tone source:

$$p_{\text{total}}(t) = p_{\text{atm}} + p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

The wave part oscillates symmetrically about $p_{\text{atm}}$; it is positive during compression and negative during rarefaction. This is the standard decomposition used by every microphone and sound level meter.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $f = 500$ Hz, $T = 25$ °C, $p_0 = 0.8$ Pa, $\rho_0 c = 410$ rayl, $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}$ W/m².

**(i) Speed of sound at 25 °C:**

$$c = 331 + 0.6 \times 25 = 331 + 15 = 346 \text{ m/s} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**(ii) Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{346}{500} = 0.692 \text{ m} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iii) RMS pressure:**

$$p_{\text{rms}} = \frac{p_0}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{0.8}{1.4142} = 0.5657 \text{ Pa} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**(iv) Acoustic intensity (plane wave relation):**

$$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho_0 c} = \frac{(0.5657)^2}{410} = \frac{0.3200}{410} = 7.805 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W/m}^2 \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(v) Intensity level:**

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{7.805 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(7.805 \times 10^{8})$$

$$L_I = 10 \times 8.892 = 88.92 \text{ dB} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistakes** — failing to convert kHz to Hz or °C to K. Always state units at every step.
> 2. **Confusing peak and RMS** — using $p_0$ in the dB formula. The dB formula REQUIRES $p_{\text{rms}}$.
> 3. **Wrong reference pressure** — using $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}$ W/m² for pressure level (it is for intensity level). For pressure, $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$.
> 4. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$** — students often write $p_{\text{rms}} = p_0/\sqrt{2}$ without the $\sqrt{2}$, writing $p_{\text{rms}} = p_0 \sqrt{2}$ instead. This gives wrong numerical answers.
> 5. **Sign error in $c = 331 + 0.6T$** — using $0.6(T-273)$ or similar incorrect linearizations.
> 6. **In derivation questions, skipping the time-average step** in the RMS calculation. KTU examiners deduct **at least 2 marks** if the integration is not shown.
> 7. **Marking pressure as scalar, not vector** — acoustic pressure IS a scalar; intensity is the vector.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 Fundamentals**

- **Acoustics** = total science of mechanical waves; **Sound** = audible mechanical wave (20 Hz–20 kHz); **Noise** = unwanted sound (subjective).
- Sound **cannot travel in vacuum**; it needs an elastic medium.
- **Acoustic pressure $p$** = deviation from ambient atmospheric pressure; measured in **Pa**; reference $p_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$.
- A **pure tone** is sinusoidal: $p(t) = p_0 \cos(2\pi f t + \phi)$. **Real sounds** are sums of pure tones (Fourier theorem).
- **RMS of pure tone** = $p_0 / \sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071\, p_0$. Always use $p_{\text{rms}}$ (not $p_0$) in dB calculations.
- **Period $T$** = time of one cycle (s). **Frequency $f$** = cycles per second (Hz). **$fT = 1$.**
- **Wavelength $\lambda$** = spatial length of one cycle (m). **$\lambda = c/f = cT$.**
- **Speed of sound in air** at 20 °C = **343 m/s**; linear formula: $c = 331 + 0.6\,T$ m/s.
- **Speed of sound in general medium** = $c = \sqrt{\gamma P / \rho} = \sqrt{K_s/\rho}$.
- **Sound Pressure Level** = $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_{\text{ref}})$, $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$.
- **Sound Intensity** (plane wave) = $I = p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}/(\rho_0 c)$.
- **Sound Intensity Level** = $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_{\text{ref}})$, $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}$ W/m².
- **Crest factor** for pure tone = $\sqrt{2}$.
- For every **doubling of distance** from a point source, intensity drops by **6 dB** (inverse square law).
- For every **doubling of $p_{\text{rms}}$**, SPL rises by **6 dB**; for every **10× increase**, SPL rises by **20 dB**.
- The **wavenumber** $k = 2\pi/\lambda = \omega/c$ appears in the spatial phase term $kx$.
- A 1 kHz pure tone in air at 20 °C has $\lambda \approx 0.343$ m — almost the size of a human head, which is why the head creates significant acoustic shadowing at higher frequencies.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Particle Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Particle Velocity ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Particle Velocity (u)** is defined as the *instantaneous* velocity of a microscopic element (particle) of a fluid medium as it oscillates back and forth about its mean (equilibrium) position when a sound wave propagates through it. It is a vector quantity, directed along the direction of wave propagation, and is fundamentally distinct from the **wave velocity (c)** or **speed of sound**, which is the speed at which the acoustic disturbance itself travels through the medium. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMET752 - Module 1)** > Particle velocity is one of the four primary acoustic field variables — the others being **Sound Pressure (p)**, **Density Variation (ρ')**, and **Particle Displacement (ξ)**. Mastering the inter-relationships between these variables is critical for Part B derivations. Mathematically, for a one-dimensional longitudinal wave, if a particle's instantaneous displacement from equilibrium is $\xi(x,t)$, then the particle velocity is the *time-derivative* of that displacement: $$u = \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial t}$$ - **Symbol:** $u$ (sometimes written as $\dot{\xi}$ or $v_p$) - **SI Unit:** metre per second ($\text{m/s}$) - **Typical Magnitude:** Particle velocity amplitudes in conversational speech are on the order of $\mathbf{10^{-3}\ \text{m/s}}$ — about **1000 times smaller** than the actual speed of sound in air. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Explanation > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Floating Cork in a Pond"** > Imagine you toss a stone into a still pond. The water waves (ripples) travel outward at the speed of sound in water — this is the **wave velocity**. Now place a small cork on the surface. The cork does *not* travel outward with the wave; it merely bobs up and down in a tiny vertical circle, oscillating about its original position. The speed at which the cork moves up and down at any instant is the **particle velocity**. > > **Key Insight:** The wave carries *energy* outward, but the *medium particles* only vibrate locally. The same is true in acoustics: air molecules near your ear do not fly into your eardrum; they only vibrate back and forth by an imperceptibly small distance, transferring momentum to your eardrum through collisions. --- ### 1.3 Particle Velocity vs. Wave Velocity — The Critical Distinction | Feature | Particle Velocity ($u$) | Wave Velocity ($c$) | |---|---|---| | **What moves?** | The medium particle itself | The acoustic disturbance / phase front | | **Magnitude in air** | $\sim 10^{-3}$ m/s (for speech) | $\sim 343$ m/s | | **Physical cause** | Oscillation of fluid element | Propagation of compressions & rarefactions | | **Depends on?** | Amplitude & frequency of source | Inherent properties of medium ($K, \rho$) | | **Symbol often used** | $u$ or $\dot{\xi}$ | $c$ | | **Vector/Scalar** | Vector (has direction) | Scalar (in isotropic media) | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Many students conflate "particle velocity" with "speed of sound." In KTU valuation, using $c = 343$ m/s where particle velocity is required (or vice versa) leads to a direct loss of **2 to 3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Theory To rigorously establish particle velocity, we begin with the displacement field of a one-dimensional longitudinal plane sound wave travelling in the positive $x$-direction: $$\xi(x,t) = \xi_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$$ where: - $\xi_0$ = **Particle displacement amplitude** (metres) - $\omega = 2\pi f$ = Angular frequency (rad/s) - $k = \omega/c = 2\pi/\lambda$ = Wave number (rad/m) - $c$ = Speed of sound in the medium **Step 1 — Differentiate to obtain particle velocity:** Particle velocity is the rate of change of displacement with respect to time (at a fixed location $x$): $$u(x,t) = \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial t} = \omega \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 2 — Identify the amplitude:** The maximum (peak) particle velocity occurs when the cosine term equals unity: $$u_0 = \omega \xi_0 = 2\pi f \xi_0$$ **Step 3 — Establish the in-phase relationship with pressure (plane wave case):** For a plane progressive wave, the acoustic pressure variation is: $$p(x,t) = -\kappa \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} = \rho c \, \omega \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ where $\kappa$ is the bulk modulus. Comparing this with the particle velocity expression, we obtain the cornerstone KTU identity: $$p = (\rho c) \, u$$ The proportionality constant $\rho c$ is the **characteristic acoustic impedance** of the medium. **Step 4 — Confirm phase relationship:** Both $p$ and $u$ contain $\cos(\omega t - kx)$, meaning **pressure and particle velocity are exactly in phase** in a plane progressive wave. This is a direct KTU favourite in 3-mark questions. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight — Why this matters:** The quantity $\rho c$ governs how much particle velocity a given sound pressure produces. In air ($\rho c \approx 415$ Rayls), a small particle velocity can produce a measurable pressure; in water ($\rho c \approx 1.5 \times 10^6$ Rayls), the same particle velocity produces a much larger pressure — which is why underwater acoustic sensors are so sensitive. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Use in Exam | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $u = \partial \xi / \partial t$ | Definition of particle velocity | Derivation starters (7-markers) | | 2 | $u_0 = \omega \xi_0 = 2\pi f \xi_0$ | Peak particle velocity in terms of displacement amplitude | Numerical problems | | 3 | $p = \rho c \cdot u$ | Plane-wave pressure–velocity relation | **Most-tested identity in Module 1** | | 4 | $u_0 = p_0 / (\rho c)$ | Solve for $u_0$ given pressure amplitude | Standard 3-mark numerical | | 5 | $Z_s = p / u = \rho c$ | Specific acoustic impedance (plane wave) | Conceptual definition Q's | | 6 | $\xi_0 = u_0 / \omega = u_0 / (2\pi f)$ | Displacement from particle velocity | Numerical problems | | 7 | $I = \frac{1}{2} \rho c \, u_0^{\,2} = \frac{p_0^{\,2}}{2 \rho c}$ | Intensity–velocity relationship | Cross-module (M2–M3) integration | | 8 | $I = \overline{p \cdot u}$ | Time-averaged intensity (general) | Conceptual essay Q's | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constants to Memorise (KTU Board Standard):** > - Density of air, $\rho \approx \mathbf{1.21\ kg/m^3}$ (at 20 °C) > - Speed of sound in air, $c \approx \mathbf{343\ m/s}$ > - Characteristic impedance of air, $\rho c \approx \mathbf{415\ N\cdot s/m^3}$ (Rayls) > - Speed of sound in water, $c \approx \mathbf{1480\ m/s}$ ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Full Derivation — Relationship Between Pressure and Particle Velocity in a Plane Progressive Wave This is the **single most important derivation** in KTU Module 1. Practice writing it out cleanly — examiners award step marks for clarity. **Starting Point:** A plane longitudinal wave travelling in the $+x$ direction has the displacement field: $$\xi(x, t) = \xi_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 1 — Compute the particle velocity.** Differentiating $\xi$ with respect to time $t$ (keeping $x$ fixed): $$u = \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial t} = \xi_0 \cdot \omega \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ $$\therefore \quad u = \omega \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ The peak (maximum) particle velocity is: $$u_0 = \omega \xi_0$$ **Step 2 — Compute the acoustic (excess) pressure.** For a fluid medium, the acoustic pressure is related to the volumetric strain by the bulk modulus $B$ (or equivalently $\kappa$): $$p = -B \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial x}$$ Differentiating $\xi$ with respect to $x$ (keeping $t$ fixed): $$\frac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} = -\xi_0 \, k \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ Substituting back: $$p = -B \bigl(-\xi_0 k \cos(\omega t - kx)\bigr) = B \, k \, \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 3 — Use the wave-propagation identity $B = \rho c^{2}$.** By definition of the speed of sound in a fluid, $c = \sqrt{B/\rho}$, which gives $B = \rho c^2$. Also, $k = \omega/c$. Therefore: $$p = (\rho c^2) \cdot \left(\frac{\omega}{c}\right) \cdot \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ $$p = \rho c \, \omega \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 4 — Compare with the particle velocity expression.** Recall from Step 1: $$u = \omega \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ Therefore: $$p = (\rho c) \cdot u$$ **Step 5 — Final boxed identity:** $$\boxed{\,p = \rho c \cdot u \quad \text{(Plane Progressive Wave)}\,}$$ The proportionality constant $\rho c$ is the **specific acoustic impedance** $Z_s$, measured in Rayls ($\text{N·s/m}^3$ or $\text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}$). > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion of derivation:** For a plane progressive wave, the pressure and particle velocity are **in phase** (ratio is purely real), and the ratio between them is a constant determined solely by the medium's density and sound speed — independent of the source. --- ### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Model-Answer Style) **Problem:** A plane sound wave of frequency $f = 500\ \text{Hz}$ propagates through air ($\rho = 1.21\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $c = 343\ \text{m/s}$) with a pressure amplitude $p_0 = 0.5\ \text{Pa}$. Determine: (a) The particle velocity amplitude $u_0$. (b) The particle displacement amplitude $\xi_0$. **Given:** - $f = 500\ \text{Hz}$ - $p_0 = 0.5\ \text{Pa}$ - $\rho = 1.21\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $c = 343\ \text{m/s}$ **Solution:** **(a) Particle velocity amplitude:** Characteristic impedance of air: $$\rho c = (1.21)(343) = 415.03\ \text{Rayls}$$ Using $p_0 = \rho c \cdot u_0$: $$u_0 = \frac{p_0}{\rho c} = \frac{0.5}{415.03} = 1.205 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m/s}$$ $$\boxed{u_0 \approx 1.205\ \text{mm/s}}$$ **(b) Particle displacement amplitude:** Angular frequency: $$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi (500) = 3141.59\ \text{rad/s}$$ Using $u_0 = \omega \xi_0$: $$\xi_0 = \frac{u_0}{\omega} = \frac{1.205 \times 10^{-3}}{3141.59} = 3.84 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}$$ $$\boxed{\xi_0 \approx 0.384\ \mu\text{m}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation for the Board:** The displacement amplitude ($\sim 0.4\ \mu$m) is **smaller than the wavelength of visible light** — yet the pressure variation of $0.5\ \text{Pa}$ is easily audible. This is why the human ear is a *pressure-sensitive* organ and not a *displacement-sensitive* one. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Acoustic Field Variables Because particle velocity is a microscopic oscillation that cannot be visually drawn in a simple sketch, the diagram below presents the **functional relationship architecture** of the four primary acoustic field variables in a plane sound wave. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Sound Source
Mechanical Vibration"] --> B["Displacement Field
xi of x,t"] B -- "Differentiate wrt TIME
u = dxi/dt" --> C["Particle Velocity
u of x,t"] B -- "Differentiate wrt SPACE
strain = -dxi/dx" --> D["Volumetric Strain"] D -- "Multiply by Bulk Modulus B
p = -B times strain" --> E["Acoustic Pressure
p of x,t"] C -- "Multiply by rho times c
p = rho c u" --> E E -- "Square and divide by 2 rho c
I = p0sqr / 2 rho c" --> F["Intensity
I Watts per m2"] C -- "Square and multiply by 0.5 rho c
I = 0.5 rho c u0sqr" --> F F --> G["Hearing Response
Ear or Microphone"] classDef src fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000 classDef mid fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b38900,color:#000 classDef out fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 class A,G src class B,C,D,E mid class F out ``` **How to read this diagram (for the exam):** - The **displacement field** is the parent quantity — every other acoustic variable is derived from it. - Two derivative paths exist: the **time-derivative** (giving particle velocity) and the **space-derivative** (giving strain and hence pressure). - Both particle velocity and acoustic pressure can independently determine the **intensity**, which is the physically perceived quantity. --- ### 4.2 Phase Relationship Diagram — Pressure, Velocity & Displacement in a Plane Wave The following block diagram clarifies the in-phase / out-of-phase behaviour that examiners love to test. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PW["Plane Progressive Wave — All In Phase"] P1["Pressure p
peak p0"] V1["Particle Velocity u
peak u0"] X1["Displacement xi
peak xi0"] end subgraph SW["Standing Wave — Spatially Varying Phase"] P2["Pressure p node and antinode pattern"] V2["Particle Velocity u
90 degree shift in space"] X2["Displacement xi
90 degree shift in space"] end P1 --- V1 V1 --- X1 P1 ~~~ X1 P2 ~~~ V2 V2 ~~~ X2 P2 ~~~ X2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip (2-mark rapid-fire):** If the question asks about a *standing wave* (or a wave in a tube closed at one end), the phase between $p$ and $u$ becomes **spatially dependent (90° out of phase at pressure nodes)**. Always re-read the wave-type before quoting $p = \rho c \cdot u$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **Define particle velocity. How is it different from the velocity of sound?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Particle velocity** is the instantaneous velocity of a small element of the fluid medium as it vibrates about its equilibrium position when a sound wave passes through it. Mathematically, $u = \partial \xi / \partial t$. **Difference:** | Particle Velocity ($u$) | Velocity of Sound ($c$) | |---|---| | Velocity of an oscillating medium particle | Speed at which the disturbance / phase propagates | | Vector quantity | Scalar quantity | | Depends on amplitude & frequency of source | Depends on medium properties ($K, \rho$) | | Typical value $\sim 10^{-3}$ m/s in speech | $\sim 343$ m/s in air | *(Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the table, 1 mark for the comparison conclusion.)* --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **For a plane progressive sound wave, show that acoustic pressure and particle velocity are in phase.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The displacement field of a plane progressive wave is $\xi = \xi_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$. - Particle velocity: $u = \partial \xi / \partial t = \omega \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$. - Acoustic pressure: $p = -B \partial \xi / \partial x = Bk \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx) = \rho c \, \omega \xi_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$. Both expressions contain the *same* phase factor $\cos(\omega t - kx)$. Hence the phase difference $\Delta \phi = 0$, and **pressure and particle velocity are in phase** in a plane progressive wave. ∎ --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > **Q1(A).** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relation between acoustic pressure and particle velocity for a plane progressive sound wave propagating through a fluid medium. State the significance of the constant of proportionality. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** A plane sound wave of frequency 1 kHz has a pressure amplitude of 1 Pa in air. Calculate (i) the particle velocity amplitude, (ii) the particle displacement amplitude, and (iii) the intensity of the wave. Take $\rho = 1.21\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $c = 343\ \text{m/s}$. **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation [7 marks]** — See Section 3.1 for the complete 5-step derivation. The constant of proportionality $\rho c$ is the **characteristic acoustic impedance** of the medium (in Rayls), which determines the efficiency of energy transfer from the source to the medium. **(b) Numerical [7 marks]** (i) $\rho c = 1.21 \times 343 = 415.03\ \text{Rayls}$ $$u_0 = \frac{p_0}{\rho c} = \frac{1}{415.03} = 2.41 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m/s} = 2.41\ \text{mm/s}$$ (ii) $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi (1000) = 6283.19\ \text{rad/s}$ $$\xi_0 = \frac{u_0}{\omega} = \frac{2.41 \times 10^{-3}}{6283.19} = 3.84 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} = 0.384\ \mu\text{m}$$ (iii) Intensity: $$I = \frac{p_0^{\,2}}{2 \rho c} = \frac{(1)^2}{2 \times 415.03} = 1.205 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W/m}^2 \approx 1.2\ \text{mW/m}^2$$ --- > **Q1(B).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the displacement equation of a plane sound wave, derive expressions for (i) particle velocity and (ii) acoustic pressure. Hence obtain the specific acoustic impedance. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** A loudspeaker produces a sound wave with a particle velocity amplitude of $5\ \text{mm/s}$ in air. Determine (i) the sound pressure level in dB relative to $20\ \mu\text{Pa}$, and (ii) the displacement amplitude if the frequency is 250 Hz. **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 marks]** — Refer Section 3.1 derivation. Specific acoustic impedance $Z_s = p/u = \rho c$. **(b) Numerical [7 marks]** (i) $p_0 = \rho c \cdot u_0 = 415.03 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 2.075\ \text{Pa}$ Sound Pressure Level: $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_0}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.075}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$ $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}(103750) = 20 \times 5.016 = 100.32\ \text{dB}$$ (ii) $\omega = 2\pi f = 1570.8\ \text{rad/s}$ $$\xi_0 = \frac{u_0}{\omega} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1570.8} = 3.18 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} = 3.18\ \mu\text{m}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Unit slip-up:** Forgetting to convert mm/s to m/s before substituting — costs 1 mark per occurrence. > 2. **Reference pressure confusion:** In SPL problems, $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ **in air** (not 1 Pa, not $1\ \mu\text{Pa}$). Mixing up reference values is a classic 1–2 mark trap. > 3. **Phase confusion:** Stating "in phase" without proving it (via common phase factor) — examiners deduct 1 mark. > 4. **Mixing up formulas:** Using $c$ where $u$ is required (or vice versa) in the displacement–velocity relationship. Always re-derive $u = \omega \xi_0$ explicitly. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Particle Velocity** > > - **Definition:** $u = \partial \xi / \partial t$ — instantaneous velocity of an oscillating medium particle. > - **Units:** $\text{m/s}$. Magnitude in speech $\sim 10^{-3}\ \text{m/s}$ — vastly smaller than $c = 343\ \text{m/s}$. > - **Amplitude relation:** $u_0 = \omega \xi_0 = 2\pi f \xi_0$. > - **Plane-wave identity:** $p = \rho c \cdot u$ — pressure and velocity are **in phase**. > - **Specific acoustic impedance:** $Z_s = p/u = \rho c$, in **Rayls** ($\text{kg·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$). > - **Air values:** $\rho c \approx 415$ Rayls. Water: $\rho c \approx 1.5 \times 10^6$ Rayls. > - **Intensity link:** $I = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho c \, u_0^{\,2} = p_0^{\,2} / (2 \rho c)$. > - **Displacement recovery:** $\xi_0 = u_0 / (2\pi f)$ — frequency matters. > - **Phase rule of thumb:** In plane progressive waves, $p \parallel u$. In standing waves, $p$ and $u$ are spatially 90° apart. > - **Engineering relevance:** Microphones measure $p$, but particle-velocity sensors (e.g., Microflown) measure $u$ directly — useful in flow-noise and directional sound-field analysis.

topics.title: Acoustic Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Acoustic Impedance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔊 Module 1 — Acoustic Impedance

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acoustic Impedance (Z)** is defined as the complex ratio of the sound pressure (p) to the corresponding particle velocity (u) at a given surface or within a medium. It characterises the opposition a medium offers to the propagation of acoustic energy.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$Z = \frac{p}{u} \quad \left[ \text{Pa·s/m}^3 \right]$$

Since both pressure and particle velocity are alternating (sinusoidal) quantities, the ratio is a **complex number** carrying both magnitude and phase:

$$Z = R_a + jX_a$$

where:
- $R_a$ = Acoustic Resistance (real part) — represents energy dissipation
- $X_a$ = Acoustic Reactance (imaginary part) — represents energy storage (mass/reactance or stiffness/reactance)

---

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine blowing air through a **narrow straw** versus a **wide pipe**. The narrow straw resists airflow — it has high acoustic impedance. The wide pipe allows easy flow — low impedance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of acoustic impedance as the "electrical resistance" of sound.** Just as an electrical resistor opposes current flow, acoustic impedance opposes the flow of acoustic energy. However, unlike simple resistance, acoustic impedance also stores energy (like a spring stores mechanical energy, or a mass stores kinetic energy) — hence it is a *complex* quantity, not a purely real one.

### 🔄 The Three Principal Variants (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The KTU syllabus distinguishes **three specific forms** of acoustic impedance that students must master:

| Variant | Symbol | Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Specific Acoustic Impedance** | $z$ | Ratio of sound pressure to particle velocity at a **point** in a medium | **Rayl** ($\text{Pa·s/m}$) |
| **Characteristic (Intrinsic) Impedance** | $\rho c$ | Specific impedance of a **plane progressive wave** in an unbounded medium | **Rayl** ($\text{Pa·s/m}$) |
| **Acoustic Impedance** | $Z$ | Ratio of pressure to **volume velocity** across a surface | **Acoustic Ohm** ($\text{Pa·s/m}^3$) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Board Exams:** *Specific = point*; *Characteristic = medium property*; *Acoustic (general) = across a surface*.

### 📐 Standard Values of Characteristic Impedance (Examinable)

| Medium | Density $\rho$ ($\text{kg/m}^3$) | Sound Speed $c$ ($\text{m/s}$) | $\rho c$ (Rayl) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Air (20 °C)** | 1.21 | 343 | **≈ 415 Rayl** |
| Water (20 °C) | 998 | 1480 | ≈ 1.48 × 10⁶ Rayl |
| Steel | 7850 | 5050 | ≈ 39.6 × 10⁶ Rayl |

> [!NOTE]
> The value **$\rho c = 415$ Rayl** for air at standard conditions is a frequently tested constant in KTU exams — memorise it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Each Impedance Variant

### 🔹 Specific Acoustic Impedance ($z$)

- It is a **local, point-based** property of the acoustic field.
- Depends on the **type of wave** (progressive vs. standing vs. evanescent).
- For a *plane progressive wave travelling in the +x direction* in an unbounded medium, it is purely real and equal to $\rho c$.

### 🔹 Characteristic Acoustic Impedance ($\rho c$)

- It is an **intrinsic property of the medium** alone — independent of wave type or boundary conditions.
- Represents the impedance a plane wave "sees" when travelling freely without reflections.
- Plays the same role as the surge impedance of a transmission line in electrical engineering.

### 🔹 Acoustic Impedance ($Z$) — Across a Surface

- Generalised form: $Z = p / U$, where $U = u \cdot S$ is the **volume velocity** (m³/s).
- Used when describing devices, openings, ducts, and loudspeaker diaphragms.

---

## 2.2 Why Acoustic Impedance is a Complex Quantity

The **physical reason** is rooted in Newton's second law and the equation of state:

> Acoustic impedance is complex because the medium possesses **two energy-storage mechanisms**:
> 1. **Inertance (mass-like reactance)** — due to the mass of air particles being accelerated.
> 2. **Compliance (stiffness-like reactance)** — due to compression/rarefaction of the medium.

These are exactly analogous to **inductance** and **capacitance** in electrical circuits.

### 🔁 Direct Electrical ↔ Acoustic Analogy (High-Yield for KTU)

| Acoustic Quantity | Symbol | Electrical Analogue | Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sound pressure | $p$ | Voltage | $V$ |
| Volume velocity | $U$ | Current | $I$ |
| Acoustic impedance | $Z$ | Electrical impedance | $Z_e$ |
| Acoustic mass (inertance) | $M_a$ | Inductance | $L$ |
| Acoustic compliance | $C_a$ | Capacitance | $C$ |
| Acoustic resistance | $R_a$ | Resistance | $R$ |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Acoustic Impedance (Board-Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print-friendly Table — All formulas required for Module 1 problems**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific acoustic impedance | $z = p/u$ | Rayl ($\text{Pa·s/m}$) |
| 2 | Characteristic impedance | $\rho c$ | Medium constant; **415 Rayl** for air |
| 3 | General (complex) impedance | $Z = R_a + jX_a$ | $R_a$ resistive, $X_a$ reactive part |
| 4 | Plane progressive wave | $z = \rho c$ (purely real) | No reflections, no phase shift |
| 5 | Standing wave | $z = -j\rho c \cot(kx)$ | Reactive; varies with position |
| 6 | Closed end of tube | $Z = \infty$ (hard wall) | $u = 0$, pressure antinode |
| 7 | Open end of tube (ideal) | $Z = 0$ (pressure-release) | $p = 0$, velocity antinode |
| 8 | Helmholtz resonator neck (mass) | $M_a = \rho L / S$ | $L$: neck length, $S$: neck area |
| 9 | Helmholtz resonator cavity (compliance) | $C_a = V / (\rho c^2)$ | $V$: cavity volume |
| 10 | Helmholtz resonant frequency | $f_r = \dfrac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{S}{L_e V}}$ | **$L_e = L + 0.8\sqrt{S}$** end correction |
| 11 | Absorption coefficient | $\alpha = \dfrac{4\,\text{Re}(Z_s)/\rho c}{\bigl(\text{Re}(Z_s)/\rho c + 1\bigr)^2 + \bigl(\text{Im}(Z_s)/\rho c\bigr)^2}$ | $Z_s$: surface impedance |
| 12 | Absorption coeff. (special: $Z_s$ real) | $\alpha = \dfrac{4\,R/\rho c}{\bigl(R/\rho c + 1\bigr)^2}$ | Purely resistive surface |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Acoustic impedance is **the central concept** behind every practical noise-control device:

| Application | How Impedance is Used |
|---|---|
| 🎙️ Microphone design | Match microphone diaphragm impedance to air for maximum energy transfer. |
| 🔊 Loudspeaker enclosures | Bass-reflex ports are tuned to match impedance peaks. |
| 🏭 Industrial silencers | Reactive silencers use **impedance mismatch** to reflect noise. |
| 🎧 Hearing aids | Custom earpiece impedance matching to the ear canal. |
| 🚗 Automotive NVH | Absorptive materials designed for $\rho c$ matching. |
| 🏛️ Concert hall acoustics | Surface impedance determines reverberation time. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Specific Acoustic Impedance of a Plane Progressive Wave

**Objective:** Prove that for a plane wave travelling in $+x$ direction, $z = \rho c$ (purely real).

### Starting Point — Plane Wave Equations

Consider a plane harmonic wave travelling in the $+x$ direction in an unbounded, lossless medium. The acoustic pressure and particle velocity are:

$$p(x,t) = p_0 \, e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$$

$$u(x,t) = u_0 \, e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$$

### Step 1 — Apply Euler's Linearised Momentum Equation

Newton's second law for a fluid element (linearised form) gives:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate $p$ With Respect to $x$

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[ p_0 \, e^{j(\omega t - kx)} \right] = -jk \, p_0 \, e^{j(\omega t - kx)} = -jk \, p$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate $u$ With Respect to $t$

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left[ u_0 \, e^{j(\omega t - kx)} \right] = j\omega \, u$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into Euler's Equation

$$\rho_0 \, (j\omega u) = -(-jk \, p) = jk \, p$$

$$j\omega \rho_0 u = jk \, p$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Ratio $p/u$

Cancel $j$ from both sides:

$$\omega \rho_0 u = k \, p$$

$$\frac{p}{u} = \frac{\omega \rho_0}{k}$$

### Step 6 — Introduce the Wave Speed $c = \omega/k$

Since $c = \omega / k$, we have $\omega/k = c$. Substituting:

$$\frac{p}{u} = \rho_0 \, c$$

### Step 7 — Conclusion

$$\boxed{\,z = \frac{p}{u} = \rho_0 c\,}$$

> **Interpretation:** The specific acoustic impedance of a plane progressive wave is a **purely real number** equal to the product of ambient density and sound speed. This is the **characteristic acoustic impedance**. Since no phase shift exists between $p$ and $u$, **no energy is stored** — all energy propagates forward. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Specific Acoustic Impedance for Standing Waves in a Closed Tube

**Objective:** Show that standing waves produce a **reactive (imaginary) impedance** that depends on position.

### Standing Wave Construction

A standing wave forms from the superposition of two plane waves of equal amplitude travelling in opposite directions:

$$p(x,t) = p_0 \left[ e^{j(\omega t - kx)} + e^{j(\omega t + kx)} \right]$$

Using Euler's formula, this simplifies to:

$$p(x,t) = 2 p_0 \cos(kx) \, e^{j\omega t}$$

For the particle velocity (differentiating with respect to $t$):

$$u(x,t) = \frac{2 p_0}{\rho c} j\sin(kx) \, e^{j\omega t}$$

> *Note the $j$ factor in $u$ — this signals a 90° phase shift relative to $p$.*

### Step 1 — Form the Specific Impedance $z = p/u$

$$z(x) = \frac{2 p_0 \cos(kx) \, e^{j\omega t}}{\frac{2 p_0}{\rho c} j\sin(kx) \, e^{j\omega t}}$$

### Step 2 — Cancel Common Terms

$$z(x) = \frac{\cos(kx)}{j\sin(kx)} \cdot \rho c$$

### Step 3 — Use $1/j = -j$

$$z(x) = -j\rho c \cot(kx)$$

### Step 4 — Final Result

$$\boxed{\,z(x) = -j\rho c \cot(kx)\,}$$

### Step 5 — Physical Boundary Interpretations

| Location in tube | $\cot(kx)$ | Specific Impedance $z$ | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure antinode (closed end) | $0$ | $z = 0$ ? — *Wait, re-check* | Re-examine carefully below |
| Velocity antinode (open end) | $\infty$ | $z = -j\rho c \cdot 0 = 0$ | Pressure node ✓ |
| Closed end ($u=0$) | $\cot \to \infty$ | $z \to \infty$ | Hard wall ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Careful — the table above should be read as:** At the **closed end** ($x=0$ for a tube closed at $x=0$), $\cot(0) \to \infty$, so $z \to \infty$. At the **open end**, where $kx = \pi/2$, $\cot(\pi/2) = 0$, so $z = 0$. This matches the physical boundary conditions.

**Engineering Insight:** The purely imaginary impedance proves that **standing waves store energy** but transfer *no net energy*. This is why concert halls with strong standing waves exhibit poor acoustic clarity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Resonant Frequency of a Helmholtz Resonator

**Setup:** A rigid-walled cavity of volume $V$ connected to the outside air through a short neck of cross-sectional area $S$ and effective length $L_e$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Two Energy-Storage Elements

The Helmholtz resonator behaves as a **lumped acoustic system**:
- **Inertance (mass)** $M_a$ of air oscillating in the neck.
- **Compliance (spring)** $C_a$ of the air trapped in the cavity.

### Step 2 — Calculate the Acoustic Mass $M_a$

The mass of air in the neck undergoing oscillatory motion:

$$M_a = \frac{\rho L_e}{S}$$

where $L_e = L + 0.8\sqrt{S}$ is the **effective length** including the end correction.

### Step 3 — Calculate the Acoustic Compliance $C_a$

The compliance is volume divided by bulk modulus ($\rho c^2$):

$$C_a = \frac{V}{\rho c^2}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Standard Mass–Spring Resonance Formula

The mechanical resonance of a mass–spring system:

$$\omega_r = \frac{1}{\sqrt{M_a C_a}}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $M_a$ and $C_a$

$$\omega_r = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho L_e}{S} \cdot \dfrac{V}{\rho c^2}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\dfrac{L_e V}{S c^2}}}$$

### Step 6 — Simplify

$$\omega_r = c \sqrt{\frac{S}{L_e V}}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_r = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{S}{L_e V}}\,}$$

> **Numerical Example (KTU Typical):** For $V = 1000 \text{ cm}^3$, $S = 10 \text{ cm}^2$, $L = 5 \text{ cm}$, in air at $c = 343$ m/s, then $L_e \approx 5 + 0.8\sqrt{10} \approx 7.53$ cm, giving $f_r \approx 179$ Hz — useful for attenuating low-frequency rumble.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Wave / Boundary | $z$ Value | Energy Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Plane wave (free field) | $\rho c$ (real) | Pure propagation ✓ |
| Standing wave at pressure antinode | $\to \infty$ | Maximum pressure storage |
| Standing wave at velocity antinode | $0$ | Maximum velocity storage |
| Closed end (rigid wall) | $\to \infty$ | Total reflection, $u = 0$ |
| Open end (ideal pressure release) | $0$ | Total reflection, $p = 0$ |
| Helmholtz resonator (at $f_r$) | Real and maximum | Maximum absorption possible |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Acoustic Impedance (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following functional architecture flow maps the **types, sub-types, and physical meanings** of acoustic impedance — replacing what would otherwise be a complex multi-panel illustration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acoustic Impedance Domain] --> B[Specific Acoustic Impedance z]
    A --> C[Characteristic Impedance rho_c]
    A --> D[Acoustic Impedance Z]

    B --> B1[Local point property]
    B --> B2[Complex quantity]
    B --> B3[Varies with wave type]

    B3 --> B31[Plane wave: z equals rho_c real]
    B3 --> B32[Standing wave: reactive cotangent]
    B3 --> B33[Evanescent wave: imaginary exponential]

    C --> C1[Medium property only]
    C --> C2[Air 415 Rayl]
    C --> C3[Water 1.48 MRayl]

    D --> D1[Surface or device level]
    D --> D2[Ratio of p to volume velocity U]
    D --> D3[Used in duct and muffler design]

    D3 --> D31[Closed tube end: infinite Z]
    D3 --> D32[Open tube end: zero Z]
    D3 --> D33[Helmholtz resonator at resonance: matched Z]
```

---

## 4.2 Impedance Behaviour Along a Closed-Open Tube (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Closed End x equals 0] -->|z tends to infinity| P1[Pressure Antinode Region]
    P1 -->|cot decreases| P2[Quarter Wavelength Point]
    P2 -->|z equals zero| P3[Open End x equals L]
    P3 -->|pressure node| P4[Velocity Antinode Region]

    P0 -.Boundary.-> BNote1[u equals 0 rigid wall]
    P3 -.Boundary.-> BNote2[p equals 0 pressure release]

    classDef boundary fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffcc00,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef region fill:#2d5a87,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    class P0,P3 boundary
    class P1,P2,P4 region
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — How Impedance Connects to Engineering Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IM[Acoustic Impedance Concept] --> APP1[Noise Control]
    IM --> APP2[Audio Engineering]
    IM --> APP3[Room Acoustics]

    APP1 --> APP1a[Reactive Silencers]
    APP1 --> APP1b[Absorptive Linings]
    APP1 --> APP1c[Helmholtz Resonators]

    APP2 --> APP2a[Microphone Matching]
    APP2 --> APP2b[Loudspeaker Enclosures]

    APP3 --> APP3a[Reverberation Control]
    APP3 --> APP3b[Surface Absorption Design]

    APP1c --> FR[Resonant Frequency fr equals c over 2pi times sqrt of S over L V]
    APP1b --> AC[Absorption Coefficient alpha equals function of Zs over rho_c]

    classDef appbox fill:#0d3b66,stroke:#faa613,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef corebox fill:#faa613,stroke:#0d3b66,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    class APP1,APP2,APP3,APP1a,APP1b,APP1c,APP2a,APP2b,APP3a,APP3b appbox
    class IM,FR,AC corebox
```

> **Reading Guide:** The yellow (core) blocks are the **fundamental equations** you must memorise; the blue (application) blocks indicate **where they show up in real engineering** — exactly the kind of "application" question KTU examiners love to set.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

---

### **Q1. Define acoustic impedance. Mention its SI unit and state its significance in noise control engineering.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acoustic Impedance (Z)** is defined as the complex ratio of sound pressure to the corresponding particle velocity (or volume velocity) at a surface or in a medium.
> 
> $$Z = \frac{p}{u} \quad \text{or} \quad Z = \frac{p}{U}$$
> 
> **SI Unit:** $\text{Pa·s/m}^3$ (acoustic ohm) for surface impedance; Rayl ($\text{Pa·s/m}$) for specific acoustic impedance.
> 
> **Significance:**
> 1. Determines how much acoustic energy is reflected vs. transmitted/absorbed at a boundary — fundamental to **silencer and absorber design**.
> 2. Allows prediction of standing-wave patterns in ducts and rooms.
> 3. Used to characterise the human ear's response to different frequencies.

---

### **Q2. Distinguish between specific acoustic impedance and characteristic acoustic impedance.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Specific Acoustic Impedance ($z$) | Characteristic Impedance ($\rho c$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Ratio of sound pressure to particle velocity at a point | Specific impedance of a plane progressive wave in an unbounded medium |
| **Nature** | Generally complex; depends on wave type | Purely real for plane waves in lossless media |
| **Depends on** | Boundary conditions, wave geometry | Medium properties ($\rho$, $c$) only |
| **Unit** | Rayl | Rayl |
| **Value in air (20 °C)** | Varies (e.g., $\rho c$ for plane waves; $-j\rho c\cot(kx)$ for standing waves) | **415 Rayl** (constant) |

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Module Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**
> *Each question carries sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Internal Choice is provided between Question A and Question B.*

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Module 1: Acoustic Impedance)*

#### **Part (a): Derive the expression for specific acoustic impedance of a plane progressive wave. Show that it equals the characteristic impedance of the medium.** *(7 Marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the wave equations.** For a plane harmonic wave travelling in $+x$ direction:

$$p(x,t) = p_0 \, e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$$

$$u(x,t) = u_0 \, e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Euler's linearised equation of motion.**

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate to obtain the spatial and temporal derivatives.**

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = -jk\, p, \qquad \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = j\omega\, u$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Euler's equation.**

$$\rho_0 \, (j\omega u) = -(-jk\, p) \implies j\omega\rho_0 u = jk\, p$$

**Step 5 — Take the ratio $p/u$.**

$$\frac{p}{u} = \frac{\omega\rho_0}{k}$$

**Step 6 — Use $c = \omega / k$.**

$$\boxed{\,z = \frac{p}{u} = \rho_0 c\,}$$

**Step 7 — Conclude.** Since $\rho_0 c$ is purely real, a plane progressive wave has no phase difference between pressure and velocity. Hence $z = \rho_0 c$ is identically equal to the **characteristic impedance** of the medium. ✅

---

#### **Part (b): A uniform tube of length 1.5 m is closed at one end and open at the other. Calculate the first three resonant frequencies. Take $c = 343$ m/s.** *(7 Marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the boundary condition.** Closed–open tube supports standing waves at **odd quarter-wavelengths only**:

$$L = \frac{(2n-1)\lambda_n}{4}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

**Step 2 — Express the resonant frequency.**

$$f_n = \frac{(2n-1)c}{4L}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate for $n = 1$.**

$$f_1 = \frac{(1)(343)}{4 \times 1.5} = \frac{343}{6} \approx 57.17 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate for $n = 2$.**

$$f_2 = \frac{(3)(343)}{6} = \frac{1029}{6} \approx 171.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate for $n = 3$.**

$$f_3 = \frac{(5)(343)}{6} = \frac{1715}{6} \approx 285.83 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 6 — Tabulate the results.**

| Mode ($n$) | Resonant Frequency |
|---|---|
| 1 | **57.17 Hz** |
| 2 | **171.5 Hz** |
| 3 | **285.83 Hz** |

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative to Question A)*

#### **Part (a): Derive the expression for the resonant frequency of a Helmholtz resonator. Explain its application as a noise-control device.** *(7 Marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.** A Helmholtz resonator consists of a rigid cavity of volume $V$ connected to the outside through a neck of area $S$ and effective length $L_e = L + 0.8\sqrt{S}$.

**Step 2 — Identify acoustic mass (inertance) of neck air.**

$$M_a = \frac{\rho L_e}{S}$$

**Step 3 — Identify acoustic compliance (springiness) of cavity air.**

$$C_a = \frac{V}{\rho c^2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply resonance condition for a mass–spring system.**

$$\omega_r = \frac{1}{\sqrt{M_a C_a}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$\omega_r = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho L_e}{S} \cdot \dfrac{V}{\rho c^2}}} = c\sqrt{\frac{S}{L_e V}}$$

**Step 6 — Resonant frequency.**

$$\boxed{\,f_r = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{S}{L_e V}}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Noise-control application.** A Helmholtz resonator, when tuned to a target frequency, presents a high acoustic resistance at that frequency — **absorbing** rather than reflecting the acoustic energy. This makes it ideal for attenuating **low-frequency tonal noise** (e.g., diesel-generator hum, transformer noise, HVAC duct rumble).

---

#### **Part (b): With a neat diagram, explain the concept of acoustic impedance matching and impedance mismatch at a boundary.** *(7 Marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define impedance matching.** Acoustic impedance matching refers to the condition in which the impedance of two adjacent media (or a source and its load) is equal, ensuring **maximum energy transfer** with **minimum reflection**.

**Step 2 — Reflection coefficient.** The pressure reflection coefficient at a boundary between media of impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ is:

$$R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

**Step 3 — Special cases.**
- If $Z_1 = Z_2$, then $R = 0$ → **perfect transmission, no reflection** (matched).
- If $Z_2 \gg Z_1$ (e.g., air–steel), then $R \to 1$ → **total reflection** (mismatched).

**Step 4 — Impedance mismatch at a closed/open end.**
- **Closed end** ($\rho c \to \infty$): acts as a hard boundary — full reflection with pressure doubling.
- **Open end** ($\rho c \to 0$): acts as a pressure-release — full reflection with phase inversion.

**Step 5 — Engineering example.** A loudspeaker diaphragm (very high impedance source) couples poorly to free air. A **horn** is inserted to provide a **gradual impedance transition** from the high diaphragm impedance to the low air impedance, dramatically improving efficiency — this is the principle of the megaphone.

**Step 6 — Schematic description.**

```
   Source (Z1)  ----gradual taper---->  Load (Z2)
   high impedance                       low impedance
        |                                     |
        +----- smooth horn/wedge -----+
        reflection: MIN   transmission: MAX
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Confusing **Rayl** (specific) with **acoustic ohm** (general). KTU examiners deduct **½ mark** each time you mix them up.
> 2. **End correction oversight:** Helmholtz and tube problems frequently forget the **$+0.8\sqrt{S}$ end correction** on the neck length. This costs **1–2 marks** in part (b) numerical problems.
> 3. **Imaginary part sign error:** Writing $\cot$ when the correct form is $-j\cot$ (or vice versa) for standing-wave specific impedance. Verify by checking the **closed-end limit** ($z \to \infty$).
> 4. **Forgetting $V$ in Helmholtz formula:** Students often write $f = (c/2\pi)\sqrt{S/L_e}$, omitting the volume $V$ — a guaranteed **2-mark loss**.
> 5. **Plug-and-chug without explanation:** KTU awards marks for stating the **physical interpretation** of the result, not just the numerical answer. Always end derivations with a **one-line conclusion**.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary for Last-Minute Revision**

- ✅ **Acoustic Impedance** $Z = p/u$ — a **complex** quantity (because pressure and velocity can be out of phase).
- ✅ Three flavours: **Specific** ($z$, in Rayl), **Characteristic** ($\rho c$, in Rayl), **General** ($Z$, in acoustic ohm).
- ✅ **$\rho c = 415$ Rayl** for air at 20 °C — **memorise this number**.
- ✅ For a **plane progressive wave**: $z = \rho c$ (purely real, no phase shift).
- ✅ For a **standing wave**: $z = -j\rho c \cot(kx)$ (purely imaginary, energy stored).
- ✅ **Closed end**: $z \to \infty$ (hard wall, pressure antinode).
- ✅ **Open end**: $z \to 0$ (pressure-release, velocity antinode).
- ✅ **Helmholtz resonator frequency:** $f_r = \dfrac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{S}{L_e V}}$ with $L_e = L + 0.8\sqrt{S}$.
- ✅ **Resonance** occurs when inertial and compliance reactances cancel — impedance is purely resistive.
- ✅ **Maximum absorption** happens when $\text{Re}(Z_s) = \rho c$ (impedance matching condition).
- ✅ **Acoustic ↔ Electrical analogy:** $p \leftrightarrow V$, $U \leftrightarrow I$, $Z \leftrightarrow Z_e$, $M_a \leftrightarrow L$, $C_a \leftrightarrow C$.
- ✅ Real part of $Z$ = dissipation; imaginary part = energy storage.
- ✅ Engineering uses: silencer design, room acoustics, speaker enclosures, hearing aids, NVH.
- ✅ Closed-open tubes resonate at **odd quarter wavelengths**: $f_n = (2n-1)c/4L$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acoustic Power and Intensity, Inverse Square Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Acoustic Power and Intensity, Inverse Square Law # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Acoustic (Sound) Power **Acoustic Power ($W$)** is formally defined as the time-averaged rate at which sound energy is radiated by a source per unit time into the surrounding medium. It is a property *of the source itself* and is independent of the listener's position or the surrounding environment (as long as the medium is lossless). - **SI Unit:** **Watt (W)** - **Dimensional Formula:** $[M L^2 T^{-3}]$ - **Typical Magnitudes:** A normal human conversation produces about **$10^{-5}$ W** (10 microwatts). A jet engine at takeoff radiates roughly **$10^{3}$ to $10^{4}$ W**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Sound power is an *intrinsic property of the source*. It cannot be measured directly — it must be calculated from sound pressure or sound intensity measurements taken in a defined acoustic environment. ## 1.2 Acoustic (Sound) Intensity **Sound Intensity ($I$)** is defined as the time-averaged acoustic power transmitted per unit area in a specified direction.
