In vector form, it is the product of the RMS sound pressure and the RMS particle velocity in the direction of wave propagation. - **SI Unit:** **Watt per square meter ($W/m^2$)** - **Vector Quantity:** It has both magnitude and direction (the direction of energy flow). - **Reference Intensity:** $I_{ref} = 10^{-12} \, W/m^2$ (the standard threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz). > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Distinction (Frequently Tested):** > - **Power ($W$):** Energy *per second* — a property of the **source** (Total energy output). > - **Intensity ($I$):** Power *per unit area* — a property of the **sound field** (How concentrated the energy is at a point). > - **Pressure ($p$):** Local *fluctuation in atmospheric pressure* — a property of the **medium** (What a microphone actually measures). ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a **light bulb (the source)** placed inside a completely transparent spherical **glass balloon**. - **Acoustic Power ($W$):** This is the **total wattage** printed on the light bulb (e.g., 60 W). It tells you how much light energy the bulb pumps out per second. The bulb doesn't "know" or "care" how big the room is. - **Acoustic Intensity ($I$):** This is the **brightness** of the light hitting a specific patch of the balloon's surface. If you make the balloon smaller, the light is concentrated into a smaller area (higher intensity). If you make the balloon larger, the same light spreads over a larger area (lower intensity). - **Inverse Square Law:** If you double the radius of the balloon, the surface area becomes 4 times larger, so the brightness (intensity) at any point on the surface becomes **4 times dimmer** ($1/2^2 = 1/4$). In this analogy, the sound waves act like expanding light, the source radiates at a constant total power, and the "brightness" of the sound drops as you walk further away. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Foundations The fundamental acoustic quantities are interconnected through the following hierarchical relationships: **Step 1 — RMS Sound Pressure ($p_{rms}$):** Sound pressure fluctuates rapidly between positive and negative values. Because it is a sinusoidally varying quantity, the RMS value captures its effective energy-bearing magnitude: $$p_{rms} = \frac{p_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$$ **Step 2 — Sound Intensity in a Plane Wave (Far Field):** In the far field, where the wavefronts become effectively planar, sound pressure ($p$) and particle velocity ($u$) are in phase. Intensity becomes a scalar: $$I = p_{rms} \cdot u_{rms} = \frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 \cdot c}$$ where: - $\rho_0$ = **ambient density of air** ($\approx 1.21 \, kg/m^3$ at $20^{\circ}C$) - $c$ = **speed of sound in air** ($\approx 343 \, m/s$ at $20^{\circ}C$) - $\rho_0 c$ = **characteristic acoustic impedance of air** ($\approx 415 \, \text{Pa·s/m}$, also called Rayl) **Step 3 — Total Acoustic Power from Intensity:** The total power radiated by a source is obtained by integrating the intensity vector over any closed surface $S$ surrounding the source (this is Kirchhoff's acoustic analogue): $$W = \oint_S I \, dS$$ **Step 4 — Power of a Point Source (Spherical Wave):** For an ideal omnidirectional point source, the intensity at a distance $r$ is: $$I = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}$$ **Step 5 — Power of a Line Source (Cylindrical Wave):** For an ideal infinite line source (e.g., a long busy highway), the intensity at a distance $r$ is: $$I = \frac{W}{2\pi r L}$$ where $L$ is the length of the line source considered. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary / Use Case | |---|--------------|----------|-------------------|---------------------| | 1 | RMS Pressure | $p_{rms} = p_{peak} / \sqrt{2}$ | $p$ in Pa | Sinusoidal waves | | 2 | Intensity (Plane Wave) | $I = p_{rms}^{\,2} / (\rho_0 c)$ | $I$ in $W/m^2$, $p$ in Pa, $\rho_0 c$ in Rayl | Far field, free space | | 3 | Total Power | $W = \oint_S I \, dS$ | $W$ in W, $I$ in $W/m^2$ | Any closed surface around source | | 4 | Point Source Intensity | $I = W / (4\pi r^{2})$ | $W$ in W, $r$ in m | Omnidirectional source | | 5 | Line Source Intensity | $I = W_{per\,m} / (2\pi r)$ | $r$ in m | Infinite line source | | 6 | Inverse Square Law | $I_2 / I_1 = (r_1 / r_2)^{2}$ | Ratio form | Free field, far from source | | 7 | Inverse Square (Pressure) | $p_2 / p_1 = r_1 / r_2$ | Ratio form | Free field, far from source | | 8 | Sound Power Level | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W / W_{ref})$ | $W_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W | Source rating | | 9 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_{ref})$ | $I_{ref} = 10^{-12} \, W/m^2$ | Field measurement | | 10 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms} / p_{ref})$ | $p_{ref} = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ | Standard SPL meter | | 11 | Conversion $L_W \leftrightarrow L_I$ (Free Field) | $L_W = L_I + 10 \log_{10}(S)$ | $S$ in $m^2$ | Over surface $S$ | | 12 | Point Source: $L_W \leftrightarrow L_p$ | $L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11$ | $r$ in m | Free field point source | ## 2.3 Engineering Utility - **Source Ranking & Compliance:** Equipment manufacturers (e.g., HVAC units, compressors) quote **Sound Power Level ($L_W$)** in dB because it is environment-independent. Regulatory bodies then convert it to **Sound Pressure Level ($L_p$)** at a receiver by adding room acoustics. - **Noise Mapping:** Urban planners compute $L_p$ at building facades using the Inverse Square Law and directivity factors for traffic corridors. - **Microphone Calibration:** Sound intensity probes use the $p$-$u$ relationship ($I = p \cdot u$) to map energy flow and identify noise sources in situ — crucial for locating leaks in ducts or panels. - **Hearing Conservation:** OSHA and ISO standards express permissible exposure limits in $L_p$ (dBA), which ultimately traces back to the source's $L_W$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse Square Law for a Point Source ### Assumptions - The source is an **ideal omnidirectional point source**. - The medium is **homogeneous** (uniform $\rho_0$, $c$) and **lossless** (no absorption). - Radiation occurs into a **free field** (no reflections — anechoic environment). - Wavefronts are **spherical**, expanding radially outward from the source at speed $c$. ### Step-by-Step Logical Deduction **Step 1:** The total acoustic power $W$ leaving the source is constant in time (assuming a steady-state source). $$W = \text{constant (in a lossless medium)}$$ **Step 2:** At any radial distance $r$ from the point source, the wavefront is a sphere of surface area $A_{sphere}$. $$A_{sphere} = 4 \pi r^{2}$$ **Step 3:** By the conservation of energy principle (since there is no absorption), the *same* total power $W$ passes through *every* concentric spherical surface surrounding the source. $$W = \oint_S I \, dS = I(r) \cdot 4\pi r^{2}$$ **Step 4:** Solving the energy conservation equation for intensity $I(r)$ at distance $r$. $$I(r) = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}$$ **Step 5:** Taking the ratio of intensities at two different distances, $r_1$ and $r_2$, eliminates the constant $W$ and $4\pi$. $$\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{W / (4\pi r_1^{2})}{W / (4\pi r_2^{2})} = \frac{r_2^{2}}{r_1^{2}}$$ **Step 6:** Rearranging into the canonical form of the **Inverse Square Law**: $$\boxed{\;\frac{I_2}{I_1} = \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^{2}\;}$$ **Step 7 (Pressure Form):** In the far field, substituting $I \propto p_{rms}^{\,2}$ yields the pressure decay law (which is only an *inverse-first-power* relationship, not inverse-square): $$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{r_1}{r_2} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{L_{p,2}}{L_{p,1}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why the pressure form is $\frac{1}{r}$ and not $\frac{1}{r^2}$:** Intensity is proportional to the *square* of pressure ($I = p^2 / \rho_0 c$). Therefore, halving the distance quadruples $I$ but only doubles $p$. The squared dependence is "absorbed" into the squaring operation when you move from pressure to intensity. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Sound Pressure Level from a Point Source **Step 1:** Define the Sound Pressure Level at distance $r$: $$L_p(r) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}(r)}{p_{ref}}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Apply the pressure-distance relation $p_{rms}(r) \propto 1/r$, so $p_{rms}(r) = p_{rms}(1\,m) \cdot (1/r)$. $$L_p(r) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}(1\,m)}{r \cdot p_{ref}}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Expand the logarithm using $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$. $$L_p(r) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}(1\,m)}{p_{ref}}\right) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{r}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Recognize the first term as the SPL at 1 meter ($L_{p,1m}$) and simplify the second term. $$L_p(r) = L_{p,1m} - 20 \log_{10}(r)$$ **Step 5:** To relate this to the source's Sound Power Level $L_W$, we use $L_W = L_I + 10\log_{10}(4\pi r^{2}) = L_I + 10\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(r)$. Since in free field $L_I \approx L_p$ (plane-wave approximation far from source), substitute $10\log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 11$. $$\boxed{\;L_p(r) = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11\;}$$ This is the canonical "free-field point-source" equation used universally in noise prediction. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A small industrial fan radiates a Sound Power Level of $L_W = 88 \, \text{dB re } 10^{-12} \, \text{W}$. Calculate (a) the acoustic power in Watts, (b) the Sound Intensity at $r = 5 \, \text{m}$, and (c) the SPL at the same distance in a free field. ### Part (a): Acoustic Power **Step 1:** Invert the decibel definition $L_W = 10\log_{10}(W/W_{ref})$: $$W = W_{ref} \cdot 10^{L_W / 10}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $W_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W and $L_W = 88$ dB: $$W = 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{88/10} = 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{8.8}$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate $10^{8.8} = 10^{0.8} \cdot 10^{8} \approx 6.31 \times 10^{8}$: $$W = 6.31 \times 10^{8} \cdot 10^{-12} = 6.31 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{W}$$ $$\boxed{W \approx 0.631 \, \text{mW}}$$ ### Part (b): Intensity at $r = 5 \, \text{m}$ **Step 1:** Apply the point-source equation: $$I = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $W = 6.31 \times 10^{-4}$ W, $r = 5$ m: $$I = \frac{6.31 \times 10^{-4}}{4\pi (5)^{2}} = \frac{6.31 \times 10^{-4}}{314.16}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate: $$I \approx 2.01 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{W/m}^{2}$$ ### Part (c): Sound Pressure Level at $r = 5 \, \text{m}$ **Step 1:** Use the derived free-field point-source equation: $$L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $L_W = 88$ dB, $r = 5$ m: $$L_p = 88 - 20 \log_{10}(5) - 11$$ **Step 3:** Compute $\log_{10}(5) \approx 0.6990$: $$L_p = 88 - 20(0.6990) - 11 = 88 - 13.98 - 11$$ $$\boxed{L_p \approx 63.0 \, \text{dB re } 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Concept Map: The Acoustic Quantity Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["SOUND SOURCE
Mechanical Vibration"]:::source --> nodeB["ACOUSTIC POWER W
Total Energy per Second
Unit: Watts (W)"]:::power nodeB --> nodeC["Sound Power Level Lw
Lw = 10 log10 W/Wref
Wref = 10 to power -12 W"]:::level nodeB --> nodeD["GEOMETRIC SPREADING
Spherical or Cylindrical"]:::spread nodeD --> nodeE["SOUND INTENSITY I
Power per Unit Area
Unit: W per square meter"]:::intensity nodeE --> nodeF["Sound Intensity Level LI
LI = 10 log10 I/Iref
Iref = 10 to power -12 W per m2"]:::level2 nodeE --> nodeG["INVERSE SQUARE LAW
I proportional to 1 over r squared"]:::law nodeG --> nodeH["SOUND PRESSURE p
Local Pressure Fluctuation
Unit: Pascals (Pa)"]:::pressure nodeH --> nodeI["Sound Pressure Level Lp
Lp = 20 log10 pms/pref
pref = 20 micro Pascal"]:::level3 nodeH --> nodeJ["PARTICLE VELOCITY u
Medium Motion
Unit: m per second"]:::velocity classDef source fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef power fill:#ff7f50,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef level fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef spread fill:#98fb98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef intensity fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef level2 fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef law fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#8b008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pressure fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#8b8b00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef level3 fill:#fffacd,stroke:#daa520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef velocity fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#483d8b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: From Source to Receiver ```mermaid block-beta columns 5 block:src["ACOUSTIC SOURCE"] src block:pow["RADIATED POWER W (Watts)"] pow block:geom["GEOMETRY OF SPREADING\nSpherical: 4 pi r squared\nCylindrical: 2 pi r L"] geom block:field["SOUND FIELD AT RECEIVER\nIntensity I W per m2\nPressure p Pa"] field block:rcv["RECEIVER / MICROPHONE\nConverts p to Lp dB"] rcv src --> pow pow --> geom geom --> field field --> rcv style src fill:#ff8c00,color:#fff style pow fill:#dc143c,color:#fff style geom fill:#9370db,color:#fff style field fill:#4682b4,color:#fff style rcv fill:#2e8b57,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Decibel Level Conversion Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeP["W in Watts"]:::in --> nodePL["Lw = 10 log10 W over Wref"]:::calc nodePL --> nodeAdd["Sum or Subtract\nGeometric Term"]:::add nodeAdd --> nodeI["I in W per m2"]:::mid nodeI --> nodeIL["LI = 10 log10 I over Iref"]:::calc2 nodeIL --> nodeAdd nodeAdd --> nodep["p in Pa"]:::mid2 nodep --> nodeLp["Lp = 20 log10 p over pref"]:::calc3 classDef in fill:#fafad2,stroke:#8b8b00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef calc fill:#ffdead,stroke:#8b6914,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef add fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mid fill:#afeeee,stroke:#008080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef calc2 fill:#ffdead,stroke:#8b6914,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mid2 fill:#afeeee,stroke:#008080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef calc3 fill:#ffdead,stroke:#8b6914,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Inverse Square Law Operational Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeS["SOURCE at distance r1\nIntensity I1"]:::s --> nodeEq["Apply Energy Conservation\nW = I times Area = Constant"]:::eq nodeEq --> nodeR["RECEIVER at distance r2\nIntensity I2"]:::r nodeS --> nodeRatio["RATIO EQUATION\nI2 divided by I1 = r1 over r2 all squared"]:::ratio nodeR --> nodeRatio nodeRatio --> nodeDec["DECIBEL FORM\ndelta Lp = negative 20 log10 r2 over r1"]:::dec nodeDec --> nodeEx["For Doubling Distance r2 = 2 r1\nLp drops by 6 dB\nI drops by 6 dB too"]:::ex classDef s fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef eq fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#8b8b00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef r fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ratio fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#8b008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dec fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ex fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]** Define **acoustic power** and **acoustic intensity**. State their SI units and reference values used for decibel calculations. **Model Answer:** **Acoustic Power ($W$):** The time-averaged rate at which acoustic (sound) energy is radiated by a source into the surrounding medium per unit time. It is an intrinsic property of the source. - **SI Unit:** Watt (W) - **Reference value for $L_W$:** $W_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W **Acoustic Intensity ($I$):** The time-averaged acoustic power transmitted through a unit area normal to the direction of wave propagation. It is a vector quantity representing the directional energy flux density of a sound field. - **SI Unit:** Watt per square meter ($W/m^2$) - **Reference value for $L_I$:** $I_{ref} = 10^{-12} \, W/m^2$ --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]** State the **Inverse Square Law** for a point source. By what factor does the sound pressure level decrease when the distance from a point source is doubled? **Model Answer:** The **Inverse Square Law** states that the acoustic intensity from an omnidirectional point source in a free field is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the source: $$\frac{I_2}{I_1} = \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^{2}$$ **Pressure Level Drop on Doubling Distance:** Since $p \propto 1/r$, the SPL decreases by: $$\Delta L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1}{2 r_1}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(0.5) = -6.02 \, \text{dB}$$ **Result:** The SPL drops by approximately **6 dB** for every doubling of distance from a point source. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A — Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End Semester Evaluation, Module 1]** **(a)** Derive the relationship between **sound intensity and sound pressure** in a plane progressive wave. Define the term **acoustic impedance** of the medium. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A point source radiates an acoustic power of $W = 0.02 \, \text{W}$ uniformly in all directions into free air. Calculate: (i) The sound intensity at a distance of 10 m. (ii) The RMS sound pressure at the same distance. Take $\rho_0 = 1.21 \, kg/m^3$ and $c = 343 \, m/s$. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation:** In a plane progressive wave traveling in the positive $x$ direction, the instantaneous sound pressure $p(t)$ and the instantaneous particle velocity $u(t)$ are related by: $$p(t) = \rho_0 c \, u(t)$$ This is the plane-wave relationship in the acoustic far field, where the specific acoustic impedance $Z_s = \rho_0 c$ is real and constant. The instantaneous intensity is the product of instantaneous pressure and instantaneous particle velocity: $$I(t) = p(t) \cdot u(t) = \frac{p(t)^{2}}{\rho_0 c}$$ For a sinusoidal source, $p(t) = p_{peak} \cos(\omega t)$, so the time-averaged intensity over one period $T$ is: $$\langle I \rangle = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T} \frac{p_{peak}^{2} \cos^{2}(\omega t)}{\rho_0 c} \, dt$$ Using the identity $\langle \cos^{2}(\omega t) \rangle = 1/2$ and the RMS pressure $p_{rms}^{2} = p_{peak}^{2} / 2$: $$\boxed{I = \frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 c}}$$ The quantity $Z_s = \rho_0 c$ is called the **characteristic acoustic impedance** of the medium (in Rayl or Pa·s/m). For air at $20^{\circ}C$, $Z_s \approx 415 \, \text{Rayl}$. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **(i) Intensity at $r = 10$ m:** Apply the point-source spreading equation: $$I = \frac{W}{4 \pi r^{2}}$$ Substitute $W = 0.02 \, \text{W}$, $r = 10 \, \text{m}$: $$I = \frac{0.02}{4\pi (10)^{2}} = \frac{0.02}{1256.64}$$ $$I \approx 1.5915 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{W/m}^{2}$$ **(ii) RMS Pressure at $r = 10$ m:** Rearrange the intensity equation $I = p_{rms}^{2} / \rho_0 c$: $$p_{rms} = \sqrt{I \cdot \rho_0 c}$$ Substitute $I = 1.5915 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{W/m}^{2}$, $\rho_0 c = 1.21 \times 343 = 415.03 \, \text{Rayl}$: $$p_{rms} = \sqrt{1.5915 \times 10^{-5} \times 415.03} = \sqrt{6.605 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$p_{rms} \approx 0.0813 \, \text{Pa} = 81.3 \, \text{mPa}$$ --- ### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1 ESE]** **(a)** Explain the **Inverse Square Law** as applied to a point acoustic source. State clearly the assumptions under which it is valid. Show that doubling the distance from a point source reduces the SPL by 6 dB. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A workshop contains a machine whose Sound Power Level is $L_W = 95 \, \text{dB re } 10^{-12} \, \text{W}$. Assuming free-field conditions, calculate: (i) The acoustic power radiated by the machine. (ii) The Sound Pressure Level at $r = 3 \, \text{m}$. (iii) The distance at which the SPL drops to 70 dB. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Inverse Square Law Derivation:** **Assumptions:** 1. The source is an ideal **point source** (dimensionally small compared to wavelength and distance). 2. The medium is **homogeneous** and **lossless** (no absorption in air). 3. Radiation occurs in a **free field** (anechoic — no reflections). 4. Wavefronts are perfectly **spherical**. **Derivation:** By conservation of energy, total power $W$ through any closed spherical surface of radius $r$ around the source is constant: $$W = I(r) \cdot 4\pi r^{2}$$ Solving for $I(r)$: $$I(r) = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}$$ Hence intensity $\propto 1/r^{2}$. For two distances: $$\frac{I_2}{I_1} = \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^{2}$$ **Doubling Distance Proof:** Set $r_2 = 2 r_1$. The SPL ratio is: $$\Delta L_p = L_{p,2} - L_{p,1} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_2}{I_1}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) = -6.02 \, \text{dB}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta L_p \approx -6 \, \text{dB}}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **(i) Acoustic Power $W$:** Invert $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W / W_{ref})$: $$W = W_{ref} \cdot 10^{L_W/10} = 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{95/10} = 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{9.5}$$ Compute $10^{9.5} = 10^{0.5} \cdot 10^{9} = 3.162 \times 10^{9}$: $$W = 3.162 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{W} \approx 3.16 \, \text{mW}$$ **(ii) SPL at $r = 3$ m (Free Field):** Use the canonical point-source formula: $$L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11$$ Substitute $L_W = 95$ dB, $r = 3$ m: $$L_p = 95 - 20 \log_{10}(3) - 11 = 95 - 20(0.4771) - 11$$ $$L_p = 95 - 9.542 - 11 = 74.46 \, \text{dB}$$ $$\boxed{L_p \approx 74.5 \, \text{dB re } 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}}$$ **(iii) Distance for $L_p = 70$ dB:** Rearrange the same formula for $r$: $$r = 10^{\,(L_W - L_p - 11)/20}$$ Substitute $L_W = 95$, $L_p = 70$: $$r = 10^{\,(95 - 70 - 11)/20} = 10^{14/20} = 10^{0.7}$$ Compute $10^{0.7} \approx 5.012$: $$\boxed{r \approx 5.01 \, \text{m}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing $L_W$, $L_I$, and $L_p$:** Examiners strictly deduct 1–2 marks if a student uses $L_W$ where $L_p$ is required (or vice-versa). Remember the *constant offsets*: in a free field on a sphere, $L_W = L_p + 20 \log_{10}(r) + 11$. > 2. **Forgetting the "$-11$" term:** The factor $10 \log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 11$ is often missed. Students write $L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r)$ and lose a full mark. > 3. **Wrong reference values:** Using $p_{ref} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa in Pascal calculations vs $20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ in dB — these are the same value, but writing the wrong form invites confusion. > 4. **Inverse Square vs Inverse First Power:** Many students incorrectly state that *pressure* obeys an inverse-square law. Pressure obeys an *inverse-first-power* law ($p \propto 1/r$); only **intensity** follows $1/r^{2}$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Acoustic Power ($W$):** Total energy radiated per second by the source (Watts). Source-intrinsic, environment-independent. - **Acoustic Intensity ($I$):** Power per unit area in a given direction ($W/m^2$). A vector field quantity. - **Characteristic Impedance ($\rho_0 c$):** $\approx 415 \, \text{Rayl}$ in air at $20^{\circ}C$. - **Plane-Wave Relation:** $I = p_{rms}^{2} / \rho_0 c$. - **Inverse Square Law:** $I \propto 1/r^{2}$; $p \propto 1/r$; **6 dB drop per doubling of distance** for SPL. - **Decibel Scales:** $L_W$ uses $W_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W with factor **10**; $L_p$ uses $p_{ref} = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ with factor **20**. - **Free-Field Point-Source Master Equation:** $L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11$. - **Assumptions Checklist for Inverse Square Law:** Point source + lossless medium + free field + spherical wavefronts. Violate any one and the law fails. - **Validity Limit:** The law holds for $r$ greater than roughly $\lambda / (2\pi)$ (Fraunhofer distance) — beyond the near field. - **Reciprocity Tip:** Doubling distance → $-6$ dB SPL. Halving distance → $+6$ dB SPL. Ten times the distance → $-20$ dB SPL. - **Engineering Link:** $L_W$ rates the source; $L_p$ describes what reaches the listener. Convert via geometry and environment.

topics.title: Acoustic Energy Density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Acoustic Energy Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔊 ACOUSTIC ENERGY DENSITY — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acoustic Energy Density (E or *w*)** is defined as the **total instantaneous acoustic energy contained per unit volume** of the medium through which a sound wave is propagating. It is the spatial analog of acoustic energy and represents the *energy stored* in the compressions and rarefactions of the elastic medium (typically air).

Mathematically, it is the sum of two physically distinct contributions:

$$
E \;\triangleq\; e_K + e_P
$$

where:

- **$e_K$** = instantaneous **kinetic energy density** (energy due to particle motion).
- **$e_P$** = instantaneous **potential energy density** (energy due to local compression/rarefaction of the medium).

> [!NOTE]
> **SI Unit:** Joule per cubic metre (**J/m³**), which is dimensionally identical to **Pascal (Pa)** since $1\ \text{Pa} = 1\ \text{N/m}^2 = 1\ \text{J/m}^3$. This duality is highly convenient in acoustics.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Invisible Spring Ocean" 🌊

Imagine a still pond. Now drop a stone into it — circular ripples spread outward. The water itself does **not** travel; instead, **energy** is transported across the surface.

| Wave Element | Pond Analogy | Acoustic Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Water surface displacement | Vertical bobbing of water molecules | Particle displacement $\xi$ in air |
| Local compression | Bunching of water molecules | Pressure variation $p$ |
| Local motion energy | Kinetic energy of moving water | Kinetic energy density $e_K$ |
| Stored strain energy | Elastic stretching of surface tension | Potential energy density $e_P$ |

**Acoustic Energy Density** is the *total local energy* carried in every tiny parcel of the air through which the sound wave passes. A microphone does not "catch" the air — it intercepts the **oscillating energy density field** and converts it into an electrical signal.

> [!TIP]
> Think of acoustic energy density as the *acoustic equivalent of battery charge*. Just as a fully charged battery stores electrical energy in a small volume, a region of compressed (or rarefied) air stores mechanical energy that can be delivered to a transducer or to our eardrums.

---

## 1.3 Why Acoustic Energy Density Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This topic is a **cornerstone of Module 1** because it bridges the kinematic description (pressure, particle velocity) to the dynamic description (intensity, power, decibel scales). Nearly every higher-order topic — sound intensity, sound pressure level, reverberation, room acoustics — depends on a clear understanding of $E$.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants to Remember

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value (Air at 20 °C) |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Density of air | **1.21 kg/m³** |
| $c$ | Speed of sound in air | **343 m/s** |
| $\rho c$ | Characteristic impedance of air | **≈ 415 Pa·s/m (rayl)** |
| $Z_0$ | Specific acoustic impedance | **≈ 415 rayl** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔊 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Constituents of Acoustic Energy Density

### 2.1.1 Kinetic Energy Density ($e_K$)

Each infinitesimal air parcel of mass $dm = \rho\, dV$ vibrates with instantaneous particle velocity $u$. Its kinetic energy is $\tfrac{1}{2}\,dm\,u^2$. Dividing by volume:

$$
e_K \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, u^{2}
$$

### 2.1.2 Potential Energy Density ($e_P$)

When air is locally compressed (or rarefied), it stores elastic strain energy analogously to a compressed spring. For adiabatic processes in an ideal gas, the local acoustic pressure $p$ produces:

$$
e_P \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\frac{p^{2}}{\rho c^{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The denominator $\rho c^2$ is the **bulk modulus** (adiabatic) of the medium, $B = \rho c^2$. In air, $B \approx 1.42 \times 10^{5}$ Pa.

### 2.1.3 Total Instantaneous Energy Density

$$
E(t,x) \;=\; e_K + e_P \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, u^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\frac{p^{2}}{\rho c^{2}}
$$

---

## 2.2 The Equipartition Principle for Plane Progressive Waves

For a plane progressive (travelling) wave:

$$
p(x,t) = p_m \cos(\omega t - kx), \qquad u(x,t) = u_m \cos(\omega t - kx)
$$

with the rigid relation $p_m = \rho c\, u_m$.

Substituting and simplifying produces the famous **equipartition result**:

$$
e_K(t) = e_P(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, u_m^{2} \cos^{2}(\omega t - kx)
$$

So **kinetic and potential energy densities are *equal at every instant and at every point***. The total energy density oscillates between $0$ and $\rho u_m^2$:

$$
E(t,x) \;=\; \rho\, u_m^{2}\,\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx) \;=\; \frac{p_m^{2}}{\rho c^{2}}\,\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx)
$$

---

## 2.3 Time-Averaged Energy Density — The KTU Workhorse

Because acoustic quantities oscillate rapidly (typically $20$–$20{,}000$ Hz), only their **RMS / time-averaged** values are physically meaningful for perception and measurement. Using $\langle \cos^{2}\theta \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}$:

$$
\boxed{\;\bar{E} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, u_m^{2} \;=\; \frac{p_m^{2}}{2\,\rho c^{2}} \;=\; \rho\, u_{\text{rms}}^{2} \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c^{2}}\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The last form $\bar{E} = p_{\text{rms}}^{2}/(\rho c^{2})$ is the **most exam-relevant**, because microphones measure $p_{\text{rms}}$, not $u_m$.

---

## 2.4 Linking Energy Density to Sound Intensity

The sound **intensity** $I$ is the *energy flux* (energy per unit area per unit time) transported by a travelling wave. It equals the energy density multiplied by the wave speed:

$$
\boxed{\;I \;=\; \bar{E}\,c \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c}\;}
$$

This is the **fundamental intensity formula** used in decibel calculations.

---

## 2.5 Energy Density for Stationary (Standing) Waves

For a standing wave formed by superposition of two counter-travelling plane waves:

$$
p = 2\,p_m \sin(kx)\,\cos(\omega t), \qquad u = 2\,u_m \cos(kx)\,\sin(\omega t)
$$

The two energy densities now have *different spatial distributions*:

$$
e_K = 2\,\rho\, u_m^{2}\,\cos^{2}(kx)\,\sin^{2}(\omega t)
$$

$$
e_P = 2\,\rho\, u_m^{2}\,\sin^{2}(kx)\,\cos^{2}(\omega t)
$$

Time-averaged:

$$
\bar{e}_K = \rho\, u_m^{2}\,\cos^{2}(kx), \qquad \bar{e}_P = \rho\, u_m^{2}\,\sin^{2}(kx)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\bar{E}_{\text{stand}} \;=\; \rho\, u_m^{2}\;=\;\text{uniform in space}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** In a standing wave, energy does *not* propagate (time-averaged intensity is zero everywhere), yet the time-averaged energy density is **spatially uniform** and **non-zero**. This counter-intuitive fact is a frequent examination point.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Notes / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kinetic energy density | $e_K = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u^{2}$ | J/m³ | Instantaneous, point-wise |
| 2 | Potential energy density | $e_P = \tfrac{1}{2}\,p^{2}/(\rho c^{2})$ | J/m³ | Instantaneous, point-wise |
| 3 | Total instantaneous energy density | $E = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,p^{2}/(\rho c^{2})$ | J/m³ | General (any wave type) |
| 4 | Plane-wave equipartition | $e_K = e_P$ | — | Only for *travelling* plane waves |
| 5 | Avg. energy density (plane) | $\bar{E} = p_{\text{rms}}^{2}/(\rho c^{2}) = \rho\, u_{\text{rms}}^{2}$ | J/m³ | Most common exam form |
| 6 | Peak energy density (plane) | $E_{\max} = \rho u_m^{2} = p_m^{2}/(\rho c^{2})$ | J/m³ | Twice the average |
| 7 | Standing-wave avg. energy density | $\bar{E}_{\text{stand}} = \rho u_m^{2}$ | J/m³ | **Spatially uniform** |
| 8 | Sound intensity | $I = \bar{E}\,c = p_{\text{rms}}^{2}/(\rho c)$ | W/m² | Energy flux of a plane wave |
| 9 | Spherical-wave intensity | $I = W/(4\pi r^{2})$ | W/m² | $W$ = total acoustic power |
| 10 | Energy density of spherical wave | $\bar{E} = I/c = W/(4\pi r^{2} c)$ | J/m³ | Falls as $1/r^{2}$ |
| 11 | Intensity → Energy Density | $\bar{E} = I/c$ | J/m³ | Universal for travelling waves |
| 12 | Reference intensity | $I_{0} = 10^{-12}$ W/m² | W/m² | Threshold of hearing at 1 kHz |
| 13 | Sound Intensity Level | $\text{SIL} = 10\log_{10}(I/I_{0})$ | dB | dB scale |
| 14 | Sound Pressure Level | $\text{SPL} = 20\log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_{0})$ | dB | $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ |
| 15 | Characteristic impedance | $Z_0 = \rho c$ | rayl (Pa·s/m) | Air ≈ 415 rayl |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** For **standing waves**, $\bar{E}_{\text{stand}} = \rho u_m^{2}$ — *not* $p_m^{2}/(\rho c^{2})$ alone. The pressure amplitude used is the *standing-wave antinode* pressure $2p_m$. Always clarify whether you are dealing with a *travelling* or *standing* wave before plugging values in.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Energy Density |
|---|---|
| **Architectural Acoustics** | Determining reverberation time, $RT_{60} = 0.161\,V/\bar{\alpha}S$, requires knowledge of energy density decay |
| **Ultrasonic Cleaning / Sonar** | Cavitation threshold ≈ energy density > $10$ J/m³ |
| **Hearing Conservation (OSHA)** | Workplace noise limits (85 dB / 8 h) derived from permissible energy density exposure |
| **Microphone Calibration** | Free-field sensitivity $\mathcal{M} = V_{\text{oc}}/p_{\text{rms}}$ tied to incident energy density |
| **Loudspeaker Design** | Sound power $W = \oint \bar{E}\,c\, dA$; enclosure tuning minimises stored energy |
| **Aero-acoustics** | Lighthill's acoustic analogy expresses far-field $E \propto (\partial^{2} T_{ij}/\partial t^{2})^{2}/(\rho_{0} c_{0}^{5})$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔊 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Instantaneous Energy Density of a Plane Progressive Wave

**Goal:** Derive $E(x,t) = \rho\,u_m^{2}\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx)$ starting from first principles.

**Step 1 — Specify the wave field.**
A plane progressive harmonic wave travelling along $+x$ in a lossless medium is described by:

$$
p(x,t) = p_m \cos(\omega t - kx)
$$

The associated particle velocity, obtained from Euler's equation, is in-phase:

$$
u(x,t) = \frac{p_m}{\rho c}\cos(\omega t - kx) = u_m\cos(\omega t - kx)
$$

We therefore have the rigid plane-wave relation $p_m = \rho c\, u_m$.

**Step 2 — Express the kinetic energy density.**

$$
e_K(x,t) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, u^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, u_m^{2}\,\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx)
$$

**Step 3 — Express the potential energy density.**
Using $p = p_m\cos(\omega t - kx)$ and $p_m = \rho c\, u_m$:

$$
e_P(x,t) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\frac{p^{2}}{\rho c^{2}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\frac{p_m^{2}}{\rho c^{2}}\,\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, u_m^{2}\,\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx)
$$

**Step 4 — Add the two contributions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(x,t) &= e_K + e_P \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u_m^{2}\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx) + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u_m^{2}\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx) \\
&= \rho\, u_m^{2}\,\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Alternative form in terms of pressure amplitude.**

$$
\boxed{\;E(x,t) \;=\; \frac{p_m^{2}}{\rho c^{2}}\,\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx)\;}
$$

Both expressions are equivalent because $p_m = \rho c\, u_m$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Time-Averaged Energy Density and Equivalence with Intensity

**Goal:** Prove $\bar{E} = I/c$ for a plane wave.

**Step 1 — Average over one period $T = 2\pi/\omega$.**
For any harmonic function $\langle \cos^{2}\theta \rangle_T = \tfrac{1}{2}$:

$$
\bar{E} \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T}\rho u_m^{2}\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx)\, dt \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, u_m^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Express in RMS form.**
With $u_{\text{rms}} = u_m/\sqrt{2}$:

$$
\bar{E} \;=\; \rho\, u_{\text{rms}}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of RMS pressure.**
Using $u_{\text{rms}} = p_{\text{rms}}/(\rho c)$:

$$
\bar{E} \;=\; \rho\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{\rho c}\right)^{2} \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Define intensity $I$ as energy flux.**
Intensity is the average rate at which energy crosses unit area perpendicular to propagation. In time $T$, a "slab" of thickness $c\,T$ is emptied of its energy $\bar{E}\,c\,T$ through unit area. Hence:

$$
I \;=\; \bar{E}\,c \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c}
$$

**Step 5 — Inverse relation.**

$$
\boxed{\;\bar{E} \;=\; \frac{I}{c}\;}
$$

This is the **universal plane-wave energy-density–intensity relation**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Standing-Wave Energy Density from Superposition

**Goal:** Show that the time-averaged energy density of a standing wave is spatially uniform.

**Step 1 — Form the standing wave.**
Sum a forward wave of amplitude $p_m$ and a backward wave of equal amplitude:

$$
p_{\text{stand}}(x,t) = p_m\cos(\omega t - kx) + p_m\cos(\omega t + kx) = 2p_m\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)
$$

Similarly, with the sign-flip on velocity for the backward wave:

$$
u_{\text{stand}}(x,t) = u_m\cos(\omega t - kx) - u_m\cos(\omega t + kx) = 2u_m\cos(kx)\sin(\omega t)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $e_K$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_K(x,t) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,u_{\text{stand}}^{2} \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,(2u_m)^{2}\cos^{2}(kx)\sin^{2}(\omega t) \\
&= 2\,\rho\, u_m^{2}\,\cos^{2}(kx)\,\sin^{2}(\omega t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $e_P$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_P(x,t) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\frac{p_{\text{stand}}^{2}}{\rho c^{2}} \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\frac{(2p_m)^{2}\sin^{2}(kx)\cos^{2}(\omega t)}{\rho c^{2}} \\
&= 2\,\rho\, u_m^{2}\,\sin^{2}(kx)\,\cos^{2}(\omega t) \quad\left[\text{since } \frac{p_m^{2}}{\rho c^{2}}=\rho u_m^{2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total instantaneous energy density.**

$$
E_{\text{stand}}(x,t) = 2\rho u_m^{2}\!\left[\cos^{2}(kx)\sin^{2}(\omega t) + \sin^{2}(kx)\cos^{2}(\omega t)\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Time-average.**
Using $\langle\sin^{2}\omega t\rangle = \langle\cos^{2}\omega t\rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{E}_{\text{stand}}(x) &= 2\rho u_m^{2}\!\left[\tfrac{1}{2}\cos^{2}(kx) + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin^{2}(kx)\right] \\
&= \rho u_m^{2}\!\left[\cos^{2}(kx) + \sin^{2}(kx)\right] \\
&= \rho u_m^{2} \quad (\text{independent of } x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

$$
\boxed{\;\bar{E}_{\text{stand}} = \rho u_m^{2} = \text{constant in space}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Although energy is *present* everywhere with equal density, **no net energy flows**: the time-averaged intensity $\bar{I} = \langle p\,u \rangle = 0$. Energy oscillates back and forth between kinetic and potential reservoirs at each location — a beautiful manifestation of **non-propagating stored energy**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Spherical-Wave Energy Density and the Inverse-Square Law

**Step 1 — Power conservation.**
A point source radiates total acoustic power $W$ uniformly outward. Through any spherical surface of radius $r$:

$$
W \;=\; \oint I\, dA \;=\; I(r)\,\bigl(4\pi r^{2}\bigr) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad I(r) = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to energy density using $I = \bar{E}\,c$.**

$$
\boxed{\;\bar{E}(r) \;=\; \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}\,c}\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Inverse-square behaviour.**
Both $I$ and $\bar{E}$ fall as $1/r^{2}$, doubling $r$ reduces energy density to one quarter.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Travelling vs Standing Wave Energy Properties

| Property | Plane Progressive Wave | Plane Standing Wave | Spherical Wave |
|---|---|---|---|
| Instantaneous $E$ | $\rho u_m^{2}\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx)$ | $2\rho u_m^{2}[\cos^{2}(kx)\sin^{2}\omega t + \sin^{2}(kx)\cos^{2}\omega t]$ | $(W/(4\pi r^{2}c))$ modulated |
| Equipartition $e_K = e_P$? | ✅ Yes (always, in-phase) | ❌ No (90° out of phase spatially) | ✅ Yes (locally) |
| Time-averaged $\bar{E}$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho u_m^{2}$ | $\rho u_m^{2}$ | $W/(4\pi r^{2}c)$ |
| Spatial dependence | Uniform | **Uniform** | $1/r^{2}$ |
| Time-averaged intensity | $I = \bar{E}\,c$ | $0$ | $W/(4\pi r^{2})$ |
| Energy transport? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (stored) | ✅ Yes |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A plane sound wave in air ($\rho = 1.21$ kg/m³, $c = 343$ m/s) has an RMS pressure of $0.1$ Pa. Compute (a) average energy density, (b) peak energy density, (c) intensity.

**Solution:**

(a) Average energy density:

$$
\bar{E} = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c^{2}} = \frac{(0.1)^{2}}{1.21 \times (343)^{2}} = \frac{0.01}{1.21 \times 117649} \approx 7.03 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{J/m}^{3}
$$

(b) Peak energy density is exactly twice the average:

$$
E_{\max} = 2\bar{E} \approx 1.41 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{J/m}^{3}
$$

(c) Intensity:

$$
I = \bar{E}\,c = 7.03 \times 10^{-8} \times 343 \approx 2.41 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Sound Pressure Level:**

$$
\text{SPL} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.1}{20\times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20\log_{10}(5000) \approx 73.98\ \text{dB}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🔊 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Acoustic Energy Density Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sound Source Excitation] --> B[Pressure Variation p_x_t]
    A --> C[Particle Velocity u_x_t]

    B --> D[Potential Energy Density e_P = p^2 / 2 rho c^2]
    C --> E[Kinetic Energy Density e_K = rho u^2 / 2]

    D --> F[Sum e_K + e_P]
    E --> F

    F --> G{Wave Type Classifier}

    G -->|Plane Travelling| H[Equipartition: e_K = e_P]
    G -->|Plane Standing| I[Spatial Decoupling: 90 deg phase]
    G -->|Spherical Outward| J[Inverse-Square Attenuation]

    H --> K[Time Average: E_bar = p_rms^2 / rho c^2]
    I --> L[Time Average: E_bar = rho u_m^2 uniform]
    J --> M[Time Average: E_bar = W / 4 pi r^2 c]

    K --> N[Intensity I = E_bar c]
    L --> O[Net Intensity = 0]
    M --> P[Intensity I = W / 4 pi r^2]

    N --> Q[Engineering Applications: SPL, SIL, dB Scales]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q

    Q --> R[Architectural Acoustics]
    Q --> S[Noise Control Engineering]
    Q --> T[Ultrasonic Devices]
    Q --> U[Hearing Conservation]
```

# 🔊 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / PEMET752]`

**Define acoustic energy density. State its SI unit and the two components into which it can be split.**

**Model Answer:**

> Acoustic energy density is the instantaneous acoustic energy stored per unit volume of the medium through which a sound wave propagates. Mathematically,
>
> $$E \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,u^{2} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\frac{p^{2}}{\rho c^{2}}$$
>
> It has two components:
> 1. **Kinetic energy density** $e_K = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u^{2}$, due to particle motion.
> 2. **Potential energy density** $e_P = \tfrac{1}{2}p^{2}/(\rho c^{2})$, due to local compression of the medium.
>
> **SI unit:** J/m³ (dimensionally equivalent to Pascal).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / PEMET752]`

**For a plane progressive sound wave, show that the time-averaged kinetic and potential energy densities are equal.**

**Model Answer:**

> For a plane progressive wave $p = p_m\cos(\omega t - kx)$ and $u = u_m\cos(\omega t - kx)$ with $p_m = \rho c\, u_m$.
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \bar{e}_K &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\,u_{\text{rms}}^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\cdot\frac{u_m^{2}}{2} = \tfrac{1}{4}\rho u_m^{2} \\
> \bar{e}_P &= \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c^{2}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{(u_m/\sqrt{2})^{2}\cdot(\rho c)^{2}}{\rho c^{2}} = \tfrac{1}{4}\rho u_m^{2}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> Hence $\bar{e}_K = \bar{e}_P$, demonstrating equipartition. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B for Module 1 topics typically carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The internal choice means **either Question A or Question B** must be answered.

---

### **🅰️ Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / PEMET752]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the instantaneous acoustic energy density of a plane progressive harmonic sound wave. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the energy-density expression, obtain the relationship between sound intensity and RMS pressure for a plane wave. Compute the intensity in air ($\rho = 1.21$ kg/m³, $c = 343$ m/s) when the RMS sound pressure is $0.2$ Pa. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

Step 1 — A plane progressive harmonic wave is described by:

$$p(x,t) = p_m\cos(\omega t - kx), \qquad u(x,t) = u_m\cos(\omega t - kx)$$

with $p_m = \rho c\, u_m$.

Step 2 — Kinetic energy density:

$$e_K = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u_m^{2}\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx)$$

Step 3 — Potential energy density:

$$e_P = \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{p^{2}}{\rho c^{2}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u_m^{2}\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx)$$

Step 4 — Total:

$$E(x,t) = e_K + e_P = \rho u_m^{2}\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx) = \frac{p_m^{2}}{\rho c^{2}}\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx)$$

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

Step 1 — Time-average over one period:

$$\bar{E} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u_m^{2} = \frac{p_m^{2}}{2\rho c^{2}} = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c^{2}}$$

Step 2 — Sound intensity equals the average energy flux through unit area:

$$I = \bar{E}\,c = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c}$$

Step 3 — Substitute values ($p_{\text{rms}} = 0.2$ Pa):

$$I = \frac{(0.2)^{2}}{1.21 \times 343} = \frac{0.04}{415.03} \approx 9.64 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{W/m}^{2}$$

---

### **🅱️ Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / PEMET752]`

**(a)** For a plane standing wave formed by two counter-propagating waves of equal amplitude, obtain expressions for the instantaneous and time-averaged energy densities. Show that the time-averaged energy density is spatially uniform. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A point source radiates $0.1$ W of acoustic power uniformly in all directions. Find (i) the intensity at $r = 2$ m, (ii) the average energy density at $r = 2$ m, and (iii) the intensity and energy density at $r = 5$ m. Take $c = 343$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

Step 1 — Form the standing wave by superposition:

$$p = 2p_m\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t), \qquad u = 2u_m\cos(kx)\sin(\omega t)$$

Step 2 — Compute kinetic and potential energy densities:

$$e_K = 2\rho u_m^{2}\cos^{2}(kx)\sin^{2}(\omega t), \qquad e_P = 2\rho u_m^{2}\sin^{2}(kx)\cos^{2}(\omega t)$$

Step 3 — Sum to get total instantaneous energy density:

$$E = 2\rho u_m^{2}\!\left[\cos^{2}(kx)\sin^{2}(\omega t) + \sin^{2}(kx)\cos^{2}(\omega t)\right]$$

Step 4 — Time-average using $\langle\sin^{2}\rangle = \langle\cos^{2}\rangle = 1/2$:

$$\bar{E} = 2\rho u_m^{2}\!\left[\tfrac{1}{2}\cos^{2}(kx) + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin^{2}(kx)\right] = \rho u_m^{2}$$

This is independent of $x$, hence **spatially uniform**. Intensity is zero (no net transport).

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

Step 1 — Intensity at $r = 2$ m:

$$I(2) = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}} = \frac{0.1}{4\pi (2)^{2}} = \frac{0.1}{50.27} \approx 1.989 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W/m}^{2}$$

Step 2 — Energy density at $r = 2$ m:

$$\bar{E}(2) = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{1.989\times 10^{-3}}{343} \approx 5.80 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{J/m}^{3}$$

Step 3 — At $r = 5$ m:

$$I(5) = \frac{0.1}{4\pi(5)^{2}} = \frac{0.1}{314.16} \approx 3.18 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{W/m}^{2}$$

$$\bar{E}(5) = \frac{3.18 \times 10^{-4}}{343} \approx 9.27 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{J/m}^{3}$$

Step 4 — Verification of inverse-square law: $I(5)/I(2) = (2/5)^{2} = 0.16$ ✓; similarly $\bar{E}(5)/\bar{E}(2) = 0.16$ ✓.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout
> 1. **Equipartition misapplied to standing waves** — Students routinely write $e_K = e_P$ for *standing* waves. This is **wrong**: standing waves have $e_K$ and $e_P$ differing by a factor of $\tan^{2}(\omega t)\tan^{2}(kx)$. Equipartition holds **only for travelling waves**.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of ½** — Time-averaging requires $\langle\cos^{2}\rangle = ½$. Writing $\bar{E} = \rho u_m^{2}$ instead of $\bar{E} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u_m^{2}$ for a *travelling* wave costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Using peak pressure instead of RMS** in the intensity formula gives intensity **twice** the correct value. Always convert: $p_{\text{rms}} = p_m/\sqrt{2}$.
> 4. **Mixing units** — Pa vs $\mu$Pa in SPL calculations: $20\log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/20\mu\text{Pa})$ — failing to convert to the reference $p_0 = 2\times10^{-5}$ Pa loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting that standing-wave energy density is uniform** — When asked "where is energy density maximum in a standing wave?", the correct answer is: **"everywhere — it is spatially uniform in time average."** Many students incorrectly cite pressure antinodes.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print-and-glance summary for last-mile revision:**

- **Definition:** Acoustic energy density = energy per unit volume stored in a sound field; SI unit **J/m³ ≡ Pa**.
- **Two components:** $e_K = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u^{2}$ (kinetic) and $e_P = \tfrac{1}{2}p^{2}/(\rho c^{2})$ (potential).
- **Plane travelling wave:** $e_K = e_P$ (equipartition); $\bar{E} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u_m^{2} = p_{\text{rms}}^{2}/(\rho c^{2})$.
- **Plane standing wave:** $e_K \neq e_P$ locally; $\bar{E}_{\text{stand}} = \rho u_m^{2}$ = **uniform in space**; intensity = **zero**.
- **Spherical wave:** $\bar{E}(r) = W/(4\pi r^{2}c)$; $I(r) = W/(4\pi r^{2})$ — both $\propto 1/r^{2}$.
- **Universal intensity–energy relation:** $I = \bar{E}\,c$ (travelling waves only).
- **Intensity formula:** $I = p_{\text{rms}}^{2}/(\rho c)$ (the most-used equation in the entire subject).
- **SPL formula:** $\text{SPL} = 20\log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/20\mu\text{Pa})$.
- **SIL formula:** $\text{SIL} = 10\log_{10}(I/I_0)$, with $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m².
- **Characteristic impedance of air:** $\rho c \approx 415$ rayl — needed for all impedance-related energy problems.
- **Peak vs average:** $E_{\max} = 2\bar{E}$ for any sinusoidal field.
- **Common exam pitfall:** Never apply travelling-wave equipartition to standing waves.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In acoustics and noise control engineering, the **Directivity Pattern** (also called the **Polar Pattern** or **Beam Pattern**) is defined as the **three-dimensional angular distribution of radiated acoustic intensity (or sound pressure) around a sound source**, expressed as a function of the angle of emission measured with respect to a reference axis (typically the axis of geometric symmetry, called the **on-axis** direction).

Mathematically, the directivity pattern is a normalized spatial function describing how the acoustic energy is concentrated in some directions and suppressed in others. It is a fundamental descriptor of any real-world source because **no physical source radiates sound equally in all directions** — the geometry, size, and mounting conditions of the source dictate the pattern.

The most important scalar metrics derived from this pattern are:
- **Directivity Factor (Q)** — a dimensionless quantity
- **Directivity Index (DI)** — expressed in **decibels (dB)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Directivity is one of the most frequently tested concepts in Module 2 of PEMET752. Mastery of the *directivity factor*, *directivity index*, and their relationship is mandatory for solving numerical problems in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine you are holding a **garden hose**:
- If the nozzle is removed, water sprays out in all directions equally (like an **omnidirectional** source — e.g., a small point source in free space).
- If you attach a narrow nozzle, water shoots out in a tight, powerful jet in one direction only (like a **highly directional** source — e.g., a megaphone, a horn loudspeaker, or a sonar array).

The **directivity pattern** is simply the acoustic "shape" of this jet — a map showing *where the sound goes strongly, where it goes weakly, and where it is essentially silent*. A microphone placed directly in front of the megaphone hears a loud signal; the same microphone placed behind it hears almost nothing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Directivity Important?**
> - It determines the **coverage area** of a loudspeaker in a hall or auditorium.
> - It governs the **rejection of unwanted noise** in microphones and hearing aids.
> - It is critical in **SONAR, RADAR, and acoustic beamforming**, where you want to "listen" in only one direction.
> - It influences the **speech intelligibility** and **uniformity** in room acoustics design.

## 1.3 Reference Axis Convention

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Reference |
|--------|---------|---------------------|
| **0° (on-axis)** | Reference direction | Axis of symmetry of the source |
| **θ** | Polar angle (from on-axis) | 0° = on-axis, 180° = directly behind |
| **φ** | Azimuthal angle (in 3D) | 0° = right, 90° = up |

The acoustic pressure at a far-field point is written as $p(r, \theta, \phi)$, and the directivity pattern is the normalized pressure ratio:

$$
D(\theta, \phi) \;=\; \frac{p(r, \theta, \phi)}{p(r, 0, 0)}
$$

so that on-axis, $D = 1$ (or **0 dB**) by definition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — Operational Concept

### Step 1 — The Physics of Sound Radiation
A vibrating surface (piston, membrane, or diaphragm) sets the surrounding air particles into motion. Because sound is a **longitudinal wave**, the particle motion is constrained along the propagation direction. The size of the radiating surface, expressed in terms of the acoustic wavelength $\lambda$, determines how directional the source becomes.

### Step 2 — When Is a Source Omnidirectional?
A source behaves as an omnidirectional (monopole) radiator **only when its physical dimensions are much smaller than the wavelength** of the sound it emits. This is the classic **point-source assumption**:

$$
ka \;=\; \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \;\ll\; 1
$$

where:
- $a$ = characteristic radius (or dimension) of the source
- $k$ = acoustic wavenumber $= \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \dfrac{\omega}{c}$
- $c$ = speed of sound in air $\approx$ **343 m/s** at 20°C
- $\lambda$ = wavelength = $c / f$

### Step 3 — When Does Directionality Emerge?
As the source size grows relative to $\lambda$, the radiation pattern becomes **progressively narrower** (more focused). This is governed by the dimensionless product **$ka$**, also called the Helmholtz number.

| Regime | Condition | Resulting Pattern |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| Point source | $ka \ll 1$ | **Omnidirectional** (spherical wavefront) |
| Intermediate | $ka \sim 1$ | Slightly directional, lobing begins |
| Highly directional | $ka \gg 1$ | **Pencil beam** with strong side lobes |

### Step 4 — Measuring Directionality: Two Complementary Metrics
Instead of plotting the entire 3D pattern, engineers condense it into two scalar numbers:

**(a) Directivity Factor (Q)** — energy ratio (dimensionless)

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{I_{\text{on-axis}}}{I_{\text{average over sphere at same } r}} \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{on-axis}}^{\,2}}{\langle p^{2}\rangle_{\text{sphere}}}
$$

**(b) Directivity Index (DI)** — logarithmic form (in dB)

$$
\text{DI} \;=\; 10 \log_{10}(Q) \quad \text{dB}
$$

A higher Q (or DI) means the source "throws" more acoustic energy along the on-axis direction and less elsewhere.

### Step 5 — Engineering Trade-off
Increasing directivity (narrower beam) yields:
- Higher on-axis Sound Pressure Level (SPL)
- Greater resistance to feedback in microphones
- Reduced reverberant pickup
- BUT a smaller coverage area — the listener must sit within the beam

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula you must memorize for KTU ESE questions on Directivity Pattern.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | Acoustic wavenumber | $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi f}{c} = \dfrac{\omega}{c}$ | $f$ = freq, $c$ = sound speed | rad/m |
| 2 | Helmholtz number | $ka = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda}$ | $a$ = source radius | dimensionless |
| 3 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | — | m |
| 4 | Directivity Factor (general) | $Q = \dfrac{I_{\text{axis}}}{\langle I \rangle_{\text{sphere}}} = \dfrac{\int_{4\pi} D^{2}(\theta,\phi)\,d\Omega}{4\pi}$ | $d\Omega = \sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Directivity Index | $\text{DI} = 10 \log_{10}(Q)$ | — | dB |
| 6 | Point source (monopole) | $Q = 1,\;\; \text{DI} = 0\text{ dB}$ | $ka \ll 1$ | — |
| 7 | Piston in infinite baffle (on-axis) | $D(\theta) = \dfrac{2 J_{1}(ka \sin\theta)}{ka \sin\theta}$ | $J_{1}$ = Bessel function of order 1 | dimensionless |
| 8 | Piston in baffle (Directivity Factor) | $Q \approx 1 + 0.73\,(ka)^{2}\quad \text{for small }ka$ | — | dimensionless |
| 9 | Half-Power Beam Width (approx) | $\text{HPBW} \approx \dfrac{58^{\circ}\,\lambda}{2a}$ | circular piston | degrees |
| 10 | Line source (length $L$) | $D(\theta) = \dfrac{\sin\!\left(\tfrac{kL}{2}\sin\theta\right)}{\tfrac{kL}{2}\sin\theta}$ | far-field array | dimensionless |
| 11 | End-fire line array | $\text{HPBW} \approx \dfrac{68^{\circ}}{\sqrt{N}}$ | $N$ = number of elements | degrees |
| 12 | Coherence radius | $r > \dfrac{2a^{2}}{\lambda}$ | far-field begins | m |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Directivity Pattern |
|-------------|------------------------------|
| **Loudspeaker design** | Determines coverage of seating areas in auditoria, mosques, stadiums |
| **Microphone design** | Cardioid, super-cardioid, hyper-cardioid patterns reject background noise |
| **Hearing aids** | Directional microphones improve speech intelligibility in noise (Speech-to-Noise Ratio ↑ by 3–6 dB) |
| **SONAR & underwater acoustics** | Beam-forming arrays detect submarines, fish, seabed |
| **Noise control** | Identifying dominant noise sources and designing acoustic barriers/shields |
| **Architectural acoustics** | Predicting reverberant field vs. direct field at a listener's seat |
| **Ultrasonic cleaning & medical US** | Focused beams target tissue without affecting surrounding regions |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Directivity Factor from First Principles

### Goal
Starting from a general acoustic source radiating into free space, derive the relationship between on-axis intensity and the spatial average of intensity over a sphere enclosing the source.

### Setup
Consider an acoustic source of characteristic dimension $a$ located at the origin. Let $p(r, \theta, \phi)$ be the time-averaged RMS acoustic pressure at a far-field point $(r, \theta, \phi)$. In the **far field** (where $r \gg \lambda$ and $r > 2a^{2}/\lambda$), the acoustic intensity is:

$$
I(r, \theta, \phi) \;=\; \frac{\vert p(r, \theta, \phi) \vert^{2}}{\rho_{0} c}
$$

where $\rho_{0} c$ is the **characteristic acoustic impedance of air**, with $\rho_{0} \approx 1.21 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$ at 20°C.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the on-axis intensity.**

Choose the on-axis direction (the symmetry axis) as $\theta = 0$. At a fixed distance $r$ on this axis:

$$
I_{\text{on-axis}} \;=\; \frac{\vert p(r, 0, 0) \vert^{2}}{\rho_{0} c}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the average intensity on a surrounding sphere.**

The total acoustic power radiated by the source is independent of the chosen measurement radius (in the far field). Therefore we can compute it on a sphere of radius $r$:

$$
P_{\text{rad}} \;=\; \oint_{S} I(r, \theta, \phi)\,dS
$$

The differential surface element on a sphere is $dS = r^{2}\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi = r^{2}\,d\Omega$, where $d\Omega$ is the differential solid angle. Thus:

$$
P_{\text{rad}} \;=\; r^{2} \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\pi} I(r, \theta, \phi)\,\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi
$$

Dividing by the total solid angle $4\pi$ gives the spatial average:

$$
\langle I \rangle_{\text{sphere}} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{rad}}}{4\pi r^{2}} \;=\; \frac{1}{4\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\pi} I(r, \theta, \phi)\,\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio (Directivity Factor).**

By definition, the directivity factor is the on-axis-to-average intensity ratio at the same distance:

$$
Q \;\equiv\; \frac{I_{\text{on-axis}}}{\langle I \rangle_{\text{sphere}}}
$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{\vert p(r, 0, 0) \vert^{2}}{\rho_{0} c}}{\dfrac{1}{4\pi}\!\int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\pi}\dfrac{\vert p(r, \theta, \phi) \vert^{2}}{\rho_{0} c}\,\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi}
$$

The $\rho_{0} c$ in numerator and denominator cancels:

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{\vert p(r, 0, 0) \vert^{2}}{\dfrac{1}{4\pi}\!\int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\pi}\vert p(r, \theta, \phi) \vert^{2}\,\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the normalized directivity function.**

Define the **normalized directivity function** $D(\theta, \phi)$:

$$
D(\theta, \phi) \;\equiv\; \frac{p(r, \theta, \phi)}{p(r, 0, 0)} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad D(0, 0) = 1
$$

Squaring and substituting:

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{\vert p(r, 0, 0) \vert^{2}}{\dfrac{\vert p(r, 0, 0) \vert^{2}}{4\pi}\!\int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\pi} D^{2}(\theta, \phi)\,\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi}
$$

The $\vert p(r, 0, 0) \vert^{2}$ terms cancel completely:

$$
\boxed{\;Q \;=\; \frac{4\pi}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\pi} D^{2}(\theta, \phi)\,\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to the Directivity Index.**

Take ten times the base-10 logarithm of $Q$:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{DI} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}(Q) \quad \text{dB}\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check: ideal point source.**

For a point source, $D(\theta, \phi) = 1$ everywhere, so the integral equals $4\pi$ and $Q = 1$. Hence $\text{DI} = 0 \text{ dB}$ — the omnidirectional baseline. ✔

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Special Cases

| Source Geometry | Directivity Function $D(\theta)$ | Approximate Q (large $ka$) | Approximate HPBW |
|-----------------|------------------------------------|-----------------------------|---------------------|
| Point monopole (free field) | $D = 1$ | $Q = 1$ | $360^{\circ}$ |
| Doublet (dipole) | $D = \cos\theta$ | $Q = 3$ | $90^{\circ}$ |
| Side-by-side dipole pair (loudspeaker) | $D = \cos\theta$ | $Q = 3$ ($\text{DI}=4.77$ dB) | $90^{\circ}$ |
| Circular piston in infinite baffle | $D(\theta) = \dfrac{2J_{1}(ka\sin\theta)}{ka\sin\theta}$ | $Q \approx \dfrac{(ka)^{2}}{1.6}\quad (ka \gg 1)$ | $\text{HPBW} \approx \dfrac{58^{\circ}\lambda}{2a}$ |
| Line source (length $L$, broadside) | $D(\theta) = \dfrac{\sin(\tfrac{kL}{2}\sin\theta)}{\tfrac{kL}{2}\sin\theta}$ | $Q \approx \dfrac{2L}{\lambda}$ | $\text{HPBW} \approx \dfrac{50.6^{\circ}\lambda}{L}$ |
| Cardioid microphone (1st-order) | $D = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | $Q = 3$ | $131^{\circ}$ |

## 3.3 Derivation of Half-Power Beam Width for a Circular Piston

### Goal
Show the origin of the engineering formula $\text{HPBW} \approx \dfrac{58^{\circ}\lambda}{2a}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — On-axis pressure for a circular piston.**

The on-axis (far-field) sound pressure produced by a baffled circular piston of radius $a$ vibrating uniformly with velocity amplitude $u_{0}$ at frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$ is:

$$
p(r, 0) \;=\; j\omega\rho_{0}\,u_{0}\,a^{2}\,\frac{e^{-jkr}}{2r}\,\left[\,1 - e^{-jk\,2a^{2}/r}\,\right]
$$

In the far field ($r \gg 2a^{2}/\lambda$), the bracket $\rightarrow 1$, simplifying to:

$$
p(r, 0) \;=\; j\omega\rho_{0}\,u_{0}\,\frac{a^{2}}{2r}\,e^{-jkr}
$$

**Step 2 — Off-axis pressure (general direction $\theta$).**

The classic Rayleigh–Sommerfeld integral gives:

$$
p(r, \theta) \;=\; p(r, 0)\!\left[\,\frac{2J_{1}(ka\sin\theta)}{ka\sin\theta}\,\right]
$$

Define the argument $x = ka\sin\theta$. The pattern factor is therefore:

$$
D(\theta) \;=\; \frac{2J_{1}(x)}{x}
$$

**Step 3 — Locate the half-power angle.**

The half-power ($-3$ dB) point occurs when $\vert D(\theta)\vert^{2} = 0.5$, i.e., $\vert D(\theta)\vert = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071$.

The first zero of $D(\theta)$ occurs at the first non-trivial zero of $J_{1}(x)$, namely $x_{1} \approx 3.83$, giving $\theta_{\text{null}} = \arcsin(3.83/ka)$.

For the $-3$ dB point, the empirical (and analytically derived) solution gives $x_{-3\text{dB}} \approx 1.62$, hence:

$$
\sin\theta_{-3\text{dB}} \;\approx\; \frac{1.62}{ka} \;\approx\; \frac{1.62\,\lambda}{2\pi a} \;\approx\; \frac{0.258\,\lambda}{a}
$$

**Step 4 — Small-angle approximation.**

For narrow beams ($\theta$ small), $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians). Convert from radians to degrees by multiplying by $180/\pi$:

$$
\theta_{-3\text{dB}} \;\approx\; \frac{0.258\,\lambda}{a}\!\left(\frac{180^{\circ}}{\pi}\right) \;\approx\; \frac{14.8^{\circ}\,\lambda}{a}
$$

The HPBW is twice this (since the $-3$ dB angle is measured on each side of the main lobe axis):

$$
\text{HPBW} \;=\; 2\theta_{-3\text{dB}} \;\approx\; \frac{29.6^{\circ}\,\lambda}{a} \;\approx\; \frac{58^{\circ}\,\lambda}{2a}
$$

This matches the textbook engineering formula ✔.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Directivity Calculation

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **engineering workflow** for computing and reporting a source's directivity characteristics. It is used here in place of a literal polar plot (which Mermaid cannot render faithfully) to illustrate the analytical procedure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Source Geometry] --> B{Determine Source Size vs Wavelength}
    B -->|ka less than 1| C[Omnidirectional Regime]
    B -->|ka approx 1| D[Intermediate Regime]
    B -->|ka greater than 1| E[Highly Directional Regime]
    C --> F[Q equals 1, DI equals 0 dB]
    D --> G[Compute D theta phi Using Rayleigh Sommerfeld]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Integrate D squared dOmega over Sphere]
    H --> I[Q equals 4 pi divided by Integral]
    I --> J[DI equals 10 log10 Q]
    J --> K[Compute HPBW from first null]
    K --> L[Plot Polar Pattern and Report Metrics]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Directional Microphone Signal Processing

This diagram shows how a real-world directional microphone (e.g., cardioid) combines signals from a front and rear port to produce its characteristic directivity pattern.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Acoustic Wave Incident at Angle theta] --> B[Front Diaphragm Port]
    A --> C[Rear Diaphragm Port with Acoustic Delay Network]
    B --> D[Transducer A: Pressure Signal P_front]
    C --> E[Transducer B: Pressure Signal P_rear delayed]
    D --> F[Subtraction and Summation Stage]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Output Voltage Proportional to D theta]
    G --> H{Directivity Pattern}
    H --> I[Omnidirectional if pure P_front]
    H --> J[Figure Eight if P_front minus P_rear]
    H --> K[Cardioid if half P_front plus half of P_front minus P_rear]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Beamforming Array

This diagram maps how a phased array of $N$ identical elements synthesizes a much narrower beam than any single element could.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Plane Wave at Angle theta] --> B[Array of N Elements Spacing d]
    B --> C1[Element 1 Signal with Phase Shift kd sin theta]
    B --> C2[Element 2 Signal with Phase Shift 2 kd sin theta]
    B --> C3[Element 3 Signal with Phase Shift 3 kd sin theta]
    B --> C4[Element N Signal with Phase Shift N kd sin theta]
    C1 --> D[Digital or Analog Delay and Summation Network]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Constructive Interference on Axis]
    D --> F[Destructive Interference off Axis]
    E --> G[Narrow Pencil Beam with HPBW approx 50.6 degrees times lambda over Nd]
    F --> G
```

## 4.4 Comparative Directivity Patterns Table (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Directivity Classification] --> B[Omnidirectional: Q equals 1]
    A --> C[Subcardioid]
    A --> D[Cardioid: Q equals 3]
    A --> E[Supercardioid: Q approx 3.7]
    A --> F[Hypercardioid: Q approx 4]
    A --> G[Figure Eight Dipole: Q equals 3]
    A --> H[Shotgun Line Array: Q greater than 10]
    B --> I[Application: Ambient Room Mics]
    D --> J[Application: Stage Vocals, Podcasting]
    E --> K[Application: Broadcast ENG]
    F --> L[Application: Film and Field Recording]
    H --> M[Application: Sports Broadcasting, Wildlife Recording]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the Directivity Factor (Q) and Directivity Index (DI) of an acoustic source. How are they mathematically related?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

> **Directivity Factor (Q)** is defined as the ratio of the acoustic intensity measured on the reference axis of the source at a given distance to the average intensity over a sphere of the same radius surrounding the source. It is dimensionless.
>
> **Directivity Index (DI)** is the logarithmic expression of Q, expressed in decibels (dB).
>
> The mathematical relationship is:
>
> $$\text{DI} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}(Q) \quad \text{dB}$$
>
> Conversely, $Q = 10^{\,\text{DI}/10}$. For an ideal point source, $Q = 1$ and $\text{DI} = 0 \text{ dB}$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A small loudspeaker in a free field measures 90 dB SPL at 1 m on-axis and 87 dB SPL on average over a sphere of radius 1 m. Calculate the Directivity Index of the speaker.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

> **Step 1** — Convert SPLs to linear intensity ratios:
>
> $$I_{\text{on-axis}} \;\propto\; 10^{90/10} \;=\; 10^{9}$$
>
> $$I_{\text{average}} \;\propto\; 10^{87/10} \;=\; 10^{8.7}$$
>
> **Step 2** — Compute directivity factor:
>
> $$Q \;=\; \frac{I_{\text{on-axis}}}{I_{\text{average}}} \;=\; \frac{10^{9}}{10^{8.7}} \;=\; 10^{0.3} \;\approx\; 1.995 \;\approx\; 2.0$$
>
> **Step 3** — Compute directivity index:
>
> $$\text{DI} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}(2.0) \;\approx\; 3.01 \text{ dB}$$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation, every Module-2 question offers an **internal choice**. Two completely independent alternatives are given below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive, with clear assumptions, the expression for the **Directivity Factor (Q)** of an arbitrary acoustic source in terms of its normalized directivity function $D(\theta, \phi)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **circular piston of radius $a = 0.1$ m mounted in an infinite rigid baffle**, radiating sound at **frequency $f = 2000$ Hz** in air ($c = 343$ m/s), calculate:
- (i) The Helmholtz number $ka$
- (ii) The approximate Directivity Index
- (iii) The half-power beam width (HPBW) in degrees

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of Q:**

(See Section 3.1, Steps 1–5 of these notes for the full derivation. The final boxed result is:)

$$
\boxed{\;Q \;=\; \frac{4\pi}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\pi} D^{2}(\theta, \phi)\,\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi}\;}
$$

Key assumptions to state explicitly:
1. Source radiates into free space (no reflections).
2. Far-field measurement radius $r \gg 2a^{2}/\lambda$.
3. Time-averaged (RMS) quantities are used.
4. Air is lossless; $\rho_{0} c$ is real and constant.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Compute the wavelength.**

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{c}{f} \;=\; \frac{343}{2000} \;=\; 0.1715 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Helmholtz number.**

$$
k \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{0.1715} \;\approx\; 36.64 \text{ rad/m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;ka \;=\; 36.64 \times 0.1 \;\approx\; 3.664\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Q using the small-$ka$ correction is no longer valid (since $ka = 3.66 > 1$), so use the large-$ka$ approximation:**

$$
Q \;\approx\; \frac{(ka)^{2}}{1.6} \;\approx\; \frac{(3.664)^{2}}{1.6} \;\approx\; \frac{13.43}{1.6} \;\approx\; 8.39
$$

*(A more precise value computed by integrating the Bessel-function pattern gives $Q \approx 8.84$. Either answer is acceptable for full marks.)*

**Step 4 — Compute DI:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{DI} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}(8.39) \;\approx\; 9.24 \text{ dB}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute HPBW:**

$$
\text{HPBW} \;\approx\; \frac{58^{\circ}\,\lambda}{2a} \;=\; \frac{58^{\circ} \times 0.1715}{2 \times 0.1} \;\approx\; \frac{58^{\circ} \times 0.1715}{0.2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{HPBW} \;\approx\; 49.7^{\circ}\;}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the directivity patterns of **(i) Omnidirectional (monopole), (ii) Dipole (figure-eight), and (iii) Cardioid** sources. State one practical application for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **line array of $N = 8$ identical loudspeakers** is arranged in a vertical line with spacing $d = 0.2$ m, radiating at $f = 1000$ Hz. Compute:
- (i) The half-power beam width (HPBW) of the array in the vertical plane
- (ii) The total length $L$ of the array
- (iii) The approximate Directivity Index

Use $c = 343$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Description of Patterns:**

| Type | Pattern Shape | Q | DI (dB) | Application |
|------|---------------|---|---------|-------------|
| (i) Omnidirectional (monopole) | Perfect circle in polar plot | 1 | 0 | Ambient room mic, subwoofer (low freq), measurement mic |
| (ii) Dipole (figure-eight) | Two equal lobes, 180° apart; null at 90° | 3 | 4.77 | Ribbon mic, B-format ambisonic mic |
| (iii) Cardioid | Heart-shaped; null at 180° | 3 | 4.77 | Stage vocal mic, podcasting, conferencing |

> [!NOTE]
> Even though **Dipole** and **Cardioid** both have $Q = 3$, they are *not* the same pattern. $Q$ captures total on-axis concentration; the **shape** of the rejection (null) region differs. This is a frequent examiner's trap.

**(b) Numerical Computation for the Line Array:**

**Step 1 — Wavelength.**

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{c}{f} \;=\; \frac{343}{1000} \;=\; 0.343 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Total length of array.**

$$
L \;=\; (N - 1)\,d \;=\; 7 \times 0.2 \;=\; 1.4 \text{ m}
$$

(Equivalently, if the question uses the "aperture" definition: $L = Nd = 1.6$ m. Either convention is acceptable — **state the convention you used**.)

**Step 3 — HPBW for the line array.**

Using the broadside line-array formula:

$$
\text{HPBW} \;\approx\; \frac{50.6^{\circ}\,\lambda}{L} \;=\; \frac{50.6^{\circ} \times 0.343}{1.4} \;\approx\; \frac{17.36}{1.4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{HPBW} \;\approx\; 12.4^{\circ}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Approximate Q (line array).**

For a uniformly weighted line source:

$$
Q \;\approx\; \frac{2L}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 1.4}{0.343} \;\approx\; 8.16
$$

**Step 5 — Directivity Index.**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{DI} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}(8.16) \;\approx\; 9.12 \text{ dB}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Directivity Problems:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Q and DI**: $Q$ is a *ratio* (dimensionless); DI is in *decibels*. Converting incorrectly (e.g., $20\log_{10} Q$ instead of $10\log_{10} Q$) costs 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the far-field condition**: $r > 2a^{2}/\lambda$. If the question gives a distance $r$ that violates this, the simple formulas do *not* apply — partial credit lost.
> 3. **Wrong convention for HPBW**: Some textbooks report HPBW as the *full* angular width (between $-3$ dB points), others as the *half-angle* from the main axis. Always specify.
> 4. **Ignoring the $4\pi$ denominator**: The denominator in the Q-integral is $4\pi$ **steradians**, not $4\pi r^{2}$. Mixing these up gives wrong Q values.
> 5. **Cardioid vs. Hypercardioid confusion**: All first-order gradient microphones have $Q = 3$ *only if* they are pure cardioid or pure dipole. Super- and hyper-cardioids have $Q > 3$.
> 6. **Numerical rounding**: Keep at least 4 significant digits through intermediate steps; round only at the end. KTU examiners deduct marks for premature rounding.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Bookmark this section. Read it the night before the exam.**

- **Directivity Pattern** = angular map of radiated sound pressure (or intensity) around a source, normalized to on-axis = 1 (or 0 dB).
- **Directivity Factor Q** = on-axis intensity ÷ average intensity over a sphere; **dimensionless**.
- **Directivity Index DI** = $10 \log_{10}(Q)$, expressed in **dB**.
- **Point source**: $Q = 1$, DI = 0 dB (omnidirectional baseline).
- **Piston in infinite baffle**: pattern governed by $\dfrac{2J_{1}(ka\sin\theta)}{ka\sin\theta}$.
- **Line array**: pattern governed by $\dfrac{\sin((kL/2)\sin\theta)}{(kL/2)\sin\theta}$.
- **Helmholtz number $ka$** is the master parameter: small $ka$ ⇒ omnidirectional; large $ka$ ⇒ narrow beam.
- **HPBW (circular piston)** $\approx \dfrac{58^{\circ}\,\lambda}{2a}$.
- **HPBW (line array, broadside)** $\approx \dfrac{50.6^{\circ}\,\lambda}{L}$.
- **Far-field rule**: measure at $r > \dfrac{2a^{2}}{\lambda}$.
- **Polar plot axis convention**: 0° = on-axis; ±90° = perpendicular; 180° = behind source.
- **Cardioid and Dipole share Q = 3 (DI ≈ 4.77 dB)** but have *different* shapes — DI does NOT uniquely describe a pattern.
- **Engineering uses**: loudspeaker coverage, microphone noise rejection, sonar/radar beams, hearing-aid directionality, acoustic barriers.
- **High-yield formula cheat sheet**: see Section 2.2 table.
- **Always specify the convention** used for HPBW (full-width vs half-angle) and array length ($L$ vs $Nd$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
