Transmission and Reflection Coefficients (Pressure, Intensity and Power), Transmission Loss
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transmission and Reflection Coefficients (Pressure, Intensity and Power), Transmission Loss

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Boundary Interaction Problem

When an acoustic wave traveling through one medium (Medium 1) strikes a planar interface with another medium (Medium 2), **three things happen simultaneously**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus)**
> At any acoustic boundary, the incident energy is partitioned into a **reflected wave** that bounces back into Medium 1 and a **transmitted wave** that propagates forward into Medium 2. The fractions of acoustic energy associated with each path are defined by the **reflection coefficient** and the **transmission coefficient**.

Mathematically, the problem is governed by two fundamental **boundary conditions** that must hold at every point on the interface (at $x = 0$ for normal incidence):

- **Continuity of acoustic pressure** (no pressure discontinuity at a massless interface).
- **Continuity of normal particle velocity** (the interface cannot store fluid).

## 1.2 The Three "Flavors" of Coefficients

A common source of confusion (and a classic KTU trick question!) is that the coefficient values differ depending on *which* acoustic quantity we track.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Coefficient Pairs in Acoustics**
> 1. **Pressure Coefficient** — tracks the ratio of pressure amplitudes (used in mathematical derivations).
> 2. **Intensity Coefficient** — tracks the ratio of acoustic intensities (energy flux per unit area).
> 3. **Power Coefficient** — for plane progressive waves, this is mathematically identical to the intensity coefficient.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Rubber Net at the Pond"

Imagine dropping a small stone into a pond (Medium 1 = water surface) where a stretchy rubber net floats (the boundary). Beyond the net lies a deeper pool of denser water (Medium 2).

- If the rubber net is **tight and heavy** ($Z_2 \gg Z_1$), almost all the wave bounces back — high reflection.
- If the rubber net is **almost invisible** ($Z_2 \approx Z_1$), almost all the wave passes through — high transmission.
- If the net is **lighter than water** ($Z_2 < Z_1$), the wave partially transmits, but interestingly the *transmitted pressure* can be **larger than the incident pressure** — this is unique to acoustic (pressure) transmission and is a frequent KTU conceptual question.

The characteristic impedance $Z = \rho c$ (in **Rayls**, $1 \text{ Rayl} = 1 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$) is the "acoustic stiffness" of the medium and entirely determines what happens at the boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric & Constant**
> Acoustic impedance $Z = \rho c$ has units of $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ (Rayls). For air at 20 °C, $Z_{\text{air}} \approx \mathbf{413 \text{ Rayls}}$. For water, $Z_{\text{water}} \approx \mathbf{1.48 \times 10^6 \text{ Rayls}}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Setting Up the Mathematical Model

Consider a plane acoustic wave traveling in the $+x$ direction, striking a boundary at $x = 0$ that separates two infinite fluid media. Let:

- Medium 1 (incident side): characteristic impedance $Z_1 = \rho_1 c_1$
- Medium 2 (transmission side): characteristic impedance $Z_2 = \rho_2 c_2$

The total instantaneous acoustic pressure in each region is a superposition of two harmonic plane waves:

$$p_1(x,t) = p_i e^{j(\omega t - k_1 x)} + p_r e^{j(\omega t + k_1 x)}$$

$$p_2(x,t) = p_t e^{j(\omega t - k_2 x)}$$

where $p_i$, $p_r$, $p_t$ are the complex **pressure amplitudes** of incident, reflected, and transmitted waves, respectively.

## 2.2 Applying the Boundary Conditions

The two continuity conditions at $x = 0$ yield two independent linear equations:

**Condition 1 — Pressure continuity:**

$$p_i + p_r = p_t$$

**Condition 2 — Particle velocity continuity** (using $u = p / (\rho c)$ for plane progressive waves):

$$\frac{p_i - p_r}{Z_1} = \frac{p_t}{Z_2}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates every formula you must memorize for Module 2. Substitute absolute values with `\mid` when transcribing to your own notes to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Range / Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure Reflection Coefficient | $R_p$ | $R_p = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | $-1 \le R_p \le 1$ |
| Pressure Transmission Coefficient | $\tau_p$ | $\tau_p = \dfrac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | $0 \le \tau_p \le 2$ |
| Pressure Conservation Check | $R_p + \tau_p$ | $\dfrac{2 Z_2 + Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | Equals **1 only at normal incidence** |
| Intensity Reflection Coefficient | $\alpha_r$ | $\alpha_r = R_p^{\,2} = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | $0 \le \alpha_r \le 1$ |
| Intensity Transmission Coefficient | $\alpha_t$ | $\alpha_t = \dfrac{Z_1}{Z_2}\,\tau_p^{\,2} = \dfrac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | $0 \le \alpha_t \le 1$ |
| Power Transmission Coefficient | $\tau_P$ | $\tau_P = \alpha_t$ (for plane waves) | $\alpha_r + \tau_P = 1$ |
| Transmission Loss (dB) | $TL$ | $TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\tau_P}\right)$ | Always $\ge 0$ dB |
| Mass Law (TL of single panel) | $TL_{\text{mass}}$ | $TL_{\text{mass}} = 20 \log_{10}(\rho_s f) - 47$ | Valid for $f > f_c$ |
| Surface Density | $\rho_s$ | $\rho_s = \rho \, d$ (kg/m²) | — |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These coefficients form the **foundational design language of architectural acoustics, noise barriers, muffler design, and ultrasonic transducer matching**. For instance:

- Designing an **anechoic chamber** wall requires $\alpha_t \to 0$, achieved by using highly porous or impedance-mismatched absorbing materials.
- Building a **studio partition** between two rooms requires maximizing **TL** using the mass law (heavy, limp, decoupled layers).
- Designing an **ultrasonic medical probe** requires the opposite: a quarter-wave matching layer is engineered so that $Z_{\text{layer}} \approx \sqrt{Z_1 Z_2}$, maximizing $\alpha_t$ to nearly 1.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Reflection and Transmission Coefficients

**Step 1: Write down the governing equations at $x = 0$.**

The pressure continuity equation gives:

$$p_i + p_r = p_t$$

The particle velocity continuity equation gives:

$$\frac{p_i - p_r}{Z_1} = \frac{p_t}{Z_2}$$

**Step 2: Solve the system by elimination.**

Multiply the velocity equation by $Z_1 Z_2$ to clear denominators:

$$Z_2(p_i - p_r) = Z_1 p_t$$

**Step 3: Substitute $p_t$ from the first equation into the second.**

$$Z_2(p_i - p_r) = Z_1 (p_i + p_r)$$

Expanding the left-hand side:

$$Z_2 p_i - Z_2 p_r = Z_1 p_i + Z_1 p_r$$

**Step 4: Group terms containing $p_r$ on one side.**

$$Z_2 p_i - Z_1 p_i = Z_1 p_r + Z_2 p_r$$

$$p_i (Z_2 - Z_1) = p_r (Z_1 + Z_2)$$

**Step 5: Define the pressure reflection coefficient $R_p = p_r / p_i$.**

$$R_p = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

**Step 6: Solve for $p_t$ using the first equation $p_t = p_i + p_r$.**

Divide by $p_i$ and substitute $R_p$:

$$\tau_p = \frac{p_t}{p_i} = 1 + R_p = 1 + \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

$$\tau_p = \frac{Z_2 + Z_1 + Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

$$\boxed{\;\tau_p = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}\;}$$

This is the definitive pressure transmission coefficient used throughout KTU board examinations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Intensity (and Power) Reflection & Transmission Coefficients

The time-averaged acoustic intensity of a plane progressive wave is given by:

$$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{\rho c} = \frac{\vert p \vert^2}{2 Z}$$

where $\vert p \vert$ denotes the pressure amplitude (peak), and the factor of $1/2$ converts peak to RMS squared.

**Step 1: Compute incident, reflected, and transmitted intensities.**

$$I_i = \frac{\vert p_i \vert^2}{2 Z_1}, \qquad I_r = \frac{\vert p_r \vert^2}{2 Z_1}, \qquad I_t = \frac{\vert p_t \vert^2}{2 Z_2}$$

**Step 2: Form the intensity reflection coefficient $\alpha_r$.**

$$\alpha_r = \frac{I_r}{I_i} = \frac{\vert p_r \vert^2 / (2 Z_1)}{\vert p_i \vert^2 / (2 Z_1)} = \left(\frac{p_r}{p_i}\right)^2$$

$$\boxed{\;\alpha_r = R_p^{\,2} = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2\;}$$

**Step 3: Form the intensity transmission coefficient $\alpha_t$.**

$$\alpha_t = \frac{I_t}{I_i} = \frac{\vert p_t \vert^2 / (2 Z_2)}{\vert p_i \vert^2 / (2 Z_1)} = \frac{Z_1}{Z_2}\left(\frac{p_t}{p_i}\right)^2$$

Substituting $\tau_p$:

$$\alpha_t = \frac{Z_1}{Z_2}\left(\frac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2 = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$

$$\boxed{\;\alpha_t = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}\;}$$

**Step 4: Verify energy conservation.**

$$\alpha_r + \alpha_t = \frac{(Z_2 - Z_1)^2 + 4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_2 + Z_1)^2} = \frac{Z_2^2 - 2 Z_1 Z_2 + Z_1^2 + 4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_2 + Z_1)^2}$$

$$\alpha_r + \alpha_t = \frac{Z_1^2 + 2 Z_1 Z_2 + Z_2^2}{(Z_2 + Z_1)^2} = \frac{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}{(Z_2 + Z_1)^2} = 1 \;\; \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transmission Loss from First Principles

**Definition.** Transmission Loss is the logarithmic ratio (in decibels) of incident acoustic power to transmitted acoustic power:

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_i}{I_t}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\alpha_t}\right)$$

Substituting $\alpha_t$:

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}{4 Z_1 Z_2}\right]$$

For the common practical case of an airborne sound wave striking a **solid partition** (Medium 1 = air, $Z_1 \approx 413$ Rayls; Medium 2 = solid, $Z_2 \gg Z_1$):

$$TL \approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{Z_2}{4 Z_1}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\rho_2 c_2}{4 \rho_1 c_1}\right)$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Mass Law

For a thin flexible panel (mass per unit area $\rho_s$, kg/m²), the panel's impedance at frequency $f$ (Hz) is dominated by its **mass reactance** $\omega \rho_s$. Replacing $Z_2$ in the TL formula with $j \omega \rho_s$ and taking the modulus:

$$TL \approx 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\omega \rho_s}{2 \rho_1 c_1}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 \pi f \rho_s}{2 \rho_1 c_1}\right)$$

Using $\rho_1 c_1 = 413$ Rayls for air:

$$TL_{\text{mass}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\pi f \rho_s\right) - 20 \log_{10}(413)$$

$$20 \log_{10}(413) \approx 20 \times 2.616 \approx 52.32$$

$$\boxed{\;TL_{\text{mass}} = 20 \log_{10}(f \rho_s) - 47 \quad \text{[dB]}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The mass law predicts that **doubling the frequency** or **doubling the surface density** both increase TL by **6 dB** (an octave drop in transmitted power).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Boundary Interaction & Coefficient Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Incident Wave in Medium 1"] --> B["Boundary at x = 0"]
    B --> C["Reflected Wave back into Medium 1"]
    B --> D["Transmitted Wave into Medium 2"]
    C --> E["Pressure Coefficient Rp"]
    D --> F["Pressure Coefficient taup"]
    E --> G["Intensity Coefficient alpha_r = Rp squared"]
    F --> H["Intensity Coefficient alpha_t = Z1 by Z2 times taup squared"]
    G --> I["Conservation Check alpha_r plus alpha_t equals 1"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Transmission Loss TL equals 10 log10 of 1 by alpha_t"]
    J --> K["Mass Law Approximation TL mass equals 20 log10 f rho_s minus 47"]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Architecture — Three-Stage Partitioning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stage1["Stage 1 Geometric Definition Incident Reflected Transmitted Waves"] --> stage2["Stage 2 Algebraic Evaluation Apply Boundary Conditions Solve Linear System"]
    stage2 --> stage3["Stage 3 Physical Interpretation Energy Partition Compute Transmission Loss"]
    stage1 --- noteA["Inputs Z1 and Z2 frequency f surface density rho_s"]
    noteA --> stage2
    stage3 --> result["Outputs Rp taup alpha_r alpha_t TL dB"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Impedance Mismatch Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{"Compare Z1 and Z2"} --> R1["Case A Z2 greater than Z1 Stiffer Medium"]
    Q --> R2["Case B Z2 equals Z1 Matched Impedance"]
    Q --> R3["Case C Z2 less than Z1 Compliant Medium"]
    R1 --> O1["Rp positive alpha_t less than 0.5 TL increases with f"]
    R2 --> O2["Rp equals 0 taup equals 1 alpha_t equals 1 TL equals 0 dB no reflection"]
    R3 --> O3["Rp negative taup greater than 1 alpha_t less than 1 phase inversion with pressure amplification"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the intensity transmission coefficient. How is it different from the pressure transmission coefficient?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The intensity transmission coefficient is defined as the ratio of transmitted acoustic intensity to incident acoustic intensity:

$$\alpha_t = \frac{I_t}{I_i} = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$

**Differences (1 Mark each):**

| Aspect | Pressure Coefficient $\tau_p$ | Intensity Coefficient $\alpha_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| Ratio tracked | Pressure amplitudes | Acoustic power flux |
| Range | $0 \le \tau_p \le 2$ | $0 \le \alpha_t \le 1$ |
| Energy conservation | Not preserved alone | Always $\alpha_r + \alpha_t = 1$ |

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and briefly explain the Mass Law for a single panel partition.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Mass Law states that the transmission loss of a thin, limp, single-panel partition in the **mass-controlled region** (above the coincidence frequency) is given by:

$$TL_{\text{mass}} = 20 \log_{10}(f \rho_s) - 47 \;\; \text{dB}$$

where $f$ is the frequency in Hz and $\rho_s = \rho \, d$ is the surface density in kg/m². **(2 Marks)**

The law implies that doubling the frequency or surface density increases TL by 6 dB. **(1 Mark)**

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]`

**A plane sound wave of frequency 500 Hz and SPL 90 dB propagates in air ($Z_1 = 413$ Rayls) and strikes perpendicularly a concrete wall of impedance $Z_2 = 8 \times 10^6$ Rayls. Determine:**

**(a)** The pressure and intensity reflection coefficients, and verify that they satisfy the energy conservation law. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The transmission loss of the wall, the transmitted intensity, and the transmitted SPL. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Identify the impedances.** (1 Mark)

$$Z_1 = 413 \text{ Rayls}, \qquad Z_2 = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ Rayls}$$

Since $Z_2 \gg Z_1$, the concrete is acoustically very "stiff" relative to air.

**Step 2: Compute the pressure reflection coefficient $R_p$.** (2 Marks)

$$R_p = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} = \frac{8 \times 10^6 - 413}{8 \times 10^6 + 413} \approx \frac{7.9996 \times 10^6}{8.0004 \times 10^6} \approx 0.9999$$

**Step 3: Compute the intensity reflection coefficient $\alpha_r$.** (2 Marks)

$$\alpha_r = R_p^{\,2} = (0.9999)^2 \approx 0.9998$$

**Step 4: Compute the intensity transmission coefficient $\alpha_t$ and verify conservation.** (2 Marks)

$$\alpha_t = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = \frac{4 \times 413 \times 8 \times 10^6}{(8.0004 \times 10^6)^2}$$

$$\alpha_t = \frac{1.3216 \times 10^{10}}{6.4006 \times 10^{13}} \approx 2.065 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Verification:**

$$\alpha_r + \alpha_t = 0.9998 + 2.065 \times 10^{-4} \approx 1.0000 \;\; \checkmark$$

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute Transmission Loss.** (3 Marks)

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\alpha_t}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{2.065 \times 10^{-4}}\right)$$

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}(4843) = 10 \times 3.685 = 36.85 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2: Convert incident SPL to intensity.** (1 Mark)

$$I_i = I_{\text{ref}} \times 10^{L_p/10} = 10^{-12} \times 10^{9} = 10^{-3} \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute transmitted intensity.** (1 Mark)

$$I_t = \alpha_t \, I_i = 2.065 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{-3} = 2.065 \times 10^{-7} \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Compute transmitted SPL.** (2 Marks)

$$L_{p,t} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_t}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.065 \times 10^{-7}}{10^{-12}}\right)$$

$$L_{p,t} = 10 \log_{10}(2.065 \times 10^{5}) = 10 \times 5.315 = 53.15 \text{ dB}$$

**Cross-check:** $90 \text{ dB} - 36.85 \text{ dB} = 53.15 \text{ dB}$ ✓

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 2]`

**Derive from first principles the expressions for:**

**(a)** The pressure reflection and transmission coefficients when a plane acoustic wave strikes a planar boundary between two media. State clearly all boundary conditions used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The intensity reflection and intensity transmission coefficients. Show that the sum equals unity and explain why the power transmission coefficient is identical to the intensity transmission coefficient for plane progressive waves. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Express the total pressure field in each medium.** (1 Mark)

$$p_1(x,t) = p_i e^{j(\omega t - k_1 x)} + p_r e^{j(\omega t + k_1 x)}$$

$$p_2(x,t) = p_t e^{j(\omega t - k_2 x)}$$

**Step 2: State the two boundary conditions at $x = 0$.** (2 Marks)

- **Pressure continuity:** $p_1(0,t) = p_2(0,t) \Rightarrow p_i + p_r = p_t$
- **Velocity continuity:** $u_1(0,t) = u_2(0,t) \Rightarrow \dfrac{p_i - p_r}{Z_1} = \dfrac{p_t}{Z_2}$

**Step 3: Eliminate $p_t$ between the two equations.** (2 Marks)

$$Z_2(p_i - p_r) = Z_1(p_i + p_r) \Rightarrow p_i(Z_2 - Z_1) = p_r(Z_1 + Z_2)$$

**Step 4: Obtain $R_p$ and $\tau_p$.** (2 Marks)

$$R_p = \frac{p_r}{p_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

$$\tau_p = \frac{p_t}{p_i} = 1 + R_p = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Recall intensity expression for plane wave.** (1 Mark)

$$I = \frac{\vert p \vert^2}{2 \rho c} = \frac{\vert p \vert^2}{2 Z}$$

**Step 2: Derive intensity reflection coefficient.** (2 Marks)

$$\alpha_r = \frac{I_r}{I_i} = \left(\frac{p_r}{p_i}\right)^2 = R_p^{\,2} = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

**Step 3: Derive intensity transmission coefficient.** (2 Marks)

$$\alpha_t = \frac{I_t}{I_i} = \frac{Z_1}{Z_2}\left(\frac{p_t}{p_i}\right)^2 = \frac{Z_1}{Z_2}\left(\frac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2 = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$

**Step 4: Prove conservation and explain equivalence.** (2 Marks)

$$\alpha_r + \alpha_t = \frac{(Z_2 - Z_1)^2 + 4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = \frac{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = 1 \;\; \checkmark$$

The **power** transmitted through any cross-section of area $A$ is $P = I \cdot A$ for plane waves (because intensity is uniform across the wavefront). Hence $\tau_P = P_t / P_i = I_t / I_i = \alpha_t$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Mixing coefficients:** A common blunder is writing $\alpha_t = \tau_p$ instead of $\alpha_t = (Z_1/Z_2)\tau_p^2$. This loses 2 marks instantly. **Always square the pressure ratio and multiply by $Z_1/Z_2$ for intensity.**
> 2. **Forgetting the Mass Law constant:** Using $-42$ instead of $-47$ dB. The constant arises from $20 \log_{10}(2 \rho_1 c_1) = 20 \log_{10}(826) \approx 58.3$, then subtracting to give $-47$ — show the working.
> 3. **Phase inversion:** When $Z_2 < Z_1$, $R_p$ is **negative**. This means a phase reversal of the reflected wave — students often forget to mention this in viva.
> 4. **SPL arithmetic:** Going from SPL (dB) to intensity requires multiplying $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}$ W/m² by $10^{L_p/10}$, **not** $L_p/20$. The factor of 10 (not 20) is for intensity-based SPL.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Two boundary conditions** at $x = 0$: pressure continuity & particle velocity continuity.
- **Pressure reflection coefficient:** $R_p = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$ — ranges $-1$ to $+1$.
- **Pressure transmission coefficient:** $\tau_p = 2 Z_2/(Z_2 + Z_1)$ — ranges $0$ to $2$.
- $R_p + \tau_p = 1$ holds **only at normal incidence** — a KTU favourite.
- **Intensity reflection coefficient:** $\alpha_r = R_p^{\,2}$ (always non-negative).
- **Intensity transmission coefficient:** $\alpha_t = (Z_1/Z_2)\,\tau_p^{\,2} = 4 Z_1 Z_2/(Z_1 + Z_2)^2$.
- **Energy conservation:** $\alpha_r + \alpha_t = 1$ (always true for plane waves).
- **Power coefficient** $\tau_P$ equals intensity coefficient for plane progressive waves.
- **Transmission Loss:** $TL = 10 \log_{10}(1/\alpha_t)$ — measured in dB.
- **Mass Law:** $TL = 20 \log_{10}(f \rho_s) - 47$ dB — doubling $f$ or $\rho_s$ adds 6 dB.
- **Phase inversion** occurs when $Z_2 < Z_1$ (softer medium behind the boundary).
- **Matched condition:** $Z_1 = Z_2 \Rightarrow R_p = 0,\; \tau_p = 1,\; TL = 0$ dB.
- **Standard impedances:** Air $\approx 413$ Rayls; Water $\approx 1.48 \times 10^6$ Rayls; Steel $\approx 4.6 \times 10^7$ Rayls.
- **Engineering applications:** anechoic walls ($\alpha_t \to 0$), partitions (maximize TL via mass law), ultrasonic matching layers ($\sqrt{Z_1 Z_2}$ for max transmission).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Absorption Coefficient, Sound Propagation in Open or a Closed Tube
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Absorption Coefficient, Sound Propagation in Open or a Closed Tube

# Absorption Coefficient & Sound Propagation in Open/Closed Tubes

## 1.1 Absorption Coefficient — The Core Definition

The **Absorption Coefficient ($\alpha$)** is formally defined as the ratio of the sound energy absorbed by a surface to the total sound energy incident upon that surface. It is a dimensionless quantity ranging from **0 to 1** (or expressed as a percentage from **0% to 100%**).

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\alpha = \frac{E_{\text{absorbed}}}{E_{\text{incident}}} = \frac{E_i - E_r}{E_i}$$

where $E_r$ is the reflected sound energy, $E_i$ is the incident sound energy, and $E_{\text{absorbed}}$ is the absorbed portion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sabine's Definition of Absorption:** A perfectly absorbing surface has $\alpha = 1.0$ (absorbs 100% of incident sound), while a perfectly reflecting surface has $\alpha = 0.0$ (reflects 100%). The **Sabine absorption coefficient** is typically measured at standard frequencies of **125 Hz, 250 Hz, 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz, and 4 kHz** in architectural acoustics.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge and the Mirror"

Imagine throwing a tennis ball at two different surfaces:

1. **A soft sponge wall (High Absorption, $\alpha \approx 0.9$):** The ball hits and hardly bounces back — its kinetic energy is converted into heat and deformation. Similarly, porous materials (foam, fiberglass) convert acoustic energy into heat through viscous friction in their tiny pores.

2. **A polished marble wall (Low Absorption, $\alpha \approx 0.02$):** The ball bounces back with almost all its energy intact. Similarly, hard, dense surfaces like concrete reflect nearly all incident sound energy.

The **Absorption Coefficient** is essentially the "sponginess number" of a material — telling us what fraction of sound energy gets trapped and dissipated rather than reflected back into the room.

---

## 1.2 Sound Propagation in Open and Closed Tubes

When sound waves travel through a tube (or pipe), they reflect off the ends, creating **standing waves** (also called stationary waves). The boundary conditions at each end of the tube dictate the allowed patterns of vibration.

### Open Tube (Both Ends Open)

When a tube is **open at both ends**, the air molecules at the open ends are free to vibrate. The pressure at the open end equals the atmospheric pressure, while the displacement of air is at its maximum.

| Boundary Condition | Open End |
|---|---|
| **Pressure** | Minimum (Pressure Antinode) — $\Delta p = 0$ |
| **Displacement** | Maximum (Displacement Antinode) |
| **Implication** | Acoustic impedance is at a **minimum** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In an open-open tube, both ends behave as **displacement antinodes**. The tube resonates with frequencies that fit half-wavelengths into its length. This is the principle behind flutes, organ pipes (open), and many wind instruments.

### Closed Tube (One End Closed, One End Open)

When one end of a tube is **closed**, the air molecules cannot vibrate at the closed end — they are physically constrained.

| Boundary Condition | Closed End | Open End |
|---|---|---|
| **Pressure** | Maximum (Pressure Antinode) | Minimum (Pressure Antinode) |
| **Displacement** | **Node** (zero displacement) | Antinode (maximum displacement) |
| **Implication** | High acoustic impedance | Low acoustic impedance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** In a closed-open tube, the closed end is always a **displacement node**, and the open end is always a **displacement antinode**. Only **odd harmonics** of the fundamental frequency are produced — this is the principle behind clarinets, stopped organ pipes, and Helmholtz resonators.

---

## 1.3 Why Tubes Matter in Acoustics

Tubes form the fundamental geometry for many real-world acoustic systems:

- **Industrial:** Exhaust mufflers in automobiles use precisely tuned closed/open tube geometries to cancel specific engine harmonics.
- **Architectural:** Helmholtz resonators (a cavity connected to the room via a narrow neck/tube) absorb low-frequency noise.
- **Measurement:** The **Standing Wave Tube (Kundt's Tube)** is used in labs to measure the absorption coefficient of materials.
- **Musical:** Wind instruments (flutes, trumpets, clarinets) are essentially tubes with specific end conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Helmholtz Resonator:** A classic example of a closed-open tube system. It consists of a rigid cavity (volume $V$) connected to the external air through a narrow neck (length $L$, cross-sectional area $S$). It has a single sharp resonant frequency given by $f_r = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{S}{V \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}}$ where $L_{\text{eff}} = L + 0.85 d$ accounts for end correction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Mechanisms of Sound Absorption

The absorption coefficient is **not a single phenomenon** — it is the sum of multiple physical mechanisms. For a given surface, the total absorption coefficient $\alpha_{\text{total}}$ is given by:

$$\alpha_{\text{total}} = \alpha_{\text{porous}} + \alpha_{\text{resonant}} + \alpha_{\text{membrane}} + \alpha_{\text{panel}}$$

#### Breakdown of Absorption Mechanisms:

1. **Porous Absorption** ($\alpha_{\text{porous}}$)
   - Sound energy is dissipated by **viscous friction** as air oscillates within the tiny pores of materials like fiberglass, mineral wool, open-cell foam, and acoustic tiles.
   - Most effective at **mid to high frequencies**.
   - The resistive component of acoustic impedance dominates.

2. **Resonant Absorption** ($\alpha_{\text{resonant}}$)
   - A cavity (like a Helmholtz resonator) vibrates at its natural frequency when excited by sound.
   - Energy is dissipated via viscous losses in the neck and thermal losses in the cavity.
   - Highly effective at **low frequencies** where porous absorbers fail.
   - Bandwidth is narrow — sharp absorption peak at $f_r$.

3. **Membrane Absorption** ($\alpha_{\text{membrane}}$)
   - A thin, flexible membrane (like plywood or vinyl) vibrates against a closed air cavity.
   - Most effective at **low frequencies** (50–500 Hz).
   - Operates on the mass-spring principle: air cavity = spring, membrane = mass.

4. **Panel Absorption** ($\alpha_{\text{panel}}$)
   - Thin panels (like gypsum board) mounted over a cavity.
   - Similar to membrane absorption — the panel-mass and air-cavity-spring form a resonant system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A common exam question asks students to identify **which type of absorber suits which frequency range**. Memorize the matching:
> - **Low frequencies (50–500 Hz):** Membrane, Panel, Helmholtz Resonator
> - **Mid frequencies (500 Hz – 2 kHz):** Porous absorbers (fiberglass, foam)
> - **High frequencies (> 2 kHz):** Thick porous absorbers, diffusers

---

### 2.2 The Helmholtz Resonator — A Worked Engineering Concept

The Helmholtz resonator is the **prototype closed-tube acoustic system**. Its working principle is best understood through the **mass-spring analogy**:

| Acoustic Component | Mechanical Analog |
|---|---|
| Air in the neck (mass $m$) | Mass on a spring |
| Air in the cavity (compliance $C$) | Spring (compressibility) |
| Viscous resistance in neck | Dashpot (damping) |
| Sound pressure driving the neck | External force |

The acoustic mass of the air in the neck is:

$$m_a = \rho_0 \cdot \frac{S \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}{S^2} = \frac{\rho_0 \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}{S}$$

where $\rho_0$ is the **density of air (≈ 1.21 kg/m³ at STP)**, $S$ is the neck cross-section, and $L_{\text{eff}}$ includes end correction.

The acoustic compliance of the cavity is:

$$C_a = \frac{V}{\rho_0 \cdot c^2}$$

where $V$ is the cavity volume and $c$ is the **speed of sound (≈ 343 m/s at 20°C)**.

The resonant frequency follows from the mass-spring formula $f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1}{m_a C_a}}$, yielding:

$$f_r = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{S}{V \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}}$$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables / Units** | **Applicable Tube Type** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Absorption Coefficient | $\alpha = \frac{E_i - E_r}{E_i}$ | Dimensionless ($0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$) | All |
| 2 | Sabine Absorption (Open Window) | $A = S \cdot \alpha$ | $A$ in sabins (m²), $S$ in m² | All |
| 3 | Reverberation Time (Sabine) | $T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \cdot V}{A}$ | $T$ in seconds, $V$ in m³ | Room acoustics |
| 4 | Open Tube Fundamental | $f_1 = \frac{c}{2L}$ | $c \approx 343$ m/s, $L$ in m | Both ends open |
| 5 | Open Tube Harmonics | $f_n = \frac{n \cdot c}{2L}$, $n = 1,2,3,\ldots$ | All harmonics present | Both ends open |
| 6 | Closed Tube Fundamental | $f_1 = \frac{c}{4L}$ | Same $c$ and $L$ | One end closed |
| 7 | Closed Tube Harmonics | $f_n = \frac{n \cdot c}{4L}$, $n = 1,3,5,\ldots$ | Only odd harmonics | One end closed |
| 8 | End Correction (flanged) | $\Delta L = 0.85 \cdot d$ | $d$ = neck diameter | Open end |
| 9 | End Correction (unflanged) | $\Delta L = 0.613 \cdot d$ | $d$ = neck diameter | Open end |
| 10 | Helmholtz Resonator $f_r$ | $f_r = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{S}{V \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}}$ | $S$ in m², $V$ in m³ | Cavity + neck |
| 11 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$ | $\lambda$ in meters | All |
| 12 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho_0 \cdot c$ (plane wave) | $\approx 413$ Rayl (Pa·s/m) | Plane waves |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Automotive Mufflers:** A typical car muffler is an arrangement of closed/open tubes tuned to cancel specific engine frequencies using destructive interference.

2. **Concert Halls:** Walls and ceilings use porous absorbers to control reverberation time to optimal values (1.8–2.2 seconds for symphony halls).

3. **Recording Studios:** Bass traps (Helmholtz resonators tuned to 40–80 Hz) are installed in corners to absorb standing low-frequency modes.

4. **Noise Barriers along Highways:** Micro-perforated panels (MPP) combine both porous and resonant absorption, providing broadband attenuation.

5. **Anechoic Chambers:** Wedges of foam with $\alpha > 0.99$ above 500 Hz simulate a free-field environment for acoustic measurements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** When designing absorption systems, the **Sabine coefficient** measured in the lab (reverberation room) is usually different from the in-situ coefficient because of edge diffraction, mounting conditions, and angle of incidence. Always design with a safety factor of **1.2–1.5×** for critical applications like auditoria.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Standing Wave Formation in an Open Tube

Consider a tube of length $L$, open at both ends. A sound source at one end generates a pressure wave that travels toward the other open end, reflects (with phase reversal for a pressure wave reflecting from a low-impedance open end), and returns.

**Step 1:** Define the incident wave traveling in the $+x$ direction:

$$p_i(x,t) = P_0 \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step 2:** The reflected wave from the open end travels in the $-x$ direction. At an open end, pressure reflection coefficient is $-1$ (phase inversion for pressure, no inversion for displacement):

$$p_r(x,t) = -P_0 \sin(kx + \omega t)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the principle of superposition to get the resultant standing wave:

$$p(x,t) = p_i(x,t) + p_r(x,t) = P_0[\sin(kx - \omega t) - \sin(kx + \omega t)]$$

**Step 4:** Apply the trigonometric identity $\sin A - \sin B = 2\cos\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)\sin\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$:

$$p(x,t) = P_0 \cdot 2 \cdot \cos(\omega t) \cdot \sin(kx)$$

$$\boxed{p(x,t) = 2 P_0 \sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **boundary condition at the open end** ($x = L$): Pressure must equal atmospheric pressure, so $\Delta p = 0$ at the open end:

$$\sin(kL) = 0 \implies kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$:

$$\frac{2\pi L}{\lambda_n} = n\pi \implies \lambda_n = \frac{2L}{n}$$

**Step 7:** Use $c = f\lambda$ to find resonant frequencies:

$$f_n = \frac{c}{\lambda_n} = \frac{n \cdot c}{2L}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 8:** Identify the **fundamental mode** ($n = 1$): $f_1 = \frac{c}{2L}$. The wavelength of the fundamental equals **twice the tube length** ($\lambda_1 = 2L$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For an open-open tube of length 0.5 m at 20°C (c = 343 m/s):
> $$f_1 = \frac{1 \cdot 343}{2 \cdot 0.5} = 343 \text{ Hz}$$
> The 2nd harmonic is 686 Hz, 3rd is 1029 Hz — all harmonics are present.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Standing Wave Formation in a Closed Tube

Consider a tube of length $L$ closed at $x = 0$ and open at $x = L$.

**Step 1:** Incident wave: $p_i(x,t) = P_0 \sin(kx - \omega t)$

**Step 2:** Reflected wave from the closed end (high-impedance boundary — no phase inversion for pressure, but full inversion for displacement):

$$p_r(x,t) = +P_0 \sin(kx + \omega t)$$

**Step 3:** Superposition:

$$p(x,t) = P_0[\sin(kx - \omega t) + \sin(kx + \omega t)]$$

**Step 4:** Apply the identity $\sin A + \sin B = 2\sin\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)\cos\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$:

$$\boxed{p(x,t) = 2 P_0 \sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)}$$

**Step 5:** Boundary conditions for closed-open tube:
- At closed end ($x = 0$): displacement is zero, pressure is maximum → already satisfied since $\sin(0) = 0$ gives $\Delta p_{\max}$.
- At open end ($x = L$): $\Delta p = 0$ → $\sin(kL) = 0 \implies kL = n\pi$.

Wait — this gives the same result as the open-open case. Let us examine displacement boundary conditions carefully.

**Step 6 (Refined):** For the closed-open tube, we need displacement to be **zero at the closed end** ($x = 0$) and **maximum at the open end** ($x = L$):

The displacement wave is $\xi(x,t) = -\frac{1}{\rho_0 c} \cdot 2P_0 \cos(kx)\cos(\omega t)$. Setting $\xi(0,t) = 0$ gives a maximum automatically. Setting $\xi(L,t)$ to be a maximum:

$$\cos(kL) = \pm 1 \implies kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$$

**Step 7:** Hence only **odd harmonics** are allowed:

$$f_n = \frac{n \cdot c}{4L}, \quad n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$$

$$\boxed{f_1 = \frac{c}{4L}, \quad f_3 = \frac{3c}{4L}, \quad f_5 = \frac{5c}{4L}, \ldots}$$

**Step 8:** For a closed tube of length $L = 0.5$ m at 20°C:
$$f_1 = \frac{343}{4 \cdot 0.5} = 171.5 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_3 = 514.5 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_5 = 857.5 \text{ Hz}$$

The even harmonics (2nd, 4th, ...) are **forbidden** — they cannot exist in a closed-open tube.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Helmholtz Resonator Resonant Frequency

**Step 1:** The acoustic mass of air in the neck (length $L$, area $S$, with end correction $\Delta L$):

$$m = \rho_0 \cdot S \cdot (L + \Delta L) = \rho_0 S L_{\text{eff}}$$

**Step 2:** The acoustic compliance (springiness) of the cavity (volume $V$):

When air in the cavity is compressed by a small volume $\Delta V$, the pressure increases by $\Delta p = \frac{\gamma p_0 \Delta V}{V}$, where $\gamma$ is the ratio of specific heats ($1.4$ for air). The compliance is:

$$C = \frac{V}{\gamma p_0} = \frac{V}{\rho_0 c^2}$$

(using $c^2 = \frac{\gamma p_0}{\rho_0}$).

**Step 3:** Treat as a mass-spring system. The resonant angular frequency is:

$$\omega_r = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m C}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho_0 c^2}{\rho_0 S L_{\text{eff}} \cdot V}} = c \sqrt{\frac{1}{S L_{\text{eff}} V}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\omega_r = 2\pi f_r$:

$$f_r = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{S}{V \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical example — A Helmholtz resonator with $V = 0.005$ m³ (5 liters), $S = 0.001$ m² (10 cm² neck), $L = 0.05$ m, neck diameter $d = 0.0353$ m:

$$L_{\text{eff}} = L + 0.85d = 0.05 + 0.85 \times 0.0353 = 0.05 + 0.030 = 0.080 \text{ m}$$

$$f_r = \frac{343}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{0.001}{0.005 \times 0.080}} = \frac{343}{2\pi}\sqrt{2.5} \approx 54.6 \times 1.581 \approx 86.3 \text{ Hz}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Open Tube (Both Ends Open)** | **Closed Tube (One End Closed)** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Boundary condition at $x = 0$ | Antinode (displacement) | Node (displacement) |
| Boundary condition at $x = L$ | Antinode (displacement) | Antinode (displacement) |
| Fundamental wavelength | $\lambda_1 = 2L$ | $\lambda_1 = 4L$ |
| Fundamental frequency | $f_1 = \dfrac{c}{2L}$ | $f_1 = \dfrac{c}{4L}$ |
| Allowed harmonics | $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ | $n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$ (odd only) |
| Number of nodes (fundamental) | 1 internal node | 1 node (at closed end) |
| Pressure variation | Maximum at center, zero at ends | Maximum at closed end, zero at open end |
| Musical example | Flute, open organ pipe | Clarinet, stopped organ pipe |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Standing Wave Modes in Open and Closed Tubes

The following Mermaid block renders the functional architecture of standing wave node/antinode positions in both tube geometries. This serves as a visual substitute for physical drawings of the actual wave envelopes.

```mermaid

    E --> F[Fundamental: lambda equals 2L]

    B -->|Closed-Open Tube| H[Node at x=0 closed end]
    H --> I[Antinode at x=L open end]
    I --> J[Fundamental: lambda equals 4L]

```

    E --> F[Acoustic Compliance C equals V over rho times c squared]
    E --> G[Thermal Losses R cavity]
