## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term **Absorption Coefficient**. Mention its range of values and explain what a coefficient of $\alpha = 0.85$ physically means for a given surface.

**Model Answer:**

The **Absorption Coefficient ($\alpha$)** is defined as the ratio of the sound energy absorbed by a surface to the total sound energy incident upon that surface:

$$\alpha = \frac{E_{\text{absorbed}}}{E_{\text{incident}}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** quantity ranging from **0 to 1** (or 0% to 100%). Physically, $\alpha = 0$ corresponds to a perfect reflector (like a polished marble wall), and $\alpha = 1$ corresponds to a perfect absorber (like an open window to free space).

An absorption coefficient of **$\alpha = 0.85$** means that **85% of the incident sound energy is absorbed** by the surface and converted to heat (or otherwise dissipated), while only **15% is reflected** back into the room. Such a value is characteristic of materials like 25 mm thick fiberglass panels at mid-frequencies (around 1 kHz).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** State the **fundamental frequency** of vibration for (a) an open tube and (b) a closed tube of length $L$. Why are only odd harmonics produced in a closed tube?

**Model Answer:**

(a) For a tube **open at both ends**, the fundamental frequency is:

$$f_1 = \frac{c}{2L}$$

(b) For a tube **closed at one end** (and open at the other), the fundamental frequency is:

$$f_1 = \frac{c}{4L}$$

**Why only odd harmonics in a closed tube:** In a closed-open tube, the closed end is a **displacement node** (fixed boundary, air cannot move) and the open end is a **displacement antinode** (free boundary, air moves freely). The boundary conditions are satisfied only when the tube length accommodates an odd multiple of quarter-wavelengths:

$$L = \frac{n\lambda}{4}, \quad n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$$

Even values of $n$ would require a node at the open end or an antinode at the closed end — both of which violate the physical boundary conditions. Hence **only odd harmonics ($f_1, 3f_1, 5f_1, \ldots$)** exist in a closed tube.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question below carries 14 marks with internal choice. You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Each contains sub-parts (a) and (b) worth 7 marks each.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **resonant frequency of a Helmholtz resonator**. A resonator has a cavity volume of 8 liters and a cylindrical neck of length 5 cm and diameter 3 cm. Calculate its resonant frequency. Take $c = 343$ m/s and assume flanged end correction.

**(b)** Explain with diagrams the **standing wave patterns** in (i) an open tube of length $L$, and (ii) a closed tube of length $L$. Tabulate the first three resonant frequencies for both cases.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Helmholtz Resonator Frequency:**

The Helmholtz resonator is modeled as a mass-spring system:

- **Acoustic mass** of air in the neck: $m = \rho_0 S L_{\text{eff}}$
- **Acoustic compliance** of cavity: $C = \dfrac{V}{\rho_0 c^2}$

The resonant angular frequency is:

$$\omega_r = \sqrt{\frac{1}{mC}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho_0 c^2}{\rho_0 S L_{\text{eff}} V}} = c\sqrt{\frac{1}{S L_{\text{eff}} V}}$$

Substituting $\omega_r = 2\pi f_r$:

$$f_r = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{S}{V \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $V = 8$ liters $= 0.008$ m³
- $L = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m
- $d = 3$ cm $= 0.03$ m
- $S = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.03)^2}{4} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- $L_{\text{eff}} = L + 0.85 d = 0.05 + 0.85 \times 0.03 = 0.05 + 0.0255 = 0.0755$ m

$$f_r = \frac{343}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{7.069 \times 10^{-4}}{0.008 \times 0.0755}}$$

$$f_r = \frac{343}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{7.069 \times 10^{-4}}{6.04 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

$$f_r = \frac{343}{2\pi}\sqrt{1.1703}$$

$$f_r = 54.60 \times 1.0818 \approx 59.07 \text{ Hz}$$

**∴ The resonant frequency is approximately 59.1 Hz.**

**(b) Standing Wave Patterns:**

(i) **Open Tube (Length $L$, both ends open):**

Both ends are **displacement antinodes**. The standing wave for the fundamental mode has:
- $\lambda_1 = 2L$
- $f_1 = \dfrac{c}{2L}$
- One pressure node at the center ($x = L/2$)
- Pressure antinodes at both open ends

The first three modes have wavelengths $2L$, $L$, and $\dfrac{2L}{3}$ respectively.

(ii) **Closed Tube (Length $L$, one end closed):**

Closed end is a **displacement node**; open end is a **displacement antinode**. The fundamental mode has:
- $\lambda_1 = 4L$
- $f_1 = \dfrac{c}{4L}$
- Pressure maximum at closed end
- Pressure minimum at open end

The first three modes have wavelengths $4L$, $\dfrac{4L}{3}$, and $\dfrac{4L}{5}$ respectively.

**Tabulation of First Three Resonant Frequencies:**

| Mode ($n$) | Open Tube Frequency | Closed Tube Frequency |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1st ($n=1$) | $\dfrac{c}{2L}$ | $\dfrac{c}{4L}$ |
| 2nd ($n=2$) | $\dfrac{2c}{2L} = \dfrac{c}{L}$ | **Not allowed** |
| 3rd ($n=3$) | $\dfrac{3c}{2L}$ | $\dfrac{3c}{4L}$ |
| 5th ($n=5$) | — | $\dfrac{5c}{4L}$ |

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define **Absorption Coefficient** and explain any **three physical mechanisms** by which sound energy is absorbed when it strikes a surface. Discuss how the absorption coefficient varies with frequency for porous absorbers.

**(b)** A standing wave tube (Kundt's tube) is used to measure the absorption coefficient of a sample. The standing wave ratio measured is 4.2. Compute the absorption coefficient and the reflection coefficient. Also state the formula used.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Mechanisms of Absorption:**

**Definition:** The absorption coefficient $\alpha$ of a surface is the ratio of sound energy absorbed by the surface to the total sound energy incident upon it. It is dimensionless and lies between 0 and 1.

**Three Physical Mechanisms:**

1. **Porous Absorption:** When sound waves enter a porous material (e.g., fiberglass, foam), air molecules oscillate within the small interconnected pores. The **viscous friction** between oscillating air and pore walls converts acoustic energy into heat. The thermal conduction between air and pore walls adds to dissipation. This mechanism is dominant in materials with open, interconnected pores.

2. **Resonant Absorption (Helmholtz Resonator type):** A cavity backed by a small opening acts as an acoustic mass-spring system. At its resonant frequency, air in the neck oscillates with maximum velocity, and energy is dissipated by viscous resistance in the neck and thermal losses in the cavity. This gives sharp absorption peaks at specific low frequencies.

3. **Membrane / Panel Absorption:** A flexible membrane (e.g., thin plywood or gypsum board) mounted over an enclosed air cavity acts as a mass-spring-damper system. The membrane provides the mass, the cavity air provides the spring, and internal friction provides damping. Energy is dissipated as the membrane vibrates. Effective at low frequencies (50–500 Hz).

**Frequency Dependence of Porous Absorbers:**

For porous materials, the absorption coefficient generally **increases with frequency** up to a point, then plateaus:

- At **low frequencies** (< 200 Hz), $\alpha$ is low because the air particle velocity in the pores is small and the sound wavelength is much larger than the material thickness — minimal viscous interaction.
- At **mid frequencies** (500 Hz – 2 kHz), $\alpha$ rises rapidly because the wavelength becomes comparable to material thickness, maximizing viscous friction.
- At **high frequencies** (> 2 kHz), $\alpha$ approaches a maximum value (often > 0.9) because the wavelength becomes much smaller than the material thickness, and the sound cannot penetrate deeply — most of it is absorbed near the surface.

**(b) Kundt's Tube Measurement:**

**Given:** Standing Wave Ratio, SWR $= n = 4.2$

**Formulas:**

The pressure reflection coefficient $r$ is related to SWR by:

$$r = \frac{n - 1}{n + 1}$$

The absorption coefficient is:

$$\alpha = 1 - r^2 = 1 - \left(\frac{n-1}{n+1}\right)^2$$

**Calculation:**

$$r = \frac{4.2 - 1}{4.2 + 1} = \frac{3.2}{5.2} = 0.6154$$

$$\alpha = 1 - (0.6154)^2 = 1 - 0.3787 = 0.6213$$

**∴ Reflection coefficient = 0.6154, Absorption coefficient = 0.6213 (≈ 62.1%).**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Pressure and Displacement Nodes/Antinodes:** Many students swap these. **MEMORIZE:**
>    - **Open end of tube** = Pressure NODE / Displacement ANTINODE
>    - **Closed end of tube** = Pressure ANTINODE / Displacement NODE
>
> 2. **Forgetting End Correction in Helmholtz Resonator:** The end correction adds **0.85d (flanged)** or **0.613d (unflanged)** to the neck length. Without this, $f_r$ will be overestimated by ~50%. Examiners specifically check for this in 14-mark derivations.
>
> 3. **Writing Only Odd Harmonics for Closed Tube:** Some students write the formula $f_n = \dfrac{nc}{4L}$ with $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ which is **WRONG**. It should be $n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$ **only**.
>
> 4. **Mixing Sabine's Formula units:** $T_{60} = \frac{0.161 V}{A}$ requires $V$ in m³ and $A$ in sabins. Students often forget the constant 0.161 (SI units) and use 0.05 (CGS), losing 2–3 marks.
>
> 5. **In Kundt's Tube problems:** The reflection coefficient formula is $r = \dfrac{n-1}{n+1}$, NOT $\dfrac{n+1}{n-1}$. This is the most common sign error in absorption coefficient measurement problems.
>
> 6. **Speed of sound:** Always use $c = 343$ m/s (at 20°C) unless the problem explicitly states another temperature. Using 330 or 340 m/s without justification loses 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Absorption Coefficient ($\alpha$):** Ratio of absorbed to incident sound energy; range **0 to 1**.
- [ ] **Sabine Absorption:** $A = S\alpha$, measured in **sabins (m²)**.
- [ ] **Reverberation Time:** $T_{60} = \dfrac{0.161 V}{A}$ (SI units).
- [ ] **Open-Open Tube:** Fundamental $f_1 = \dfrac{c}{2L}$; **all harmonics** present.
- [ ] **Closed-Open Tube:** Fundamental $f_1 = \dfrac{c}{4L}$; **only odd harmonics** ($n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$).
- [ ] **Open end** = Pressure node / Displacement antinode.
- [ ] **Closed end** = Pressure antinode / Displacement node.
- [ ] **Helmholtz Resonator:** $f_r = \dfrac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{S}{V \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}}$.
- [ ] **End Correction (flanged):** $L_{\text{eff}} = L + 0.85d$.
- [ ] **End Correction (unflanged):** $L_{\text{eff}} = L + 0.613d$.
- [ ] **Kundt's Tube:** $\alpha = 1 - \left(\dfrac{n-1}{n+1}\right)^2$ where $n$ is the standing wave ratio.
- [ ] **Speed of sound at 20°C:** $c = 343$ m/s (use unless stated otherwise).
- [ ] **Density of air at STP:** $\rho_0 = 1.21$ kg/m³.
- [ ] **Acoustic impedance of air:** $Z_0 = \rho_0 c \approx 413$ Rayl.
- [ ] **Porous absorbers** → mid-to-high frequencies; **Resonant absorbers** → low frequencies.
- [ ] **Closed tube produces a sound one octave lower** than an open tube of the same length (because $f_{\text{closed}} = f_{\text{open}}/2$).
- [ ] **Musical analogy:** Open tube ≈ Flute; Closed tube ≈ Clarinet.
- [ ] **Industrial analogy:** Mufflers, bass traps, exhaust silencers all use closed/open tube principles.

> **Final Study Tip:** In KTU exams, always **draw the standing wave pattern** with labeled nodes and antinodes for tube problems — this earns easy partial credit even if your numerical answer is slightly off.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standing Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standing Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Standing Wave** (also called a *Stationary Wave*) is the resultant waveform produced by the **superposition of two progressive waves of identical frequency, identical amplitude, and identical wavelength, traveling in opposite directions along the same medium.**

Unlike a progressive (traveling) wave which transports energy through space, a standing wave does **not** propagate — instead, its energy is *localized* and oscillates between kinetic and potential forms at fixed spatial locations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> A standing wave is formed when two waves of the same amplitude and frequency travel in opposite directions and superpose. The resulting pattern has fixed points of zero amplitude (**nodes**) and points of maximum amplitude (**antinodes**), separated by half a wavelength.

The general standing wave equation for displacement in a medium is:

$$y(x,t) = 2A \sin(kx) \cos(\omega t)$$

where:
- $A$ = amplitude of each constituent progressive wave
- $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ = angular wave number
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = angular frequency
- $x$ = position coordinate
- $t$ = time coordinate

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Rope Shake**
> Imagine holding one end of a long rope tied to a wall. If you shake the rope continuously at just the **right rhythm** (the natural frequency), the wave you send down bounces back from the wall and *interferes* with your next shake. At a specific shake-frequency, the rope settles into a pattern of **still loops and bulging loops** — the still points are *nodes*, the bulging maxima are *antinodes*. This frozen-looking vibrating rope is a standing wave.

**Real-World Acoustic Examples:**

| Source | Physical Manifestation |
|---|---|
| **Violin / Guitar string** | Vibrating string between nut and bridge → fixed-fixed boundary standing wave |
| **Organ pipe (church)** | Air column resonates with standing pressure waves |
| **Room modes in acoustics** | Low-frequency resonances between parallel walls of a small room |
| **Microwave oven** | Electromagnetic standing wave cooks food at antinodes (hot spots) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway for KTU students:** Standing waves are the *fundamental mechanism of resonance* in virtually every musical instrument and acoustic enclosure. Without standing waves, there is no music and no architectural acoustics.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Speed of sound in air at **20°C** → $c = \mathbf{343 \text{ m/s}}$
> - Speed of sound in air at **0°C** → $c = \mathbf{331 \text{ m/s}}$
> - Standard reference pressure → $p_0 = \mathbf{20 \, \mu\text{Pa}}$
> - Density of air at STP → $\rho_0 = \mathbf{1.21 \text{ kg/m}^3}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Physical Logic Behind Standing Wave Formation

The phenomenon of a standing wave emerges from **four sequential physical conditions**:

- **Step 1 — Two Counter-Propagating Waves Exist:** A wave traveling in the +x direction, $y_1 = A \sin(kx - \omega t)$, coexists with an identical wave traveling in the -x direction, $y_2 = A \sin(kx + \omega t)$.

- **Step 2 — Superposition Principle Activates:** According to the linear superposition principle, the net displacement at every point in the medium equals the *algebraic sum* of the individual displacements: $y_{net} = y_1 + y_2$.

- **Step 3 — Spatial-Temporal Separation Occurs:** Using the trigonometric identity $\sin(\alpha \pm \beta) = \sin\alpha\cos\beta \pm \cos\alpha\sin\beta$, the equation factors into a *purely spatial* part $\sin(kx)$ and a *purely temporal* part $\cos(\omega t)$. This separation is the mathematical signature of a standing wave.

- **Step 4 — Fixed Nodes and Antinodes Emerge:** The spatial envelope $2A\sin(kx)$ never moves. Wherever $\sin(kx) = 0$, the medium remains permanently at rest — these are **nodes**. Wherever $\vert\sin(kx)\vert = 1$, the medium oscillates with maximum amplitude $2A$ — these are **antinodes**.

---

## 2.2 Nodes, Antinodes, and Wavelength Geometry

The wavelength-related positions are:

- **Node locations:** $\quad x_n = \dfrac{n\lambda}{2}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$

- **Antinode locations:** $\quad x_a = \dfrac{(2n+1)\lambda}{4}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$

- **Node-to-node (or antinode-to-antinode) distance:** $\quad \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$

- **Node-to-nearest-antinode distance:** $\quad \dfrac{\lambda}{4}$

---

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions (The Heart of KTU Problems)

Different acoustic systems impose different boundary conditions. The 2024-scheme examiner heavily tests the following four canonical cases:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions Master Rule:**
> - A **rigid wall / closed end** → displacement node (pressure antinode)
> - An **open end** → displacement antinode (pressure node)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | System / Configuration | Resonant Wavelengths Allowed | Fundamental Frequency $f_1$ | Allowed Harmonics | Node-Anti Pattern |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | String — **Both Ends Fixed** | $\lambda_n = \dfrac{2L}{n}$ | $f_1 = \dfrac{v}{2L}$ | $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ | Node–Node |
| 2 | Pipe — **Both Ends Open** | $\lambda_n = \dfrac{2L}{n}$ | $f_1 = \dfrac{v}{2L}$ | $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ | Anti–Anti |
| 3 | Pipe — **One End Closed** | $\lambda_n = \dfrac{4L}{n}$ | $f_1 = \dfrac{v}{4L}$ | $n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$ (odd only) | Node–Anti |
| 4 | Pipe — **Both Ends Closed** | $\lambda_n = \dfrac{2L}{n}$ | $f_1 = \dfrac{v}{2L}$ | $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ | Node–Node |

**Universal standing wave relations (all cases):**

$$f_n = n \cdot f_1 \quad \text{(all harmonics present — closed/open-open case)}$$

$$f_n = (2n-1) \cdot f_1 \quad \text{(only odd harmonics — one-end-closed case)}$$

$$v = f\lambda \quad \text{(wave velocity relation — always holds)}$$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU Engineers Must Master Standing Waves:**
> - **Musical Instrument Design:** Calculation of fret positions, pipe lengths, and drum membrane tensions.
> - **Room Acoustics:** Predicting room-mode frequencies to avoid low-frequency buildup (boom) in recording studios and home theaters.
> - **Noise Control:** Identifying resonant frequencies of ducts and panels so that **absorptive liners** or **Helmholtz resonators** can be tuned to destructive interference.
> - **Ultrasonic Cleaning & SONAR:** Designing transducer cavities that operate at standing-wave maxima for maximum energy transfer.
> - **Architectural Acoustics:** Determining where to place bass traps based on predicted antinode locations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Standing Wave Equation

### **Derivation 1 — From Two Counter-Propagating Progressive Waves**

**Given:** Two identical progressive waves traveling in opposite directions:

$$y_1(x,t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

$$y_2(x,t) = A \sin(kx + \omega t)$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Principle of Superposition:**

$$y(x,t) = y_1 + y_2$$

$$y(x,t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t) + A \sin(kx + \omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Expand each sinusoidal term using $\sin(\alpha \pm \beta) = \sin\alpha\cos\beta \pm \cos\alpha\sin\beta$:**

For the first wave:
$$y_1 = A \left[ \sin(kx)\cos(\omega t) - \cos(kx)\sin(\omega t) \right]$$

For the second wave:
$$y_2 = A \left[ \sin(kx)\cos(\omega t) + \cos(kx)\sin(\omega t) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Add the two expansions together. The $\cos(kx)\sin(\omega t)$ terms cancel identically:**

$$y(x,t) = 2A \sin(kx)\cos(\omega t) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Result:** The expression has been *factorized* into a spatial-only factor $2A\sin(kx)$ and a temporal-only factor $\cos(\omega t)$. This factorization is the defining mathematical fingerprint of a standing wave — the spatial profile is frozen in time, and the entire waveform simply "breathes" with amplitude $\pm 2A\sin(kx)$.

---

### **Derivation 2 — Position of Nodes and Antinodes**

From $y(x,t) = 2A\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)$, the maximum value of $\vert y \vert$ at a given point $x$ is the **spatial amplitude envelope**:

$$Y_{max}(x) = \vert 2A \sin(kx) \vert$$

**Nodes (always zero displacement):** Set $Y_{max} = 0$:

$$\sin(kx) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; kx = n\pi \;\Rightarrow\; x_n = \dfrac{n\pi}{k} = \dfrac{n\lambda}{2}, \quad n = 0,1,2,\ldots$$

**Antinodes (maximum displacement amplitude):** Set $\vert\sin(kx)\vert = 1$:

$$kx = \dfrac{(2n+1)\pi}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; x_a = \dfrac{(2n+1)\lambda}{4}, \quad n = 0,1,2,\ldots \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### **Derivation 3 — Frequencies of a String Fixed at Both Ends**

**Boundary Conditions Applied:**
- At $x = 0$ (fixed end): $y(0,t) = 0$ for all $t$
- At $x = L$ (fixed end): $y(L,t) = 0$ for all $t$

**Step 1 — Start from the standing wave equation:**

$$y(x,t) = 2A \sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the condition at $x = 0$:**

$$y(0,t) = 2A \sin(0)\cos(\omega t) = 0$$

This is automatically satisfied (no constraint).

**Step 3 — Apply the condition at $x = L$:**

$$y(L,t) = 2A \sin(kL)\cos(\omega t) = 0 \;\;\text{for all } t$$

Since $2A\cos(\omega t)$ is generally not zero, we require:

$$\sin(kL) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$:**

$$\dfrac{2\pi L}{\lambda_n} = n\pi \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda_n = \dfrac{2L}{n}$$

**Step 5 — Convert wavelength to frequency using $v = f\lambda$:**

$$f_n = \dfrac{v}{\lambda_n} = \dfrac{nv}{2L} = n \cdot f_1, \quad \text{where} \quad f_1 = \dfrac{v}{2L} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### **Derivation 4 — Frequencies of a Pipe Closed at One End**

**Boundary Conditions Applied:**
- At $x = 0$ (closed end): displacement node → $\sin(k\cdot 0) = 0$ ✓
- At $x = L$ (open end): displacement antinode → $\sin(kL) = \pm 1$

**Step 1 — Apply the antinode condition:**

$$\sin(kL) = \pm 1 \;\Rightarrow\; kL = \dfrac{(2n-1)\pi}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$:**

$$\dfrac{2\pi L}{\lambda_n} = \dfrac{(2n-1)\pi}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda_n = \dfrac{4L}{2n-1}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to frequency:**

$$f_n = \dfrac{v}{\lambda_n} = \dfrac{(2n-1)v}{4L} = (2n-1) \cdot f_1, \quad \text{where} \quad f_1 = \dfrac{v}{4L} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight — Why Only Odd Harmonics?**
> The closed-open geometry imposes *one* node and *one* antinode at the boundaries. Since node-to-antinode distance is $\lambda/4$, only wavelengths that are **odd multiples of $4L$** fit — i.e., $\lambda = 4L, \dfrac{4L}{3}, \dfrac{4L}{5}, \ldots$ This is why a stopped organ pipe produces a hollow, "stopped" tone — its spectrum lacks even harmonics.

---

### **Derivation 5 — Energy in a Standing Wave**

The instantaneous kinetic energy density of the wave is:

$$KE_{density} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \left(\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial t}\right)^2$$

The instantaneous potential energy density (for a stretched string under tension $T$) is:

$$PE_{density} = \tfrac{1}{2}T \left(\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial x}\right)^2$$

Computing the partial derivatives:

$$\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial t} = -2A\omega \sin(kx)\sin(\omega t)$$

$$\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial x} = 2Ak \cos(kx)\cos(\omega t)$$

Therefore:

$$KE_{density} = 2\rho A^2 \omega^2 \sin^2(kx)\sin^2(\omega t)$$

$$PE_{density} = 2T A^2 k^2 \cos^2(kx)\cos^2(\omega t)$$

**Step 6 — Sum total energy density:**

$$E_{total} = 2A^2 \left[ \rho\omega^2 \sin^2(kx)\sin^2(\omega t) + Tk^2 \cos^2(kx)\cos^2(\omega t) \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Result:** Integrating over a full wavelength (or over $[0, L]$), and using the identities $\langle\sin^2\rangle = \langle\cos^2\rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}$, one finds that the *time-averaged* total energy per unit length is **constant** — it does not propagate. Energy merely sloshes back and forth between kinetic and potential forms every quarter-cycle. This is the mathematical proof that **standing waves do not transport net energy.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Formation of a Standing Wave

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Wave y1] -->|Progressive Rightward| B[Superposition Point]
    C[Reflected Wave y2] -->|Progressive Leftward| B
    B --> D[Resultant Wave y = y1 plus y2]
    D --> E{Spatial Condition}
    E -->|sin kx = 0| F[NODE - Zero Displacement]
    E -->|sin kx equal to plus minus 1| G[ANTINODE - Max Amplitude 2A]
    F --> H[Spacing lambda over 2 apart]
    G --> I[Spacing lambda over 2 apart]
    H --> J[Frozen Standing Wave Pattern]
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Standing Wave Resonance Categories

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Acoustic Resonator] --> R1{Type of Boundary}
    R1 -->|String Fixed-Fixed| S1[Both Ends Nodes]
    R1 -->|Pipe Open-Open| S2[Both Ends Antinodes]
    R1 -->|Pipe Closed-Closed| S3[Both Ends Nodes]
    R1 -->|Pipe One-End-Closed| S4[One Node One Antinode]
    S1 --> F1[Fundamental f1 equals v over 2L]
    S2 --> F1
    S3 --> F1
    S4 --> F2[Fundamental f1 equals v over 4L]
    F1 --> H1[All Harmonics n equals 1,2,3...]
    F2 --> H2[Only Odd Harmonics n equals 1,3,5...]
    H1 --> APP[Musical Strings, Flutes, Drums]
    H2 --> APP2[Stopped Organ Pipes, Clarinet]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Standing Wave Field Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ACOUSTIC_FIELD [Standing Wave Acoustic Field along x-axis]
        N1([NODE x=0]) --- AN1([ANTINODE lambda over 4]) --- N2([NODE lambda over 2]) --- AN2([ANTINODE 3 lambda over 4]) --- N3([NODE lambda])
    end
    ACOUSTIC_FIELD --> ENERGY[Energy Localized - No Net Transport]
    ENERGY --> KIN[Kinetic Energy Maximum at Nodes]
    ENERGY --> POT[Potential Energy Maximum at Antinodes]
    KIN --> SLOSH[Quarter Cycle Energy Sloshing]
    POT --> SLOSH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `N1`, `AN1`) to comply with Mermaid syntax safeguards. Labels use plain uppercase text only — no markdown or HTML inside the quoted label strings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Define a standing wave. What are nodes and antinodes?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A standing wave is the resultant waveform formed by the superposition of two progressive waves of identical amplitude, frequency, and wavelength traveling in **opposite directions** along the same medium.

- **Node:** A point of *zero amplitude* (permanent rest) where the two constituent waves always cancel. Located at $x_n = \dfrac{n\lambda}{2}$.
- **Antinode:** A point of *maximum amplitude* (oscillates with amplitude $2A$) where the two waves reinforce constructively. Located at $x_a = \dfrac{(2n+1)\lambda}{4}$.

The resultant equation is $y(x,t) = 2A\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**State the fundamental difference between a progressive wave and a standing wave.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Progressive Wave | Standing Wave |
|---|---|---|
| Energy transport | Energy propagates with the wave | No net energy transport |
| Amplitude distribution | Uniform $A$ everywhere | Varies: nodes (0) to antinodes ($2A$) |
| Phase relationship | All points in phase (or uniformly shifted) | All points between two adjacent nodes are in phase; phases flip across a node |
| Mathematical form | $A\sin(kx - \omega t)$ | $2A\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Starting from two progressive waves $y_1 = A\sin(kx - \omega t)$ and $y_2 = A\sin(kx + \omega t)$ traveling in opposite directions, derive the standing wave equation. Locate the positions of nodes and antinodes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stretched string of length 0.6 m is fixed at both ends. A transverse wave travels on it at 240 m/s. Find the frequencies of the first four harmonics. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A(a):**

**Step 1:** Apply superposition:

$$y(x,t) = A\sin(kx - \omega t) + A\sin(kx + \omega t)$$

**Step 2:** Expand using the identity $\sin(\alpha \pm \beta)$:

$$y(x,t) = A\left[\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t) - \cos(kx)\sin(\omega t)\right] + A\left[\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t) + \cos(kx)\sin(\omega t)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Cancel the $\cos(kx)\sin(\omega t)$ terms:

$$y(x,t) = 2A\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t) \quad \text{(standing wave equation)}$$

**Step 4:** Nodes occur when $\sin(kx) = 0$:

$$kx = n\pi \;\Rightarrow\; x_n = \dfrac{n\lambda}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Antinodes occur when $\vert\sin(kx)\vert = 1$:

$$kx = \dfrac{(2n+1)\pi}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; x_a = \dfrac{(2n+1)\lambda}{4}$$

---

#### **Solution to Question A(b):**

Given: $L = 0.6$ m, $v = 240$ m/s, string fixed at both ends.

**Step 1:** Compute the fundamental frequency:

$$f_1 = \dfrac{v}{2L} = \dfrac{240}{2 \times 0.6} = 200 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2:** Compute first four harmonics ($n = 1, 2, 3, 4$):

$$f_1 = 1 \times 200 = \mathbf{200 \text{ Hz}}$$

$$f_2 = 2 \times 200 = \mathbf{400 \text{ Hz}}$$

$$f_3 = 3 \times 200 = \mathbf{600 \text{ Hz}}$$

$$f_4 = 4 \times 200 = \mathbf{800 \text{ Hz}}$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Alternative] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the frequencies of standing waves in (i) a string fixed at both ends, and (ii) an air column closed at one end. State the allowed harmonics in each case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pipe closed at one end has a length of 0.5 m. If the speed of sound is 340 m/s, find the frequencies of the first three resonant modes and the wavelength of the second overtone. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a):**

**(i) String fixed at both ends:**

Applying $y(L,t) = 0$ to the standing wave equation yields:

$$kL = n\pi \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda_n = \dfrac{2L}{n}$$

$$f_n = \dfrac{nv}{2L}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \quad \text{(all harmonics allowed)}$$

**(ii) Air column closed at one end:**

At the closed end there is a displacement node; at the open end there is a displacement antinode. The node-to-antinode distance must equal $(2n-1)\lambda/4 = L$:

$$L = \dfrac{(2n-1)\lambda_n}{4} \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda_n = \dfrac{4L}{2n-1}$$

$$f_n = \dfrac{(2n-1)v}{4L}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \quad \text{(only odd harmonics)}$$

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b):**

Given: $L = 0.5$ m, $v = 340$ m/s, one end closed.

**Step 1:** Compute fundamental frequency:

$$f_1 = \dfrac{v}{4L} = \dfrac{340}{4 \times 0.5} = 170 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2:** First three resonant modes (only odd harmonics):

$$f_1 = 1 \times 170 = \mathbf{170 \text{ Hz}} \quad (n=1)$$

$$f_3 = 3 \times 170 = \mathbf{510 \text{ Hz}} \quad (n=2 \text{ mode})$$

$$f_5 = 5 \times 170 = \mathbf{850 \text{ Hz}} \quad (n=3 \text{ mode})$$

**Step 3:** Second overtone corresponds to $n = 3$ (the third allowed mode, i.e., $f_5$):

$$\lambda_3 = \dfrac{4L}{2(3)-1} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.5}{5} = \mathbf{0.4 \text{ m}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting "only odd harmonics" in stopped pipes:** Many students write $f_n = n\cdot f_1$ for closed-open pipes — this is WRONG. The correct form is $f_n = (2n-1)\cdot f_1$.
> 2. **Confusing "overtone" with "harmonic":** The 1st overtone = 2nd harmonic. The 2nd overtone = 3rd harmonic. In a stopped pipe, the 2nd overtone corresponds to $n=3$ in the allowed-harmonics formula, giving a frequency of $5f_1$, NOT $3f_1$.
> 3. **Mixing up $v$ (wave speed) with $f$ (frequency):** Always show $v = f\lambda$ explicitly in derivations; partial credit is awarded for the relation alone.
> 4. **Forgetting units:** Always state Hz, m/s, m explicitly. A numeric answer without units loses 0.5 marks minimum in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Skipping boundary-condition reasoning:** Just stating the formula without justifying *why* $\sin(kL) = 0$ or $= \pm 1$ costs the "derivation" marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Standing Waves**

- **Definition:** Superposition of two identical waves traveling in opposite directions.
- **Equation:** $y(x,t) = 2A\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)$ — *product* (not sum) of spatial and temporal factors.
- **Node:** $\sin(kx) = 0$ → $x_n = n\lambda/2$, permanent zero displacement.
- **Antinode:** $\vert\sin(kx)\vert = 1$ → $x_a = (2n+1)\lambda/4$, max amplitude $2A$.
- **Node-to-node distance:** $\lambda/2$.
- **Fixed end of string / closed pipe end:** **Displacement node** (pressure antinode).
- **Open pipe end:** **Displacement antinode** (pressure node).
- **String fixed at both ends:** $f_n = nv/(2L)$, **all harmonics** allowed.
- **Pipe open at both ends:** $f_n = nv/(2L)$, **all harmonics** allowed.
- **Pipe closed at both ends:** $f_n = nv/(2L)$, **all harmonics** allowed (same as string).
- **Pipe closed at one end only:** $f_n = (2n-1)v/(4L)$, **only odd harmonics** allowed.
- **Energy:** Does *not* propagate — sloshes between KE (max at nodes) and PE (max at antinodes) every quarter cycle.
- **Engineering uses:** Musical instrument design, room-mode analysis, noise control via absorbers tuned to standing-wave antinodes, ultrasonic transducer cavities.
- **Common KTU trap:** Stopped pipe harmonics are odd multiples; "overtone" indexing starts at $n=2$ for the first overtone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ear: Structure and Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ear: Structure and Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The human **ear** is a complex sensory organ that performs two principal physiological functions: **(i) Hearing (Auditory function)** — the detection, transduction, and neural encoding of sound waves, and **(ii) Equilibrium (Vestibular function)** — the sensing of head position and motion. From an acoustical engineering perspective, the ear is best modeled as a **three-stage mechanical-to-electrical transducer** comprising the **Outer Ear**, **Middle Ear**, and **Inner Ear**, which collectively convert airborne acoustic pressure fluctuations (typically in the range **20 Hz – 20 kHz**) into neural impulses interpreted by the cerebral cortex.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For acoustics and noise control engineering, the ear is NOT just a biological organ — it is the **final receptor** in the acoustic measurement chain. All noise regulations, permissible exposure limits (PEL), and dB-weighting networks (A, B, C) are designed to match the **frequency-dependent sensitivity** of the human auditory system.

## 1.2 The Three Sub-Divisions of the Ear

| Sub-Division | Primary Function | Acoustic Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Outer Ear** | Collects & channels sound | Acoustic impedance matching at entry |
| **Middle Ear** | Mechanical amplification | Impedance transformation (air → fluid) |
| **Inner Ear** | Frequency analysis & transduction | Spectral decomposition (Fourier-like) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Ear as a "Microphone + Equalizer + Spectrum Analyzer"

Imagine you are standing at the entrance of a large concert hall:

- The **Outer Ear (Pinna + Ear Canal)** acts like the **funnel/cone of a microphone** — it gathers scattered sound waves and funnels them efficiently to a small sensitive diaphragm (the eardrum). Just as a megaphone concentrates sound, the pinna and concha boost sounds in the **2 kHz – 5 kHz** band by roughly **10–15 dB**.
- The **Middle Ear (Ossicles)** acts like an **impedance-matching transformer** in an electrical circuit. Sound waves traveling in air (low acoustic impedance, ~**0.0004 N·s/m³**) must drive fluid in the cochlea (high acoustic impedance, ~**1.4 N·s/m³**). Without the ossicles, ~**99.9% (30 dB)** of acoustic energy would reflect back — similar to what happens when you shout into water. The ossicles solve this by providing a mechanical lever and areal ratio gain of ~**22×**, recovering ~**30 dB** of transmission loss.
- The **Inner Ear (Cochlea)** acts like a **real-time spectrum analyzer** (analogous to an FFT processor). The basilar membrane inside the cochlea mechanically separates incoming sound into its constituent frequencies — high frequencies at the base, low frequencies at the apex — much like a prism separates white light into colors. Hair cells then transduce these mechanical vibrations into electrical nerve signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Parallel:** The cochlea performs a **mechanical Fourier Transform** in real time without consuming any external power — a feat that no man-made chip can yet replicate efficiently. This is why biomimetic audio processors (cochlear implants) remain an active research frontier.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Noise Control Engineering

- **Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL)** set by OSHA, NIOSH, and ISO 1999 are derived from the ear's susceptibility to damage.
- The **A-weighted decibel scale (dBA)** is designed to mimic the ear's frequency response at low sound levels.
- Designing hearing protection devices (earplugs, earmuffs) requires understanding of the ear's **critical bands** and **transfer function**.
- Architectural acoustics of auditoriums is evaluated subjectively by the human ear's masking characteristics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Outer Ear (Auricle + External Auditory Meatus)

**Anatomical Components:**
- **Pinna (Auricle):** The visible cartilaginous flap. Provides directional cues and a mild amplification of **~5 dB** in the 2–5 kHz region via the **concha resonance**.
- **External Auditory Canal (Ear Canal / Meatus):** A tube of length **~2.5 cm** and diameter **~0.7 cm**, terminated by the tympanic membrane.
- **Tympanic Membrane (Eardrum):** A thin, conical membrane (~**0.1 mm** thick, area ~**55 mm²**) that converts acoustic pressure into mechanical vibration.

**Acoustic Behavior of the Ear Canal:**
The ear canal behaves as a **quarter-wave resonator** (closed at one end by the eardrum, open at the other).

$$f_{\text{res}} = \frac{c}{4L} = \frac{343 \text{ m/s}}{4 \times 0.025 \text{ m}} \approx 3.43 \text{ kHz}$$

This resonance provides a natural amplification of **~10–15 dB** near 3 kHz, which coincides with the **peak sensitivity of human hearing** — a remarkable evolutionary design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** The ear canal's quarter-wave resonance (~3 kHz) explains why the human ear is most sensitive at this frequency, and why noise-induced hearing loss typically begins in the **3–6 kHz** region (the "**audiometric notch**" at 4 kHz).

## 2.2 The Middle Ear (Tympanic Cavity)

**Anatomical Components:**
- **Ossicles:** Three smallest bones in the human body —
  1. **Malleus (Hammer):** Attached to the eardrum.
  2. **Incus (Anvil):** Intermediate bone.
  3. **Stapes (Stirrup):** Attached to the **oval window** of the cochlea.
- **Eustachian Tube (Pharyngotympanic Tube):** Connects middle ear to the nasopharynx; equalizes static pressure.
- **Tensor Tympani & Stapedius Muscles:** Protective reflex muscles (acoustic reflex).

**The Impedance Matching Problem:**
The acoustic impedance mismatch between air and cochlear fluid is enormous:

$$Z_{\text{air}} \approx 0.0004 \text{ N·s/m}^3 \quad ; \quad Z_{\text{fluid}} \approx 1.4 \text{ N·s/m}^3$$

The transmission coefficient (intensity) without the middle ear would be:

$$\alpha_T = \frac{4 Z_{\text{air}} Z_{\text{fluid}}}{(Z_{\text{air}} + Z_{\text{fluid}})^2} \approx 0.001 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 0.1\%)$$

This corresponds to a **30 dB transmission loss** — a catastrophic loss of acoustic energy.

**The Middle Ear Solution — Two Mechanical Advantages:**

1. **Areal Ratio (Hydraulic Lever):**
$$A_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{A_{\text{eardrum}}}{A_{\text{oval window}}} = \frac{55 \text{ mm}^2}{3.2 \text{ mm}^2} \approx 17.2$$

Pressure amplification:
$$P_{\text{gain}} = A_{\text{ratio}} \approx 17.2 \;\;(\approx 24.7 \text{ dB})$$

2. **Ossicular Lever Action:**
$$L_{\text{ratio}} \approx 1.3 \;\;(\approx 2.3 \text{ dB})$$

3. **Total Gain:**
$$G_{\text{total}} = A_{\text{ratio}} \times L_{\text{ratio}} \approx 22.4 \;\;(\approx 27 \text{ dB})$$

This recovers nearly all the 30 dB loss, leaving a residual loss of only ~**3 dB** — biologically near-perfect impedance matching.

## 2.3 The Inner Ear (Cochlea + Vestibular System)

**Anatomical Components:**
- **Cochlea:** Snail-shaped, ~**35 mm** long, coiled **2.5 turns**.
- **Three Chambers (Scalae):**
  - **Scala Vestibuli** (top, contains perilymph)
  - **Scala Media (Cochlear Duct)** (middle, contains endolymph)
  - **Scala Tympani** (bottom, contains perilymph)
- **Basilar Membrane:** The mechanical frequency analyzer.
- **Organ of Corti:** Contains the **hair cells** (inner & outer) — the true transduction elements.
- **Oval Window & Round Window:** Pressure relief and entry points.

**Frequency Selectivity (Place Theory of Hearing — Bekesy):**

| Basilar Membrane Location | Best Frequency (Characteristic Frequency) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Base (near oval window) | High frequencies (~**20 kHz**) |
| Middle | Mid frequencies (~**1–2 kHz**) |
| Apex (helicotrema) | Low frequencies (~**20 Hz**) |

The basilar membrane is **stiff and narrow at the base** and **floppy and wide at the apex**, hence its frequency-to-place mapping.

**Traveling Wave Velocity:**
$$\nu = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} \cdot f(\text{geometry})$$

where $E$ is the elastic modulus of the basilar membrane (position-dependent) and $\rho$ is the fluid density.

## 2.4 Frequency Response of the Human Ear

- **Hearing Range:** **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz** (decreases with age — *presbycusis*).
- **Threshold of Hearing (TOH):** $p_0 = 20 \;\mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ **Pa** at 1 kHz.
- **Threshold of Pain (TOP):** $p_{\text{pain}} = 20$ **Pa** at 1 kHz.
- **Dynamic Range:** $20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{20}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = 120$ **dB SPL**.

**Equal-Loudness Contours (Fletcher-Munson Curves):**
These contours illustrate that the ear is **NOT equally sensitive** at all frequencies. At low SPL, the ear is most sensitive around **3–4 kHz** and progressively less sensitive at low and high frequencies. This is precisely why **A-weighting** was introduced.

**Phon Scale:** Loudness level in **phons** = SPL in dB of a **1 kHz** pure tone judged equally loud.
**Sone Scale:** Loudness in **sones** (1 sone = 40 phons; doubling of sones ≈ 10 phon increase).

$$L_N (\text{phons}) = 40 + 10 \log_2(S) = 40 + 33.22 \log_{10}(S)$$

where $S$ is the loudness in sones.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Typical Value / Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)$ | $p_0 = 20 \;\mu\text{Pa}$ |
| 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² |
| 3 | Ear Canal Quarter-Wave Res. | $f_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{c}{4L}$ | $\approx 3.43$ kHz (L = 2.5 cm) |
| 4 | Impedance Ratio (Air/Fluid) | $\dfrac{Z_{\text{fluid}}}{Z_{\text{air}}}$ | $\approx 3500$ |
| 5 | Transmission Loss (no M.E.) | $TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\alpha_T}\right)$ | $\approx 30$ dB |
| 6 | Areal Ratio Gain | $G_A = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{\text{TM}}}{A_{\text{OW}}}\right)$ | $\approx 24.7$ dB |
| 7 | Lever Ratio Gain | $G_L = 20 \log_{10}(L_{\text{ratio}})$ | $\approx 2.3$ dB |
| 8 | Total Middle Ear Gain | $G_{\text{total}} = G_A + G_L$ | $\approx 27$ dB |
| 9 | Dynamic Range | $\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\max}}{p_0}\right)$ | $120$ dB SPL |
| 10 | Phon-Sone Conversion | $L_N = 40 + 33.22 \log_{10}(S)$ | — |
| 11 | Critical Bandwidth (approx) | $CB(f) \approx 25 + 75\!\left(1 + 1.4 f^2\right)^{0.69}$ | Hz (Zwicker) |
| 12 | A-Weighting (1 kHz ref) | $R_A(f)$ | $0$ dB at 1 kHz |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audiology & Hearing Aids:** The frequency-place map of the cochlea is used to program cochlear implant electrode arrays (each electrode stimulates a specific frequency region).
- **Noise Meters:** A-weighting network emulates the 40-phon Fletcher-Munson contour.
- **Speech Intelligibility:** Critical band theory underpins the **Speech Intelligibility Index (SII)** used to design PA systems in auditoria and airports.
- **Hearing Conservation Programs:** OSHA's 85 dBA / 8-hour limit and NIOSH's 80 dBA / 8-hour limit are based on equal-energy hypothesis and the ear's temporary/permanent threshold shift (TTS/PTS) behavior.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ear Canal Quarter-Wave Resonance Frequency

**Problem Setup:** Determine the first resonance frequency of the human external ear canal, modeled as a closed-open tube (closed at the eardrum, open at the pinna).

**Step 1 — Boundary Conditions:**
A closed-open tube supports only **odd harmonics** of a quarter-wavelength. The fundamental mode has a node at the closed end (eardrum) and an antinode at the open end (pinna). The condition for resonance is:

$$L = \frac{\lambda}{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 4L$$

**Step 2 — Convert Wavelength to Frequency:**
Using the wave relation $c = f\lambda$:

$$f_{\text{res}} = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{c}{4L}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{res}} &= \frac{343 \text{ m/s}}{4 \times 0.025 \text{ m}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{343}{0.100} \text{ Hz} \\[4pt]
&= 3430 \text{ Hz} \;\; \approx \; 3.43 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**
This 3.43 kHz amplification coincides with the **formant region of human speech** (especially consonants like /s/, /t/, /k/), which is why speech intelligibility peaks in this band.

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanatory Logic:** The conversion is purely a geometric-acoustic relation. The ear canal's length $L$ is fixed by anatomy; the speed of sound $c$ is fixed by the medium (air, body temperature). Hence the resonance is *invariable* across healthy adults — a critical design constant for audiometric calibration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Middle Ear Impedance Matching — Transmission Coefficient

**Problem Setup:** Without the middle ear, calculate the intensity transmission coefficient when sound passes from air ($Z_1 = 0.0004$ N·s/m³) into cochlear fluid ($Z_2 = 1.4$ N·s/m³). Then determine the gain introduced by the ossicular chain.

**Step 1 — Apply the Transmission Coefficient Formula for Normal Incidence:**

$$\alpha_T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha_T &= \frac{4 \times 0.0004 \times 1.4}{(0.0004 + 1.4)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.00224}{(1.4004)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.00224}{1.96112} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.143 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \approx \; 0.114\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Transmission Loss in Decibels:**

$$\begin{aligned}
TL &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\alpha_T}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{1.143 \times 10^{-3}}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}(875.0) \\[4pt]
&\approx 10 \times 2.942 \\[4pt]
&\approx 29.4 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Middle Ear Compensation:**

Areal ratio:

$$A_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{55 \text{ mm}^2}{3.2 \text{ mm}^2} = 17.19$$

Pressure gain (linear):

$$G_p = A_{\text{ratio}} \times L_{\text{ratio}} = 17.19 \times 1.3 = 22.34$$

Intensity gain (proportional to $G_p^2$):

$$G_I = 22.34^2 \approx 499.1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 10 \log_{10}(499.1) \approx 27.0 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Residual Loss:**

$$TL_{\text{net}} = 29.4 \text{ dB} - 27.0 \text{ dB} = 2.4 \text{ dB}$$

This is the residual loss; a small portion of acoustic energy is still reflected, but the ear functions at near-100% efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logical Justification:** Without the middle ear's mechanical transformation, only 1 part in 1000 of incident acoustic power would reach the cochlea. The ossicles therefore provide the *single most critical amplification stage* in mammalian hearing — a fact that motivates the design of **middle-ear implants** and **active hearing prostheses**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sound Pressure Level at Threshold of Hearing

**Problem Setup:** Convert the threshold of hearing pressure ($p_0 = 20 \;\mu\text{Pa}$) and threshold of pain ($p_{\text{pain}} = 20$ Pa) to the SPL scale, and determine the ear's dynamic range.

**Step 1 — Threshold of Hearing:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{p,\text{TOH}} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{20 \times 10^{-6}}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 20 \log_{10}(1) = 0 \text{ dB SPL}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Threshold of Pain:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{p,\text{TOP}} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{20}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 20 \log_{10}(10^6) = 20 \times 6 = 120 \text{ dB SPL}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic Range:**

$$\Delta L = L_{\text{TOP}} - L_{\text{TOH}} = 120 - 0 = 120 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Linear Equivalence:**
Since dB is logarithmic, 120 dB corresponds to a $10^6$ (one-million-fold) ratio in sound pressure, and a $10^{12}$ (one-trillion-fold) ratio in acoustic intensity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The decibel scale was specifically adopted for acoustic measurements because the ear's response is *logarithmic* (Weber-Fechner law). A linear scale (Pa) would be impractical over 12 orders of magnitude.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Loudness Conversion — Phons to Sones

**Problem Setup:** A sound has a loudness of 8 sones. Express this in phons.

**Step 1 — Recall the Relation:**

$$L_N = 40 + 10 \log_2(S)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $S = 8$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_N &= 40 + 10 \log_2(8) \\[4pt]
&= 40 + 10 \times 3 \\[4pt]
&= 40 + 30 = 70 \text{ phons}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
A sound judged "8 times as loud" as the 1-sone reference corresponds to 70 phons. Doubling sones = adding 10 phons (perceptual doubling law).

> [!TIP]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** Students often confuse **Phons** (loudness level, dB-equivalent) with **Sones** (loudness, linear scale). Phons are NOT additive to dB SPL except at 1 kHz.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Critical Bandwidth (Zwicker's Approximation)

**Problem Setup:** Estimate the critical bandwidth of the human ear at 1 kHz using Zwicker's empirical formula.

**Step 1 — Zwicker's Formula:**

$$CB(f) = 25 + 75 \left(1 + 1.4 f^2\right)^{0.69}$$

where $f$ is in kHz and $CB$ is in Hz.

**Step 2 — Substitute $f = 1$ kHz:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CB(1) &= 25 + 75 \left(1 + 1.4 \times 1^2\right)^{0.69} \\[4pt]
&= 25 + 75 \left(1 + 1.4\right)^{0.69} \\[4pt]
&= 25 + 75 \left(2.4\right)^{0.69}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Power:**

$$2.4^{0.69} \approx e^{0.69 \ln 2.4} = e^{0.69 \times 0.8755} = e^{0.6041} \approx 1.829$$

**Step 4 — Final Value:**

$$CB(1) \approx 25 + 75 \times 1.829 \approx 25 + 137.2 = 162.2 \text{ Hz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Relevance:** Critical bandwidth underlies **psychoacoustic masking**, which is exploited in MP3/AAC codecs to discard inaudible frequency components within each critical band — yielding ~**10:1 compression** with minimal perceptual loss.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of Ear Regions

| Parameter | Outer Ear | Middle Ear | Inner Ear |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Medium** | Air | Air / Mechanical | Fluid (Perilymph/Endolymph) |
| **Energy Form** | Acoustic (pressure) | Mechanical (vibration) | Hydro-mech + Electrical |
| **Main Element** | Pinna, Canal, Tympanic Membrane | Ossicles (Malleus, Incus, Stapes) | Basilar Membrane, Hair Cells |
| **Frequency Response** | Peaks at ~3 kHz | Flat (broadband) | 20 Hz – 20 kHz (place-coded) |
| **Gain** | ~10–15 dB (passive) | ~27 dB (impedance matching) | N/A (transduction only) |
| **Function** | Collection, funneling | Amplification, impedance matching | Frequency analysis, transduction |
| **Failure Mode** | Wax blockage, infection | Otosclerosis (stapes fixation) | Sensorineural hearing loss |
| **Clinical Test** | Audiometry, otoscopy | Tympanometry | OAE, ABR (auditory brainstem) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram of the Human Ear (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["SOUND WAVE IN AIR 20 Hz to 20 kHz"] --> B["PINNA AURICLE FUNNELS AND DIRECTS"]
    B --> C["EXTERNAL AUDITORY CANAL 2.5 cm TUBE"]
    C --> D["EAR CANAL RESONANCE 3.43 kHz +10 to 15 dB"]
    D --> E["TYMPANIC MEMBRANE AREA 55 mm2 PRESSURE TO MECHANICAL"]
    E --> F["MALLEUS HAMMER"]
    F --> G["INCUS ANVIL"]
    G --> H["STAPES STIRUP ATTACHES TO OVAL WINDOW"]
    H --> I["OVAL WINDOW AREA 3.2 mm2 IMPEDANCE MATCHING"]
    I --> J["COCHLEA SCALA VESTIBULI PERILYMPH"]
    J --> K["BASILAR MEMBRANE FREQUENCY PLACE MAP"]
    K --> L["ORGAN OF CORTI INNER AND OUTER HAIR CELLS"]
    L --> M["MECHANICAL TO ELECTRICAL TRANSDUCTION"]
    M --> N["AUDITORY NERVE CN VIII ACTION POTENTIALS"]
    N --> O["BRAINSTEM COCHLEAR NUCLEUS"]
    O --> P["AUDITORY CORTEX TEMPORAL LOBE PERCEPTION"]
    
    Q["EUSTACHIAN TUBE PRESSURE EQUALIZATION"] -.-> H
    R["STAPEDIUS AND TENSOR TYMPANI ACOUSTIC REFLEX"] -.-> F
    S["ROUND WINDOW PRESSURE RELIEF"] -.-> J
    T["VESTIBULAR SYSTEM SEMICIRCULAR CANALS BALANCE"] -.-> N
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Top horizontal chain (A→P)** traces the **afferent auditory pathway** — from free-field sound to conscious perception.
- **Dashed off-shoots (Q, R, S, T)** show **supporting functions** — pressure equalization, protective reflexes, hydrodynamic relief, and vestibular (balance) function.
- Each block is a **functional unit**, not a literal anatomical drawing. The architecture mirrors the **signal-flow** of a sensor system: *Acquisition → Filtering → Amplification → Transduction → Neural Encoding → Perception*.

## 4.2 Impedance-Matching Concept Diagram (Middle Ear Lever Action)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["AIR Z = 0.0004 Ns per m3 LOW IMPEDANCE"] -->|INCIDENT ACOUSTIC WAVE| B["TYMPANIC MEMBRANE LARGE AREA 55 mm2"]
    B -->|OSSILE CHAIN LEVER 1.3 to 1| C["OVAL WINDOW SMALL AREA 3.2 mm2"]
    C -->|PRESSURE GAIN 22.4 TIMES| D["COCHLEAR FLUID Z = 1.4 Ns per m3 HIGH IMPEDANCE"]
    D -->|EFFICIENT TRANSMISSION 97 percent| E["BASILAR MEMBRANE EXCITATION"]
    
    F["WITHOUT MIDDLE EAR 30 dB LOSS"] -.-> A
    G["WITH MIDDLE EAR 27 dB RECOVERY"] -.-> E
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **linear chain A→E** represents the *intended* transmission path with impedance matching.
- The **dotted branches F and G** contrast the *with* and *without* middle ear scenarios.

## 4.3 Frequency-to-Place Mapping on the Basilar Membrane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["HIGH FREQ 20 kHz"] -->|BASE STIFF AND NARROW| B["CHARACTERISTIC FREQUENCY HIGH"]
    C["MID FREQ 1 to 2 kHz"] -->|MIDDLE REGION| D["CHARACTERISTIC FREQUENCY MID"]
    E["LOW FREQ 20 Hz"] -->|APEX WIDE AND FLOPPY| F["CHARACTERISTIC FREQUENCY LOW"]
    
    B --> G["INNER HAIR CELLS IHC NEURAL FIRING"]
    D --> G
    F --> G
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The basilar membrane acts as a **mechanical spectrum analyzer**. The mapping is roughly **logarithmic** in frequency (each octave occupies equal physical length).
