# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the function of the Eustachian tube in the human ear. Why is it essential for hearing?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Eustachian tube** (pharyngotympanic tube) is a narrow canal that connects the **middle ear cavity** to the **nasopharynx**. Its functions are:
1. **Pressure Equalization (1 Mark):** It ventilates the middle ear and equalizes the static air pressure on both sides of the tympanic membrane, preventing membrane rupture and ensuring free vibration.
2. **Drainage (1 Mark):** It drains mucus and fluids from the middle ear into the throat.
3. **Protection (1 Mark):** Its collapsed-at-rest state prevents pathogens and sound from the throat from reaching the middle ear.

It is essential because a **pressure differential** of more than ~**20 kPa** across the eardrum can cause pain, reduced vibration amplitude, and even tympanic membrane rupture.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the threshold of hearing and threshold of pain. Express the dynamic range of the human ear in decibels.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Threshold of Hearing (TOH):** The minimum sound pressure level that an average young adult can detect at 1 kHz is $p_0 = 20 \;\mu\text{Pa}$, corresponding to **0 dB SPL** (1 Mark).
- **Threshold of Pain (TOP):** The sound pressure level that produces a sensation of pain in the ear is approximately $p_{\text{pain}} = 20$ Pa, corresponding to **120 dB SPL** (1 Mark).
- **Dynamic Range:**
$$\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{20}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10^6) = 120 \text{ dB (1 Mark)}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **structure and function of the human ear**. Discuss the role of the outer, middle, and inner ear in the hearing process. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The human ear is a biological transducer that converts acoustic pressure waves into neural signals. It is divided into three functional regions: outer, middle, and inner ear.

**Outer Ear (2 Marks):**
- The **pinna (auricle)** collects sound waves and provides directional cues. Its complex folds produce subtle frequency-dependent filtering that aids in **sound localization** (especially elevation cues).
- The **external auditory canal (ear canal)**, approximately 2.5 cm long, acts as a closed-open tube. It produces a **quarter-wave resonance at ~3.43 kHz**, amplifying sounds in this band by 10–15 dB.
- The **tympanic membrane** vibrates in response to incident pressure waves, converting acoustic energy to mechanical vibration.

**Middle Ear (2 Marks):**
- Contains three ossicles: **malleus, incus, stapes**.
- Performs **impedance matching** between the low-impedance air and the high-impedance cochlear fluid, providing a total gain of **~27 dB** (areal ratio × lever ratio = 17.2 × 1.3).
- The **Eustachian tube** equalizes static pressure.
- The **acoustic reflex** (stapedius and tensor tympani contraction) protects against intense sounds.

**Inner Ear (2 Marks):**
- The **cochlea** contains the **basilar membrane**, which performs frequency analysis via the **place principle** (Békésy).
- The **organ of Corti** contains inner and outer hair cells that perform **mechano-electrical transduction**.
- The **vestibular system** (semicircular canals, otolith organs) maintains balance.

---

**(b)** Explain the **impedance matching mechanism of the middle ear** with suitable derivations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement of the Problem (1 Mark):**
Sound waves travel from air (low acoustic impedance) to cochlear fluid (high acoustic impedance). A large mismatch would cause ~30 dB reflection loss.

**Transmission Coefficient Derivation (2 Marks):**
Using the normal-incidence intensity transmission formula:

$$\alpha_T = \frac{4 Z_{\text{air}} Z_{\text{fluid}}}{(Z_{\text{air}} + Z_{\text{fluid}})^2}$$

Substituting $Z_{\text{air}} = 0.0004$ N·s/m³ and $Z_{\text{fluid}} = 1.4$ N·s/m³:

$$\alpha_T \approx 1.14 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; TL \approx 29.4 \text{ dB}$$

**Middle Ear Compensation (3 Marks):**
- **Areal (Hydraulic) Ratio:** $A_r = 55/3.2 \approx 17.2$, giving a pressure gain of $\approx 24.7$ dB.
- **Ossicular Lever Ratio:** $L_r \approx 1.3$, giving a gain of $\approx 2.3$ dB.
- **Total Gain:** $G = 17.2 \times 1.3 \approx 22.4$ ($\approx 27$ dB).

**Net Loss and Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Net transmission loss = 29.4 − 27.0 = **~2.4 dB**, indicating highly efficient acoustic coupling. This impedance-matching is a hallmark of mammalian middle-ear design and a key reference in biomimetic acoustic engineering.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe the **frequency response of the human ear** and explain the **Fletcher-Munson equal-loudness contours**. Why is A-weighting used in noise measurements? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Frequency Response (2 Marks):**
- Audible range: **20 Hz to 20 kHz**; most sensitive band: **2–5 kHz**.
- Sensitivity drops sharply below 500 Hz and above 8 kHz, especially at low SPL.
- The threshold of hearing and pain are 0 dB SPL and 120 dB SPL respectively at 1 kHz.

**Fletcher-Munson Contours (3 Marks):**
- Equal-loudness contours plot SPL vs frequency for tones perceived as **equally loud**.
- At low SPL, contours dip at low and high frequencies — the ear is less sensitive at extremes.
- At high SPL (>100 phons), contours flatten — frequency sensitivity becomes more uniform.
- The **40-phon contour** is the basis for the **A-weighting network**.

**A-Weighting in Noise Measurement (2 Marks):**
- A-weighting applies a frequency-dependent filter that approximates the 40-phon equal-loudness contour.
- It de-emphasizes low frequencies (<1 kHz) and high frequencies (>5 kHz).
- It correlates well with human **perceived loudness and hearing damage risk** for most industrial and environmental noise.
- Regulatory limits (e.g., OSHA 85 dBA, NIOSH 80 dBA) are specified in **dBA** for this reason.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **threshold of hearing, threshold of pain, and equal-loudness contours**. Define **phon** and **sone**, and derive the relation between them. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions (2 Marks):**
- **Threshold of Hearing (TOH):** The minimum SPL that the ear can detect; $p_0 = 20 \;\mu\text{Pa}$ at 1 kHz.
- **Threshold of Pain (TOP):** SPL producing a tickling or painful sensation in the ear; ~120 dB SPL.
- **Phon:** The loudness level (in dB) of a 1 kHz tone that is judged equally loud to a given sound.
- **Sone:** A linear unit of loudness; 1 sone = 40 phons; doubling sones = doubling perceived loudness.

**Equal-Loudness Contours (2 Marks):**
Contours of constant phon level across frequency — they illustrate frequency-dependent sensitivity and the basis for A/B/C weighting networks.

**Phon-Sone Derivation (3 Marks):**
Empirically, a doubling of loudness (sones) corresponds to a 10-phon increase:

$$L_N = 40 + 10 \log_2(S)$$

Using $\log_2(S) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(S)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{\log_{10}(S)}{0.3010}$:

$$L_N = 40 + \frac{10}{0.3010} \log_{10}(S) = 40 + 33.22 \log_{10}(S)$$

**Example:** $S = 4$ sones $\Rightarrow L_N = 40 + 33.22 \log_{10}(4) = 40 + 20 = 60$ phons.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Phons ≠ dB SPL** (except at 1 kHz). Students often write "70 phons = 70 dB" — this is WRONG except at 1 kHz.
> 2. **Sones are linear**, not logarithmic. Don't apply $\log$ to sones.
> 3. **A-weighting is only valid** for moderate SPL (40–80 dB). At very high or very low SPL, C-weighting or Z-weighting may be more appropriate.
> 4. **Don't confuse "areal ratio" (mechanical advantage)** with "intensity ratio" (energy). Pressure gain uses $20\log_{10}$, intensity uses $10\log_{10}$.
> 5. In impedance-matching derivations, **always state the assumed normal incidence** — oblique incidence yields different (Fresnel) coefficients.
> 6. The dynamic range of the ear is **120 dB**, NOT 200 dB or 140 dB. A common student error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam):**

- **Three Sub-divisions of Ear:** Outer (collection), Middle (amplification/impedance matching), Inner (transduction + frequency analysis).
- **Ear Canal Resonance:** $f = c / 4L \approx 3.43$ kHz; gain 10–15 dB.
- **Eardrum Area:** 55 mm²; **Oval Window Area:** 3.2 mm².
- **Areal Ratio:** ~17.2; **Lever Ratio:** ~1.3; **Total Middle Ear Gain:** ~27 dB.
- **Transmission Loss without middle ear:** ~30 dB.
- **Audible Range:** 20 Hz – 20 kHz; **Dynamic Range:** 120 dB.
- **Reference Pressure $p_0$:** $20 \;\mu\text{Pa}$ at 1 kHz.
- **Reference Intensity $I_0$:** $10^{-12}$ W/m².
- **Threshold of Hearing:** 0 dB SPL; **Threshold of Pain:** 120 dB SPL.
- **Fletcher-Munson Contours:** Basis for A-weighting.
- **Phon-Sone Relation:** $L_N = 40 + 33.22 \log_{10}(S)$.
- **A-Weighting:** Emulates 40-phon equal-loudness contour.
- **Basilar Membrane:** Place theory — high freq at base, low freq at apex.
- **Hair Cells:** Inner (sensory, ~3500) + Outer (amplifier, ~12000).
- **Eustachian Tube:** Pressure equalization.
- **Acoustic Reflex:** Stapedius + Tensor Tympani, protects against loud sounds.
- **Zwicker Critical Bandwidth:** $CB(f) = 25 + 75(1 + 1.4 f^2)^{0.69}$, used in MP3/audio coding.
- **Audiometric Notch:** Hearing loss typically begins at 4 kHz.
- **Presbycusis:** Age-related high-frequency hearing loss.
- **Otosclerosis:** Stapes fixation; cause of conductive hearing loss.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** "**O-M-I-H**" — **O**uter, **M**iddle, **I**nner — **H**earing pathway follows the same order: collect → match → analyze → transduce → perceive.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subjective and Objective Assessment of Sound
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Subjective and Objective Assessment of Sound ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Sound assessment** refers to the systematic evaluation and quantification of acoustic phenomena, which is broadly classified into two complementary paradigms: - **Objective Assessment of Sound**: The physical, instrument-based measurement of acoustic quantities expressed in absolute, reproducible physical units such as Pascals (Pa), Watts (W), and decibels (dB). It relies on microphones, sound level meters, and analyzers. It is independent of the listener. - **Subjective Assessment of Sound**: The psychophysical evaluation of how sound is *perceived* by the human auditory system. It involves psychoacoustic metrics like **Loudness (Sone)**, **Loudness Level (Phon)**, **Pitch (Mel)**, and **Timbre**. It is listener-dependent. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** > The core of this module deals with bridging the gap between the *physical stimulus* (an acoustic pressure wave) and the *perceptual response* (how loud a person feels it is). The unit **decibel (dB)** is a logarithmic, dimensionless ratio — never an absolute unit. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you step into a hot bath: - The **thermometer** floating in the water might read **45 °C** — that is the **objective** measurement. Anyone using the same calibrated thermometer gets the same number. - Your *feeling* — "Ah, this is hot!" or "It's just warm" — is the **subjective** assessment. It depends on your past experience, your body's state, and your personal sensitivity. > [!NOTE] > **In Sound:** The microphone of a sound level meter might read **85 dB SPL** (objective), but to one person it may *sound like polite chatter*, while to another it is *unbearable noise*. The latter is the **subjective** response. Engineers use **psychoacoustic scales (Phon, Sone, Mel)** to quantify that human "feeling" mathematically. ### 1.3 Why Both Assessments Are Needed | Aspect | Objective | Subjective | |---|---|---| | Tool | Sound Level Meter, Microphone | Human listener / psychoacoustic models | | Quantity | Pressure, Power, Intensity | Loudness, Pitch, Sharpness | | Reference | Physical constants (20 µPa, 10⁻¹² W) | Threshold of hearing (0 Phon) | | Reproducibility | Very high | Varies between listeners | | Used in | Regulations, engineering design | Product design, annoyance studies | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Objective Sound Assessment Sound is an energy-carrying pressure wave. Three physical quantities describe its objective strength: 1. **Sound Pressure (p)** — the local pressure deviation around atmospheric pressure. Measured in Pascals (Pa). 2. **Sound Intensity (I)** — the sound power transmitted per unit area in a specified direction. Measured in W/m². 3. **Sound Power (W)** — the total acoustic energy radiated by a source per unit time. Measured in Watts (W). Because the human ear responds to a vast range of pressures (from **20 µPa** to **200 Pa**, a 10-million-fold span), a logarithmic compression is used — the **decibel**. ### 2.2 Subjective Metrics — The Human Side of Sound - **Loudness Level (L_N)** in **Phons**: Defined as the SPL (in dB) of a 1 kHz pure tone judged *equally loud* as the given sound. - **Loudness (N)** in **Sones**: A linear, scalar measure. **1 Sone = 40 Phons**. Every doubling of Sones corresponds to roughly a **10 Phon** increase. - **Pitch (P)** in **Mels**: A subjective scale of perceived frequency. **1000 Mel = 1000 Hz** at the reference threshold. - **Timbre**: The perceptual attribute distinguishing two sounds of equal pitch and loudness (e.g., a violin vs. a flute playing the same note). > [!NOTE] > **Pitch vs Frequency Analogy:** Frequency is to Pitch what Wavelength of light is to Color. Frequency is a physical measurement; Pitch is a *perception*. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) $$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|c|} \hline \textbf{Quantity} & \textbf{Symbol} & \textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Unit} \\ \hline \text{Sound Pressure Level} & L_p & 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) & \text{dB} \\ \hline \text{Sound Intensity Level} & L_I & 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right) & \text{dB} \\ \hline \text{Sound Power Level} & L_W & 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_{\text{ref}}}\right) & \text{dB} \\ \hline \text{Reference Pressure} & p_{\text{ref}} & 20 \times 10^{-6} & \text{Pa} \\ \hline \text{Reference Intensity} & I_{\text{ref}} & 10^{-12} & \text{W/m}^2 \\ \hline \text{Reference Power} & W_{\text{ref}} & 10^{-12} & \text{W} \\ \hline \text{Loudness (Stevens)} & N & 2^{0.1\,(L_N - 40)} & \text{Sone} \\ \hline \text{Pitch (Stevens)} & P & 1000 \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{f}{700}\right) & \text{Mel} \\ \hline \text{Threshold of Hearing} & L_{TH} & 0 & \text{dB SPL} \\ \hline \text{Threshold of Pain} & L_{TP} & \approx 120\text{–}130 & \text{dB SPL} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Exam Point:** The multiplying factor in the log is **20** for pressure-based ratios (because power is proportional to pressure-squared) and **10** for power/intensity ratios. Confusing these two is the single most common mistake in KTU valuation. ### 2.4 Frequency Weighting Networks — Bridging Objective and Subjective To approximate human hearing on a meter, standard **A, B, C weightings** are used. Today, **A-weighting (dBA)** is the global standard for environmental noise. - **A-weighting**: Approximates the **40-Phon equal-loudness contour** — best mimics the ear at low to moderate levels. - **B-weighting**: Approximates the **70-Phon contour** — largely obsolete. - **C-weighting**: Approximates the **100-Phon contour** — used for high-level industrial noise. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Architectural Acoustics**: A-weighting helps design HVAC ducts and classrooms to be perceptually quiet, not just numerically low. - **Automotive NVH**: Sound Power Level of an engine determines cabin loudness; Sone rating decides if customers will find the car "premium" or "buzzy." - **Hearing Conservation**: OSHA and KTU-aligned industrial standards mandate exposure limits in **dBA** (e.g., 85 dBA for 8 hours). - **Audio Codecs (mp3, AAC)**: Use psychoacoustic masking models (Sone/Mel-based) to compress files by removing perceptually inaudible components. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Sound Pressure Level (SPL) **Step 1 — Starting from Power Proportionality** For a plane acoustic wave, the average intensity $I$ is proportional to the *square* of the RMS pressure: $$ I \propto p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2} $$ **Step 2 — Define the Level Function** A "Level" is defined as ten times the base-10 logarithm of the ratio of a quantity to its reference value: $$ L_x = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{x}{x_{\text{ref}}}\right) \text{ dB} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute $I \propto p^2$ for Intensity Level** $$ L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{p_{\text{ref}}^{\,2}}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Apply Logarithm Property $\log(a^2) = 2\log(a)$** $$ L_I = 10 \cdot 2 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) $$ **Step 5 — Final SPL Formula** $$ \boxed{\,L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) \text{ dB},\quad p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}\,} $$ This is *why* the factor is 20 and not 10 — purely from the squaring relationship between pressure and intensity. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Loudness (Sones) from Loudness Level (Phons) **Step 1 — Establish Reference Anchors** By definition, $N = 1$ Sone when $L_N = 40$ Phons. Also, by Stevens' Power Law, doubling the perceived loudness corresponds to a **10-Phon** increase: $$ N(L_N + 10) = 2\,N(L_N) $$ **Step 2 — General Exponential Form** Assume the relation between $N$ and $L_N$ is exponential: $$ N = N_0 \cdot k^{(L_N - 40)} $$ **Step 3 — Apply Boundary Conditions** From $N(40) = 1$: $\;N_0 = 1$. From $N(50) = 2$: $\;2 = 1 \cdot k^{10} \;\Rightarrow\; k = 2^{0.1}$. **Step 4 — Final Form** $$ \boxed{\,N = 2^{0.1\,(L_N - 40)} \text{ Sones}\,} $$ Equivalently: $$ \boxed{\,L_N = 40 + 10 \log_2(N) \text{ Phons}\,} $$ --- ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A compressor emits an RMS sound pressure of **0.2 Pa**. Find: (a) The SPL in dB. (b) The SPL in dBA if the 1 kHz octave band has an A-weighting correction of **0 dB**. (c) The Loudness Level in Phons if the same tone at 1 kHz is rated as **0.1 Sone** of perceived loudness. **Solution (a):** $$ L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) $$ $$ = 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000) = 20 \times 4 = \mathbf{80\ dB\ SPL} $$ **Solution (b):** $$ L_A = L_p + \Delta_A = 80 + 0 = \mathbf{80\ dBA} $$ **Solution (c):** $$ L_N = 40 + 10 \log_2(0.1) $$ Since $\log_2(0.1) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(0.1)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{-1}{0.301} \approx -3.322$: $$ L_N = 40 + 10(-3.322) \approx \mathbf{6.78\ Phons} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Although the meter reads a healthy **80 dB**, the perceived loudness is only **~7 Phons** because Phons reference the 1 kHz tone at the threshold of hearing. This shows how subjective metrics can de-emphasize low-frequency energy that the ear naturally filters. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix $$ \begin{array}{|l|c|c|} \hline \textbf{Feature} & \textbf{Objective} & \textbf{Subjective} \\ \hline \text{Logarithmic?} & \text{Yes (dB)} & \text{Mixed (Phon = log, Sone = linear)} \\ \hline \text{Depends on listener?} & \text{No} & \text{Yes} \\ \hline \text{Ear sensitivity modeled?} & \text{Only via A-weighting} & \text{Fully (via Phons/Sones)} \\ \hline \text{Frequency weighting} & \text{A, B, C, Z} & \text{Equal-loudness contours} \\ \hline \text{Reference} & 20\,\mu\text{Pa} & 1\,\text{Sone} = 40\,\text{Phons at 1 kHz} \\ \hline \text{Used in KTU papers for} & \text{SPL calculations} & \text{Annoyance, NVH, masking} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Sound Assessment The following block diagram illustrates the workflow from physical stimulus through measurement to perception, which is the conceptual backbone of Module 3. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sound Source
Physical Stimulus] --> B[Acoustic Pressure Wave
p rms] B --> C{Assessment Type} C -->|Objective Path| D[Microphone + SLM] D --> E[SPL, SIL, PWL
in dB] E --> F[Frequency Weighting
A, B, C, Z] F --> G[dBA Reading
on Display] C -->|Subjective Path| H[Human Auditory System
Cochlea + Brain] H --> I[Equal-Loudness
Contours] I --> J[Loudness Level
Phons] J --> K[Loudness
Sones] I --> L[Pitch
Mels] H --> M[Timbre
Quality] G --> N[Final Noise
Evaluation Report] K --> N L --> N M --> N ``` ### 4.2 Conversion Flowchart: Phons ↔ Sones ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Phons L sub N] -->|Subtract 40| P2[L sub N minus 40] P2 -->|Multiply by 0.1| P3[0.1 times L sub N minus 40] P3 -->|2 to the power| S1[Sones N] S1 -->|log base 2| S2[log base 2 of N] S2 -->|Multiply by 10| S3[10 log base 2 N] S3 -->|Add 40| P4[40 plus 10 log base 2 N] P4 --> P1 ``` ### 4.3 Equal-Loudness Contour Block Map (Fletcher-Munson) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Low_Level[Low SPL Region] A1[Threshold Curve 4 Phons] end subgraph Mid_Level[Mid SPL Region] A2[40 Phon Curve - A weighted reference] end subgraph High_Level[High SPL Region] A3[100 Phon Curve - C weighted reference] end A1 --> A2 --> A3 A2 --> B1[dBA Weighting Filter] A3 --> B2[dBC Weighting Filter] ``` ### 4.4 Subjective vs Objective — Decision Block ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Is the question about
human perception?] -->|Yes| S1[Use Subjective Scales
Sones, Phons, Mels] Q1 -->|No| O1[Use Objective Scales
dB SPL, dBA, dBC] S1 --> S2[Required for
Annoyance Studies] O1 --> O2[Required for
Regulatory Compliance] S2 --> F[Final Design Decision] O2 --> F ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** The arrows in flowchart `LR` are top-to-bottom; `TD` is left-to-right. The "Subjective Path" (right branch) is what your ear does, while the "Objective Path" (left branch) is what a Sound Level Meter does. KTU Module 3 fundamentally asks: *How do we make the meter imitate the ear?* — that is the role of A-weighting. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > Distinguish between **objective** and **subjective** assessment of sound. Give one example of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Objective assessment** involves the physical measurement of sound using instruments such as microphones and sound level meters. The result is expressed in absolute physical units like **dB SPL**. *Example:* Measuring the sound pressure level of a fan as 78 dB. - **Subjective assessment** involves the perception of sound by the human ear. It depends on the listener and is quantified using psychoacoustic scales like **Phons** and **Sones**. *Example:* A fan measured at 78 dB SPL may "sound" subjectively loud (high Sone value) or quiet (low Sone value) depending on its spectral content and the listener's sensitivity. *(1 mark each for definition + 1 mark each for example with correct unit.)* --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** > Define the unit **Sone** and state its relation with **Phon**. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Sone** is the linear, psychoacoustic unit of **loudness**. By definition, **1 Sone = 40 Phons** for a 1 kHz pure tone heard directly in front of the listener. The relation is given by Stevens' Power Law: $$ N = 2^{0.1\,(L_N - 40)} \text{ Sones} $$ A **doubling of Sones** corresponds to a **10-Phon** increase in loudness level. (1 mark definition, 1 mark equation, 1 mark relation to Phons.) --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 3 Pattern]* > **(a)** Derive the expression for **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** in decibels. Explain why a factor of 20 (and not 10) appears in the numerator. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A factory machine produces an RMS sound pressure of **0.632 Pa**. Calculate: (i) SPL in dB; (ii) Sound Intensity Level assuming plane wave conditions; (iii) Loudness in Sones if the operator judges the sound at 1 kHz to be **75 Phons**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of SPL (7 marks):** - Step 1: For a plane acoustic wave, intensity $I$ is proportional to $p_{\text{rms}}^2$. (1 mark) - Step 2: Level defined as $L_x = 10 \log_{10}(x/x_{\text{ref}})$. (1 mark) - Step 3: Substituting intensity: $$ L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{p_{\text{ref}}^2}\right) $$ (1 mark) - Step 4: Using $\log(a^2) = 2\log(a)$: $$ L_I = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) = L_p $$ (2 marks) - Step 5: $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ is the standard threshold of hearing reference. (1 mark) - Step 6: Justification — factor 20 arises from the squaring relationship between pressure amplitude and acoustic intensity (energy); pressure amplitudes don't add linearly but energies do. (1 mark) **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) SPL:** $$ L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.632}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(31{,}600) \approx 20 \times 4.5 = \mathbf{90\ dB\ SPL} $$ (2 marks) **(ii) Sound Intensity Level (Plane wave: $L_I = L_p$):** $$ L_I \approx \mathbf{90\ dB} $$ (2 marks) **(iii) Loudness in Sones:** $$ N = 2^{0.1\,(75 - 40)} = 2^{3.5} = 2^{3} \cdot 2^{0.5} = 8 \times 1.414 \approx \mathbf{11.31\ Sones} $$ (3 marks) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain how the **human auditory system** performs subjective assessment. Discuss the role of **equal-loudness contours** in the design of frequency weighting networks. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** The A-weighted sound pressure level in an industry is **88 dBA**. If the equivalent continuous level over 8 hours is also 88 dBA, determine: (i) The permissible exposure time per OSHA standards; (ii) The corresponding unweighted SPL at 1 kHz where A-weighting correction is 0 dB; (iii) The Loudness Level in Phons if the 1 kHz tone is judged **4 Sones** loud. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Auditory System & Weighting (7 marks):** - The pinna collects acoustic waves and funnels them via the ear canal to the tympanic membrane. Vibrations are transmitted through the ossicles to the cochlea, where the basilar membrane performs a mechanical **Fourier-like frequency decomposition**. (2 marks) - Hair cells transduce mechanical motion into neural spikes sent to the auditory cortex. The brain integrates these into perceptions of **loudness, pitch, and timbre**. (1 mark) - **Equal-loudness contours** (Fletcher-Munson curves) plot SPL required at each frequency to sound as loud as a 1 kHz reference. They reveal that the ear is most sensitive around **2–5 kHz** and less sensitive at very low and very high frequencies. (2 marks) - **A-weighting** approximates the **40-Phon contour**, **C-weighting** approximates the **100-Phon contour**. Thus A-weighting penalizes low-frequency energy that the ear naturally ignores at conversational levels — making the meter "hear" like a human. (2 marks) **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) OSHA Permissible Exposure:** OSHA rule: permissible time halves for every 5 dB increase above 90 dBA. Reference: 90 dBA → 8 hours. For 88 dBA: 2 dB below 90 dBA → time doubles. $$ T = 8 \times 2^{0.4} \approx 8 \times 1.32 \approx \mathbf{10.55\ hours} $$ But OSHA caps at 8 hours and other regulatory limits apply. Standard textbook answer: **≈ 10.6 hours** (or simply *"not exceeding 8-hour shift limit"*). (2 marks) **(ii) Unweighted SPL at 1 kHz:** $$ L_p = L_A - \Delta_A = 88 - 0 = \mathbf{88\ dB\ SPL} $$ (1 mark) **(iii) Loudness Level in Phons:** $$ L_N = 40 + 10 \log_2(4) = 40 + 10 \times 2 = \mathbf{60\ Phons} $$ (2 marks) **Bonus interpretation:** 88 dB SPL is moderate-loud; 60 Phons means it sounds about as loud as ordinary conversation (typical voice at 1 m). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 20** vs 10 in dB formulas — leads to **wrong answer** and ~3-mark deduction. > 2. **Using $20 \log$ for intensity levels** — incorrect; intensity uses factor 10. > 3. **Confusing Phons and Sones**: Phons are logarithmic, Sones are linear. Never write "Sone = 60"; only "60 Phons." > 4. **Not stating the reference value** ($20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ or $10^{-12}$ W) — KTU examiners specifically look for this in 14-mark answers. > 5. **Skipping the dB reference** in the final answer line. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Objective assessment** = physical measurement in **dB**; uses microphones, SLM, weighting networks (A, B, C, Z). - **Subjective assessment** = human perception in **Phons / Sones / Mels**; uses equal-loudness contours and psychoacoustic models. - **SPL formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_{\text{ref}})$; **SIL/PWL:** factor is 10. - **Reference values:** $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$, $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}$ W/m², $W_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}$ W. - **Sone ↔ Phon:** $N = 2^{0.1(L_N - 40)}$; 1 Sone = 40 Phons; doubling Sones = +10 Phons. - **A-weighting** mimics the 40-Phon curve — used for environmental & occupational noise regulations. - **Equal-loudness contours** (Fletcher-Munson) show ear is most sensitive at **2–5 kHz**. - **Threshold of hearing:** 0 dB SPL at 1 kHz; **Threshold of pain:** ~120–130 dB SPL. - **Plane wave** condition: $L_p = L_I$ (numerically equal in dB). - KTU Module 3 essentially answers: *Can we make the meter "hear" like a human?* — through weighting networks. - **Always quote units** (dB, dBA, Phon, Sone) — missing units = lost marks. - **For 14-mark questions**, examiners expect: derivation + justification + numerical + interpretation. Missing any one loses 2–3 marks.

topics.title: Hearing Threshold
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hearing Threshold ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Hearing Threshold** (also called the **Threshold of Hearing** or **TOH**) is defined as the minimum sound pressure level (SPL) at which an average human ear with normal hearing capability can just barely detect the presence of a sound, for a specified frequency, in the absence of any other external sounds (anechoic condition). The internationally standardized reference value is: - **Reference Sound Pressure: $P_0 = 20\ \mu Pa = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ Pa$** - This corresponds to **0 dB SPL** at **1 kHz** - The standard is defined in **ISO 389-7** and **ANSI S1.6** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The hearing threshold forms the basis for the decibel (dB) scale used universally in acoustics. Every sound pressure measurement in noise control is referenced against the human threshold of hearing at 1 kHz. ### 1.2 Conceptually Related Thresholds | Threshold Type | Typical SPL Value | Description | |----------------|-------------------|-------------| | **Threshold of Hearing (TOH)** | **0 dB** (at 1 kHz) | Minimum audible sound | | **Threshold of Discomfort** | ~85–90 dB | Sound begins to feel uncomfortable | | **Threshold of Pain** | **120–130 dB** | Sound causes physical pain | | **Threshold of Damage** | >140 dB | Immediate auditory injury | ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy / Real-World Understanding > [!NOTE] > **Think of hearing threshold like a "silent alarm" sensitivity knob:** Imagine your ear is a sensitive microphone connected to an amplifier. The hearing threshold represents the *minimum signal strength* the amplifier (your ear) can register before the signal becomes lost in the internal "noise" (thermal noise in the cochlea, blood flow, etc.). Just as a high-end microphone can pick up a whisper in a quiet room, a healthy human ear can detect a mosquito's wing flap (~0 dB) but cannot hear frequencies outside 20 Hz–20 kHz — that range is like the "frequency window" of the ear. ### 1.4 Historical Context The hearing threshold was first standardized in **1934** by the American Standards Association (now ANSI). The reference pressure of **20 µPa** was chosen because it approximates the faintest sound a young, healthy adult can hear at **1 kHz**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why 20 µPa? The Reference Pressure Logic The decibel scale in acoustics is *referenced* to the weakest sound the average human ear can hear. This logarithmic compression allows the enormous range of human hearing (from mosquito flutter to jet engine roar) to be expressed on a manageable scale of 0–140 dB. ### 2.2 Operational Breakdown of Hearing Mechanism The hearing threshold is determined by a chain of physiological and physical events: 1. **Outer Ear (Pinna + Ear Canal):** Collects sound waves and funnels them to the eardrum (tympanic membrane). 2. **Middle Ear (Ossicles: Malleus, Incus, Stapes):** Mechanical impedance matching; converts air vibrations to fluid vibrations in the cochlea. Provides ~22 dB of amplification. 3. **Inner Ear (Cochlea):** Fluid-filled chamber with hair cells along the **Basilar Membrane**. Different frequencies excite different locations (place theory). 4. **Auditory Nerve:** Transmits electrical signals to the auditory cortex. 5. **Brain Processing:** Perceived as sound. The threshold is **not constant** — it varies dramatically with frequency. The ear is most sensitive around **2–5 kHz**. ### 2.3 Frequency Dependence of the Threshold The ear is *not* equally sensitive at all frequencies. The threshold of hearing curve (Minimum Audible Pressure, MAP) shows: - At **1 kHz**: 0 dB SPL - At **100 Hz**: ~30 dB SPL (the ear is *less sensitive*) - At **20 Hz**: ~70 dB SPL (very insensitive) - At **10 kHz**: ~20 dB SPL - At **20 kHz**: ~80 dB SPL (sharp drop in sensitivity) ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Expression | Units | Notes | |---|-------------------|---------------------|-------|-------| | 1 | **Sound Pressure Level** | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | dB | $P_0 = 20\ \mu Pa$ | | 2 | **Sound Intensity Level** | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | dB | $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ W/m^2$ | | 3 | **Sound Power Level** | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right)$ | dB | $W_0 = 10^{-12}\ W$ | | 4 | **Threshold of Hearing (ref.)** | $P_{TOH} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ Pa$ | Pa | At 1 kHz | | 5 | **Threshold of Pain** | $L_p \approx 120$ to $130\ dB$ | dB | Frequency dependent | | 6 | **Loudness in Phons** | $L_N = L_p \text{ at 1 kHz}$ | phon | Same as SPL at 1 kHz | | 7 | **Loudness in Sones** | $S = 2^{(P-40)/10}$ | sone | P = loudness in phons | | 8 | **Hearing Range** | $20\ Hz \leq f \leq 20{,}000\ Hz$ | Hz | For young healthy adults | | 9 | **Inverse Square Law** | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | dB | Point source, free field | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Distinction:** The decibel (dB) measures the *physical* sound pressure. The phon measures the *perceived* loudness accounting for frequency. The sone measures *perceived magnitude* on a linear scale. Students often confuse these — remember: **dB = physical; phon = perceived loudness level; sone = perceived loudness magnitude.** ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hearing Conservation:** OSHA sets permissible exposure limits (PEL) at **90 dB for 8 hours**, with a 5 dB exchange rate. Threshold data drives workplace noise regulations. - **Audiometer Calibration:** Clinical hearing tests rely on standardized threshold curves. - **Audio Equipment Design:** Microphones, speakers, and hearing aids are designed to span the threshold-to-pain range. - **Environmental Noise Mapping:** Building acoustics and city noise ordinances use hearing threshold curves as weighting references (A-weighted dB). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Sound Pressure Level (SPL) Equation The decibel scale is logarithmic because the human ear responds to *ratios* of pressure, not absolute differences. The derivation proceeds as follows: **Step 1: Define the ratio of two sound pressures.** Let $P$ be the measured RMS sound pressure and $P_0$ be the reference pressure (threshold of hearing at 1 kHz). $$R = \frac{P}{P_0}$$ **Step 2: Express the ratio on a logarithmic (base-10) scale to compress the range.** Since the human ear can detect pressures from $20\ \mu Pa$ to $20\ Pa$ (a ratio of 1,000,000:1), we use base-10 logarithm. $$N_B = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)$$ This gives the number of **Bels** ($N_B$). **Step 3: Convert Bels to decibels by multiplying by 10 (since 1 Bel = 10 dB).** $$L_p = 10 \cdot N_B = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)$$ Wait — this is for *power* ratios. Since sound *intensity* is proportional to the square of sound pressure ($I \propto P^2$), we must adjust. **Step 4: Account for the pressure-squared relationship.** $$L_p = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P^2}{P_0^2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)^2$$ Applying the logarithm power rule $\log(x^n) = n \log(x)$: $$L_p = 10 \cdot 2 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\,L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)\ \text{dB SPL}\,}$$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1: Calculating SPL from Pressure **Problem:** A machine produces a sound pressure of $P = 0.02\ Pa$. Calculate its sound pressure level relative to the threshold of hearing ($P_0 = 20\ \mu Pa$). **Step 1:** Substitute into the SPL formula. $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.02}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Compute the ratio inside the logarithm. $$\frac{0.02}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.02}{0.00002} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 10^3 = 1000$$ **Step 3:** Apply $\log_{10}(1000) = 3$. $$L_p = 20 \times 3 = 60\ \text{dB SPL}$$ **Answer:** The machine produces a sound level of **60 dB SPL**. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2: Pressure from SPL **Problem:** The sound level in a factory is measured as $L_p = 94\ dB$. Find the actual RMS sound pressure. **Step 1:** Rearrange the SPL formula to solve for $P$. $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) \implies \frac{L_p}{20} = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Apply the inverse logarithm. $$\frac{P}{P_0} = 10^{L_p/20} = 10^{94/20} = 10^{4.7}$$ **Step 3:** Compute $10^{4.7}$. $$10^{4.7} = 10^{0.7} \times 10^4 \approx 5.012 \times 10^4$$ **Step 4:** Multiply by $P_0$. $$P = 5.012 \times 10^4 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 5.012 \times 10^4 \times 2 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$P = 10.024 \times 10^{-1} = 1.0024\ Pa \approx 1.0\ Pa$$ **Answer:** The RMS sound pressure is approximately **1 Pa**. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3: Inverse Square Law (Distance Effect) **Problem:** A point source produces $L_{p1} = 80\ dB$ at $r_1 = 1\ m$. Find the SPL at $r_2 = 10\ m$. **Step 1:** Apply the inverse square law in dB form. $$L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Compute the distance ratio. $$\frac{r_2}{r_1} = \frac{10}{1} = 10$$ **Step 3:** Apply $\log_{10}(10) = 1$. $$L_{p2} = 80 - 20 \times 1 = 80 - 20 = 60\ \text{dB}$$ **Answer:** At 10 m, the SPL drops to **60 dB** (a 20 dB drop per decade of distance — a *doubling* of distance gives −6 dB). --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 4: Loudness Conversion (Phons to Sones) **Problem:** A tone has a loudness level of $P = 60\ \text{phons}$. Find its loudness in sones. **Step 1:** Apply the sone conversion formula. $$S = 2^{(P - 40)/10}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the exponent. $$(P - 40)/10 = (60 - 40)/10 = 20/10 = 2$$ **Step 3:** Compute $2^2$. $$S = 4\ \text{sones}$$ **Answer:** The perceived loudness is **4 sones** (i.e., 4 times the loudness of a 40-phon reference tone). --- ### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix | Quantity | Symbol | Value at TOH | Value at Threshold of Pain | Ratio | |----------|--------|--------------|----------------------------|-------| | Sound Pressure | $P$ | $20\ \mu Pa$ | $20\ Pa$ | $10^6$ | | Intensity | $I$ | $10^{-12}\ W/m^2$ | $1\ W/m^2$ | $10^{12}$ | | SPL | $L_p$ | $0\ dB$ | $120\ dB$ | Linear in log | | Power | $W$ | $10^{-12}\ W$ | $1\ W$ | $10^{12}$ | This table clearly demonstrates the **enormous dynamic range** of human hearing — spanning **12 orders of magnitude** in intensity — which is why the logarithmic decibel scale is mathematically necessary. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Human Hearing & Threshold Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sound Source
Pressure P] --> B[Outer Ear
Pinna and Ear Canal
Gain approx 2 to 3 dB] B --> C[Middle Ear
Ossicles Impedance Match
Gain approx 22 dB] C --> D[Inner Ear Cochlea
Basilar Membrane Vibration
Frequency to Place Mapping] D --> E[Hair Cell Stimulation
Mechanical to Electrical] E --> F[Auditory Nerve
Signal Transmission] F --> G[Auditory Cortex
Perception] G --> H{Threshold Check} H --> I[P less than TOH
No Perception] H --> J[P equal to TOH
Just Audible] H --> K[P greater than TOH
Audible Sound] H --> L[P greater than 120 dB
Threshold of Pain] H --> M[P greater than 140 dB
Auditory Damage] style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style C fill:#fff4e1 style D fill:#ffe1f5 style E fill:#ffe1f5 style F fill:#e1ffe1 style G fill:#e1ffe1 style H fill:#f5e1ff style I fill:#ffcccc style J fill:#fff4cc style K fill:#ccffcc style L fill:#ffaa66 style M fill:#ff6666 ``` ### 4.2 Threshold of Hearing Frequency Map (Conceptual Block) ```mermaid graph LR A[Very Low Frequency
20 Hz
SPL approx 70 dB] --> B[Low Frequency
100 Hz
SPL approx 30 dB] B --> C[Mid Frequency
500 Hz to 1 kHz
SPL approx 0 dB] C --> D[High Sensitivity Zone
2 to 5 kHz
SPL approx minus 5 dB] D --> E[High Frequency
10 kHz
SPL approx 20 dB] E --> F[Very High Frequency
20 kHz
SPL approx 80 dB] style A fill:#ff9999 style B fill:#ffcc99 style C fill:#99ff99 style D fill:#66ff66 style E fill:#ffcc99 style F fill:#ff9999 ``` ### 4.3 Decibel Reference Scale Diagram ```mermaid graph TD A[0 dB
Threshold of Hearing
20 microPa] --> B[20 dB
Quiet Whisper
Leaves Rustling] B --> C[40 dB
Quiet Library] C --> D[60 dB
Normal Conversation] D --> E[80 dB
Busy Traffic] E --> F[100 dB
Power Tools] F --> G[120 dB
Threshold of Pain
Jet Engine Nearby] G --> H[140 dB
Immediate Damage
Gunshot] style A fill:#e1ffe1 style B fill:#d4f5d4 style C fill:#c4ecc4 style D fill:#b4e2b4 style E fill:#ffd4a1 style F fill:#ffb380 style G fill:#ff8566 style H fill:#ff3333 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Notice that each step on the decibel scale represents a **tenfold** increase in sound *intensity* (energy), but only a perceived *doubling* of loudness occurs roughly every **10 dB**. The "U-shape" of the threshold curve explains why equal sound pressures at different frequencies do not sound equally loud — this motivated the creation of equal-loudness contours (Fletcher-Munson curves, covered in Module 3 next sections). --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > Define **hearing threshold**. State its standard reference value and the frequency at which this reference is defined. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **hearing threshold** is the minimum sound pressure level that the average human ear with normal hearing can detect under quiet, anechoic conditions for a given frequency. - **Standard Reference Pressure:** $P_0 = 20\ \mu Pa = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ Pa$ - **Reference Frequency:** $f = 1\ kHz$ - **Corresponding SPL:** $0\ dB$ This reference forms the basis of the decibel scale used in all acoustic measurements (ANSI S1.6 / ISO 389). --- #### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > Distinguish between **dB**, **phon**, and **sone**. Why are multiple units needed to quantify sound? **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Unit | Measures | Type | Reference | |------|----------|------|-----------| | **dB** | Sound pressure or intensity | Physical | $20\ \mu Pa$ | | **phon** | Perceived loudness level | Equal-loudness contour | SPL at 1 kHz | | **sone** | Perceived loudness magnitude | Linear scale | 1 sone = 40 phons | **Reason for multiple units:** The ear responds non-linearly across frequencies. **dB** captures only physical sound magnitude; **phon** accounts for frequency-dependent ear sensitivity; **sone** gives a linear, perceptually-meaningful loudness scale (e.g., 2 sones = twice as loud). --- ### 📝 PART B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following full questions. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Derive the equation for **Sound Pressure Level** in decibels, starting from the definition of a Bel. State clearly the reference pressure value and explain why a logarithmic scale is used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A workshop has an SPL of **86 dB** measured at the operator's position. Calculate: (i) The actual RMS sound pressure in Pascals. (ii) The SPL if the operator moves **3 times farther** from the source. (iii) The equivalent loudness in sones, given that the measured SPL is at 1 kHz. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A** **(a) Derivation of SPL Formula — 7 marks** The human ear responds to a vast range of pressures (from $20\ \mu Pa$ to $20\ Pa$). A linear scale is impractical; therefore, a **logarithmic scale** is used, defined as follows: **Step 1:** Define the Bel (B) as the base-10 logarithm of a power ratio. Since sound intensity is proportional to the square of sound pressure: $$N_B = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P^2}{P_0^2}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Apply the power rule of logarithms: $$N_B = 2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Convert Bels to decibels (1 Bel = 10 dB): $$L_p = 10 \times 2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\,L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)\ \text{dB SPL}\,}$$ **Reference value:** $P_0 = 20\ \mu Pa = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ Pa$ (threshold of hearing at 1 kHz). **Why logarithmic?** 1. Compresses 12 orders of magnitude into a manageable scale. 2. Matches the ear's logarithmic response (Weber-Fechner law). 3. Simplifies multiplication/division of sound levels into addition/subtraction. --- **(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks** **(i) RMS Sound Pressure at 86 dB** **Step 1:** Rearrange the SPL formula: $$\frac{L_p}{20} = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)$$ $$\frac{P}{P_0} = 10^{L_p/20} = 10^{86/20} = 10^{4.3}$$ **Step 2:** Compute $10^{4.3}$: $$10^{4.3} = 10^{0.3} \times 10^4 \approx 1.9953 \times 10^4$$ **Step 3:** Multiply by $P_0$: $$P = 1.9953 \times 10^4 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 1.9953 \times 10^4 \times 2 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$P \approx 0.399\ Pa$$ **Answer:** $P \approx 0.4\ Pa$ --- **(ii) SPL at 3 times the distance (Inverse Square Law)** $$L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$ $$L_{p2} = 86 - 20 \log_{10}(3)$$ $$\log_{10}(3) \approx 0.477$$ $$L_{p2} = 86 - 20 \times 0.477 = 86 - 9.54 \approx 76.46\ \text{dB}$$ **Answer:** $\approx 76.5\ dB$ --- **(iii) Loudness in Sones** Since the SPL is at 1 kHz, the loudness in phons equals the SPL: $$P = 86\ \text{phons}$$ $$S = 2^{(P - 40)/10} = 2^{(86 - 40)/10} = 2^{4.6}$$ $$S \approx 24.25\ \text{sones}$$ **Answer:** $\approx 24.3\ \text{sones}$ (i.e., ~24 times louder than the reference 40-phon tone). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU Pattern]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **equal-loudness contours** (Fletcher-Munson curves). Explain why 1 kHz is chosen as the reference frequency for the phon scale. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two sound sources operate simultaneously in a hall. Source A produces **75 dB** and Source B produces **78 dB** at the listener's ear. Calculate the **combined SPL**. If the listener's threshold at the prevailing frequency is **10 dB SPL**, find the **sound pressure level above threshold** for the combined source. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B** **(a) Equal-Loudness Contours — 7 marks** **Definition:** Equal-loudness contours (Fletcher-Munson curves, 1933; revised in ISO 226:2003) are curves that connect points of equal perceived loudness across different frequencies. **Key Features:** - Each curve represents a constant loudness level (in phons). - The **1 kHz point** lies on a 45° line (no correction needed). - The ear is most sensitive between **2–5 kHz** (contours dip lowest here). - Below 500 Hz and above 5 kHz, contours rise steeply — the ear is *less sensitive*. - At very low (20 Hz) and very high (20 kHz) frequencies, the threshold rises to ~70–80 dB. **Reference Frequency Justification:** - The human ear is most sensitive around 2–5 kHz, but **1 kHz** is chosen as the reference because: 1. It falls in the flat region of the equal-loudness contours. 2. It is easy to generate accurately in laboratories. 3. It avoids the resonance peak at 2.5–4 kHz (which would complicate standardization). 4. Historical convention — used since the 1930s for audiometer calibration. **Sketch:** (Describe a U-shape at 0 phons, with successive contours at 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 phons rising in SPL but flattening at mid frequencies.) --- **(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks** **Step 1:** Combine two uncorrelated sound sources using energy addition (not dB addition directly). Sound pressures squared add: $$P_{total}^2 = P_A^2 + P_B^2$$ In decibel form: $$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_A/10} + 10^{L_B/10}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute values: $$L_A = 75\ \text{dB},\quad L_B = 78\ \text{dB}$$ $$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{75/10} + 10^{78/10}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{7.5} + 10^{7.8}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Compute each term: $$10^{7.5} = 3.162 \times 10^7$$ $$10^{7.8} = 6.310 \times 10^7$$ **Step 4:** Sum and apply log: $$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(3.162 \times 10^7 + 6.310 \times 10^7\right)$$ $$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(9.472 \times 10^7\right)$$ $$L_{total} = 10 \times (7.9765) = 79.77\ \text{dB} \approx 79.8\ \text{dB}$$ **Approximation rule:** When two sources differ by ≤10 dB, the combined SPL = higher value + 3 dB (since 78 + 3 ≈ 81 is too high here; use exact calculation). **Quick rule for small differences:** $\Delta L = 78 - 75 = 3\ dB$. Using the **3 dB rule for identical sources** and looking up a 3 dB difference correction table: $$L_{total} \approx 78 + 1.76 \approx 79.76\ \text{dB}$$ **Answer:** $L_{total} \approx 79.8\ dB$ --- **Step 5:** SPL above threshold (Sensation Level): $$SL = L_{total} - L_{threshold}$$ $$SL = 79.8 - 10 = 69.8\ \text{dB}$$ **Answer:** **Sensation Level ≈ 69.8 dB** above the listener's threshold. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Adding dB values directly** is the #1 mistake. You CANNOT add 75 + 78 = 153 dB. Always convert to intensities/pressures first, sum, then convert back. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 20** in the SPL formula (students often write $10 \log$ instead of $20 \log$ for pressure). > 3. **Using $P_0 = 10\ \mu Pa$** instead of $20\ \mu Pa$ — this is the acoustics reference, *not* the air pressure reference. > 4. **Confusing dB SPL with dBA** — dBA is A-weighted (frequency-corrected); dB SPL is unweighted. > 5. **Inverse square law** assumes a *point source* in a *free field* (anechoic). Do not apply blindly in enclosed spaces. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **Hearing Threshold = 0 dB SPL = $20\ \mu Pa$ at 1 kHz** (memorize this cold). - ⭐ **Threshold of Pain ≈ 120–130 dB** (frequency dependent). - ⭐ **Hearing Range:** 20 Hz to 20 kHz. - ⭐ **SPL Formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(P/P_0)$ - ⭐ **Intensity Formula:** $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ - ⭐ **Inverse Square Law:** Doubling distance → −6 dB; 10× distance → −20 dB. - ⭐ **Phons:** Loudness level, numerically equal to SPL at 1 kHz. - ⭐ **Sones:** Perceived loudness magnitude; $S = 2^{(P-40)/10}$, where 1 sone = 40 phons. - ⭐ **Most Sensitive Frequency Range:** 2–5 kHz (explains the U-shape of equal-loudness contours). - ⭐ **Reference Pressure:** Always use $P_0 = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ Pa$ in SPL calculations (NOT $10\ \mu Pa$). - ⭐ **Reference Intensity:** $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ W/m^2$. - ⭐ **Two sources differ by >10 dB:** Combined SPL ≈ louder source alone. - ⭐ **Two sources equal:** Combined SPL = louder + 3 dB. - ⭐ **Sensation Level (SL):** $SL = L_p - L_{threshold}$ - ⭐ **A-weighting** adjusts for ear sensitivity (used in noise meters; A-weighted dB ≈ dBA). ---

topics.title: Octave Band Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Octave Band Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Octave Band Analysis** is a frequency-domain method of sound measurement and characterization in which the audible frequency spectrum (typically **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz**) is divided into discrete logarithmic frequency intervals (bands), each spanning a ratio of **2:1** between its upper and lower cutoff frequencies. The term *"octave"* is borrowed directly from music, where it represents an 8-fold musical interval between two notes.

The defining mathematical condition for any octave band is:

$$f_{upper} = 2 \cdot f_{lower}$$

For an octave band, the **center frequency** $f_c$ is defined as the **geometric mean** of the upper and lower cutoff frequencies:

$$f_c = \sqrt{f_1 \cdot f_2}$$

where $f_1$ is the lower cutoff frequency and $f_2$ is the upper cutoff frequency. Because $f_2 = 2 f_1$, the relationship simplifies elegantly to $f_c = f_1 \sqrt{2} = f_2 / \sqrt{2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Octave band analysis is the cornerstone technique for noise source identification, environmental noise mapping, occupational hearing conservation, and evaluating the effectiveness of acoustic treatments (mufflers, barriers, enclosures). It is *the* analytical framework required to interpret virtually all modern sound-level meter readings and noise spectra.

## 1.2 Standardized Octave Bands

To ensure universal reproducibility, the **ISO 266:1997** (and ANSI S1.11) standards prescribe **10 standard octave bands** spanning the audio range:

| Band Index (n) | Center Frequency $f_c$ (Hz) | Lower Cutoff $f_1$ (Hz) | Upper Cutoff $f_2$ (Hz) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 31.5 | 22.4 | 44.7 |
| 2 | 63 | 44.7 | 89.1 |
| 3 | 125 | 89.1 | 177.8 |
| 4 | 250 | 177.8 | 354.8 |
| 5 | 500 | 354.8 | 707.1 |
| 6 | 1000 | 707.1 | 1414.2 |
| 7 | 2000 | 1414.2 | 2828.4 |
| 8 | 4000 | 2828.4 | 5656.9 |
| 9 | 8000 | 5656.9 | 11250.0 |
| 10 | 16000 | 11250.0 | 22000.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> Each successive standard octave band's center frequency is exactly **double** the previous one. This logarithmic doubling matches the human auditory system's own perceptual scale, making octave band data far more meaningful than raw linear frequency data for engineering purposes.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Acoustic Lens"

Imagine you are looking at a rainbow through **10 different colored magnifying glasses simultaneously**. Each glass transmits only *one* color range (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet, etc.). By stacking all 10 readings together, you obtain a complete spectral fingerprint of the light.

**Octave band analysis works identically for sound:**
- Instead of light, we analyze **sound pressure variations**.
- Instead of color, we divide the audible range into **10 logarithmically spaced frequency bands**.
- The "color intensity" in each glass is the **Sound Pressure Level (SPL) in dB** within that band.
- The complete picture (the rainbow) is the **sound spectrum** of the source.

**Real-world example:** A noisy industrial fan measured at **85 dB(A) overall** may, upon octave band analysis, reveal peaks at **250 Hz and 1000 Hz bands**. This tells the engineer *which tonal components dominate*, guiding precise treatment (e.g., adding a tuned resonator at 250 Hz rather than wasting money on broadband absorption).

## 1.4 Fractional Octave Bands

For higher spectral resolution, the audio range is further subdivided into **fractional octaves**:

$$N = \frac{1}{B}$$

where $N$ is the number of bands per octave and $B$ is the bandwidth fraction. The most common are:

- **1/1 octave** (N = 1): coarse analysis (10 bands total).
- **1/3 octave** (N = 3): standard for detailed engineering work (**30 bands**).
- **1/6 octave** (N = 6): used in precision acoustics and modal analysis.
- **1/12 octave** (N = 12): musical acoustics and high-resolution research.

For any fractional octave of order $1/n$:

$$f_2 / f_1 = 2^{1/n}$$

$$f_c(n,k) = f_{ref} \cdot 2^{(k / n)}$$

where $k$ is the band index and $f_{ref}$ is a reference frequency (typically 1000 Hz).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why logarithmic (and not linear) banding?** The human ear perceives frequency on a **logarithmic scale** (Weber-Fechner law). Doubling of frequency corresponds to a perceived "musical octave". Linear bands would crowd most measurements into low frequencies, producing poor resolution where the ear is most sensitive (1 kHz – 4 kHz). Octave bands give perceptually uniform resolution.

## 1.5 Engineering Significance

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, octave band analysis is foundational because it bridges the gap between raw time-domain sound signals and the frequency-domain information needed for:

1. **Noise Source Identification** — locating dominant tonal components.
2. **Hearing Conservation** — applying A-weighting corrections band-by-band.
3. **Acoustic Material Selection** — choosing absorbers/reactors tuned to peak bands.
4. **Regulatory Compliance** — meeting OSHA, ISO 1996, and CPCB noise standards.
5. **Muffler Design** — calculating transmission loss at band center frequencies.
6. **Room Acoustics** — identifying problematic modal resonances.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of Constant-Percentage Bandwidth

The defining property of an octave (or fractional-octave) filter is its **constant percentage bandwidth**:

$$\frac{\Delta f}{f_c} = \text{constant}$$

where $\Delta f = f_2 - f_1$ and $f_c$ is the center (geometric mean) frequency.

For an octave band, deriving this constant:

$$\frac{\Delta f}{f_c} = \frac{f_2 - f_1}{\sqrt{f_1 f_2}}$$

Substituting $f_2 = 2 f_1$:

$$\frac{\Delta f}{f_c} = \frac{2f_1 - f_1}{\sqrt{f_1 \cdot 2 f_1}} = \frac{f_1}{f_1 \sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Therefore, for any octave band:

$$\frac{\Delta f}{f_c} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \quad \text{(or 70.71\%)}$$

This is a universal property — it holds for **every** octave band regardless of its center frequency.

For a general fractional octave of order $1/n$:

$$\frac{\Delta f}{f_c} = 2^{1/(2n)} - 2^{-1/(2n)} = 2 \sinh\left(\frac{\ln 2}{2n}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** A "constant percentage bandwidth" means that as we move up in frequency, the filter gets **proportionally wider in Hz** but maintains the **same proportional resolution**. This mirrors human pitch perception (a just-noticeable difference in pitch is approximately 0.6% to 1% in frequency across most of the audio range).

## 2.2 Cutoff Frequency Relationships

Given any one of the three defining frequencies, the other two can be derived:

$$f_c = \sqrt{f_1 f_2}$$

$$f_1 = f_c / \sqrt{2} = f_c \cdot 2^{-1/2}$$

$$f_2 = f_c \cdot \sqrt{2} = f_c \cdot 2^{+1/2}$$

For successive standard octave band center frequencies (factor of 2):

$$f_{c,k+1} = 2 \cdot f_{c,k}$$

This means each successive octave band captures **twice as much spectrum width** (in Hz), but maintains identical proportional resolution.

## 2.3 Energy Summation (Band Combination)

When we measure SPL in each octave band, the **overall broadband SPL** is obtained by **logarithmic (energy) summation**, not arithmetic averaging:

$$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} 10^{L_i / 10} \right)$$

where $L_i$ is the SPL (in dB) of the $i$-th band and $N$ is the total number of bands (typically 8 or 10 for octave, 30 for 1/3-octave).

### Useful Approximation Rules

| Rule | Condition | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Two equal bands | $L_1 = L_2$ | Add **+3 dB** |
| Band higher by ≥ 10 dB | $L_1 - L_2 \geq 10$ | Neglect weaker band (error < 0.5 dB) |
| Band higher by 3 dB | $L_1 - L_2 = 3$ | Add **+1.76 dB** to higher |
| Band higher by 6 dB | $L_1 - L_2 = 6$ | Add **+1.25 dB** to higher |
| Band higher by 10 dB | $L_1 - L_2 = 10$ | Add **+0.41 dB** to higher |

## 2.4 Conversion Between Octave, 1/3-Octave, and Overall SPL

The bandwidth of a 1/3-octave band is approximately **1/3** the bandwidth of an octave band centered at the same frequency. To combine three contiguous 1/3-octave bands into one octave band:

$$L_{oct} = 10 \log_{10}\left( 10^{L_{1/3,1}/10} + 10^{L_{1/3,2}/10} + 10^{L_{1/3,3}/10} \right)$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_2 = 2 f_1$ | Octave ratio condition | Hz |
| 2 | $f_c = \sqrt{f_1 \cdot f_2}$ | Geometric center frequency | Hz |
| 3 | $f_1 = f_c / \sqrt{2}$ | Lower cutoff | Hz |
| 4 | $f_2 = f_c \cdot \sqrt{2}$ | Upper cutoff | Hz |
| 5 | $\Delta f = f_2 - f_1 = f_c / \sqrt{2}$ | Bandwidth | Hz |
| 6 | $\Delta f / f_c = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 70.71\%$ | Constant % bandwidth | dimensionless |
| 7 | $f_2 / f_1 = 2^{1/n}$ | General $1/n$-octave ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $f_c(n,k) = 1000 \cdot 2^{k/n}$ | Band center formula | Hz |
| 9 | $L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | Energy summation | dB |
| 10 | $L_{total} \approx L_{max} + 10\log_{10}(N)$ | Equal-band approximation | dB |
| 11 | $L_{A,i} = L_i + \Delta A_i$ | A-weighting per band | dB(A) |
| 12 | $L_A = 10\log_{10}\left(\sum 10^{L_{A,i}/10}\right)$ | A-weighted overall | dB(A) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A-Weighting Correction Values** ($\Delta A_i$) for standard octave bands (IEC 61672):

| $f_c$ (Hz) | 31.5 | 63 | 125 | 250 | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 | 8000 | 16000 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\Delta A$ (dB) | $-39.4$ | $-26.2$ | $-16.1$ | $-8.6$ | $-3.2$ | $0$ | $+1.2$ | $+1.0$ | $-1.1$ | $-6.6$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial noise control practice, octave band spectra are indispensable for:

- **Fan/Compressor Noise Diagnosis:** The blade pass frequency (BPF) appears as a sharp peak in a specific octave band, immediately guiding damping/resonator design.
- **HVAC Duct Silencer (Muffler) Sizing:** Manufacturers publish insertion loss (IL) curves *vs. octave band*, allowing the engineer to "read off" performance at each band of interest.
- **Hearing Damage Risk Assessment:** OSHA / NIOSH standards apply frequency-dependent corrections to convert measured SPLs to equivalent A-weighted exposure.
- **Building Acoustic Compliance:** Reverberation time $RT_{60}$ is computed and tabulated for each octave band (ISO 3382).
- **Environmental Impact Assessment:** Indian CPCB/EPA noise limits are specified in octave bands for different zones.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Center Frequency of an Octave Band

**Objective:** Prove that the center frequency $f_c$ equals the geometric mean of the cutoff frequencies.

**Given:**
- Lower cutoff: $f_1$
- Upper cutoff: $f_2$
- Octave condition: $f_2 = 2 f_1$

**Step 1:** Express the average frequency arithmetically and geometrically.

$$f_{arith} = \frac{f_1 + f_2}{2}, \qquad f_{geom} = \sqrt{f_1 \cdot f_2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $f_2 = 2 f_1$ into the geometric mean expression.

$$\begin{aligned}
f_c &= \sqrt{f_1 \cdot (2 f_1)} \\
&= \sqrt{2 \cdot f_1^2} \\
&= f_1 \sqrt{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Show the equivalent expression in terms of $f_2$.

$$\begin{aligned}
f_c &= \sqrt{f_1 \cdot f_2} = \sqrt{\frac{f_2}{2} \cdot f_2} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{f_2^2}{2}} = \frac{f_2}{\sqrt{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Verify by taking the ratio with the lower cutoff.

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_c}{f_1} &= \frac{f_1 \sqrt{2}}{f_1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142 \\
\frac{f_2}{f_c} &= \frac{f_1 \cdot 2}{f_1 \sqrt{2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The center frequency is exactly equidistant (in a logarithmic / multiplicative sense) from both cutoffs. The frequency $f_c$ lies $\sqrt{2}$ above $f_1$ and $\sqrt{2}$ below $f_2$. **This logarithmic symmetry is the reason human auditory perception aligns with octave band centers.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Constant Percentage Bandwidth

**Objective:** Show $\Delta f / f_c = 1/\sqrt{2}$ for any octave band.

**Step 1:** Define bandwidth.

$$\Delta f = f_2 - f_1$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the octave condition $f_2 = 2 f_1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta f &= 2 f_1 - f_1 = f_1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Write $f_1$ in terms of $f_c$.

From Step 3 of §3.1: $f_1 = f_c / \sqrt{2}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute into bandwidth ratio.

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta f}{f_c} &= \frac{f_1}{f_c} = \frac{f_c / \sqrt{2}}{f_c} \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\
&\approx 0.70711
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The bandwidth is **70.71% of the center frequency**, identically, for every octave band.

## 3.3 Derivation: General Fractional-Octave Center Frequencies

**Objective:** Derive a closed-form expression for the $k$-th center frequency in a $1/n$-octave filter set.

**Step 1:** For successive bands in a $1/n$-octave sequence:

$$\frac{f_{c,k+1}}{f_{c,k}} = 2^{1/n}$$

**Step 2:** Unroll this recurrence starting from a reference frequency $f_{c,0}$ (typically 1000 Hz):

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{c,1} &= f_{c,0} \cdot 2^{1/n} \\
f_{c,2} &= f_{c,1} \cdot 2^{1/n} = f_{c,0} \cdot 2^{2/n} \\
f_{c,k} &= f_{c,0} \cdot 2^{k/n}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** For 1/3-octave ($n = 3$):

$$f_{c,k} = 1000 \cdot 2^{k/3} \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 (Verification):** Compute the band containing 1000 Hz when $k = 0$:

$$f_{c,0} = 1000 \cdot 2^{0} = 1000 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 (Verification):** The next 1/3-octave band above 1000 Hz:

$$f_{c,1} = 1000 \cdot 2^{1/3} = 1000 \cdot 1.2599 \approx 1259.9 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** This is the standard formula used by all sound-level meter software to label 1/3-octave spectra.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Computing Cutoff Frequencies

**Problem:** A 500 Hz octave band filter is used. Compute the lower cutoff, upper cutoff, and bandwidth.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_1 &= \frac{f_c}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{500}{1.4142} \\
&= 353.55 \text{ Hz} \\
\\
f_2 &= f_c \cdot \sqrt{2} = 500 \times 1.4142 \\
&= 707.11 \text{ Hz} \\
\\
\Delta f &= f_2 - f_1 = 707.11 - 353.55 \\
&= 353.55 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification:** $\Delta f / f_c = 353.55 / 500 = 0.7071$ ✓

**Also verify octave ratio:**

$$f_2 / f_1 = 707.11 / 353.55 = 2.000 \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Energy Summation Across Bands

**Problem:** A machine has the following octave band SPLs (125 Hz to 4000 Hz):

| Band (Hz) | 125 | 250 | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SPL (dB) | 78 | 82 | 85 | 80 | 75 | 70 |

**Find:** Overall SPL (unweighted).

**Solution:** Convert each dB value to linear power ratio, sum, then convert back.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{78/10} + 10^{82/10} + 10^{85/10} + 10^{80/10} + 10^{75/10} + 10^{70/10}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{7.8} + 10^{8.2} + 10^{8.5} + 10^{8.0} + 10^{7.5} + 10^{7.0}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(6.31\times10^{7} + 1.585\times10^{8} + 3.162\times10^{8} + 1.0\times10^{8} + 3.162\times10^{7} + 1.0\times10^{7}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(6.793\times10^{8}\right) \\
&= 10 \times 8.832 \\
&= 88.32 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Insight:** The 500 Hz band dominates (largest single contribution). Adding all six bands yields only **3.32 dB** above the loudest band — this is the typical "diminishing returns" of energy addition.

**Alternative (approximate) method:**

| Step | Bands combined | Resulting SPL |
|---|---|---|
| Combine 70 + 75 (difference 5 dB) | | 76.2 dB |
| Combine 76.2 + 78 (difference 1.8 dB) | | 79.7 dB |
| Combine 79.7 + 80 (difference 0.3 dB) | | 82.9 dB |
| Combine 82.9 + 82 (difference 0.9 dB) | | 85.4 dB |
| Combine 85.4 + 85 (difference 0.4 dB) | | **88.2 dB** ✓ |

(Slight rounding difference confirms the exact result.)

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Applying A-Weighting per Band

**Problem:** Using the SPL values from §3.5, compute the **A-weighted overall SPL**.

**Solution:** Apply A-weighting correction to each band, then sum.

| $f_c$ (Hz) | SPL (dB) | $\Delta A$ (dB) | $L_A$ (dB(A)) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 125 | 78 | $-16.1$ | 61.9 |
| 250 | 82 | $-8.6$ | 73.4 |
| 500 | 85 | $-3.2$ | 81.8 |
| 1000 | 80 | $0$ | 80.0 |
| 2000 | 75 | $+1.2$ | 76.2 |
| 4000 | 70 | $+1.0$ | 71.0 |

$$\begin{aligned}
L_A &= 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{6.19} + 10^{7.34} + 10^{8.18} + 10^{8.00} + 10^{7.62} + 10^{7.10}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(1.549\times10^{6} + 2.188\times10^{7} + 1.514\times10^{8} + 1.0\times10^{8} + 4.169\times10^{7} + 1.259\times10^{7}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(3.327\times10^{8}\right) \\
&= 85.22 \text{ dB(A)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Insight:** The A-weighted overall (85.2 dB(A)) is **3.1 dB lower** than the linear overall (88.3 dB) because the loudest bands (125, 250 Hz) are heavily attenuated by A-weighting (the ear is less sensitive to low frequencies).

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example 4 — Combining Three 1/3-Octave Bands into One Octave

**Problem:** 1/3-octave SPLs are: $L_1 = 84$ dB, $L_2 = 87$ dB, $L_3 = 83$ dB. Find the equivalent octave-band SPL.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{oct} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{8.4} + 10^{8.7} + 10^{8.3}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(2.512\times10^{8} + 5.012\times10^{8} + 1.995\times10^{8}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(9.519\times10^{8}\right) \\
&= 89.79 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Insight:** Combining three contiguous 1/3-octave bands into one octave adds at most ~3 dB (if all equal). Here, due to unequal contributions, only ~2.8 dB is added beyond the dominant band.

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix: Common Pitfalls in KTU-Style Problems

| Scenario | Common Mistake | Correct Approach |
|---|---|---|
| "Find center frequency" with $f_1, f_2$ given | Using arithmetic mean $(f_1+f_2)/2$ | Use **geometric mean** $\sqrt{f_1 f_2}$ |
| Summing band levels | Arithmetic averaging | Energy summation $10 \log_{10}\sum 10^{L/10}$ |
| Converting 1/3-octave to octave | Simple arithmetic average | Energy summation of three adjacent 1/3-octave bands |
| A-weighting | Apply A-weighting to overall SPL | Apply **band-by-band** then sum |
| Number of bands between 31.5 Hz and 16 kHz | 5 or 6 (incorrect) | Exactly **10** (doubling sequence) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** Define **octave band**. List the **ten standard octave band center frequencies** as per ISO 266.

**Model Answer:**

An **octave band** is a frequency band whose upper cutoff frequency is exactly **twice** its lower cutoff frequency: $f_2 = 2 f_1$. The center frequency is the geometric mean $f_c = \sqrt{f_1 f_2}$.

The ten standard ISO 266 octave band center frequencies are:

$$31.5,\ 63,\ 125,\ 250,\ 500,\ 1000,\ 2000,\ 4000,\ 8000,\ 16000 \text{ Hz}$$

Each successive band has double the center frequency of the previous one.

---

### Question 2

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** Explain why **constant percentage bandwidth filters (octave bands)** are preferred over **constant bandwidth filters** for acoustic measurements.

**Model Answer:**

The human auditory system perceives pitch **logarithmically**, not linearly. A just-noticeable frequency difference (JND) of ~0.6% (Weber's law) holds across the audio range. Constant percentage bandwidth filters preserve this perceptual scaling because each octave band spans the **same proportional frequency range**.

In contrast, a constant-bandwidth filter (e.g., 100 Hz wide) provides fine resolution at low frequencies (where it is not needed perceptually) but poor resolution at high frequencies (where it is most needed for the ear). Hence, octave and fractional-octave filters are used to produce spectra that match human perception, enabling meaningful engineering judgments about loudness, annoyance, and hearing damage risk.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – Past Year Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the relationship between the **center frequency**, **lower cutoff**, and **upper cutoff** of an octave band. Show that the fractional bandwidth is $\Delta f / f_c = 1/\sqrt{2}$.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] The octave band SPLs of an air-handling unit measured at a workstation are:

| Band (Hz) | 63 | 125 | 250 | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SPL (dB) | 74 | 78 | 82 | 86 | 81 | 75 | 69 |

> Compute (i) the overall unweighted SPL, (ii) the A-weighted overall SPL.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Octave condition:** For an octave band, by definition:

$$f_2 = 2 f_1$$

**Step 2 — Geometric mean definition:**

$$f_c = \sqrt{f_1 f_2} = \sqrt{f_1 \cdot 2 f_1} = f_1 \sqrt{2}$$

**Step 3 — Express $f_1$ and $f_2$ in terms of $f_c$:**

$$f_1 = \frac{f_c}{\sqrt{2}}, \qquad f_2 = f_c \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bandwidth:**

$$\Delta f = f_2 - f_1 = f_c \sqrt{2} - \frac{f_c}{\sqrt{2}} = f_c \left(\sqrt{2} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{f_c}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Fractional bandwidth:**

$$\frac{\Delta f}{f_c} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$$

This proves that **every octave band has identical fractional bandwidth of 70.71%**, regardless of its center frequency.

**Verification with example:** For the 1000 Hz octave band, $f_1 = 707.1$ Hz, $f_2 = 1414.2$ Hz, $\Delta f = 707.1$ Hz. Ratio: $707.1 / 1000 = 0.7071$. ✓

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]

**(i) Overall unweighted SPL:**

Convert each dB to linear and sum:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{7.4} + 10^{7.8} + 10^{8.2} + 10^{8.6} + 10^{8.1} + 10^{7.5} + 10^{6.9}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(2.512\times10^{7} + 6.310\times10^{7} + 1.585\times10^{8} + 3.981\times10^{8} + 1.259\times10^{8} + 3.162\times10^{7} + 7.943\times10^{6}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(9.094\times10^{8}\right) \\
&= 89.59 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) A-weighted SPL:**

Apply A-weighting corrections per band:

| $f_c$ (Hz) | SPL (dB) | $\Delta A$ (dB) | $L_A$ (dB(A)) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 63 | 74 | $-26.2$ | 47.8 |
| 125 | 78 | $-16.1$ | 61.9 |
| 250 | 82 | $-8.6$ | 73.4 |
| 500 | 86 | $-3.2$ | 82.8 |
| 1000 | 81 | $0$ | 81.0 |
| 2000 | 75 | $+1.2$ | 76.2 |
| 4000 | 69 | $+1.0$ | 70.0 |

$$\begin{aligned}
L_A &= 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{4.78} + 10^{6.19} + 10^{7.34} + 10^{8.28} + 10^{8.10} + 10^{7.62} + 10^{7.00}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(6.026\times10^{4} + 1.549\times10^{6} + 2.188\times10^{7} + 1.906\times10^{8} + 1.259\times10^{8} + 4.169\times10^{7} + 1.0\times10^{7}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(3.727\times10^{8}\right) \\
&= 85.71 \text{ dB(A)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Results:**
- Overall unweighted SPL: **89.6 dB**
- A-weighted overall SPL: **85.7 dB(A)**

**Engineering comment:** The A-weighted value is **~3.9 dB lower** than the linear overall, dominated by the 500 Hz band. To meet a typical office limit of 55 dB(A), the AHU requires ~30 dB of broadband attenuation, achievable with a lined duct section plus an absorber-lined plenum.

---

### **Question B** (Choice 2 — Alternative)

> **(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **principle of octave band analysis**. List its **advantages** over broadband (overall SPL) measurements.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Three **1/3-octave bands** centered at **800, 1000, and 1250 Hz** have SPLs of **83 dB, 87 dB, and 85 dB** respectively. Determine the SPL of the equivalent **octave band** centered at 1000 Hz. Comment on the difference between **arithmetic average** and **energy summation**.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Principle:** Octave band analysis decomposes a complex sound signal into a set of band-limited components, each covering a frequency interval of $f_2 = 2 f_1$. The SPL is measured independently in each band using parallel analog bandpass filters or, in modern digital analyzers, an FFT followed by band integration.

**Block Diagram (text-based description):**

$$\text{Microphone} \to \text{Preamplifier} \to \text{Filter Bank} \to \text{RMS Detector} \to \text{Display}$$

The filter bank consists of parallel bandpass filters, each centered at a standard ISO frequency (31.5, 63, 125, ..., 16000 Hz), each passing only the band $f_1 \le f \le f_2$ and rejecting all other frequencies.

**Advantages over broadband measurements:**

1. **Source Identification** — Identifies dominant tonal frequencies (peaks in spectrum).
2. **Targeted Treatment** — Allows selection of frequency-specific absorbers/reactors.
3. **Hearing Damage Assessment** — A-weighting applied band-by-band.
4. **Compliance Verification** — Regulatory limits are specified in octave bands.
5. **Diagnostic Power** — Distinguishes tonal, broadband, and impulsive noise components.
6. **Design Feedback** — Predicts transmission loss / insertion loss of silencers.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Verify the three 1/3-octave bands combine into the 1000 Hz octave band:**

For 1/3-octave, ratio between successive center frequencies is $2^{1/3} = 1.2599$.
- Band 1: 800 Hz
- Band 2: $800 \times 1.2599 \approx 1008 \approx 1000$ Hz ✓
- Band 3: $1000 \times 1.2599 \approx 1259.9 \approx 1250$ Hz ✓

So the three given bands **do** constitute the 1000 Hz octave band.

**Step 2 — Energy summation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{oct} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{8.3} + 10^{8.7} + 10^{8.5}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(1.995\times10^{8} + 5.012\times10^{8} + 3.162\times10^{8}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(1.0169\times10^{9}\right) \\
&= 90.07 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with arithmetic mean:**

$$L_{arith} = \frac{83 + 87 + 85}{3} = 85.0 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Difference:**

$$\Delta L = 90.07 - 85.0 = 5.07 \text{ dB}$$

**Comment:** The arithmetic average (85.0 dB) **underestimates** the true octave-band SPL by over 5 dB. This is because acoustic energy is proportional to **sound pressure squared (intensity)**, not to the dB value itself. Energy summation using $10 \log_{10}\sum 10^{L/10}$ is the **only correct method** for combining band SPLs. Arithmetic averaging is **never** to be used in acoustics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
