Mixed Hearing Loss - **Site:** Combination of CHL + SNHL in the same ear. - **Audiogram Signature:** Elevated BC + an additional air–bone gap. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The **Notch at 4000 Hz** is the *hallmark* audiometric signature of occupational NIHL. The 6 kHz notch is also common in early-stage NIHL, but the **4 kHz dip** is the classic textbook pattern, attributed to: > - Mechanical resonance of the ear canal (~2.5–4 kHz amplification zone) > - Vulnerability of Outer Hair Cells (OHCs) in the **basal turn** of the cochlea (high-frequency region). ### 2.2 Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL) — The Industrial Perspective NIHL is the most prevalent occupational disease worldwide and is the **central focus** of PEMET752 Module 3. #### 2.2.1 Two Phases of NIHL | Phase | Full Name | Reversibility | Onset | Audiometric Pattern | |:-----:|:---------:|:-------------:|:-----:|:--------------------| | **TTS** | Temporary Threshold Shift | Reversible (within hours–days of rest) | After short, loud exposure | Broad, flat shift | | **PTS** | Permanent Threshold Shift | **Irreversible** | After repeated or traumatic exposure | **4 kHz notch** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap:** A student must *never* use "TTS" and "PTS" interchangeably. TTS precedes PTS in chronic exposure — repeated TTS without adequate recovery time eventually "burns in" as a PTS. This is the basis of the **equal-energy hypothesis**. #### 2.2.2 Damage Risk Criteria (DRC) A *Damage Risk Criterion* specifies the **combination of noise level (L) and exposure duration (T)** that will not cause measurable PTS in a specified percentage of the exposed population. ##### The OSHA Standard (US, 29 CFR 1910.95) $$L_{OSHA} = 90 + 16 \log_{10}\left(\frac{T}{8}\right)$$ - **PEL (Permissible Exposure Limit):** **90 dBA** for **8 hours**. - **Exchange Rate:** **5 dB** — every **+5 dB** halves allowable exposure time. - **Action Level:** **85 dBA** (triggers hearing conservation program). ##### The NIOSH / ISO 1999 Recommendation $$T_{allowed} = \frac{8}{2^{(L-85)/3}}$$ - **REL (Recommended Exposure Limit):** **85 dBA** for **8 hours**. - **Exchange Rate:** **3 dB** (equal-energy principle) — every **+3 dB** halves allowable exposure time. - **Criterion Level (L_C):** **85 dBA** (ISO 1999:2013 uses 85 dBA as the reference). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table compiles **all KTU-tested equations** for hearing-loss assessment. Memorize the units carefully — examiner frequently deducts marks for unit inconsistency. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |:-:|:---------|:--------|:------|:------| | 1 | Hearing Level reference | $0\ \text{dB HL} \equiv 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | Pa | ISO 8253-1 | | 2 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | dB SPL | $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | | 3 | OSHA Allowable Time | $T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}$ | hours | 5 dB exchange rate | | 4 | NIOSH Allowable Time | $T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-85)/3}}$ | hours | 3 dB exchange rate | | 5 | OSHA Dose (5 dB ER) | $D\% = \dfrac{C_1}{T_1} \times 100$ | % | $C_1$ = actual time, $T_1$ = allowed time | | 6 | NIOSH Dose (3 dB ER) | $D\% = 100 \sum_{i} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | % | Summation over all tasks | | 7 | Time-Weighted Average | $TWA = 16.61 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{100}\right) + 90$ | dBA | OSHA (90 dB criterion) | | 8 | NIOSH TWA | $TWA = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{100}\right) + 85$ | dBA | NIOSH (85 dB criterion) | | 9 | Equivalent SPL ($L_{eq}$) | $L_{eq} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\sum_i 10^{L_i/10} \cdot t_i\right)$ | dB | Energy-average level | | 10 | OSHA Relative Level | $L_{OSHA} = 90 + 16\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{T}{8}\right)$ | dBA | Solve for level given time | | 11 | Exposure Reduction Factor | $NRR_{adj} = NRR - 7$ | dB | OSHA derating for field use | ### 2.4 Engineering & Industrial Utility - **Workplace Noise Surveys:** Use of sound-level meters (Type 1/2 IEC 61672) and personal noise dosimeters to compute TWA. - **Audiometric Surveillance Programs:** Periodic pure-tone audiometry (annual) for workers exposed above the Action Level (**85 dBA OSHA / 85 dBA NIOSH**). - **Engineering Control Hierarchy:** *Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls (enclosures, damping, isolation) → Administrative Controls (job rotation, limiting shift) → PPE (earplugs, earmuffs)*. - **Hearing Protection Devices (HPD):** Earmuffs (NRR 20–30 dB), earplugs (NRR 25–33 dB), and double protection (>35 dB combined) for exposures ≥100 dBA. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The OSHA 5 dB Exchange Rate Formula The OSHA standard is built on the observation that for every **5 dB** increase in noise level above **90 dBA**, the permissible exposure time is **halved**. **Step 1 — State the doubling principle.** $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = 2 \quad \text{when} \quad L_2 - L_1 = 5\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 2 — Express the relationship in logarithmic form.** The ratio $T_1/T_2$ can be written as: $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{L_2 - L_1}{k}$$ where $k$ is the exchange-rate denominator. For OSHA, $k = 16$ (derived below). **Step 3 — Solve for k using the anchor point.** At $L_1 = 90$ dBA, $T_1 = 8$ hours. At $L_2 = 95$ dBA, $T_2 = 4$ hours. $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{8}{4}\right) = \frac{95 - 90}{k} \implies \log_{10}(2) = \frac{5}{k}$$ $$\implies k = \frac{5}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{5}{0.30103} \approx 16.61$$ **Step 4 — Invert to get the canonical OSHA equation.** $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) = \frac{L_1 - L_2}{16.61}$$ Rearranging with $L_1 = 90$, $T_1 = 8$, and renaming $T_2 \to T$, $L_2 \to L$: $$\boxed{\,L = 90 + 16.61 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T}{8}\right)\,}$$ (OSHA rounds the 16.61 → 16 in its published tables, but the exact value **16.61** is required for KTU derivations.) **Step 5 — Solve the canonical inversion.** For computing allowable exposure duration $T$ given noise level $L$: $$\frac{L - 90}{16.61} = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T}{8}\right)$$ $$\frac{T}{8} = 10^{(L-90)/16.61}$$ $$\boxed{\,T = \dfrac{8}{10^{(L-90)/16.61}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}\,}$$ The final form uses $2^{(L-90)/5}$ because $10^{x/16.61} = 2^{x/5}$ (since $\log_{10} 2 \approx 0.30103 = 1/3.3219$ and $16.61/5 \approx 3.32$). --- ### 3.2 Derivation: The NIOSH / ISO 1999 Equal-Energy Formula The 3 dB exchange rate follows from the **equal-energy hypothesis** — doubling sound energy doubles hearing hazard, and energy doubles for every 3 dB increase. **Step 1 — Equal-energy hypothesis:** $$E = p^2 \cdot t = \text{constant at threshold}$$ Since $L_p \propto 10\log_{10}(p^2/p_0^2)$, doubling $p^2$ adds 3 dB. **Step 2 — Establish the 3 dB rule:** $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = 2 \quad \text{when} \quad L_2 - L_1 = 3\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 3 — Solve with anchor $L = 85$ dBA, $T = 8$ h:** $$\boxed{\,T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-85)/3}}\,}$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Style]** > A worker is exposed to the following noise levels throughout an 8-hour shift: > - **Task A:** 95 dBA for 2 hours > - **Task B:** 88 dBA for 3 hours > - **Task C:** 82 dBA for 3 hours > > Using the **NIOSH 3 dB exchange rate** (criterion = 85 dBA), determine: > (a) The OSHA 5-dB-based dose and corresponding TWA. > (b) The NIOSH 3-dB-based dose and corresponding TWA. > (c) Whether the exposure exceeds either standard. #### Solution — Part (a): OSHA 5 dB Dose **Step 1 — Compute OSHA-allowable times $T_i$ for each task:** $$T_A = \frac{8}{2^{(95-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^1} = 4\ \text{h}$$ $$T_B = \frac{8}{2^{(88-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{-0.4}} = 8 \times 2^{0.4} \approx 8 \times 1.3195 = 10.556\ \text{h}$$ $$T_C = \frac{8}{2^{(82-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{-1.6}} = 8 \times 2^{1.6} \approx 8 \times 3.0314 = 24.252\ \text{h}$$ **Step 2 — Compute partial doses $C_i/T_i$:** $$D_A = \frac{2}{4} = 0.500$$ $$D_B = \frac{3}{10.556} \approx 0.284$$ $$D_C = \frac{3}{24.252} \approx 0.124$$ **Step 3 — Sum to get total OSHA dose:** $$D_{OSHA} = 0.500 + 0.284 + 0.124 = 0.908 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{90.8\%}$$ **Step 4 — Compute OSHA TWA (criterion = 90 dB):** $$TWA_{OSHA} = 16.61 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{90.8}{100}\right) + 90$$ $$= 16.61 \times \log_{10}(0.908) + 90$$ $$= 16.61 \times (-0.04194) + 90$$ $$= -0.697 + 90 = \mathbf{89.30\ \text{dBA}}$$ > **Verdict (OSHA):** Dose 90.8% < 100% → **Within OSHA PEL**. ✓ --- #### Solution — Part (b): NIOSH 3 dB Dose **Step 1 — Compute NIOSH-allowable times:** $$T_A = \frac{8}{2^{(95-85)/3}} = \frac{8}{2^{10/3}} = \frac{8}{2^{3.333}} = \frac{8}{10.079} \approx 0.7937\ \text{h}$$ $$T_B = \frac{8}{2^{(88-85)/3}} = \frac{8}{2^{1}} = 4\ \text{h}$$ $$T_C = \frac{8}{2^{(82-85)/3}} = \frac{8}{2^{-1}} = 16\ \text{h}$$ **Step 2 — Compute partial doses:** $$D_A = \frac{2}{0.7937} = 2.520$$ $$D_B = \frac{3}{4} = 0.750$$ $$D_C = \frac{3}{16} = 0.1875$$ **Step 3 — Sum:** $$D_{NIOSH} = 2.520 + 0.750 + 0.1875 = 3.458 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{345.8\%}$$ **Step 4 — Compute NIOSH TWA (criterion = 85 dB):** $$TWA_{NIOSH} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{345.8}{100}\right) + 85$$ $$= 10 \times \log_{10}(3.458) + 85$$ $$= 10 \times 0.5390 + 85 = 5.390 + 85 = \mathbf{90.39\ \text{dBA}}$$ > **Verdict (NIOSH):** Dose 345.8% > 100% → **EXCEEDS NIOSH REL**. ✗ --- #### Solution — Part (c): Conclusion - Under the more conservative **NIOSH/ISO** criterion, the worker is **overexposed** (D = 346%) and requires immediate engineering/administrative controls. - The **OSHA** standard allows the same exposure legally, demonstrating the **regulatory discrepancy** that KTU examiners frequently test. - **Recommended Action:** Reduce $T_A$ or implement hearing protection with NRR ≥ 10 dB. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Standard | Criterion | OSHA (US) | NIOSH / ISO 1999 (Global) | |:---------:|:---------:|:-------------------------:| | Criterion Level | 90 dBA | 85 dBA | | Exchange Rate | 5 dB | 3 dB | | Action Level | 85 dBA | 80 dBA | | TWA Reference | 90 dBA | 85 dBA | | Dose 100% | 90 dBA × 8 h | 85 dBA × 8 h | | Conservativeness | More permissive | **More protective** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** India follows the **Factories Act 1948** (Model Rules under Schedule VI) which prescribes **90 dBA** for 8 hours (5 dB ER). However, the **Indian National Building Code (NBC 2016)** and **Ministry of Labour recommendations** are gradually shifting toward the **85 dBA / 3 dB** ISO-aligned limits. Always **state both** in a KTU answer and justify the choice. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Figure 1 — Functional Architecture of the Human Auditory System The diagram below maps the **signal-flow pathway** of sound from the external environment to the auditory cortex, with the three hearing-loss types overlaid at the relevant anatomical blocks. ```mermaid flowchart LR src["SOUND SOURCE
Noise Environment"] --> A1["OUTER EAR
Pinna and Ear Canal
Funnel and Amplify"] A1 --> A2["MIDDLE EAR
Tympanic Membrane and Ossicles
Mechanical Impedance Match"] A2 --> A3["INNER EAR
Cochlea and Hair Cells
Mechanical to Electrical Transduction"] A3 --> A4["AUDITORY NERVE
Cranial Nerve VIII
Neural Encoding"] A4 --> A5["BRAIN
Auditory Cortex
Perception and Interpretation"] A1 -. "CHL Cause
Wax Blockage" .-> L1["CONDUCTIVE LOSS"] A2 -. "CHL Cause
Otosclerosis" .-> L1 A3 -. "SNHL Cause
NIHL or Presbycusis" .-> L2["SENSORINEURAL LOSS"] A4 -. "SNHL Cause
Acoustic Neuroma" .-> L2 A3 -. "MIXED Cause
Multiple Pathologies" .-> L3["MIXED LOSS"] A3 -. "Hair Cell Damage at 4 kHz
4 kHz Audiometric Notch" .-> P1["PTS at 4000 Hz"] A3 -. "Temporary Fatigue
Recoverable" .-> P2["TTS Reversible Shift"] style src fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A2 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A3 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A4 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A5 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333,color:#000 style L1 fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style L2 fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style L3 fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style P1 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#333,color:#000 style P2 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Figure 2 — Hierarchy of Noise Control & Hearing Conservation ```mermaid flowchart TB HV["NOISE HAZARD
Exceeds 85 dBA"] --> H1 H1["HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS"] --> H2["ELIMINATION
Remove the Source"] H1 --> H3["SUBSTITUTION
Quieter Process"] H1 --> H4["ENGINEERING CONTROLS
Enclosures, Damping, Isolation"] H1 --> H5["ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS
Job Rotation and Shift Limits"] H1 --> H6["PERSONAL PROTECTION
Earplugs and Earmuffs"] H6 --> H7["HPD SELECTION
Target NRR = SPL minus 85"] H2 --> MON["MONITORING and AUDIOMETRY"] H3 --> MON H4 --> MON H5 --> MON H6 --> MON H7 --> MON MON --> OUT1["Annual Pure Tone Audiogram"] MON --> OUT2["Standard Threshold Shift STS"] MON --> OUT3["Recordable on OSHA 300 Log"] style HV fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#333,color:#000 style H1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333,color:#000 style MON fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#333,color:#000 style OUT1 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333,color:#000 style OUT2 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333,color:#000 style OUT3 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Figure 3 — Block Diagram of TTS → PTS Progression ```mermaid flowchart LR A0["DAILY NOISE EXPOSURE
Greater than 85 dBA"] --> A1 A1["ACOUSTIC TRAUMA
Short High Level Impulse"] --> A2["TTS Phase 1
Temporary Shift"] A2 --> A3{"ADEQUATE
RECOVERY?"} A3 -- Yes --> A4["RECOVERY
Threshold Returns to Baseline"] A3 -- No --> A5["TTS Phase 2
Compounding Shift"] A5 --> A6["REPEATED TTS CYCLES
Over Months and Years"] A6 --> A7["HAIR CELL DEATH
Irreversible"] A7 --> A8["PTS PHASE
4 kHz Notch on Audiogram"] A8 --> A9["CLINICAL NIHL
Permanent Disability"] style A0 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A2 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A5 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A7 fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A8 fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A9 fill:#991b1b,stroke:#fff,color:#000 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style | Module 3 | 3 Marks Each]** **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Conductive Hearing Loss (CHL)** and **Sensorineural Hearing Loss (SNHL)**. Mention **two** distinguishing audiometric features for each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | CHL | SNHL | |:--------|:----|:-----| | **Site of Lesion** | Outer / Middle ear (mechanical obstruction) | Inner ear / Auditory nerve / CNS (neural or sensory) | | **Reversibility** | Often reversible (medical/surgical) | Usually **permanent** | | **Audiogram Sign** | **Air–bone gap** ≥ 15 dB; BC normal | **No air–bone gap**; both AC and BC equally elevated | | **Bone Conduction** | **Normal** | **Elevated** | | **Typical Causes** | Wax, otitis media, otosclerosis | NIHL, presbycusis, ototoxicity | | **Speech Discrimination** | Preserved if loud enough | **Impaired** even when amplified | **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark each for any correct distinguishing pair (table form preferred for full marks). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Explain the **4 kHz notch** observed in occupational noise-induced hearing loss. Why is **4000 Hz** specifically affected? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The 4 kHz audiometric notch is the **hallmark signature** of occupational NIHL, characterized by a sharp dip in hearing threshold at 3000–6000 Hz with relative preservation at lower frequencies. **Reasons for 4 kHz vulnerability:** 1. **Acoustic resonance of the external ear canal** produces a natural amplification peak between 2500–4000 Hz, intensifying the SPL reaching the tympanic membrane by ~10–15 dB. **(1 mark)** 2. **Cochlear tonotopic organization** — the **basal turn** of the cochlea (which encodes high frequencies ~3000–6000 Hz) is structurally the most fragile region, receiving the highest mechanical stress and metabolic load. The **outer hair cells (OHCs)** here are the first to undergo apoptosis under noise trauma. **(1 mark)** 3. **Limited blood supply** to the basal turn (watershed zone of the cochlear artery) makes these hair cells more vulnerable to metabolic exhaustion from sustained noise exposure. **(1 mark)** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Valuation Warning:** Students often confuse the 4 kHz notch with the **6 kHz notch**. The classic **"Boilermaker's Notch"** is at **4000 Hz with recovery at 8000 Hz**. Simply writing "high-frequency loss" without specifying the notch and its recovery pattern loses 1 mark. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — Choose ONE) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *OSHA / Damage Risk Criteria Focus* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]** **(a)** Derive the OSHA permissible exposure duration formula $$T = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}$$ starting from the 5 dB exchange rate and the anchor point (90 dBA, 8 hours). **(7 marks)** **(b)** A factory worker has the following daily noise exposure: | Task | Sound Level (dBA) | Duration (hours) | |:----:|:-----------------:|:----------------:| | A | 102 | 1.0 | | B | 94 | 2.5 | | C | 88 | 4.0 | Calculate the **OSHA dose (%)** and the **corresponding TWA**. State whether the exposure complies with the OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL). **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution — Q.A(a): Derivation (7 marks)** **Step 1 — State the OSHA hypothesis** *(1 mark)* > The OSHA standard adopts a **5 dB exchange rate** with a **90 dBA / 8-hour** anchor point. Every **+5 dB** increase halves the permissible exposure time. $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = 2 \quad \text{when} \quad L_2 - L_1 = 5\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 2 — Apply logarithms to linearize** *(2 marks)* $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{L_2 - L_1}{k}$$ Substitute $T_1 = 8$ h, $L_1 = 90$ dBA, $T_2 = 4$ h, $L_2 = 95$ dBA: $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{8}{4}\right) = \frac{95 - 90}{k} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \log_{10}(2) = \frac{5}{k}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for k** *(1 mark)* $$k = \frac{5}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{5}{0.30103} = 16.61$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange for T given L** *(2 marks)* $$\frac{L - 90}{16.61} = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T}{8}\right)$$ $$10^{(L-90)/16.61} = \frac{T}{8}$$ $$T = 8 \cdot 10^{-(L-90)/16.61}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify using $10^{-x/16.61} = 2^{-x/5}$** *(1 mark)* $$\boxed{T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}}$$ --- ##### **Model Solution — Q.A(b): Dose Calculation (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Compute OSHA-allowed times** *(3 marks)* $$T_A = \frac{8}{2^{(102-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{2.4}} = \frac{8}{5.278} = 1.516\ \text{h}$$ $$T_B = \frac{8}{2^{(94-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{0.8}} = \frac{8}{1.7411} = 4.595\ \text{h}$$ $$T_C = \frac{8}{2^{(88-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{-0.4}} = 8 \times 2^{0.4} = 10.556\ \text{h}$$ **Step 2 — Compute partial dose fractions** *(2 marks)* $$\frac{C_A}{T_A} = \frac{1.0}{1.516} = 0.6597$$ $$\frac{C_B}{T_B} = \frac{2.5}{4.595} = 0.5441$$ $$\frac{C_C}{T_C} = \frac{4.0}{10.556} = 0.3789$$ **Step 3 — Total OSHA Dose** *(1 mark)* $$D_{OSHA} = 0.6597 + 0.5441 + 0.3789 = 1.5827 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{158.27\%}$$ **Step 4 — OSHA TWA** *(1 mark)* $$TWA = 16.61 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{158.27}{100}\right) + 90 = 16.61 \times 0.1994 + 90 = \mathbf{93.31\ dBA}$$ **Conclusion (OSHA):** Since $D_{OSHA} = 158.3\% > 100\%$, the exposure **EXCEEDS the OSHA PEL**. Mandatory engineering/administrative controls and hearing protection are required immediately. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *NIOSH / Audiometric / Comprehensive Focus* **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]** **(a)** Compare the **OSHA** and **NIOSH** damage-risk criteria. Highlight **any four** key differences in tabular form, and explain why the 3 dB (NIOSH) exchange rate is considered more physiologically accurate. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 35-year-old machine operator has worked in a textile mill for 10 years. His recent audiogram shows bilateral sensorineural hearing loss with thresholds (in dB HL) as given below: | Frequency (Hz) | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 3000 | 4000 | 6000 | 8000 | |:--------------:|:---:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:| | Threshold (dB HL) — Left | 15 | 18 | 22 | 38 | **62** | 48 | 32 | | Threshold (dB HL) — Right | 12 | 16 | 20 | 40 | **65** | 50 | 30 | Identify the audiometric pattern, classify the hearing loss per WHO grading, and recommend an engineering control measure. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution — Q.B(a): OSHA vs NIOSH Comparison (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Tabular comparison** *(4 marks — 1 mark per correct row)* | Parameter | OSHA (29 CFR 1910.95) | NIOSH / ISO 1999 | |:----------|:---------------------|:-----------------| | Criterion Level | **90 dBA** | **85 dBA** | | Exchange Rate | 5 dB | **3 dB** (equal-energy) | | PEL/REL for 8 h | 90 dBA | 85 dBA | | Action Level | 85 dBA | **80 dBA** | | TWA Reference | 90 dBA | 85 dBA | | Dose = 100% means | At PEL exactly | At REL exactly | | Conservativeness | More permissive | **More protective** | **Step 2 — Why 3 dB is physiologically accurate** *(3 marks)* 1. **Equal-Energy Hypothesis:** Laboratory animal and human temporal-bone studies (e.g., Ward, 1991) confirm that equal sound **energy** (proportional to $p^2 t$) produces equal cochlear damage. Since $p^2$ doubles for every **+3 dB**, halving time compensates exactly. The 5 dB rule under-protects because it ignores the non-linear growth of hazard with level. **(1 mark)** 2. **ISO 1999 Database:** Empirical epidemiological data (ISO 1999:2013 Annex E) shows PTS growth closer to 3 dB than 5 dB for long-term industrial exposures. **(1 mark)** 3. **Impulse vs Continuous Noise:** The 5 dB rule was derived in the 1960s from limited data on steady-state noise. Modern research on **impulse/impact noise** (riveting, drop-forging) clearly supports the 3 dB doubling principle. **(1 mark)** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write *"OSHA is wrong"* — this loses marks. The correct framing is **"OSHA reflects a 1968 compromise balancing protection with industrial feasibility; NIOSH reflects modern physiological evidence."** --- ##### **Model Solution — Q.B(b): Audiogram Interpretation (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Identify the pattern** *(2 marks)* The audiogram shows a sharp threshold elevation **peaking at 4000 Hz** (62 dB HL left, 65 dB HL right) with **partial recovery at 8000 Hz** (32 dB HL left, 30 dB HL right) and relatively preserved low frequencies (500–2000 Hz ≤ 22 dB HL). > **Pattern: Classic 4 kHz "Boilermaker's Notch" — diagnostic of Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL).** *(2 marks)* **Step 2 — WHO Grading using the Better Ear (Right)** *(3 marks)* The Right ear thresholds at the four PTA frequencies (500, 1000, 2000, 4000 Hz): $$\text{PTA}_R = \frac{12 + 16 + 20 + 65}{4} = \frac{113}{4} = 28.25\ \text{dB HL}$$ Per WHO classification (Table 1.1): 26–40 dB HL → **Mild Hearing Loss** in the Right ear. *(1 mark)* The Left ear PTA: $$\text{PTA}_L = \frac{15 + 18 + 22 + 62}{4} = \frac{117}{4} = 29.25\ \text{dB HL}$$ → Also **Mild Hearing Loss** in the Left ear. *(1 mark)* **Bilateral classification:** Bilateral **Mild Sensorineural Hearing Loss** with NIHL pattern. *(1 mark)* **Step 3 — Recommended Engineering Controls** *(2 marks)* 1. **Enclosure / Acoustic Hood:** Install noise-isolating enclosures around the loom/shuttle machinery to reduce ambient noise by ≥ 10 dB. *(1 mark)* 2. **Vibration Isolation Mounts & Damping Pads:** Mount rotating machinery on Sorbothane / rubber isolators to reduce structure-borne noise transmission. *(1 mark)* 3. **Job Rotation + Audiometric Surveillance:** Annual pure-tone audiometry with Standard Threshold Shift (STS) tracking; mandatory HPD use (earplugs NRR ≥ 25 dB or double protection for >100 dBA zones). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Quick-Recall Summary for Last-Minute Revision** - ✅ Hearing loss is measured in **dB HL**, referenced to ISO audiometric zero (**0 dB HL = 20 µPa at 1 kHz**). - ✅ Three types: **CHL** (outer/middle ear, reversible, air–bone gap), **SNHL** (inner ear/nerve, permanent, no gap), and **Mixed**. - ✅ **NIHL hallmark = 4 kHz notch** (the "Boilermaker's Notch") due to ear canal resonance (~2.5–4 kHz) + basal cochlear vulnerability. - ✅ **TTS = Temporary** (recoverable in hours–days); **PTS = Permanent** (hair-cell apoptosis, irreversible). - ✅ **OSHA:** 90 dBA / 8 h, **5 dB** exchange rate. - ✅ **NIOSH/ISO 1999:** 85 dBA / 8 h, **3 dB** exchange rate (equal-energy principle). - ✅ Key formulas to memorize: - $T_{OSHA} = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}$ - $T_{NIOSH} = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-85)/3}}$ - $TWA_{OSHA} = 16.61 \log_{10}(D/100) + 90$ - $TWA_{NIOSH} = 10 \log_{10}(D/100) + 85$ - ✅ **Control Hierarchy:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE. - ✅ **HPD derating (OSHA):** $NRR_{adj} = NRR - 7$ for field use; $NRR_{adj} = (NRR - 7) \times 0.5$ to estimate workplace protection. - ✅ **India reference:** Factories Act Schedule VI = **90 dBA / 8 h / 5 dB ER**; modern NBC 2016 trends toward **85 dBA / 3 dB ER**. - ✅ **4 kHz notch recovery at 8 kHz** is the single most distinguishing audiometric sign of NIHL vs Presbycusis (which slopes continuously downward with no notch). ---

topics.title: Masking, Speech Interference Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Masking, Speech Interference Level

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Masking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Masking** is the auditory phenomenon in which the threshold of audibility of one sound (the **masked sound** or **signal**) is raised by the presence of another sound (the **masking sound** or **masker**). In simpler words, a loud sound can make a softer, simultaneous sound inaudible.

Formally, masking is defined as the **process by which the threshold of audibility for one sound is elevated due to the presence of another (masking) sound**. It is a fundamental psychoacoustic property of the human auditory system, governed by the frequency selectivity of the cochlea (the **critical band** mechanism).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to have a quiet conversation with a friend in a library. Suddenly, someone starts a chainsaw outside. The chainsaw completely "drowns out" your friend's voice — even though your friend is still speaking at the same volume. The chainsaw is the **masker**, your friend's voice is the **masked sound**, and the masking threshold has been pushed up so high that the conversation is no longer audible.

In technical terms: the **masking threshold curve** (the minimum sound pressure level required for a tone to be just audible in the presence of a masker) rises in the frequency region near the masker. Any signal whose level falls **below** this raised threshold is inaudible.

### 1.3 Definition of Speech Interference Level (SIL)

> [!NOTE]
> **Speech Interference Level (SIL)** is a single-number acoustic rating developed by **Beranek (1947)** that quantifies the ability of background noise to interfere with speech communication. It is calculated as the arithmetic average of the sound pressure levels measured in the **500 Hz, 1000 Hz, 2000 Hz, and 4000 Hz octave bands** — the four bands most critical for speech intelligibility.

A closely related, more modern variant — the **Preferred Speech Interference Level (PSIL)** — uses only the **500 Hz, 1000 Hz, and 2000 Hz** octave bands and is preferred in current engineering practice because it correlates better with speech intelligibility across a wider range of voice levels.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Reference speech level:** Typical male voice ≈ **69 dB SPL at 1 m**; female voice ≈ **64 dB SPL at 1 m**; raised voice ≈ **78 dB SPL at 1 m**.
- **Critical Speech Level** for intelligible communication: signal-to-noise ratio ≥ **+6 to +10 dB** in the speech bands.
- **Standard octave bands for SIL:** 500, 1000, 2000, 4000 Hz.
- **Standard octave bands for PSIL:** 500, 1000, 2000 Hz.
- **Hearing threshold of the human ear at 1 kHz:** ≈ **0 dB SPL** (4 × 10⁻¹⁰ W/m² reference intensity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **critical bandwidth** of the human ear at 1 kHz is approximately **160 Hz**. Masking is most effective when the masker and signal lie within the same critical band.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Masking

Masking phenomena can be categorized into several classes based on temporal and spectral relationships:

1. **Simultaneous (Frequency) Masking**
   - Both the masker and the masked sound occur at the same instant.
   - The masker and signal are at similar frequencies — the masker renders the signal inaudible.
   - This is the most common type considered in noise control engineering.

2. **Temporal Masking**
   - **Forward Masking:** A loud masker raises the threshold of audibility for a signal presented *after* the masker ceases (typically lasting up to **200 ms**).
   - **Backward Masking:** A signal presented *before* a loud masker is partially masked (effect lasts only up to **20–50 ms**).

3. **Central (Central Auditory) Masking**
   - Masking produced by a sound presented to the **contralateral ear** via neural pathways (rare in practical noise control).

### 2.2 Masking Curves and Critical Band Concept

The masking curve is plotted as **masked threshold (dB SPL) vs frequency**, with the masker level as a parameter.

Key insights:

- **Low-frequency maskers are more effective than high-frequency maskers** at masking frequencies above them. The masking curve is asymmetric, with a steeper slope on the high-frequency side (typically **–20 to –30 dB/octave**) and a gentler slope on the low-frequency side (typically **+20 to +40 dB/octave**).
- The masker is most effective within a **critical bandwidth** around its center frequency.
- Masking is essentially a **frequency-domain phenomenon** governed by the **basilar membrane's mechanical filtering** in the cochlea.

### 2.3 Speech Interference Level — Operational Logic

**Step 1:** Measure the octave-band sound pressure levels of the background noise at the listener's position in the four (SIL) or three (PSIL) octave bands.

**Step 2:** Compute the arithmetic mean:

$$SIL = \frac{L_{500} + L_{1000} + L_{2000} + L_{4000}}{4}$$

**Step 3:** For PSIL, drop the 4000 Hz octave band:

$$PSIL = \frac{L_{500} + L_{1000} + L_{2000}}{3}$$

**Step 4:** Use empirical tables (Beranek tables) to determine the **maximum distance** at which reliable speech communication is possible for a given voice effort (relaxed, normal, raised, very loud, shouting).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Original SIL | $\text{SIL} = \dfrac{L_{500} + L_{1000} + L_{2000} + L_{4000}}{4}$ | dB | Background noise rating for speech |
| Preferred SIL | $\text{PSIL} = \dfrac{L_{500} + L_{1000} + L_{2000}}{3}$ | dB | Modern preferred metric |
| Speech Level at distance d | $L_p(d) = L_p(d_0) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | dB SPL | Spherical spreading |
| Speech Level at distance d (directional) | $L_p(d) = L_p(1\,\text{m}) - 20 \log_{10}(d) - 8$ | dB SPL | Talker-to-listener line |
| Critical Bandwidth (approx.) | $\text{CB} \approx 25 + 75\!\left(1 + 1.4 f_c^2\right)^{0.69}$ | Hz | Zwicker's formula |
| SNR Requirement | $\text{SNR} \geq +6\,\text{dB}$ (acceptable) | dB | Speech intelligibility |
| Masking Slope (high freq) | $\approx -20$ to $-30\,\text{dB/oct}$ | dB/oct | Asymmetry of masking curve |
| Articulation Index | $\text{AI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot S_i$ | dimensionless | Frequency-weighted speech |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** SIL is the cornerstone of **office acoustics, classroom design, HVAC noise control, hospital room design, and industrial communication zoning**. The Beranek tables linking SIL to maximum talker-listener distance are used worldwide to specify noise criteria for spaces where speech intelligibility matters.

### 2.5 Why SIL Works — The Underlying Physical Reasoning

Human speech energy is concentrated in the **500 Hz to 4000 Hz** frequency range. This is where consonant sounds (essential for intelligibility) reside. By averaging the noise levels in these bands, SIL provides a **single number** that correlates with the masking effect the noise will have on speech perception. The masking threshold in these bands determines whether consonants are audible against the noise floor.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Speech SPL at Distance from a Point Source

Assume speech radiates from a point source. For a **free-field, spherical-spreading** condition, sound intensity falls off as $1/r^2$, and sound pressure falls off as $1/r$. The SPL reduction is therefore:

$$\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d_0}{d}\right)$$

Starting from a reference distance $d_0 = 1\,\text{m}$ with reference SPL $L_0$:

$$L_p(d) = L_0 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{1}\right)$$

**For a directional talker** (human mouth has directionality, roughly Q = 2 in front), the additional 3 dB on-axis gain is offset by an 8 dB hemispherical-to-spherical correction, yielding the empirical engineering equation used in KTU problems:

$$L_p(d) = L_p(1\,\text{m}) - 20 \log_{10}(d) - 8$$

**Step-by-step numerical illustration:** A talker produces $L_p(1\,\text{m}) = 60\,\text{dB}$ at 1 m. What is the SPL at 3 m?

$$\begin{aligned}
L_p(3\,\text{m}) &= 60 - 20 \log_{10}(3) - 8 \\
&= 60 - 20 (0.477) - 8 \\
&= 60 - 9.54 - 8 \\
&= 42.46\,\text{dB SPL}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Speech-to-Noise Ratio at Listener Position

The **Speech-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** at the listener's ear is the difference between the speech SPL arriving from the talker and the noise SPL at the listener's location:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR} &= L_{p,\text{speech}}(d) - L_{p,\text{noise}} \\
&= \big[L_p(1\,\text{m}) - 20 \log_{10}(d) - 8\big] - L_{p,\text{noise}}
\end{aligned}$$

**KTU Design Rule:** For 100% sentence intelligibility and acceptable communication, SNR ≥ **+10 dB** at the listener. For acceptable communication, SNR ≥ **+6 dB**.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — SIL Calculation

**Problem:** The octave-band sound pressure levels measured at a workstation are:

| Octave Band (Hz) | 125 | 250 | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SPL (dB) | 70 | 65 | 60 | 55 | 50 | 45 |

**Find (a)** the SIL and **(b)** the PSIL. Comment on the suitability of communication at 2 m for normal voice effort.

**Solution (a):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SIL} &= \frac{L_{500} + L_{1000} + L_{2000} + L_{4000}}{4} \\
&= \frac{60 + 55 + 50 + 45}{4} \\
&= \frac{210}{4} \\
&= 52.5\,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PSIL} &= \frac{L_{500} + L_{1000} + L_{2000}}{3} \\
&= \frac{60 + 55 + 50}{3} \\
&= \frac{165}{3} \\
&= 55.0\,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** From the standard Beranek table for normal voice ($L_p(1\,\text{m}) = 69\,\text{dB}$ for males):

| Voice Level | Max Distance for SIL ≈ 50 dB |
|---|---|
| Normal (male, 69 dB) | ≈ 1 m |
| Raised (78 dB) | ≈ 2.5 m |
| Very Loud (84 dB) | ≈ 5 m |

At SIL = 52.5 dB, the maximum distance for normal voice is only ≈ **0.9 m**, so communication at **2 m** with normal voice is **marginal** — the talker must **raise the voice** to be reliably understood.

### 3.4 Articulation Index (AI) — Conceptual Derivation

The Articulation Index weights each octave band's contribution to speech intelligibility based on:

1. The SNR in that band
2. The phonetic content of speech in that band

$$\text{AI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot S_i$$

where $W_i$ is the **band weighting factor** (peak $\approx 0.20$ around 1000–2000 Hz) and $S_i$ is the **SNR function** (0 to 1):

$$S_i = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{SNR} < -12\,\text{dB} \\ \dfrac{\text{SNR} + 12}{30}, & -12 \le \text{SNR} \le +18\,\text{dB} \\ 1, & \text{SNR} > +18\,\text{dB} \end{cases}$$

| AI Range | Intelligibility |
|---|---|
| 0.0 – 0.2 | Bad |
| 0.2 – 0.4 | Poor |
| 0.4 – 0.6 | Fair |
| 0.6 – 0.8 | Good |
| 0.8 – 1.0 | Excellent |

### 3.5 Critical Bandwidth — Zwicker's Formula Derivation

The bandwidth within which masking is most effective:

$$\text{CB}(f_c) = 25 + 75 \left(1 + 1.4 f_c^2\right)^{0.69}$$

At $f_c = 1000\,\text{Hz}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CB} &= 25 + 75 (1 + 1.4 \cdot 10^6)^{0.69} \\
&\approx 25 + 75 \cdot (1{,}400{,}001)^{0.69} \\
&\approx 25 + 75 \cdot 160 \\
&\approx 160\,\text{Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

This ≈ 160 Hz is the canonical "critical band at 1 kHz" used throughout psychoacoustics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Masking Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Masker Source"]:::src --> B["Propagation Medium"]
    B --> C["Listener Cochlea"]
    D["Signal Source"]:::sig --> E["Propagation Medium"]
    E --> C
    C --> F{"Same Critical Band?"}:::decision
    F -- Yes --> G["Masking Occurs"]
    G --> H["Raised Audibility Threshold"]
    H --> I["Signal Inaudible if Below Threshold"]
    F -- No --> J["Minimal Masking"]
    J --> K["Signal Audibly Perceived"]

    classDef src fill:#fbb,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#bfb,stroke:#070,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#ffd,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: SIL/PSIL Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Measure Octave Band SPLs at Listener"] --> B["Extract 500, 1000, 2000, 4000 Hz"]
    B --> C{"Compute SIL or PSIL?"}
    C -- "Original SIL" --> D["Arithmetic Average of 4 Bands"]
    C -- "Preferred PSIL" --> E["Arithmetic Average of 3 Bands"]
    D --> F["Apply Beranek Table"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Determine Max Talker Listener Distance"]
    G --> H{"Communication Acceptable?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Zone is Adequate for Speech"]
    H -- No --> J["Apply Noise Control Measures"]
    J --> K["HVAC Damping, Absorption Panels, Q Set"]

    classDef proc fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A,B,D,E,F,G proc
    class C,H decision
    class I,J,K result
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Masking Curve Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Low Frequency Slope"]:::lfs -- "Approx 20 to 40 dB per Oct" --> B["Masker Center Frequency"]
    B -- "Approx Minus 20 to Minus 30 dB per Oct" --> C["High Frequency Slope"]:::hfs
    B --> D["Masker SPL Level"]
    D --> E["Critical Band Region"]
    E --> F["Maximum Masking Zone"]

    classDef lfs fill:#f9d5e5,stroke:#a22041,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hfs fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2a7a2a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctr fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    class B ctr
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Speech Communication System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Transmission Chain"
    A1["Talker"] --> A2["Voice Effort"]
    A2 --> A3["SPL at 1 meter"]
    A3 --> A4["Distance d SPL"]
    end

    subgraph "Ambient Channel"
    B1["Noise Sources"] --> B2["Propagation"] --> B3["SPL at Listener"]
    end

    A4 --> C1["Listener Ear"]
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2["SNR Determination"]
    C2 --> C3{"SIL or PSIL in Spec?"}
    C3 -- Yes --> C4["Acceptable"]
    C3 -- No --> C5["Mitigation Required"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic above models the **end-to-end communication chain** governed by SIL/PSIL. The talker produces speech at a known effort level; geometric spreading attenuates it; the ambient noise competes at the listener's ear; the resulting SNR is compared against the SIL-derived allowable distance.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define masking in acoustics. What is meant by the critical band?

**Model Answer:**
Masking is the psychoacoustic phenomenon in which the threshold of audibility of one sound (the masked sound or signal) is elevated by the presence of another sound (the masking sound or masker). The masking effect is most pronounced when the masker and signal share similar frequencies. The **critical band** refers to the narrow frequency bandwidth (≈ 160 Hz at 1 kHz) within which a masker exerts its strongest masking influence on signals. Masking effectiveness falls off rapidly outside the critical band. The concept is fundamental to SIL calculations because speech energy lies predominantly in the 500–4000 Hz region, and noise within the same critical band as speech will mask it most effectively.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define Speech Interference Level (SIL). How is it computed, and why are the 500, 1000, 2000, and 4000 Hz octave bands used?

**Model Answer:**
SIL is a single-number rating of background noise in terms of its interference with speech communication. It is computed as the **arithmetic mean** of the sound pressure levels measured in the four octave bands centered at 500, 1000, 2000, and 4000 Hz:

$$\text{SIL} = \frac{L_{500} + L_{1000} + L_{2000} + L_{4000}}{4}$$

These bands are used because they encompass the **frequency range of greatest speech energy and intelligibility-critical consonant sounds**. By averaging the noise level across these bands, SIL provides a robust predictor of how much the noise will interfere with verbal communication.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of masking in detail. Discuss the role of the critical band and describe how masking curves are constructed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define and derive the Speech Interference Level (SIL). A factory floor has the following octave-band SPL measurements:

| Octave Band (Hz) | 250 | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SPL (dB) | 80 | 78 | 75 | 72 | 68 |

Compute the SIL and PSIL. Comment on the suitability of speech communication at 5 m for normal and raised voice effort. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

Masking is the process by which the audibility of a sound (signal) is reduced by the presence of another sound (masker). When a masker is present, the masked threshold of the signal rises; the greater the masker level, the higher the masking threshold.

**Role of the Critical Band:**
The human auditory system performs a frequency analysis via the **cochlea's basilar membrane**, which behaves like a bank of bandpass filters. Each filter has a bandwidth known as the **critical bandwidth** (CB). Within the critical band, the masker efficiently raises the masked threshold. Outside the CB, the masking effect diminishes. Zwicker's empirical formula:

$$\text{CB}(f_c) = 25 + 75 (1 + 1.4 f_c^2)^{0.69}\;\text{Hz}$$

The CB is ≈ 100 Hz at 200 Hz and grows to ≈ 1000 Hz at 5 kHz.

**Construction of Masking Curves:**
A masking curve is a plot of the **minimum SPL of a probe tone (signal) that is just audible in the presence of a fixed-level masker**, plotted against signal frequency. Procedure:
1. Fix masker at frequency $f_m$ and level $L_m$.
2. For each probe frequency $f_s$ near $f_m$, find the lowest probe level $L_s$ for which the probe is just audible.
3. Plot $L_s$ vs $f_s$ — this is the masking pattern.

The curve is **asymmetric**:
- Steep on the **high-frequency side** (–20 to –30 dB/oct).
- Gentle on the **low-frequency side** (+20 to +40 dB/oct).

Hence **low-frequency maskers are very effective at masking higher frequencies** — a key principle in noise control of fan and HVAC systems.

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given octave-band SPLs, the SIL uses the four bands 500, 1000, 2000, 4000 Hz.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SIL} &= \frac{L_{500} + L_{1000} + L_{2000} + L_{4000}}{4} \\
&= \frac{78 + 75 + 72 + 68}{4} \\
&= \frac{293}{4} \\
&= 73.25\,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

PSIL uses only the three bands 500, 1000, 2000 Hz:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PSIL} &= \frac{L_{500} + L_{1000} + L_{2000}}{3} \\
&= \frac{78 + 75 + 72}{3} \\
&= \frac{225}{3} \\
&= 75.0\,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on Communication at 5 m:**

Reference: Beranek's tables give the maximum talker-to-listener distance for reliable speech communication.

| Voice Level | $L_p(1\,\text{m})$ | Max distance for SIL = 73 dB |
|---|---|---|
| Normal male voice | ≈ 69 dB | < 0.25 m (impractical) |
| Raised voice | ≈ 78 dB | ≈ 1 m |
| Very loud | ≈ 84 dB | ≈ 3 m |

At SIL = 73.25 dB, communication at **5 m is not feasible**, even with shouting. Engineering intervention (enclosures, absorption, isolation of noisy equipment) is required.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** What is the Preferred Speech Interference Level (PSIL)? Why is PSIL preferred over the original SIL in modern practice? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A speech signal has an SPL of 70 dB at 1 m from a talker. The background noise SPL is 60 dB (constant at all distances). Using the relation $L_p(d) = L_p(1\,\text{m}) - 20 \log_{10}(d) - 8$, determine the maximum distance at which the SNR is at least +6 dB. Also determine the SIL that would cause this. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Definition of PSIL:**
PSIL is the **arithmetic mean** of the octave-band SPLs in the **500 Hz, 1000 Hz, and 2000 Hz** octave bands:

$$\text{PSIL} = \frac{L_{500} + L_{1000} + L_{2000}}{3}$$

**Why PSIL is preferred:**
1. **Better correlation:** PSIL correlates more strongly with the Speech Intelligibility Index (SII) and Articulation Index (AI) than the original four-band SIL, especially for low voice levels.
2. **Avoids contamination from high-frequency noise:** The 4000 Hz octave band contains less speech information and more high-frequency noise (e.g., from bearings, valves); including it can skew the rating.
3. **Sensitivity:** PSIL is more sensitive to variations in noise spectra that affect intelligibility.
4. **Standard adoption:** PSIL is recommended in **ANSI/ASA S3.14** and is widely used in **HVAC and architectural acoustics**.

**Model Solution to (b):**

Required: SNR ≥ +6 dB at the listener.

Speech SPL at distance $d$:

$$L_{p,\text{speech}}(d) = 70 - 20 \log_{10}(d) - 8$$

Noise SPL is constant at 60 dB.

SNR condition:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{p,\text{speech}}(d) - 60 &\geq 6 \\
L_{p,\text{speech}}(d) &\geq 66 \\
70 - 20 \log_{10}(d) - 8 &\geq 66 \\
62 - 20 \log_{10}(d) &\geq 66 \\
-20 \log_{10}(d) &\geq 4 \\
\log_{10}(d) &\leq -0.2 \\
d &\leq 10^{-0.2} \\
d &\leq 0.631\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

So the maximum distance for acceptable communication is **d ≈ 0.63 m**.

**Corresponding SIL:** The maximum allowable noise level (PSIL) such that communication is just possible at d = 0.63 m requires noise SPL ≤ 60 dB (broadband). If the noise is uniformly distributed across bands, then:

$$\text{PSIL} \approx 60\,\text{dB}$$

Checking against Beranek's table: At PSIL = 60 dB and normal voice ($L_p(1\,\text{m}) = 69$ dB), the maximum distance for intelligible speech is ≈ **0.7 m**, which matches our calculation within engineering approximation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Using only the arithmetic mean** for SIL without specifying the four bands. Always state which octave bands are included.
> 2. **Confusing SIL with dBA:** dBA is a broadband weighted level; SIL is band-specific. They are NOT interchangeable.
> 3. **Forgetting the −8 dB correction** in the directional speech-spreading formula. The −8 dB accounts for the human talker's hemispherical-to-spherical radiation pattern.
> 4. **Mixing up PSIL vs SIL:** PSIL drops the 4000 Hz band. The problem statement must be read carefully.
> 5. **Failing to comment** on the practical implication of a SIL value — examiners expect the student to link the numerical result to a maximum communication distance using the Beranek table.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Masking & SIL**

- **Masking** = elevation of audibility threshold of one sound by another. Driven by **critical band** mechanism in cochlea.
- **Critical bandwidth** at 1 kHz ≈ **160 Hz** (Zwicker's formula).
- Masking curve is **asymmetric**: steeper on the **high-frequency side**; **low-frequency maskers dominate** higher frequencies.
- **SIL** = arithmetic mean of SPLs in **500, 1000, 2000, 4000 Hz** octave bands.
- **PSIL** = arithmetic mean of SPLs in **500, 1000, 2000 Hz** octave bands — preferred modern metric.
- **Speech SPL at distance d:** $L_p(d) = L_p(1\,\text{m}) - 20 \log_{10}(d) - 8$ (engineering formula with talker directivity).
- **For reliable communication:** SNR ≥ **+10 dB**; **acceptable** at SNR ≥ **+6 dB**.
- Reference voice levels: Normal male ≈ **69 dB**, raised ≈ **78 dB**, very loud ≈ **84 dB**, shouting ≈ **90 dB** (all at 1 m).
- **Articulation Index (AI)** ranges from 0 to 1; values **> 0.6** indicate good speech intelligibility.
- Practical applications: **HVAC noise limits, classroom design (NC-30 to NC-35), office acoustics (NC-25 to NC-30), hospital wards (NC-25), industrial control rooms (NC-40)**.
- Masking-based noise control: place quiet zones away from low-frequency dominant sources (transformers, fans, compressors).
- Always specify **octave band identity** when computing SIL — never report a single-number dB value as SIL without context.
- The **4000 Hz band** is dropped in PSIL because it contributes little to speech intelligibility while picking up high-frequency noise from mechanical sources.
- **Beranek's tables** are the canonical reference linking SIL to maximum talker-listener distance for each voice effort level.

---

**End of Notes — Module 3: Masking, Speech Interference Level**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perceived Noise Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Perceived Noise Level

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Perceived Noise Level (PNL)** is a unit of measure of effective perceived noisiness, expressed in **PNdB (Perceived Noise decibels)**, evaluated from Sound Pressure Level measurements within the audible frequency range of **45 Hz to 11,350 Hz** according to the procedure prescribed in **ICAO Annex 16, Volume I** (originally developed by Kryter and subsequently standardized as **Mark VII procedure**, later refined as **SAE ARP 866A**).

Mathematically, PNL is defined by the relation:

$$PNL = 40 + \frac{10}{\log_{10}2} \cdot \log_{10}(N_{max})$$

where $N_{max}$ is the **Maximum Noy value** obtained from the **Mark VII Noy Contours** (a set of 24 standardized equal-annoyance curves spanning 50 Hz to 10,000 Hz).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike Sound Pressure Level (SPL in dB) which is a purely physical measurement, PNL is a **psycho-acoustic quantity** that quantifies the subjective *noisiness* of a sound as perceived by the average human ear/brain system. A 100 dB SPL pure tone at 1 kHz does NOT produce the same PNL as a 100 dB SPL pure tone at 100 Hz.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are sitting in a quiet library and two people walk in:
- **Person A** drops a heavy book — *BANG!*
- **Person B** gently rustles a plastic bag continuously for 5 seconds — *crinkle, crinkle, crinkle...*

Even if a sound level meter reads the **same SPL (dB)** for both events, your brain will register Person A's book drop as *far more annoying* than the rustling. Why? Because:
1. The book drop has more **low-frequency energy** (which our ears perceive as "rumbly" and intrusive).
2. The plastic bag has more **high-frequency content** (which our ears tolerate better).
3. Your ear has a **non-flat frequency response** — it is most sensitive around 2–5 kHz.

**Perceived Noise Level** mathematically captures this exact intuition by:
- Dividing the audio spectrum into **24 critical bands (Bark-like, but here in octave-octave steps)**.
- Computing a **noisiness value (Noy)** in each band.
- Applying a tone correction for pure-tone components.
- Combining them into a single number representing total perceived noisiness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** Perceived Noise Level was developed in the 1950s–1960s by the U.S. Air Force and FAA to certify aircraft noise. Before PNL, the FAA used *Overall Sound Pressure Level (OASPL)*, which unfairly penalized quieter aircraft with "peaky" spectra. PNL was the first widely-adopted metric to account for the **equal-annoyance contours** of human hearing.

### Fundamental Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Noy** = unit of perceived noisiness (a 1 kHz tone at **40 dB SPL** = **1 Noy** by definition).
- **PNdB** = Perceived Noise decibel (logarithmic scaling of Noy).
- **Mark VII Noy Contour Reference:** 1 Noy corresponds to a 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL.
- **Standard Frequency Range:** 45 Hz to 11,350 Hz (24 bands at half-octave spacing).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic: How PNL is Computed (Step-by-Step Logic)

The Mark VII procedure (the standardized method to compute PNL) follows this structured logic:

**Step 1 — Band SPL Extraction:**
The broadband sound is measured (or synthesized) and the SPL in each of the **24 frequency bands** (from 50 Hz to 10,000 Hz in ½-octave steps) is recorded.

**Step 2 — Band SPL → Noy Conversion:**
For each band, the SPL value is converted to a Noy value using **empirical Noy tables** (Mark VII standard tables). The general shape of the conversion is **piecewise logarithmic**:

- Below threshold: $N = 0$
- Linear log region: $N = 10^{(SPL - a)/b}$ where $a, b$ are band-specific constants.
- Above threshold: $N$ saturates to a maximum.

**Step 3 — Total Noy Computation:**
The total perceived noisiness is computed by **summation across bands** (not a simple dB addition):

$$N_{total} = N_{max} + 0.15 \cdot \left[ \left( \sum_{i} N_i \right) - N_{max} \right]$$

The factor **0.15** accounts for the masking effect — secondary bands contribute only 15% of their Noy value compared to the loudest band.

**Step 4 — Maximum Noy Selection:**
$N_{max}$ = the largest Noy from any single band. This dominates perceived noisiness.

**Step 5 — Logarithmic Compression:**
Finally, $N_{max}$ is converted to PNL using the standard PNL formula.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Reason |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Psycho-acoustic studies show the ear integrates sound in roughly **½-octave bands** (critical bands). |
| 2 | Noy tables were derived from **equal-noisiness contour** laboratory experiments on thousands of human subjects. |
| 3 | Pure summation overestimates noisiness; the 0.15 factor is empirically tuned to match subjective ratings. |
| 4 | The dominant band "masks" neighbouring bands; a tone at 1 kHz annoys us more than its energy alone would suggest. |
| 5 | A logarithmic scale matches **Fechner's Law** (perceived intensity is logarithmic in stimulus). |

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for Exams:** The following table summarizes all formulas you must memorize. Always use `\vert` instead of `\` for absolute values to maintain table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $N_{total} = N_{max} + 0.15\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{24} N_i\right) - N_{max}\right]$ | Total Noy value combining 24 bands |
| 2 | $PNL = 40 + \frac{10}{\log_{10}2} \cdot \log_{10}(N_{max})$ | Standard PNL definition (in PNdB) |
| 3 | $PNL = 40 + 33.22 \cdot \log_{10}(N_{max})$ | Numerical simplification ($\frac{10}{\log_{10}2} \approx 33.22$) |
| 4 | $C_t = f(SPL_{band}, SPL_{neighbours})$ | Tone correction (1–3 PNdB based on pure-tone detection) |
| 5 | $PNLT = PNL + C_t$ | Tone-Corrected Perceived Noise Level |
| 6 | $D_c = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{T_{dur}}{10}\right)$ | Duration correction (reference duration = 10 s) |
| 7 | $EPNL = PNLT + D_c$ | **Effective Perceived Noise Level** (used in ICAO certification) |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Aircraft Noise Certification:** ICAO Chapter 2, Chapter 3, Chapter 4, and Chapter 14 noise limits are all expressed in **EPNL (Effective PNL)** measured at three flyover/approach/side-line measurement points.
2. **Environmental Impact Assessment:** PNL is used to evaluate the community noise impact of airports and heliports.
3. **Aircraft Design Trade-offs:** Engineers use PNL to assess engine placement, fan blade count (affecting Blade Passage Frequency tone), and exhaust nozzle design.
4. **Comparison with Other Metrics:** Unlike **Loudness Level (Phons)** or **Loudness (Sones)**, PNL is specifically tuned to **noisiness** (annoyance), not just loudness — making it the gold standard for aviation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 3.1: From Noy to PNL (The Master Conversion)

The relationship between Noy and PNdB is fundamentally a logarithmic one, designed so that **a doubling of noisiness corresponds to a 10 PNdB increase** (similar to how a doubling of sound power corresponds to a 3 dB SPL increase, but here perceived noisiness doubles every 10 PNdB).

**Starting Definition:**
A 1 kHz pure tone at 40 dB SPL is defined to have **N = 1 Noy**, hence **PNL = 40 PNdB**.

**General postulate:** For Noy $N$, the PNL is some linear function of $\log_{10} N$:

$$PNL = A + B \cdot \log_{10} N$$

**Anchor Point 1:** At $N = 1$, $PNL = 40$:

$$40 = A + B \cdot \log_{10} 1 = A + 0$$

$$\therefore A = 40$$

**Anchor Point 2 (Slope Definition):** Doubling of Noy must produce a 10 PNdB increase (by definition of the PNdB scale):

$$40 + 10 = 40 + B \cdot \log_{10}(2 \cdot 1) = 40 + B \cdot \log_{10} 2$$

$$\therefore B \cdot \log_{10} 2 = 10$$

$$\therefore B = \frac{10}{\log_{10} 2} \approx 33.219$$

**Final Master Formula:**

$$PNL = 40 + \frac{10}{\log_{10} 2} \cdot \log_{10} N_{max}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** For $N = 2$: $PNL = 40 + 33.22 \cdot \log_{10}(2) = 40 + 33.22 \cdot 0.301 = 40 + 10 = 50$ PNdB. ✓ Confirms the "doubling = +10 PNdB" rule.

---

### Derivation 3.2: Total Noy Combination (Why 0.15?)

The empirical formula combining multiple bands is:

$$N_{total} = N_{max} + 0.15 \left[ \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i\right) - N_{max} \right]$$

**Step-by-Step Logic of Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Define total noisiness as the loudest band plus a **partial contribution** from all other bands. If we naively summed all Noys, we would **overestimate noisiness** because of psycho-acoustic masking.

$$N_{total} = N_{max} + \alpha \cdot \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i - N_{max}\right)$$

**Step 2:** The term $\left(\sum N_i - N_{max}\right)$ represents the **sum of all secondary (non-dominant) bands**.

**Step 3:** The constant $\alpha = 0.15$ was determined empirically by Kryter (1959) from laboratory experiments where listeners rated the noisiness of broadband noise. The factor 0.15 best matched the subjective responses when averaged across the population.

**Step 4:** Re-arranging for clarity:

$$N_{total} = (1 - \alpha) \cdot N_{max} + \alpha \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i$$

$$N_{total} = 0.85 \cdot N_{max} + 0.15 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i$$

This is the equivalent form. It shows that 85% of the dominant band's Noy is preserved, while each other band contributes only 15% of its Noy.

---

### Derivation 3.3: Effective Perceived Noise Level (EPNL)

**Step 1 — Start with the standard PNL:**
$P_{NL} = 40 + 33.22 \cdot \log_{10}(N_{max})$

**Step 2 — Apply Tone Correction:**
A pure tone (e.g., blade passage frequency) is more annoying than broadband noise at the same Noy. The tone correction $C_t$ is computed by comparing the SPL in each band against the SPL of its neighbouring bands. If a band exceeds its neighbours by a threshold margin, $C_t$ is added (typically 0, 1, 2, or 3 PNdB):

$$PNLT = PNL + C_t$$

**Step 3 — Apply Duration Correction:**
The reference integration time for noise certification is **10 seconds**. If the noise event has effective duration $T$ (defined as the time PNL is within 10 dB of $PNL_{max}$), then:

$$D_c = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{T}{10}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Final EPNL:**

$$EPNL = PNLT + D_c$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** EPNL is what is actually reported for aircraft certification. Always compute PNL → apply $C_t$ → apply $D_c$ in that **strict order**. Skipping or reordering will lose marks.

---

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A measured noise event has $N_{max} = 250$ Noy, a tone correction $C_t = 2$ PNdB, and an effective duration $T = 20$ s. Compute the **EPNL**.

**Solution:**

**Step A — PNL Calculation:**

$$PNL = 40 + 33.22 \cdot \log_{10}(250)$$

$$\log_{10}(250) = \log_{10}(2.5 \times 10^2) = 2 + \log_{10}(2.5) = 2 + 0.3979 = 2.3979$$

$$PNL = 40 + 33.22 \times 2.3979 = 40 + 79.66 = 119.66 \text{ PNdB}$$

**Step B — Apply Tone Correction:**

$$PNLT = PNL + C_t = 119.66 + 2 = 121.66 \text{ PNdB}$$

**Step C — Apply Duration Correction:**

$$D_c = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{20}{10}\right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(2) = 10 \times 0.3010 = 3.01 \text{ dB}$$

**Step D — Final EPNL:**

$$EPNL = PNLT + D_c = 121.66 + 3.01 = 124.67 \text{ PNdB}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{EPNL \approx 124.67 \text{ PNdB}}$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 4.1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of the PNL Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Sound Pressure Level SPL data in 24 bands 50 Hz to 10000 Hz] --> B[Band SPL Extraction from Microphone Signal]
    B --> C[Convert each band SPL to Noy value via Mark VII Noy Tables]
    C --> D[Identify Maximum Noy Nmax from 24 bands]
    D --> E[Compute Total Noy using 0.15 masking factor]
    E --> F[Apply Master Formula PNL equals 40 plus 33.22 times log of Nmax]
    F --> G[PNL Output in PNdB]
    G --> H[Apply Tone Correction Ct by comparing band SPL with neighbours]
    H --> I[PNLT Output]
    I --> J[Apply Duration Correction Dc for effective integration time]
    J --> K[EPNL Output Final Effective Perceived Noise Level in PNdB]
    K --> L[Use for ICAO Aircraft Noise Certification and Environmental Assessment]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### Diagram 4.2: Relationship Between SPL, Loudness, Noisiness, and PNL

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[SPL dB Physical Measurement] --> B[Loudness Level in Phons Subjective Loudness]
    A --> C[Perceived Noise Level in PNdB Subjective Noisiness]
    B --> D[Loudness in Sones Linear Loudness Scale]
    C --> E[Effective PNL EPNL with Tone and Duration Correction]
    D --> F[Used in Hearing Conservation]
    E --> G[Used in Aviation Noise Certification ICAO Annex 16]
    F --> H[Industrial Hygiene Occupational Noise]
    G --> I[Airports Aircraft Design Environmental Impact]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#DC143C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style I fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### Diagram 4.3: Frequency Band Partitioning (Mark VII 24-Band Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Full Audio Spectrum] --> B[Band 1: 50 to 70 Hz]
    A --> C[Band 2: 70 to 110 Hz]
    A --> D[Band 3: 110 to 170 Hz]
    A --> E[Intermediate Bands 4 to 21]
    A --> F[Band 22: 7000 to 8500 Hz]
    A --> G[Band 23: 8500 to 10500 Hz]
    A --> H[Band 24: 10500 to 11350 Hz]
    B --> I[Each band SPL converted to Noy]
    C --> I
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Sum and Find Maximum]
    J --> K[Compute PNL]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **Perceived Noise Level (PNL)**. Why is it preferred over Sound Pressure Level (SPL) for aircraft noise certification?

**Model Answer:**
PNL is a unit of measure of effective perceived noisiness expressed in PNdB. It is calculated from SPL values in 24 frequency bands (50 Hz to 10,000 Hz) using the Mark VII Noy tables and the formula:

$$PNL = 40 + \frac{10}{\log_{10}2} \cdot \log_{10}(N_{max})$$

PNL is preferred over SPL for aircraft noise certification because:
1. SPL is purely physical and treats all frequencies equally, whereas the human ear has non-flat frequency sensitivity.
2. PNL incorporates the **equal-noisiness contours** derived from human psycho-acoustic experiments.
3. PNL penalizes low-frequency and tonal components more heavily, which matches the actual annoyance reported by communities near airports.
4. PNL is the basis of ICAO Annex 16 noise certification standards.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is a **Noy**? State the reference condition for 1 Noy.

**Model Answer:**
The **Noy** is the unit of perceived noisiness. By definition:
- **1 Noy** corresponds to a **1 kHz pure tone at 40 dB SPL**.
- The scale is such that **doubling of Noy corresponds to a 10 PNdB increase** in PNL.

The Noy scale is linear in perceived noisiness, while PNL (in PNdB) is the logarithmic representation, analogous to Sones vs Phons in loudness.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Mark VII procedure** for computing Perceived Noise Level. With a neat flowchart, describe the step-by-step computation.

**Model Solution:**

The Mark VII procedure, standardized in SAE ARP 866A and ICAO Annex 16, computes PNL through the following steps:

**Step 1 — Spectral Decomposition:**
The sound signal is passed through 24 band-pass filters covering the range **50 Hz to 10,000 Hz** in half-octave increments.

**Step 2 — SPL Measurement in Each Band:**
The SPL in each of the 24 bands is recorded (typically time-averaged over the noise event).

**Step 3 — Conversion to Noy:**
Each band SPL is converted to a Noy value using **empirical Mark VII Noy tables**. The conversion function has three regions:
- Below threshold: $N_i = 0$
- Linear-log region: $N_i = 10^{(SPL_i - a_i)/b_i}$
- Saturation region: $N_i = N_{i,max}$

**Step 4 — Identify Maximum Noy:**
$N_{max} = \max(N_1, N_2, \ldots, N_{24})$

**Step 5 — Compute Total Noy:**

$$N_{total} = N_{max} + 0.15 \cdot \left[\sum_{i=1}^{24} N_i - N_{max}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Compute PNL:**

$$PNL = 40 + 33.22 \cdot \log_{10}(N_{max})$$

The PNL is the final output in PNdB. (Full Mark VII flowchart shown in Section 4, Diagram 4.1 of these notes.)

**(b)** [7 Marks] The following band Noy values were measured for an aircraft flyover. Compute the **PNL**.

| Band | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Noy | 8 | 12 | 25 | 60 | 40 | 15 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify $N_{max}$:**

$$N_{max} = \max(8, 12, 25, 60, 40, 15) = 60 \text{ Noy}$$

**Step 2 — Sum of all Noys:**

$$\sum N_i = 8 + 12 + 25 + 60 + 40 + 15 = 160 \text{ Noy}$$

**Step 3 — Total Noy:**

$$N_{total} = 60 + 0.15 \cdot (160 - 60) = 60 + 0.15 \times 100 = 60 + 15 = 75 \text{ Noy}$$

**Step 4 — Apply PNL formula:**

$$PNL = 40 + 33.22 \cdot \log_{10}(60)$$

$$\log_{10}(60) = \log_{10}(6 \times 10) = 1 + \log_{10}(6) = 1 + 0.7782 = 1.7782$$

$$PNL = 40 + 33.22 \times 1.7782 = 40 + 59.07 = 99.07 \text{ PNdB}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{PNL \approx 99.07 \text{ PNdB}}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define **Effective Perceived Noise Level (EPNL)**. Explain the role of **tone correction ($C_t$)** and **duration correction ($D_c$)** in EPNL computation.

**Model Solution:**

**Effective Perceived Noise Level (EPNL)** is the metric used for aircraft noise certification under ICAO Annex 16. It incorporates the standard PNL plus two corrections:

$$EPNL = PNL + C_t + D_c$$

**Tone Correction ($C_t$):**
Pure-tone components (e.g., from compressor or fan blade passage frequency) are more annoying than broadband noise at the same overall level. To account for this, each band's SPL is compared to its neighbours' SPLs. If a band exceeds its neighbours by:
- $\ge 1.5$ dB but $< 3$ dB: $C_t = 1$ PNdB
- $\ge 3$ dB but $< 6$ dB: $C_t = 2$ PNdB
- $\ge 6$ dB but $< 9$ dB: $C_t = 3$ PNdB
- $\ge 9$ dB: $C_t = 3$ PNdB (saturates)

The **maximum** $C_t$ across all bands is selected and added:

$$PNLT = PNL + C_t$$

**Duration Correction ($D_c$):**
The reference integration time is 10 seconds. For a noise event with effective duration $T$:

$$D_c = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{T}{10}\right)$$

The effective duration $T$ is defined as the time during which the PNL is within 10 dB of $PNL_{max}$.

**Final EPNL:**

$$EPNL = PNLT + D_c$$

**(b)** [7 Marks] An aircraft flyover noise event has $PNL = 115$ PNdB, a tone correction $C_t = 3$ PNdB, and effective duration $T = 50$ s. Compute the **EPNL**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Tone Correction:**

$$PNLT = PNL + C_t = 115 + 3 = 118 \text{ PNdB}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Duration Correction:**

$$D_c = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{50}{10}\right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(5)$$

$$\log_{10}(5) = \log_{10}(10/2) = 1 - \log_{10}(2) = 1 - 0.3010 = 0.6990$$

$$D_c = 10 \times 0.6990 = 6.99 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Final EPNL:**

$$EPNL = PNLT + D_c = 118 + 6.99 = 124.99 \text{ PNdB}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{EPNL \approx 124.99 \text{ PNdB}}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–1 mark):** Always report PNL/EPL in **PNdB**, NOT dB. They are different scales.
> 2. **Skipping $C_t$ or $D_c$ (–3 marks):** If the question says EPNL, you MUST compute both corrections. Students often only compute PNL.
> 3. **Wrong Log Base (–2 marks):** Use $\log_{10}$ (base 10), not $\ln$ (natural log). The formula has a factor of 10 already.
> 4. **Forgetting 0.15 Factor (–2 marks):** When given multiple bands, you must use the $N_{total}$ formula, not just take the max.
> 5. **Numerical Approximation Errors (–1 mark):** Round only at the **final step**. Carry 3–4 significant digits throughout.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition to Memorize:** PNL is a psycho-acoustic measure of noisiness expressed in PNdB, based on the Mark VII Noy contours.
- **Master Formula:** $PNL = 40 + 33.22 \cdot \log_{10}(N_{max})$
- **Reference Point:** 1 Noy = 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL
- **Scale Rule:** Doubling of Noy = +10 PNdB
- **Total Noy Formula:** $N_{total} = N_{max} + 0.15 \cdot \left[\sum N_i - N_{max}\right]$
- **EPNL Formula:** $EPNL = PNL + C_t + D_c$
- **Frequency Range:** 24 bands from 50 Hz to 10,000 Hz (in ½-octave steps).
- **Tone Correction:** 0 to 3 PNdB depending on tonal content.
- **Duration Correction:** Reference time is 10 s; longer events get higher EPNL.
- **ICAO Standard:** EPNL is the official certification metric for aircraft noise under ICAO Annex 16, Volume I.
- **Distinguish:** PNL ≠ SPL ≠ Loudness (Phons/Sones). PNL specifically quantifies *annoyance*, not loudness.
- **Logarithm Convention:** Always use $\log_{10}$ in PNL calculations; check $\ln$ vs $\log_{10}$ traps in numerical problems.
- **Order of Operations:** SPL → Noy (24 bands) → $N_{max}$ → PNL → PNLT (with $C_t$) → EPNL (with $D_c$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise and Number Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Noise and Number Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: Noise and Number Index (NNI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> The **Noise and Number Index (NNI)** is a composite acoustic rating index developed in the **United Kingdom (1963)** by the **Wilson Committee** for the specific purpose of quantifying the annoyance caused by **aircraft noise** in the vicinity of airports. It combines two key physical quantities — the **magnitude of the peak noise event** and the **frequency of its occurrence** — into a single unitless numerical index.

The Noise and Number Index is mathematically defined as:

$$\text{NNI} = \bar{L}_{PNL} + 15 \log_{10} N - 80$$

where:
- $\bar{L}_{PNL}$ = Average **Peak Noise Level** of aircraft flyovers, expressed in **PNdB (Perceived Noise Decibels)**
- $N$ = Total number of **noise events** (aircraft flyovers) recorded during a **24-hour day (00:00 to 24:00 hrs)**
- The coefficients **15** and **-80** are empirically derived **trade-off** and **normalization constants**, respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context for KTU Examiners**
> The NNI was the **first scientifically validated metric** that explicitly recognized the dual role of loudness and frequency in community noise annoyance. It was the official UK metric for airport noise zoning from **1963 to 1990**, before being superseded by the **Noise Exposure Forecast (NEF)** and later by **Leq-based indices (LAeq, Lden)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doorbell Annoyance" Intuition

Imagine you live in an apartment. A single, very loud doorbell ring at 3 AM is annoying. But what is **more annoying** — one *extremely loud* doorbell, or **ten moderately loud** doorbells? The answer depends on **both** how loud each event is **AND** how often it happens.

| Scenario | Peak Loudness | Number of Rings | Annoyance |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|-----------|
| A | 100 dB (very loud) | 1 ring | Moderate |
| B | 80 dB (medium) | 100 rings | Extreme |
| C | 90 dB (loud) | 10 rings | High |

**NNI does exactly this** for aircraft flyovers:
- The **peak level (L̄_PNL)** captures the *intensity* of each event.
- The **log of the count (log₁₀ N)** captures the *frequency* of events.
- The **coefficient 15** represents the *trade-off* — a 1 dB increase in peak level is considered equivalent to a **1.78× (≈ 10^(1/15)) increase in the number of flights**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the coefficient "15"?**
> The value 15 was determined from **large-scale social surveys** conducted near London Heathrow Airport in the early 1960s. Surveyors found that residents were willing to accept a **15 dB increase in peak noise** if it reduced the number of flights to just **1/10th** of the original. This means an **equal annoyance curve** has a slope of 15:1 (dB : log₁₀ N).

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Conventions Used in NNI Calculation**
> - **PNdB (Perceived Noise Decibels)** — A frequency-weighted unit that accounts for human ear sensitivity to different frequencies. Always 1–2 dB higher than dBA for the same sound.
> - **PNL (Peak Noise Level)** — The maximum PNdB value during a single flyover event, measured at the **10-second average TAN (Tone-Adjusted Noise) peak**.
> - **NNI is unitless** — It is a comparative index, not a physical quantity. Two communities with NNI = 50 are expected to experience the *same* subjective annoyance.
> - **Standard Reference Threshold:** NNI ≤ **35** is generally considered acceptable for residential areas around airports; NNI > **50** indicates severe annoyance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of NNI

The NNI formula is built upon **three structural pillars**, each addressing a different dimension of community noise annoyance:

### Pillar 1 — Peak Noise Level ($\bar{L}_{PNL}$)
- **What it captures:** The *maximum subjective loudness* of a single aircraft overflight.
- **How it is measured:** For each flyover event, the peak PNdB is recorded. For $N$ events, the **arithmetic mean** of all peak PNL values is computed.
- **Why mean and not maximum?** A single extreme event is statistically unreliable; the mean smooths out meteorological and operational variability.

### Pillar 2 — Frequency of Events ($15 \log_{10} N$)
- **What it captures:** The *cumulative exposure* to annoyance across the entire day.
- **Why logarithm?** Human perception of "how many times did this happen" is logarithmic. Going from 1 to 10 flights feels as significant as going from 10 to 100.
- **Why coefficient 15?** Empirically derived from the **equal-annoyance contour slope** in Wilson Committee social surveys.

### Pillar 3 — Normalization Constant (-80)
- **What it does:** Shifts the entire scale so that typical airport conditions produce **positive, manageable NNI values** in the 30–80 range.
- **Origin:** At 1 flight per day (N=1, log N=0) with average peak of 80 PNdB, the NNI becomes exactly **0**, defining the reference baseline.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Noise and Number Index** | $\text{NNI} = \bar{L}_{PNL} + 15 \log_{10} N - 80$ | Unitless index | Composite annoyance metric |
| 2 | Average Peak Noise Level | $\bar{L}_{PNL} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_{PNL,i}$ | PNdB | Mean of all peak PNL values |
| 3 | Number of Events Term | $15 \log_{10} N$ | PNdB-equivalent | Frequency contribution |
| 4 | Normalization Constant | $-80$ | PNdB | Reference baseline shift |
| 5 | Trade-off Slope | $1 \text{ PNdB} \leftrightarrow 10^{1/15} \approx 1.78$ times N | Ratio | Equal-annoyance substitution |
| 6 | Reference Threshold | NNI = 0 (calm condition) | — | $\bar{L}_{PNL}=80, N=1$ |
| 7 | Residential Acceptability | NNI $\leq$ 35 | — | UK planning guideline |
| 8 | Severe Annoyance | NNI $>$ 50 | — | Mandatory insulation zone |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL: Unit Consistency Rule**
> Both $\bar{L}_{PNL}$ and the constant **80** MUST be in the **same units (PNdB)**. The term $15 \log_{10} N$ is also expressed in PNdB-equivalent units to allow algebraic addition.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Role of NNI |
|------------------|-------------|
| **Airport Master Planning** | Used to draw **NNI contour maps** (e.g., NNI = 35, 40, 45, 50 lines) around runways to define land-use zones. |
| **Aircraft Certification** | Helps correlate aircraft type (Chapter 2 vs Chapter 3) with community impact. |
| **Public Health Policy** | Forms the scientific basis for **night-flight restrictions** and **curfews**. |
| **Acoustic Insulation Grants** | UK government uses NNI > 50 contours to **qualify homes** for soundproofing subsidies. |
| **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** | Acts as a baseline metric in older EIA reports (pre-1990). |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Equal-Annoyance Trade-off Coefficient

The coefficient **15** in the NNI equation is not arbitrary. It is derived from the **Equal Annoyance Curve Hypothesis**, formulated as follows:

### Assumption
