An **anechoic chamber** is an enclosed space whose internal surfaces are lined with sound-absorbing wedges that simulate a free-field acoustic environment, with the reflected sound level attenuated by **≥ 30 dB** relative to the direct sound.

**Applications (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Microphone calibration** — free-field calibration of measurement microphones against a known sound source.
2. **Loudspeaker frequency-response testing** — measurement of on-axis and off-axis response without room coloration.
3. **Sound-power determination of small sources** per ISO 3745.
4. **Directivity pattern measurement** of electro-acoustic transducers.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the difference between a free field and a diffuse field. In which chamber is each produced?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Free Field | Diffuse Field |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Sound propagates only outward from source, no reflections | Sound energy is uniform in space and equally probable in all directions |
| Achieved in | **Anechoic chamber** | **Reverberation chamber** |
| Wall treatment | Wedge absorbers (α ≈ 1) | Hard reflective surfaces (α ≈ 0) |
| Standing waves | Absent | Present, statistically smoothed |
| Use | Directivity, calibration | Absorption coefficient, sound power |

*(1 mark for the definition of each, 1 mark for chamber identification.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of an **anechoic chamber**. Derive the expression for the **cut-off frequency** of the absorbing wedges. (7 marks)

**(b)** A reverberation chamber of volume **V = 200 m³** has an empty-chamber reverberation time of **T₂ = 8 s**. A sample of area **S_s = 10 m²** is mounted inside, and the reverberation time drops to **T₁ = 3.5 s**. Calculate the **sound absorption coefficient (α_s)** of the sample. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for A(a) — Construction and Cut-off Frequency

**Step 1 — Block diagram (1.5 marks):**
Refer to *Section 4.1* of these notes. Briefly describe the outer shell, vestibule, wedge lining (walls, ceiling, floor), vibration isolation, and the cable glands.

**Step 2 — Working principle (2.5 marks):**
Wedge absorbers are long, narrow, porous (fibreglass/mineral-wool) wedges with a square base and a tapered tip. The wedge gradually changes the acoustic impedance from Z = ρ₀c (air) at the tip to Z ≈ 0 deep inside the porous material. The sound wave is forced to travel along progressively narrowing channels, where viscous and thermal losses convert acoustic energy to heat. The longer the wedge, the lower the cut-off frequency.

**Step 3 — Derivation of cut-off frequency (3 marks):**

A wedge is effective only when its length *L* is at least **one-quarter of the wavelength** of the incident sound. Mathematically:

$$L \geq \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{c}{4 f_c}$$

Solving for $f_c$:

$$\boxed{\,f_c = \frac{c}{2L}\,} \quad \text{(factor of safety 2 included in the practical formula)}$$

For L = 1 m, $f_c = 343/2 = 171.5$ Hz. For a 50 Hz cut-off, the wedge depth must be:

$$L = \frac{c}{2 f_c} = \frac{343}{2 \times 50} = 3.43 \text{ m}$$

which is why low-frequency anechoic chambers are physically enormous.

---

#### Model Solution for A(b) — Absorption Coefficient

**Given:** V = 200 m³, T₂ = 8 s (empty), T₁ = 3.5 s (with sample), S_s = 10 m².

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$\alpha_s = \frac{0.161 V}{S_s}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Substitute (3 marks):**

$$\alpha_s = \frac{0.161 \times 200}{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{3.5} - \frac{1}{8}\right)$$

$$\alpha_s = 3.22 \times (0.2857 - 0.1250)$$

$$\alpha_s = 3.22 \times 0.1607$$

$$\boxed{\,\alpha_s \approx 0.518\,}$$

**Interpretation (2 marks):** The sample absorbs roughly 52 % of the incident sound energy. This is typical of a moderately efficient porous absorber (e.g., 25-mm fibreglass at 500 Hz). The student should state that α ≤ 1 and the value is physically reasonable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is a **reverberation chamber**? Explain the criteria for a **diffuse sound field**. Derive the **Sabine formula** for reverberation time. (7 marks)

**(b)** A factory noise source is tested in a reverberation room of volume 250 m³ at 1000 Hz. The measured average SPL is **75 dB**, and the reverberation time is **T = 4 s**. Calculate the **sound power level (L_W)** of the source using the ISO 3741 formula. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for B(a) — Reverberation Chamber & Sabine Formula

**Definition (1 mark):** A reverberation chamber is an enclosed space with hard, smooth, non-parallel reflective walls engineered to produce a statistically **diffuse sound field** (mean energy density uniform in space and isotropic in direction).

**Criteria for diffuse field (2 marks):**
1. Mean-square pressure $\bar{p^2}$ is **independent of position** in the room.
2. Direction of energy propagation is **equiprobable** in all directions.
3. Mode density ≥ 20 modes per 1/3-octave band (above $f_{Sch}$).
4. Non-rectangular geometry and a rotating diffuser to break up axial modes.

**Derivation of Sabine's formula (4 marks):** See the full derivation in *Section 3.1* of these notes. Provide all 7 steps, ending with:

$$\boxed{\,T = \frac{0.161\,V}{A}\,} \quad \text{(s, m³, sabins)}$$

---

#### Model Solution for B(b) — Sound Power Level

**Given:** V = 250 m³, $\bar{L_p}$ = 75 dB, T = 4 s, ISO 3741.

**Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$L_W = \bar{L_p} + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V}{T}\right) - 14$$

**Substitute (3 marks):**

$$L_W = 75 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{250}{4}\right) - 14$$

$$L_W = 75 + 10 \log_{10}(62.5) - 14$$

$$L_W = 75 + 10 \times 1.796 - 14$$

$$L_W = 75 + 17.96 - 14$$

$$\boxed{\,L_W \approx 78.96 \text{ dB re 1 pW}\,}$$

**Comment (2.5 marks):** The sound power is roughly 4 dB above the average SPL — typical when the source is small relative to the room volume and the reverberation time is moderate. The value lies in the *typical industrial-machine* range (70–90 dB re 1 pW), confirming the calculation is realistic.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Wrong direction in $1/T_1 - 1/T_2$:** This gives a negative α. Always remember: T₁ (with sample) is *less* than T₂ (empty), so $1/T_1 > 1/T_2$.
> 2. **Wrong unit for V:** Volume must be in **m³**, not cm³ or litres.
> 3. **Confusing Sabine and Eyring formulas:** Sabine is used for **moderately absorbing** rooms. For very low absorption (empty reverberation chambers), Eyring is more accurate. KTU 2024 syllabus asks only for Sabine.
> 4. **Forgetting the 14 dB constant** in the ISO 3741 sound-power formula — this is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Not stating the cut-off frequency formula derivation** explicitly in the exam. KTU examiners reward the *four-step logical chain* (quarter-wavelength → wedge length → solve for f_c → apply factor of safety), not just the final formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Anechoic chamber** = *absorbs* reflections → produces a **free field** → wedge-lined, vibration-isolated, with a vestibule.
- **Reverberation chamber** = *reflects* sound → produces a **diffuse field** → hard, non-parallel walls + rotating diffuser.
- **Wedge cut-off frequency:** $f_c = c / (2L)$. Longer wedge = lower cut-off = bigger chamber.
- **Sabine's formula:** $T = 0.161 V / A$. Units: T in s, V in m³, A in sabins (m²).
- **Absorption coefficient from RT measurements:** $\alpha_s = (0.161 V / S_s)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$.
- **ISO 3741** → sound power in reverberation room: $L_W = \bar{L_p} + 10 \log_{10}(V/T) - 14$.
- **Schroeder frequency:** $f_{Sch} = 2000 \sqrt{T/V}$ — above this, the field is statistically diffuse.
- **Mode density criterion:** ≥ 20 modes per 1/3-octave for diffusion.
- **Reflection reduction in anechoic chamber:** ≥ 30 dB relative to direct sound.
- **Standards to remember for KTU 2024:** ISO 354, ISO 3741, ISO 3745, ISO 26101, ASTM C423.
- **Sample area in absorption test:** typically **10–12 m²** (per ISO 354).
- **Empty reverberation chamber RT** at 1 kHz: typically 5–15 seconds.
- **Always quote α** with a frequency band (e.g., α = 0.62 at 500 Hz) — never as a single number.
- **Acoustic impedance principle in wedges:** gradual transition Z = ρ₀c → Z ≈ 0 removes reflection.
- **Top-of-mind equation list for the exam:** $f_c$, Sabine, α from T, ISO 3741 L_W, Schroeder f.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Directivity Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Directivity Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Directivity Factor (Q)** of an acoustic source is formally defined as the dimensionless ratio of the **sound intensity** radiated by the source in a specified direction (typically the principal or on-axis direction) to the **average sound intensity** that would be radiated by an omnidirectional point source emitting the same total acoustic power.

Mathematically stated:

$$Q(\theta, \phi) = \frac{I(\theta, \phi)}{I_{avg}}$$

Where:
- $I(\theta, \phi)$ = Sound intensity in the direction defined by spherical coordinates $(\theta, \phi)$
- $I_{avg}$ = Mean intensity averaged over the entire spherical surface enclosing the source

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Directivity Factor is a fundamental descriptor of how an acoustic source distributes its radiated energy in space. It is intrinsically linked to the **Directivity Index (DI)** through the relation $DI = 10 \log_{10}(Q)$, expressed in decibels (dB).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Benchmark Value:** For an ideal **omnidirectional point source**, $Q = 1$ (or $DI = 0$ dB) in all directions. Real-world sources (loudspeakers, machinery, human speech) exhibit $Q > 1$ along their principal axes.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are standing in a dark room with a single candle. The candle radiates light equally in all directions — this is the **omnidirectional** ideal case where every direction receives the same "intensity" of light, so $Q = 1$.

Now replace the candle with a **flashlight**. The flashlight concentrates its beam in a specific direction (say, forward). Along the beam axis, the intensity is much greater than the omnidirectional average. The flashlight's **Directivity Factor** is essentially the multiplier that tells you "how much louder/brighter does this source appear along its main axis compared to if it spread its energy uniformly in all directions?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** Consider a public-address (PA) speaker at a stadium concert. The speaker does not waste acoustic energy radiating toward the sky or backstage — it is designed to project sound predominantly toward the audience. This concentration yields $Q \gg 1$, allowing louder on-axis coverage for the same electrical input power, thus improving acoustic efficiency.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|
| **Reference Intensity** | $I_0$ | $10^{-12}$ W/m² (threshold of hearing) |
| **Reference Pressure** | $p_0$ | $20$ µPa |
| **Solid Angle of Full Sphere** | $\Omega_{total}$ | $4\pi$ steradians |
| **Directivity Index Unit** | $DI$ | decibels (dB) |
| **Directivity Factor** | $Q$ | dimensionless ratio |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The measurement of the Directivity Factor relies on three foundational pillars of acoustics:

- **Energy Conservation:** The total radiated acoustic power is fixed; redistributing it directionally increases intensity along some axes at the cost of others.
- **Inverse-Square Law Propagation:** In the far field, sound intensity falls off as $1/r^2$ for any localized source.
- **Spatial Averaging Principle:** The average intensity over an enclosing sphere equals the total radiated power divided by the surface area of that sphere.

To extract $Q$ experimentally, we must:

1. **Step 1 — Characterize the principal direction:** Measure the sound pressure (or intensity) along the axis where radiation is maximum.
2. **Step 2 — Sample the full sphere:** Acquire pressure/intensity readings at multiple angular positions around the source, maintaining a constant measurement radius $r$.
3. **Step 3 — Compute the spatial average:** Apply the spherical integration (or a discrete numerical approximation) to determine the mean intensity.
4. **Step 4 — Form the ratio:** Divide the principal-direction intensity by the mean intensity.

---

### 2.2 Why and How — Logical Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why":** A single on-axis SPL measurement is insufficient to characterize a source. A directional speaker and an omnidirectional speaker could produce the same on-axis SPL if fed different powers. We need a *relative* measure — and that is precisely what $Q$ provides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "How":** Because $Q$ is defined as a ratio, systematic errors (such as microphone calibration drift, atmospheric absorption, or background noise) tend to cancel when computing the ratio between two intensity readings taken under identical conditions.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{I_{principal}}{I_{avg}}$ | Fundamental definition of Directivity Factor | dimensionless |
| 2 | $DI = 10 \log_{10}(Q)$ | Directivity Index in decibels | dB |
| 3 | $I_{avg} = \dfrac{W}{4\pi r^2}$ | Average intensity at radius $r$ from total power $W$ | W/m² |
| 4 | $I_{principal} = \dfrac{Q \cdot W}{4\pi r^2}$ | Intensity at on-axis point at distance $r$ | W/m² |
| 5 | $L_{p,principal} = L_W + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{4\pi r^2}\right)$ | On-axis SPL from Sound Power Level | dB |
| 6 | $\Delta L_p = L_{p,principal} - L_{p,avg} = 10 \log_{10}(Q)$ | SPL difference equals Directivity Index | dB |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{4\pi}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{4\pi} \dfrac{d\Omega}{Q(\Omega)}}$ | Reciprocity-based global Q (rare in exams) | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Note:** All absolute-value or ratio bars have been rendered using LaTeX `\dfrac` and inline parentheses to avoid breaking markdown table parsing. Do **NOT** use the literal pipe character `|` inside table cells.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Architectural Acoustics:** Selecting appropriate loudspeaker types and aiming angles for auditoria and outdoor venues.
- **Environmental Noise Control:** Predicting noise immission at community receivers from directional industrial sources (cooling towers, exhaust stacks).
- **Hearing Conservation:** Estimating worker exposure from directional noise sources in factories.
- **Product Design:** Optimizing HVAC grille and exhaust port geometry to direct noise away from sensitive zones.
- **Vehicle Engineering:** Designing quieter tires and engine mounts by characterizing noise source directivity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Q from Spherical Integration

**Starting Premise:** Consider an acoustic source of total radiated sound power $W$ enclosed by an imaginary sphere of radius $r$ in the far field. The total radiated power can be expressed as the surface integral of intensity over the sphere:

$$W = \oint_{S} I(\theta, \phi) \, dS$$

In spherical coordinates, $dS = r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$, so:

$$W = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I(\theta, \phi) \, r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

The **average intensity** over the sphere is defined as total power divided by total surface area:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{W}{4\pi r^2}$$

Substituting the integral form of $W$:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{1}{4\pi r^2} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I(\theta, \phi) \, r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

Cancel $r^2$ from numerator and denominator:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{1}{4\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I(\theta, \phi) \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

By definition of directivity factor:

$$Q = \frac{I(\theta_0, \phi_0)}{I_{avg}}$$

Substituting $I_{avg}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{I(\theta_0, \phi_0)}{\dfrac{1}{4\pi} \displaystyle\int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I(\theta, \phi) \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi} \\[10pt]
&= \frac{4\pi \cdot I(\theta_0, \phi_0)}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I(\theta, \phi) \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **continuous-form master equation** for the Directivity Factor.

---

### 3.2 Discrete Numerical Approximation (Practical Form)

In laboratory measurement, the double integral is replaced by a discrete summation over $N$ sampling points distributed (ideally uniformly) over the sphere. The Gauss-type approximation yields:

$$
Q \approx \frac{I(\theta_0, \phi_0)}{\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} I(\theta_i, \phi_i) \cdot w_i}
$$

Where $w_i$ is the spherical weighting factor (often $w_i = \sin\theta_i$ for uniform sampling in colatitude).

---

### 3.3 Derivation Using Sound Pressure Levels

Since intensity is proportional to the mean-square sound pressure ($I \propto p_{rms}^2$), we can write:

$$Q = \frac{p_{rms}^2(\theta_0, \phi_0)}{\dfrac{1}{4\pi} \displaystyle\int p_{rms}^2(\theta, \phi) \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi}$$

In decibels, using $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_0)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 \log_{10}(Q) &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}^2(\theta_0, \phi_0)}{\overline{p_{rms}^2}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= L_{p,principal} - \overline{L_{p,energy-averaged}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note:** The "energy-averaged" SPL is **NOT** the simple arithmetic mean of SPL readings. It must be computed from the **mean of the squared pressures**, then converted back to dB. This is the most common KTU exam pitfall (see warning in Section 5).

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Relationship $L_p = L_W + 10 \log_{10}(Q / 4\pi r^2)$

Starting from $I_{principal} = Q \cdot W / (4\pi r^2)$, taking $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 \log_{10}(I_{principal}) &= 10 \log_{10}(W) + 10 \log_{10}(Q) - 10 \log_{10}(4\pi r^2) \\[6pt]
L_{I,principal} &= L_W + DI - 10 \log_{10}(4\pi r^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since for plane/progressive waves $L_p \approx L_I$ (intensity level), we obtain the standard KTU formula:

$$\boxed{L_p = L_W + 10 \log_{10}(Q) - 10 \log_{10}(4\pi r^2)}$$

This equation is the foundation for indirect measurement of $Q$: measure $L_p$ at known $r$, and given (or independently measured) $L_W$, solve for $Q$.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A loudspeaker is measured in an anechoic chamber. Sound pressure levels recorded at distance $r = 2$ m on the principal axis and at ten other points on the measurement sphere are:

| Angle (θ) | $L_p$ (dB) |
|-----------|------------|
| 0° (principal) | 92.0 |
| 36° | 89.5 |
| 72° | 85.0 |
| 108° | 81.0 |
| 144° | 79.0 |
| 180° | 78.5 |

Assume the spherical mean of squared pressures yields an "energy-averaged" SPL of $\overline{L_p} = 84.3$ dB. Compute $Q$ and $DI$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DI &= L_{p,principal} - \overline{L_p} = 92.0 - 84.3 = 7.7 \text{ dB} \\[6pt]
Q &= 10^{DI/10} = 10^{7.7/10} = 10^{0.77} \\[6pt]
Q &\approx 5.888
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The loudspeaker is roughly **5.9 times more intense** on its principal axis than an omnidirectional radiator of equal total power. This is typical for a moderate-directivity mid-range speaker.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Measurement Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Position Source in Anechoic Chamber] --> B[Set Measurement Radius r in Far Field]
    B --> C[Measure SPL on Principal Axis at angle 0 deg]
    C --> D[Record as Lp Principal]
    D --> E[Rotate Source or Microphone around Sphere]
    E --> F[Sample N Discrete Angular Positions]
    F --> G[Convert Each SPL to Pressure Squared]
    G --> H[Compute Energy Averaged Mean of p squared]
    H --> I[Convert Back to Mean SPL in dB]
    I --> J[Subtract Lp Principal minus Mean Lp]
    J --> K[Obtain DI in dB]
    K --> L[Compute Q equals 10 power DI by 10]
    L --> M[Validate via Reciprocity or Repeat at Multiple Radii]
    M --> N[End: Report Q and DI]
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Directivity Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Source Under Test]
        S1[Acoustic Source]
    end
    subgraph MIC[Microphone Array]
        M1[Microphone Position 1]
        M2[Microphone Position 2]
        M3[Microphone Position N]
    end
    subgraph DAQ[Data Acquisition and Signal Conditioning]
        A1[Preamplifier]
        A2[Bandpass Filter]
        A3[ADC and Computer]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Post Processing]
        P1[SPL Calculator]
        P2[Energy Averager]
        P3[Directivity Factor Calculator]
        P4[DI in dB Output]
    end
    S1 --> M1
    S1 --> M2
    S1 --> M3
    M1 --> A1
    M2 --> A1
    M3 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic — Spherical Sampling Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C[Source at Origin] --> P0[Point at theta 0]
    C --> P1[Point at theta 30]
    C --> P2[Point at theta 60]
    C --> P3[Point at theta 90]
    C --> P4[Point at theta 120]
    C --> P5[Point at theta 150]
    C --> P6[Point at theta 180]
    P0 --> R[Constant Radius r for all points]
    P1 --> R
    P2 --> R
    P3 --> R
    P4 --> R
    P5 --> R
    P6 --> R
    R --> CALC[Compute Energy Average and Directivity Factor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `SRC`, `MIC`, `P0`, `CALC`) to comply with Mermaid parser safeguards. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) are used as standalone node IDs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Define the Directivity Factor of an acoustic source. State its value for an omnidirectional point source.

**Model Answer:**
The Directivity Factor $Q$ of an acoustic source is defined as the ratio of the sound intensity radiated in a specified direction (usually the principal direction) to the average intensity radiated by the source over all directions, when the total radiated acoustic power is held constant.

Mathematically:

$$Q = \frac{I_{principal}}{I_{avg}}$$

For an ideal **omnidirectional point source**, the intensity is uniform in every direction, so $I_{principal} = I_{avg}$, giving $Q = 1$ (or equivalently, $DI = 0$ dB).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is the Directivity Index? How is it related to the Directivity Factor?

**Model Answer:**
The Directivity Index (DI) is the decibel representation of the Directivity Factor. It expresses, in logarithmic units, how much louder (in level) a source appears on its principal axis compared to an omnidirectional source radiating the same total power.

The relationship is:

$$DI = 10 \log_{10}(Q) \quad \text{(in dB)}$$

Conversely, $Q = 10^{DI/10}$. For example, a source with $DI = 10$ dB has $Q = 10$, meaning it is ten times more intense along its axis.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the aid of a clear derivation, explain how the Directivity Factor of an acoustic source can be determined from sound pressure level measurements taken on a measurement sphere in an anechoic chamber. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A directional loudspeaker is tested in an anechoic chamber. The sound power level of the loudspeaker is measured as $L_W = 90$ dB re 1 pW. At a distance of 4 m on the principal axis, the sound pressure level is $L_p = 78$ dB re 20 µPa. Calculate:
  (i) The Directivity Factor $Q$ of the loudspeaker
  (ii) The Directivity Index $DI$ in dB
  (iii) The sound pressure level that would be measured at the **same distance** if the source were omnidirectional with the same power. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Place the source at the center of an imaginary sphere of radius $r$ in the free field (anechoic) environment. The total radiated acoustic power $W$ equals the surface integral of intensity:

$$
W = \oint_S I(\theta, \phi)\, dS = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} I(\theta, \phi)\, r^2 \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi
$$

Average intensity on the sphere:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{W}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{1}{4\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} I(\theta, \phi)\sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi$$

By definition of Directivity Factor at the principal direction $(\theta_0, \phi_0) = (0, 0)$:

$$Q = \frac{I(\theta_0, \phi_0)}{I_{avg}}$$

Using $I \propto p_{rms}^2$, this becomes:

$$Q = \frac{p_{rms}^2(0, 0)}{\overline{p_{rms}^2}}$$

Converting to decibels:

$$10\log_{10}(Q) = L_{p,principal} - \overline{L_p}$$

Hence, in practice, we measure SPL at multiple points on a sphere, compute the **energy-averaged SPL** (mean of $p^2$, not arithmetic mean of dB), and subtract it from the on-axis SPL to obtain $DI = 10\log_{10}(Q)$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $L_W = 90$ dB, $L_p = 78$ dB, $r = 4$ m.

Use the standard free-field equation for a directional source:

$$L_p = L_W + 10\log_{10}(Q) - 10\log_{10}(4\pi r^2)$$

Compute the geometric term:

$$10\log_{10}(4\pi r^2) = 10\log_{10}(4\pi \times 16) = 10\log_{10}(201.06) \approx 23.03 \text{ dB}$$

Rearranging for $DI$:

$$DI = L_p - L_W + 10\log_{10}(4\pi r^2) = 78 - 90 + 23.03 = 11.03 \text{ dB}$$

(i) Directivity Factor:

$$Q = 10^{DI/10} = 10^{1.103} \approx 12.67$$

(ii) Directivity Index: $DI \approx 11.0$ dB

(iii) For an omnidirectional source ($Q = 1$), at the same $r = 4$ m:

$$L_{p,omni} = L_W - 10\log_{10}(4\pi r^2) = 90 - 23.03 = 66.97 \text{ dB} \approx 67.0 \text{ dB}$$

The loudspeaker therefore produces about 11 dB more SPL on-axis than an omnidirectional source of equal power.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Alternative Module Question]**

**(a)** Describe the experimental procedure for measuring the Directivity Factor of a noise source in an anechoic chamber. List the major precautions to be observed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machinery noise source is tested in an anechoic chamber. The following SPL readings (in dB re 20 µPa) are obtained at constant radius $r = 3$ m at 8 angular positions (uniformly distributed in azimuth at $\theta = 90°$):

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|----------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $L_p$ (dB) | 84 | 82 | 78 | 75 | 73 | 74 | 76 | 80 |

Position 1 is the principal axis. Calculate the Directivity Factor and Directivity Index. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Test Environment:** Conduct the test in a fully anechoic chamber to eliminate reflections (anechoic wedge lining on all surfaces ensures $\alpha \approx 1$).
2. **Source Positioning:** Place the source on a turntable or use a microphone boom rotating around the source; the source should be in the geometric center of the working volume.
3. **Far-Field Criterion:** Ensure measurement radius $r$ satisfies $r \geq 2D^2/\lambda$, where $D$ is the largest source dimension and $\lambda$ is the wavelength of interest.
4. **Angular Sampling:** Measure SPL at a sufficient number of angles (typically 10–30 points) covering the full sphere; use equal-area weighting or solid-angle weighting.
5. **Constant Radius:** All measurements must be taken at the same distance $r$ from the acoustic center.
6. **Background Noise:** Maintain background noise at least 10 dB below the lowest measured SPL.
7. **Repeatability:** Average over multiple microphone positions or repeat trials to reduce random error.
8. **Precautions:** Calibrate microphones before and after each measurement; maintain steady-state source operation; correct for atmospheric absorption (especially at high frequencies and large $r$); avoid obstructing the acoustic field with the microphone or operator.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given the SPL readings, first convert each to **pressure squared** (relative to $p_0^2$):

$p_i^2 / p_0^2 = 10^{L_{p,i}/10}$

| Position | $L_p$ (dB) | $p_i^2/p_0^2$ |
|----------|------------|----------------|
| 1 (principal) | 84 | $10^{8.4} = 2.512 \times 10^{8}$ |
| 2 | 82 | $10^{8.2} = 1.585 \times 10^{8}$ |
| 3 | 78 | $10^{7.8} = 6.310 \times 10^{7}$ |
| 4 | 75 | $10^{7.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{7}$ |
| 5 | 73 | $10^{7.3} = 1.995 \times 10^{7}$ |
| 6 | 74 | $10^{7.4} = 2.512 \times 10^{7}$ |
| 7 | 76 | $10^{7.6} = 3.981 \times 10^{7}$ |
| 8 | 80 | $10^{8.0} = 1.000 \times 10^{8}$ |

Sum of $p_i^2 / p_0^2$:

$$\sum = (2.512 + 1.585 + 0.631 + 0.3162 + 0.1995 + 0.2512 + 0.3981 + 1.000) \times 10^{8}$$

$$\sum = 6.892 \times 10^{8}$$

Energy-averaged value:

$$\overline{p^2 / p_0^2} = \frac{6.892 \times 10^{8}}{8} = 8.615 \times 10^{7}$$

Convert back to dB:

$$\overline{L_p} = 10\log_{10}(8.615 \times 10^{7}) = 10(7 + \log_{10} 8.615) = 10(7 + 0.9352) = 79.35 \text{ dB}$$

Compute Directivity Index:

$$DI = L_{p,principal} - \overline{L_p} = 84 - 79.35 = 4.65 \text{ dB}$$

Compute Directivity Factor:

$$Q = 10^{DI/10} = 10^{0.465} \approx 2.92$$

**Result:** The machinery noise source has $Q \approx 2.9$ and $DI \approx 4.6$ dB. This is a mildly directional source.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Averaging in dB directly** — Students often compute the arithmetic mean of SPL readings in dB. This is WRONG. The averaging must be done on the *intensity* (or $p^2$) scale, then converted back to dB. This single error can cost 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $10\log_{10}(4\pi r^2)$ geometric term** — When using $L_p = L_W + 10\log_{10}(Q) - 10\log_{10}(4\pi r^2)$, omitting the spherical spreading term is a frequent slip.
> 3. **Confusing $DI$ with $Q$** — $Q$ is dimensionless; $DI$ is in dB. Mixing units will be penalized.
> 4. **Far-field violations** — Not mentioning the $r \geq 2D^2/\lambda$ criterion in procedure questions leads to partial credit loss.
> 5. **Wrong sign convention** — $DI = 10\log_{10}(Q)$ only because $Q \geq 1$. If a student somehow computes $Q < 1$, they should immediately realize a numerical error has occurred.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Directivity Factor $Q$** is dimensionless; **Directivity Index $DI$** is in decibels. They are linked by $DI = 10\log_{10}(Q)$.
- **Reference value:** $Q = 1$, $DI = 0$ dB for an **ideal omnidirectional point source**.
- **Measurement requirement:** Must be performed in an **anechoic chamber** (or outdoors with negligible reflections) at the **far field** radius satisfying $r \geq 2D^2/\lambda$.
- **Procedure essentials:** Spherical sampling, constant radius, energy-domain averaging, principal-axis identification.
- **Core equation to memorize:**
  $$L_p = L_W + 10\log_{10}(Q) - 10\log_{10}(4\pi r^2)$$
- **Key relationship:**
  $$DI = L_{p,principal} - \overline{L_p} \quad \text{(energy-averaged)}$$
- **Energy-averaging rule:** Always convert SPL → $p^2/p_0^2$ → mean → convert back to dB. **Never** arithmetic-average in dB directly.
- **Engineering significance:** $Q$ governs how effectively a source concentrates its acoustic energy; essential for loudspeaker design, environmental noise mapping, and hearing conservation.
- **Quick sanity check:** $Q \geq 1$ always; higher $Q$ means more directional source.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise Control at Source
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Noise Control at Source

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Noise Control at Source** is defined as the engineering discipline and primary hierarchical strategy in noise management that focuses on **reducing or eliminating the acoustic energy generated by the noise-producing mechanism itself**, before the sound wave propagates into the surrounding environment. According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with ISO 11690 and ISO 15667 standards, source control involves modifying the **generation mechanism** (force, velocity, or flow-induced turbulence), the **transmission structure**, or the **radiating surface** of the source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hierarchy of Noise Control (Mandatory Order):**
> 1. **Source Control** (Most effective — highest priority in KTU valuation)
> 2. **Path Control** (Along transmission)
> 3. **Receiver Control** (At the listener — last resort)
>
> Examiner's mantra: *Always attack the source first.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a leaking water tap in your kitchen.

- **Source Control** = Fixing the tap washer so water stops leaking (eliminates the problem at its origin).
- **Path Control** = Putting a towel under the tap (controls the water flow).
- **Receiver Control** = Moving the bucket away (protects only the receiver).

A student easily understands that **fixing the tap is cheaper and better than drying the floor repeatedly**. Similarly, in industry, reducing noise from a noisy machine is far more efficient than building soundproof walls or providing ear protection to every worker.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used (KTU Board Favourites):**
> - **Sound Power Level ($L_W$)** in **dB re 10⁻¹² W** — *fundamental descriptor of a source*.
> - **Sound Pressure Level ($L_p$)** in **dB re 20 µPa** — *measured at a receiver*.
> - **Insertion Loss (IL)** in **dB** — *reduction achieved after applying control*.
> - **Transmission Loss (TL)** in **dB** — *barrier effectiveness*.
> - **Natural Frequency ($f_n$)** in **Hz** — *critical for isolation design*.
> - **Critical Damping Ratio ($\zeta$)** — *dimensionless damping measure*.

## 1.3 Why Source Control is Most Effective

A **3 dB reduction at the source** doubles the permissible distance between the source and the receiver for the same exposure, while a **10 dB source reduction** is perceived as a halving of loudness. Source control also avoids the cascading logistics of retrofitting path or receiver controls throughout a facility.

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Question Pattern:** "Why is noise control at source preferred over control at the receiver?" — Always answer with reference to **cost, permanence, and effectiveness**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchy of Source Control Techniques

The discipline is classified into **five primary engineering strategies**. Each is discussed with the underlying physical mechanism.

### Strategy A — Source Substitution / Process Modification
- Replace a noisy process with a quieter equivalent.
- *Examples*: Hydraulic press → mechanical press; riveting → welding; gear drives → belt drives; metal cutting → water-jet cutting.
- **Why it works:** Replaces high-energy impulsive forces with continuous low-energy operations.

### Strategy B — Design Modification of the Source
- **Balancing** rotating components to eliminate tonal excitation.
- **Reducing impact velocity** (lowering drop heights of materials).
- **Smoothing airflow** to suppress turbulence (laminar flow design).
- **Sizing optimization**: larger, slower machines are typically quieter than small, high-speed units.
- *Physical principle:* Noise from a source scales roughly as $L_W \propto 10\log_{10}(v^n)$ where $v$ is the characteristic velocity and $n$ is between 5 and 7 for aerodynamic sources.

### Strategy C — Reduction of Force Excitation
- Decrease the magnitude or frequency of exciting forces.
- *Examples*: Replace reciprocating compressor with rotary screw compressor; use flexible couplings; replace impact hammers with hydraulic actuators.
- **Why it works:** Acoustic power radiated is proportional to the **square of the force amplitude** and the **radiation efficiency**.

### Strategy D — Vibration Isolation at the Source
- Interpose an elastic element between the source and its support.
- The system natural frequency must be **substantially below the excitation frequency**.
- *Typical isolation efficiency:* 80–95% when $f/f_n \geq 3$ to $5$.

### Strategy E — Damping and Absorption
- Apply **viscoelastic damping materials** to vibrating panels.
- Use **constrained layer damping (CLD)** to suppress structural resonances.
- *Why it works:* Converts mechanical vibration energy into heat, reducing the **radiation efficiency** of the surface.

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Industry Sector | Source Control Technique | Typical IL Achieved |
|------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Automotive | Engine encapsulation | 8–15 dB |
| HVAC | Duct silencers, lined plenums | 5–20 dB |
| Manufacturing | Vibration isolators under presses | 10–25 dB |
| Power Generation | Exhaust mufflers | 15–30 dB |
| Construction | Pneumatic tool silencers | 10–20 dB |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Master Table — Memorise these for ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $L_W = L_p + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{S_0}\right)$ | Sound power to pressure conversion | $S$ = surface area (m²), $S_0 = 1$ m² |
| 2 | $L_W = \overline{L_p} + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi r^2}{S_0}\right)$ | Free-field hemispherical source | $r$ = distance (m) |
| 3 | $IL = L_{\text{before}} - L_{\text{after}}$ | Insertion Loss of treatment | in dB |
| 4 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of isolator | $k$ = stiffness (N/m), $m$ = mass (kg) |
| 5 | $TR = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Transmissibility | $r = f/f_n$, $\zeta$ = damping ratio |
| 6 | $r = \dfrac{f}{f_n}$ | Frequency ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $L_W = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right)$ | Sound power level definition | $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W |
| 8 | $TL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega m}{2\rho c}\right)$ | Mass law for barriers | $\omega = 2\pi f$, $\rho c$ = acoustic impedance |
| 9 | $L_{\text{eq}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L_p/10}\,dt\right)$ | Equivalent continuous SPL | time-varying noise |
| 10 | $L_{p,\text{doubling}} = L_{p1} - 6\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | Point-source doubling rule | inverse-square law |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Board:** *"WPS-FK-TR-r-TL-EQ-DB"* — these 10 formulas cover ~90% of numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transmissibility of a Vibration Isolator

We consider a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) system comprising a mass $m$ supported on a spring of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper of damping coefficient $c$. The source excites the base with displacement $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 — Equation of Motion**

Newton's second law on the mass $m$ gives:

$$
m\ddot{y} + c(\dot{y} - \dot{x}) + k(y - x) = 0
$$

where $y(t)$ is the absolute displacement of the mass.

**Step 2 — Introduce Relative Coordinate**

Let $z = y - x$ represent the relative displacement transmitted through the isolator.

Substituting and rearranging:

$$
m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{x}
$$

**Step 3 — Steady-State Harmonic Solution**

Assume $z(t) = Z\sin(\omega t - \phi)$. The amplitude ratio is obtained by substituting into the equation and equating coefficients. The classical result is:

$$
TR = \left\vert \frac{Z}{X} \right\vert = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}
$$

where:

- $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{f}{f_n}$ is the frequency ratio
- $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ is the damping ratio

**Step 4 — Interpretation for Design**

For **good vibration isolation** (TR < 1), we need $r > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. In practice, designers target:

- $r \geq 3$ for **95% isolation** (TR ≈ 0.10, i.e., 20 dB reduction)
- $r \geq 5$ for **99% isolation** (TR ≈ 0.04, i.e., 28 dB reduction)

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A compressor of mass 80 kg is to be isolated from its foundation. The disturbing frequency is 50 Hz. Design a spring isolator that gives at least **95% isolation efficiency**.

**Step 1 — Target Frequency Ratio**

For 95% isolation, we need $r \geq 3$. Therefore:

$$
f_n \leq \frac{f}{r} = \frac{50}{3} = 16.67 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Required Stiffness**

$$
f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \implies k = (2\pi f_n)^2 \cdot m
$$

$$
k = (2\pi \times 16.67)^2 \times 80
$$

$$
k = (104.72)^2 \times 80 = 10966 \times 80 = 877{,}280 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 3 — Static Deflection**

$$
\delta = \frac{mg}{k} = \frac{80 \times 9.81}{877{,}280} = 8.95 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.9 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Isolation Efficiency**

With $r = 3$ and assuming low damping ($\zeta \approx 0.05$):

$$
TR = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2 \times 0.05 \times 3)^2}{(1-9)^2 + (2 \times 0.05 \times 3)^2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + 0.09}{64 + 0.09}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.09}{64.09}} \approx 0.130
$$

Isolation efficiency $\eta = (1 - TR) \times 100\% = 87\%$.

To reach 95% efficiency, increase $r$ to about 4 (i.e., reduce $f_n$ to 12.5 Hz).

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Scenario | Best Source Control Method | Reason |
|----------|---------------------------|--------|
| Reciprocating pump | Replace with rotary pump | Eliminates cyclic impulse forces |
| Sheet-metal stamping | Use rubber die cushion | Reduces impact velocity |
| High-speed fan | Reshape blade trailing edge | Reduces turbulence-induced noise |
| Diesel generator | Install exhaust muffler + vibration mounts | Dual attack on radiated + structure-borne noise |
| Gear mesh whine | Improve tooth profile / lubrication | Reduces excitation amplitude |
| Pipe vibration | Add damping clamp + flexible section | Reduces radiation efficiency |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Hint:** Always state the **physical principle** (force reduction, velocity reduction, radiation efficiency reduction) before naming the technique.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Source Control Strategy Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete decision flow an engineer should follow when applying noise control at the source.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Noise Source Identified] --> B{Process Substitution Possible?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Replace Noisy Process]:::good
    B -->|No| D{Reduce Excitation Force?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Modify Design, Balance, Smoothing]:::good
    D -->|No| F{Vibration Isolation Feasible?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Install Spring or Elastomer Mount]:::good
    F -->|No| H{Damping Required?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Apply Viscoelastic Damping]:::good
    H -->|No| J[Fall Back to Path Control]

    C --> K[Measure IL in dB]:::check
    E --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
    K --> L{IL Satisfactory?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Done]
    L -->|No| B

    classDef good fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#1b4332
    classDef check fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#a0791f,stroke-width:2px,color:#5c4400
```

## 4.2 Vibration Isolation System — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Disturbance Source: Motor/Compressor]:::src -->|Force F(t)| SPRING[Spring Element: k N/m]:::spring
    SPRING --> DAMPER[Damper: c Ns/m]:::damp
    DAMPER --> MASS[Rigid Mass: m kg]:::mass
    MASS -->|Residual Vibration| FOUND[Foundation]:::fnd

    SPRING -.-> KEQ[Stiffness k controls f_n]:::note
    DAMPER -.-> DEQ[Damping Ratio ζ]:::note
    MASS -.-> MEQ[Mass m affects f_n]:::note

    FOUND --> ENV[Acoustic Environment]:::env

    classDef src fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#9d0208,stroke-width:2px,color:#370617
    classDef spring fill:#e0eaff,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a1f44
    classDef damp fill:#fff0d6,stroke:#9c6f00,stroke-width:2px,color:#4a3300
    classDef mass fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#081c15
    classDef fnd fill:#e9ecef,stroke:#495057,stroke-width:2px,color:#212529
    classDef env fill:#f3d5b5,stroke:#8b4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#3e1f00
    classDef note fill:#fdf0ff,stroke:#7b2cbf,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#3c096c
```

## 4.3 Source-Path-Receiver Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[Noise Source] -->|L_W| PATH[Transmission Path]
    PATH -->|L_p, attenuated| REC[Receiver]
    SRC -.->|Control 1| SRC1[Source Control Methods]
    PATH -.->|Control 2| PATH1[Path Control: Barriers, Absorbers]
    REC -.->|Control 3| REC1[Receiver Control: PPE, Distance]

    SRC1 -.-> A[Highest Priority]
    PATH1 -.-> B[Medium Priority]
    REC1 -.-> C[Last Resort]

    classDef src fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#9d0208,stroke-width:2px,color:#370617
    classDef pth fill:#ffe9b3,stroke:#a0791f,stroke-width:2px,color:#5c4400
    classDef rec fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a1f44
    classDef c1 fill:#b7e4c7,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#081c15
    classDef c2 fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#a0791f,stroke-width:2px,color:#5c4400
    classDef c3 fill:#cdb4db,stroke:#5a189a,stroke-width:2px,color:#240046

    class SRC,SRC1 c1
    class PATH,PATH1 c2
    class REC,REC1 c3
    class A,B,C src,pth,rec
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Why is noise control at source preferred over control at the path or receiver?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Permanent solution:** Eliminates the noise at origin rather than mitigating it downstream, providing permanent relief without recurring maintenance.
2. **Energy efficient:** Requires less material, less space, and lower operating cost than building enclosures or supplying PPE to all workers.
3. **Broadest impact:** A single source-level reduction of 10 dB reduces the sound pressure at every receiver location, whereas a barrier protects only one area.
4. *Conclusion:* It is the **most cost-effective, technically efficient, and universally beneficial** noise control strategy, hence ranked first in the KTU hierarchy.

*(Distribution: 1 mark per point + 1 mark for conclusion = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"List any three methods of reducing noise at the source in industrial machinery."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Balancing rotating components** to eliminate tonal excitation from mass eccentricity.
2. **Replacing reciprocating machines with rotary machines** to suppress impulsive forces.
3. **Improving lubrication** in bearings and gear meshes to reduce friction-induced vibration.
4. *(Optional 4th)* Using vibration isolators and damping treatments.

*(Distribution: 1 mark per method = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When asked about source control, students often describe path or receiver control methods (barriers, ear muffs). This is **marked as irrelevant content** and attracts **zero marks**. Always tie your answer to **modifying the source mechanism itself**.

---

### Question A — Part (a): 7 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** *Explain the different methods of noise control at source with suitable engineering examples.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Process Substitution (2 marks)**
Replacing noisy processes with inherently quieter alternatives. *Examples:* riveting by welding, gear drives by belt drives, pneumatic hammers by hydraulic presses, metal-to-metal impacts by cushioned transfers. This eliminates impulsive forces that dominate acoustic spectra.

**(ii) Design Modification (2 marks)**
Altering geometry and operating conditions. *Examples:* balancing rotating shafts; reshaping fan blades to reduce turbulence; reducing drop heights of materials; using low-noise saw blades; smoothing airflow paths; replacing impact with continuous rolling.

**(iii) Vibration Isolation (2 marks)**
Mounting the source on resilient elements (springs, elastomers, rubber pads) to prevent vibration energy from entering the structure. The isolator's natural frequency $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ must be well below the excitation frequency for effective isolation.

**(iv) Damping Treatment (1 mark)**
Application of viscoelastic damping materials or constrained-layer damping to vibrating surfaces to dissipate vibrational energy as heat, thereby reducing the radiated sound power.

---

### Question A — Part (b): 7 Marks

**A machine of mass 120 kg operates at 1200 RPM and is to be isolated with 90% efficiency. Calculate the required stiffness and static deflection of the isolator. Take damping ratio $\zeta = 0$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert RPM to Hz**

$$
f = \frac{1200}{60} = 20 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Required Frequency Ratio for 90% Efficiency**

For 90% isolation, TR = 0.10. Solving $TR = \dfrac{1}{r^2 - 1}$ for $r$:

$$
0.10 = \frac{1}{r^2 - 1} \implies r^2 - 1 = 10 \implies r = \sqrt{11} \approx 3.32
$$

**Step 3 — Required Natural Frequency**

$$
f_n = \frac{f}{r} = \frac{20}{3.32} = 6.02 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Required Stiffness**

$$
k = (2\pi f_n)^2 \cdot m = (2\pi \times 6.02)^2 \times 120
$$

$$
k = (37.83)^2 \times 120 = 1431.3 \times 120 = 171{,}756 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 5 — Static Deflection**

$$
\delta = \frac{mg}{k} = \frac{120 \times 9.81}{171{,}756} = 6.85 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 6.85 \text{ mm}
$$

*(Distribution: Step 1 → 1 mark, Step 2 → 2 marks, Step 3 → 1 mark, Step 4 → 2 marks, Step 5 → 1 mark = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B — Part (a): 7 Marks *(Alternative Choice)*

**Discuss the role of vibration isolation and damping in noise control at source. Derive the expression for transmissibility of an isolation system.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Vibration isolation and damping attack the **structure-borne** pathway by which source vibration radiates sound. They are essential when direct source modification is impractical.

**Damping Concept (2 marks):**
Damping converts vibrational kinetic energy into heat through viscoelastic action. Common forms include:
- Free-layer damping (viscoelastic coating on a panel)
- Constrained-layer damping (sandwich with stiff constraining layer)
- Tuned mass dampers (resonant absorber)

**Vibration Isolation Theory (3 marks):**
For an SDOF system with mass $m$, stiffness $k$, damping $c$, and base excitation, the equation of motion is:

$$
m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{x}
$$

where $z = y - x$ is the relative displacement. The transmissibility magnitude is:

$$
TR = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}
$$

**Design Criterion (1 mark):**
For effective isolation, $r = f/f_n > \sqrt{2}$, and the damping ratio $\zeta$ should be small (typically $\zeta \leq 0.05$) to avoid high-frequency amplification.

---

### Question B — Part (b): 7 Marks *(Alternative Choice)*

**A diesel generator of mass 600 kg operates at 1500 RPM. Design a spring isolation system such that 95% of vibration is isolated. Assume $\zeta = 0$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Operating Frequency**

$$
f = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Required Frequency Ratio for 95% Isolation**

TR = 0.05:

$$
0.05 = \frac{1}{r^2 - 1} \implies r^2 = 21 \implies r = 4.58
$$

**Step 3 — Natural Frequency**

$$
f_n = \frac{25}{4.58} = 5.46 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Stiffness**

$$
k = (2\pi \times 5.46)^2 \times 600 = (34.31)^2 \times 600 = 1177.2 \times 600 = 706{,}320 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 5 — Static Deflection**

$$
\delta = \frac{600 \times 9.81}{706{,}320} = 8.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 8.33 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Spring Selection (Optional)**
Each spring: $k_{\text{each}} = 706{,}320 / 4 = 176{,}580$ N/m (for 4-spring mounting system).

*(Distribution: Step 1 → 1 mark, Step 2 → 2 marks, Step 3 → 1 mark, Step 4 → 2 marks, Step 5 → 1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Source Control (KTU Module 4)**

- ☐ **Hierarchy Order:** **Source → Path → Receiver** (this order is the most-favoured KTU question).
- ☐ **Three Core Strategies:** Reduce **Force**, Reduce **Velocity**, Reduce **Radiation Efficiency**.
- ☐ **Key Formula:** $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ — *appears in nearly every numerical*.
- ☐ **Transmissibility Formula:** $TR = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ — *memorise the numerator and denominator*.
- ☐ **Design Rule:** Always design for $r \geq 3$ for >85% isolation.
- ☐ **Damping Rule:** Low damping ($\zeta \leq 0.05$) is preferred in the isolation region ($r > \sqrt{2}$); high damping is needed only near resonance ($r \approx 1$).
- ☐ **Numerical Tip:** Convert **RPM to Hz by dividing by 60** before any calculation.
- ☐ **Cost Hierarchy:** Source control < Path control (in the long term) due to lower recurring PPE costs and broader coverage.
- ☐ **Common Mistake:** Using *receiver control* (ear muffs) as an answer to a *source control* question → **0 marks**.
- ☐ **Standards Reference:** ISO 11690 and ISO 15667 govern industrial noise control at source.
- ☐ **Typical IL Values:** 5–15 dB from substitution, 10–25 dB from isolation, 8–20 dB from damping.
- ☐ **A-Weighting:** Always use dB(A) when comparing against occupational exposure limits (e.g., 85 dB(A) for 8 hours).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Board Strategy:** Begin every answer by **stating the hierarchy principle**, then identify which strategy addresses the **specific physical mechanism** (force, velocity, or radiation) of the given source. This guarantees the *approach mark* even before the detailed answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control During Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control During Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Noise Control During Transmission** refers to the set of engineering strategies and physical interventions implemented *along the propagation path* of sound energy, *between* the noise source and the receiver, to attenuate acoustic energy before it reaches the listener or sensitive zone. It is the second of the three classical hierarchical noise control strategies (Source → Path → Receiver), governed by the principle that the most cost-effective mitigation occurs at the source; transmission control is employed when source modification is impractical.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, transmission control encompasses:
- **Noise Barriers** (diffraction-suppressing screens placed in the propagation path)
- **Acoustic Enclosures** (fully encapsulating partial or full enclosures around machinery)
- **Acoustic Lined Ducts / Splitter Silencers** (dissipative lining inside gas-conveying passages)
- **Reactive / Reflective Silencers** (expansion chambers and side-branch resonators)
- **Building Envelope Treatments** (walls, doors, windows designed for airborne sound insulation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Standard):**
> - **Transmission Loss (TL)** — measured in decibels (dB), the airborne sound insulation of a partition at a given frequency.
> - **Sound Transmission Class (STC)** — single-number rating widely used in architectural acoustics.
> - **Insertion Loss (IL)** — actual reduction achieved at a receiver point after a control device is installed.
> - **Noise Reduction (NR)** — space-averaged reduction inside a room, equal to TL plus a boundary-correction term.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Noise Wall" Intuition

Imagine standing in your backyard chatting with a friend, while a busy highway roars just beyond a chain-link fence. You can hear the traffic clearly because sound waves travel unimpeded through the openings in the fence.

Now suppose the municipal corporation erects a solid, tall concrete wall between the highway and your house. The wall does not *eliminate* the traffic noise at the source — the engines still roar — but the sound waves must now travel over, around, or *through* a heavy, dense obstacle. Most of the acoustic energy is either reflected back, absorbed within the wall, or forced to take a longer diffraction path over the top edge. The result is a dramatic drop in the noise reaching your ears.

This is the essence of **control during transmission**: rather than silencing the engine (source) or plugging your ears (receiver), we insert an obstacle in the middle to physically disrupt the sound wave's journey.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world KTU-relevant examples:**
> - Highway noise barriers along NH-66 in Kerala (e.g., Vyttila, Edappally corridors)
> - Acoustic enclosures around DG sets in commercial buildings
> - Splitter silencers inside HVAC ducts of hospital OTs and IT park AC plants
> - Double-glazed windows in airport-adjacent hotels near Trivandrum/Cochin airports

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Metrics

The following standard metrics govern transmission control design and must be memorized:

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transmission Loss | $TL$ | **dB** | Quantifies partition insulation |
| Insertion Loss | $IL$ | **dB** | Measures in-situ device performance |
| Noise Reduction | $NR$ | **dB** | Indoor space-averaged reduction |
| Mass per unit area | $m$ | **kg/m²** | Governs mass-law behavior |
| Critical frequency | $f_c$ | **Hz** | Marks coincidence dip |
| Air density (20°C) | $\rho_0$ = **1.204 kg/m³** | — | Standard reference |
| Speed of sound (20°C) | $c$ = **343 m/s** | — | Reference value |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Transmission Path — Three Routes Sound Can Take

When a sound wave encounters a partition (wall, barrier, enclosure), its energy splits into three directional categories:

1. **Transmitted Energy ($W_t$):** Energy that passes *through* the partition by vibrating it.
2. **Reflected Energy ($W_r$):** Energy bounced back into the source-side room.
3. **Absorbed Energy ($W_a$):** Energy dissipated as heat within the partition's internal damping.

By conservation of incident energy $W_i$:

$$W_i = W_t + W_r + W_a$$

The **Transmission Coefficient** $\tau$ (a dimensionless, frequency-dependent quantity) is defined as:

$$\tau = \frac{W_t}{W_i}$$

Transmission Loss is then formally defined as:

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\right) \;\; \text{dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A higher $TL$ value means *better* insulation. Doubling the frequency or quadrupling the surface mass raises $TL$ by 6 dB (the mass law, derived in Section 3).

### 2.2 The Three Architectural Zones of Transmission Control

**Zone 1 — Outdoor (Free-field) Transmission:**
- Sound radiates from a source into open air.
- The dominant control device is the **noise barrier**.
- Geometric spreading ($L_p \propto 20 \log_{10} r$ from a point source) reduces level naturally with distance; barriers exploit this by blocking line-of-sight.

**Zone 2 — Duct / Pipe Transmission:**
- Sound propagates within a confined gas column (HVAC ducts, exhaust stacks).
- Devices used: **splitter silencers**, **dissipative lined ducts**, **reactive expansion chambers**, **Helmholtz resonators**.

**Zone 3 — Building Element Transmission:**
- Sound crosses solid partitions between rooms (walls, floors, doors).
- Controlled by: **Mass addition**, **double-leaf construction with air gap**, **staggered studs**, **resilient isolation clips**, **mass-loaded vinyl (MLV)**.

### 2.3 Control Mechanisms — How Devices Reduce Transmission

| Mechanism | Physical Principle | Typical Device | TL Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass loading | Reflective mass-law behavior | Thick masonry, lead sheet | 4–6 dB per doubling of $m$ |
| Dissipation | Viscous friction in porous material | Mineral wool duct liner | Broadband attenuation in ducts |
| Reactance | Impedance mismatch / resonance | Expansion chamber, Helmholtz | Tuned narrowband attenuation |
| Decoupling | Breaks vibrational continuity | Resilient channels, double walls | 10–20 dB uplift at mid frequencies |
| Absorption | Converts acoustic energy to heat | Porous baffles, glass-wool panels | Effective inside enclosures |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Transmission Control Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical exam-relevant equations consolidated for rapid revision. Memorize the form and the physical meaning.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\right)$ | Fundamental definition of TL from $\tau$ |
| 2 | $TL_{mass} = 20 \log_{10}(m \cdot f) - 47$ | **Mass Law** (English units: lb/ft², Hz) |
| 3 | $TL_{mass} = 20 \log_{10}(m \cdot f) - 33$ | Mass Law in **SI units** ($m$ in kg/m², $f$ in Hz) |
| 4 | $NR = TL + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S \cdot \bar{\alpha}}{A}\right)$ | Indoor noise reduction (room correction) |
| 5 | $f_c = \frac{c^2}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{m}{D}}$ | **Coincidence (critical) frequency** of a panel |
| 6 | $TL_{double} = 20 \log_{10}(m_1 + m_2) + 20 \log_{10}(f \cdot d) - 33$ | Double-leaf TL with air-gap $d$ (m) |
| 7 | $\Delta L_{barrier} \approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{20 N}\right)$ | Barrier attenuation for large $N$ (Kurze-Anderson) |
| 8 | $N = \frac{2 \delta}{\lambda}$ | **Fresnel number**, where $\delta$ is path-length difference |
| 9 | $TL_{single-leaf} = 20 \log_{10}(m f) - 47$ | Single flexible panel TL (English units) |
| 10 | $IL = L_{p,before} - L_{p,after}$ | Insertion Loss definition (in-situ) |

> **Important notational convention:**
> - $m$ = surface density (kg/m²)
> - $f$ = frequency (Hz)
> - $D$ = bending stiffness of panel (N·m)
> - $S$ = partition area (m²); $A$ = total room absorption (m² sabin)
> - $\bar{\alpha}$ = average absorption coefficient of receiving room
> - $\delta$ = path difference around barrier (m); $\lambda$ = wavelength (m)

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Architectural acoustics**: Designers specify STC-rated assemblies (STC ≥ 50 for hotel walls between guest rooms).
- **HVAC noise control**: Duct silencers rated in dB insertion loss at 125, 250, 500, 1k, 2k, 4k Hz octave bands.
- **Industrial noise**: OSHA / IS 3483 compliance relies on enclosure IL values.
- **Environmental noise**: Noise barriers along Indian highways are governed by IS 4954 and IRC guidelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass Law of Single-Leaf Partitions

The **mass law** is the single most important relationship in airborne transmission control. It states that, for a limp, flexible, infinitely large single-leaf partition, the transmission loss increases by **6 dB** for every **doubling** of frequency, and by **6 dB** for every **doubling** of surface density.

**Step 1 — Set up the physical model.**
Consider an infinite planar partition of surface density $m$ (kg/m²) separating two semi-infinite fluid media. A plane acoustic wave of frequency $f$ impinges normally from medium 1 (air) onto the partition.

**Step 2 — Impedance ratio formulation.**
For an infinite mass-controlled partition, the transmission coefficient is governed by the impedance mismatch between the air and the panel's areal mass:

$$\tau = \left(\frac{1}{1 + \left(\frac{\omega m}{2 \rho_0 c}\right)^2}\right)$$

where $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency, $\rho_0$ is air density (1.204 kg/m³), and $c$ is the speed of sound (343 m/s).

**Step 3 — Take the logarithm.**
Substituting into $TL = 10 \log_{10}(1/\tau)$:

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \left(\frac{\omega m}{2 \rho_0 c}\right)^2\right)$$

**Step 4 — Assume the "mass-controlled" regime.**
For most practical partitions, $\frac{\omega m}{2 \rho_0 c} \gg 1$, so the "1+" term is dominated by the squared ratio, allowing us to drop the unity:

$$TL \approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\left(\frac{\omega m}{2 \rho_0 c}\right)^2\right)$$

**Step 5 — Expand and substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$:**

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 \pi f \cdot m}{2 \rho_0 c}\right)$$

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(f m) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\pi}{\rho_0 c}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the constant term.**

We compute $\frac{\pi}{\rho_0 c}$:

$$\frac{\pi}{\rho_0 c} = \frac{3.1416}{1.204 \times 343} = \frac{3.1416}{413.0} \approx 7.608 \times 10^{-3}$$

Take $20 \log_{10}(7.608 \times 10^{-3})$:

$$20 \log_{10}(7.608 \times 10^{-3}) = 20 \times (-2.119) = -42.38$$

**Step 7 — Final mass-law equation in SI units.**

$$\boxed{\;TL_{mass} \approx 20 \log_{10}(m f) - 42.4 \;\; \text{(dB, SI)}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU textbooks often quote **−33 dB** instead of **−42.4 dB**. The −33 dB version corresponds to using the energy-conservation simplification and the field-incidence average correction (random incidence factor). For board examinations, the KTU formula sheet expects:
> $$TL = 20 \log_{10}(m f) - 33 \;\; \text{(field incidence, SI units)}$$
> Always state the assumed incidence condition in your answer.

**Step 8 — Verify the 6 dB rule.**
Doubling the frequency: $f \rightarrow 2f$

$$TL_{new} = 20 \log_{10}(m \cdot 2f) - 33 = 20 \log_{10}(mf) + 20 \log_{10}(2) - 33 = TL + 6$$

Similarly, doubling the mass: $m \rightarrow 2m$ gives another $+6$ dB. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coincidence (Critical) Frequency

At certain frequencies, a bending wave in the panel matches the trace velocity of the incident acoustic wave. This is the **coincidence effect**, which produces a sharp dip in the TL curve.

**Step 1 — Trace wave condition.**
A plane acoustic wave striking the panel at grazing angle $\theta$ has a trace wavelength along the panel:

$$\lambda_{trace} = \frac{\lambda}{\sin\theta}$$

**Step 2 — Bending wave dispersion in a plate.**
For a thin isotropic plate of bending stiffness $D$ and surface density $m$, the bending wave speed $c_b$ is:

$$c_b = \sqrt{\frac{2\pi f}{k_b}\!\cdot\!\sqrt{\frac{D}{m}}}$$

The trace velocity $c_{trace} = f \cdot \lambda_{trace} = c / \sin\theta$.

**Step 3 — Set them equal at coincidence.**

$$f_c = \frac{c^2}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{m}{D}}$$

where $D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ for a plate of Young's modulus $E$, thickness $h$, Poisson's ratio $\nu$.

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.**
Below $f_c$, the panel behaves as a rigid reflector (mass law holds). Above $f_c$, the bending wave couples efficiently to airborne sound and **TL drops by 5–10 dB** at the dip. Designers typically aim to keep $f_c$ *above* the frequency range of interest.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Insertion Loss of a Thin Noise Barrier (Fresnel Diffraction)

For an infinitely long, thin outdoor barrier of height $H$, the Fresnel number characterizes the diffraction geometry:

$$N = \frac{2 \delta}{\lambda} = \frac{A + B - d}{\lambda}$$

where $A$ = source-to-barrier-top distance, $B$ = barrier-top-to-receiver distance, $d$ = straight-line source-to-receiver distance, and $\lambda = c/f$.

**Step 1 — Path difference.**

$$\delta = A + B - d$$

**Step 2 — Kurze-Anderson approximation (large $N$):**

$$\Delta L_{barrier} \approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{20 N}\right) = 5 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{N}\right) - 10 \log_{10}(20/3)$$

Wait — let's keep the standard form:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta L \approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{20 N}\right) \;\; \text{for } N \gg 1\;}$$

**Step 3 — Practical engineering form.**
For design offices, the simplification (ISO 9613-2) gives:

$$\Delta L \approx 10 \log_{10}(3) - 10 \log_{10}(20 N) = 5 - 10 \log_{10}(N) - 8 = -3 - 10 \log_{10}(N)$$

This matches the textbook expression used in KTU problems.

**Step 4 — Numerical sanity check.**
If $N = 5$ (typical), $\Delta L \approx -3 - 10 \log_{10}(5) = -3 - 7 = -10$ dB attenuation. ✓ Reasonable.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Double-Leaf Transmission Loss with Air Gap

Two independent panels separated by an air cavity behave as a **mass-spring-mass** system. At frequencies well below the mass-air-mass resonance, the composite TL approaches:

$$TL_{double} \approx TL_{m_1} + TL_{m_2} + 20 \log_{10}(f \cdot d) - 33$$

**Step 1 — Single-leaf contributions.**

$$TL_{m_1} = 20 \log_{10}(m_1 f) - 33$$
$$TL_{m_2} = 20 \log_{10}(m_2 f) - 33$$

**Step 2 — Air-cavity coupling bonus.**

$$TL_{cavity} = 20 \log_{10}(f d) + C$$

where $C \approx 0$ to 9 dB depending on cavity absorption.

**Step 3 — Combined equation:**

$$\boxed{\;TL_{double} \approx 20 \log_{10}(m_1 f) + 20 \log_{10}(m_2 f) + 20 \log_{10}(f d) - 47\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the air-gap $d$ from 50 mm to 100 mm adds ~6 dB at mid frequencies — almost as effective as doubling the panel mass, but at lower cost. This is why **double-glazed windows** are so popular.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A 4 mm thick steel plate separates two rooms. Calculate the transmission loss at 500 Hz using the mass-law equation (SI). Steel density $\rho_{steel} = 7850$ kg/m³.

**Solution:**

Surface density:

$$m = \rho_{steel} \cdot h = 7850 \times 0.004 = 31.4 \;\; \text{kg/m}^2$$

Apply mass law:

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(m f) - 33$$

Compute $m f = 31.4 \times 500 = 15700$.

$$20 \log_{10}(15700) = 20 \times 4.196 = 83.92$$

$$\boxed{\;TL \approx 83.92 - 33 = 50.92 \;\text{dB at 500 Hz}\;}$$

This is consistent with typical single-leaf steel panel performance in industry.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Sound Transmission Path Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["NOISE SOURCE"]:::src
    medA["MEDIUM AIR PATH 1"]:::media
    encA["CONTROL DEVICE TRANSMISSION ZONE"]:::control
    medB["MEDIUM AIR PATH 2"]:::media
    recA["RECEIVER PERSON OR SPACE"]:::recv

    srcA -->|"Incident Energy Wi"| medA
    medA -->|"Arrives at partition"| encA
    encA -->|"Reflected Wr"| medA
    encA -->|"Transmitted Wt"| medB
    encA -->|"Absorbed Wa becomes heat"| encA
    medB -->|"Reaches listener"| recA

    classDef src fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef media fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef recv fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Diagram B — Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Transmission Control Method


### 4.3 Diagram C — Functional Architecture of a Double-Leaf Transmission Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pane1A["LEAF 1 Mass m1 drywall or masonry"]:::leaf
    gapA["AIR GAP d with optional fiberglass"]:::gap
    pane2A["LEAF 2 Mass m2 drywall or glazing"]:::leaf
    resilientA["Resilient channel optional decoupler"]:::resilient
    targetA["Composite TL rises 10 to 20 dB over single leaf"]:::result

    pane1A -->|"Vibration transferred via air spring"| gapA
    gapA -->|"Vibration reaches"| pane2A

    classDef leaf fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000

    classDef resilient fill:#ffd1dc,stroke:#990033,color:#000
```
