
### 4.4 Diagram D — Frequency Response Behavior of a Single-Leaf Panel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regA["REGION A Stiffness controlled f below 100 Hz"]:::reg
    regB["REGION B Mass controlled f mid range 100 to 1000 Hz"]:::reg
    regC["REGION C Coincidence dip near fc"]:::reg
    regD["REGION D Mass law resumes above fc"]:::reg

    regA -->|"TL rises steeply 12 dB per octave"| regB
    regB -->|"TL rises 6 dB per octave"| regC
    regC -->|"TL dips 5 to 10 dB"| regD
    regD -->|"TL again rises 6 dB per octave"| endregA["END Stable isolation"]:::endreg

    classDef reg fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000
    classDef endreg fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
Define **Transmission Loss (TL)** and **Insertion Loss (IL)**. State one key difference between them.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Transmission Loss (TL):** The ratio, expressed in decibels, of airborne sound energy incident on a partition to the energy transmitted through it. Mathematically,
> $$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_t}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\right)$$
> It is an **intrinsic material property** of the partition, measured in a laboratory under standardized conditions.
> - **Insertion Loss (IL):** The actual reduction in sound pressure level at a specific receiver location *before and after* a control device is installed in the field:
> $$IL = L_{p,\,\text{before}} - L_{p,\,\text{after}}$$
> It accounts for real-world geometry, flanking paths, and background noise.
> - **Key difference:** TL is a material/partition property; IL is a *system-level, in-situ performance* metric. (3 marks)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
State the **Mass Law** for a single-leaf partition and mention two practical implications.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Statement:** For a limp, flexible single-leaf partition of surface density $m$ (kg/m²) at frequency $f$ (Hz), the transmission loss is given by:
> $$TL = 20 \log_{10}(m f) - 33 \;\; \text{dB (SI, field incidence)}$$
> - **Implication 1:** Doubling the surface density $m$ increases TL by **6 dB** at any given frequency. → Designers add mass layers (gypsum, MLV) to enhance insulation.
> - **Implication 2:** Doubling the frequency $f$ also raises TL by **6 dB**. → Low-frequency noise (e.g., DG exhaust) is much harder to block; thicker walls are needed. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the **mass-law equation** for a single-leaf partition, clearly stating each assumption. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 6 mm thick plywood partition (density 700 kg/m³) separates two rooms. Calculate the TL at (i) 250 Hz and (ii) 1000 Hz. Comment on the result. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**
1. **Setup:** Infinite panel of areal mass $m$, normal-incidence plane wave. (1 mark)
2. **Impedance formulation:** $\tau = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\omega m}{2 \rho_0 c}\right)^2}$ (1 mark)
3. **Mass-controlled limit:** $\omega m \gg 2 \rho_0 c$, drop "1+" → $TL \approx 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega m}{2 \rho_0 c}\right)$ (1 mark)
4. **Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$:** $TL = 20 \log_{10}(mf) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{\rho_0 c}\right)$ (1 mark)
5. **Evaluate constant:** $20 \log_{10}(\pi / (\rho_0 c)) = 20 \log_{10}(7.61 \times 10^{-3}) \approx -42.4$ (1 mark)
6. **Apply field-incidence correction (random):** adds +9 dB → final constant = $-33.4$ (1 mark)
7. **Final equation:**
$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(mf) - 33 \;\; \text{dB}$$
plus statement of assumptions (limp panel, no stiffness, no coincidence). (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**
1. Surface density: $m = 700 \times 0.006 = 4.2$ kg/m² (1 mark)
2. At $f = 250$ Hz: $mf = 4.2 \times 250 = 1050$
   $TL_{250} = 20 \log_{10}(1050) - 33 = 60.4 - 33 = 27.4$ dB (1.5 marks)
3. At $f = 1000$ Hz: $mf = 4.2 \times 1000 = 4200$
   $TL_{1000} = 20 \log_{10}(4200) - 33 = 72.5 - 33 = 39.5$ dB (1.5 marks)
4. **Doubling test:** $f$ quadrupled (250 → 1000 Hz = 4×), so $TL$ should rise by $20 \log_{10}(4) = 12$ dB. (1 mark)
5. **Check:** $39.5 - 27.4 = 12.1$ dB ✓ (matches 6 dB/octave rule). (1 mark)
6. **Comment:** Plywood alone gives poor insulation at low frequencies — typical applications use double-leaf or mass-loaded construction. (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the aid of a labeled sketch, explain the working principle of an **outdoor noise barrier**. Derive an expression for barrier insertion loss in terms of Fresnel number. (7 marks)

**(b)** A noise barrier of height 4 m is erected between a point source and a receiver located 20 m and 25 m from the barrier base, respectively. The source emits a dominant tone at 500 Hz. Estimate the barrier insertion loss. (Speed of sound = 343 m/s). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**
1. **Definition:** A noise barrier is a dense, opaque vertical screen placed in the line-of-sight between source and receiver. (1 mark)
2. **Principle:** Sound waves must diffract over the barrier top, lengthening the path and causing destructive interference. (1 mark)
3. **Fresnel number definition:**
$$N = \frac{2 \delta}{\lambda} = \frac{2(A + B - d)}{\lambda}$$
where $A$ = source-barrier-top, $B$ = barrier-top-receiver, $d$ = source-receiver direct. (1 mark)
4. **Kurze-Anderson formula (for $N \gg 1$):**
$$\Delta L \approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{20 N}\right)$$
or equivalently $\Delta L \approx -3 - 10 \log_{10}(N)$. (2 marks)
5. **Practical tips:** Barrier should be tall enough to break line-of-sight; absorbent capping helps at grazing angles. (1 mark)
6. **Limitations:** Effective only at higher frequencies; soft ground reflections can reduce performance. (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**
1. Wavelength at 500 Hz: $\lambda = c/f = 343/500 = 0.686$ m (1 mark)
2. Direct distance $d$: assume source and receiver are on opposite sides; if both lie on the line passing through the barrier midpoint at ground level, then $A = \sqrt{20^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{416} \approx 20.40$ m and $B = \sqrt{25^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{641} \approx 25.32$ m. (1.5 marks)
3. $d = A + B - \text{excess path}$: For grazing geometry, $d \approx 20 + 25 = 45$ m direct. (1 mark)
4. Path difference: $\delta = A + B - d \approx 20.40 + 25.32 - 45 = 0.72$ m (1 mark)
5. Fresnel number: $N = \dfrac{2 \delta}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.72}{0.686} \approx 2.10$ (1 mark)
6. Insertion loss: $\Delta L \approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3}{20 \times 2.10}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(0.0714) \approx -11.5$ dB
   i.e., **the barrier provides about 11.5 dB of attenuation.** (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks-losing mistakes — avoid these:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Mass Law:** Students often plug $m$ in lb/ft² but use $f$ in Hz and the **−47** constant from English units, then mix with the −33 SI constant. Pick ONE system and stick to it.
> 2. **Confusing TL with IL:** TL is a lab material property; IL is in-situ. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 3. **Forgetting to compute $\lambda$ from $c/f$** in Fresnel-number problems. Always show $\lambda = 343 / f$.
> 4. **Dropping the "1+" too early** in mass-law derivation: marks are reserved for the algebraic justification of why $\omega m / (2 \rho_0 c) \gg 1$ for the typical partition.
> 5. **In barrier problems, using direct distance $d$ as $A+B$:** students forget that path difference $\delta = A + B - d$ must account for the actual geometry, not the height alone.
> 6. **STC ≠ TL at a single frequency:** STC is a *single-number rating* fitted to a contour; it is *not* the 500 Hz TL value. Examiners love this trick question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — Control During Transmission:**
> - ✅ Transmission control sits between source and receiver; preferred after source control.
> - ✅ $TL = 10 \log_{10}(1/\tau)$ — fundamental definition in dB.
> - ✅ Mass Law (SI): $TL = 20 \log_{10}(mf) - 33$ dB → +6 dB per doubling of mass *or* frequency.
> - ✅ Coincidence frequency $f_c = (c^2/2\pi)\sqrt{m/D}$ marks a TL dip; design above or below it.
> - ✅ Double-leaf construction adds $20 \log_{10}(f d)$ bonus — doubling air gap gives +6 dB.
> - ✅ Noise barrier attenuation governed by Fresnel number $N = 2\delta/\lambda$; larger $\delta$ → better screening.
> - ✅ Insertion Loss $IL$ is the field-measured reduction; TL is lab-measured material property.
> - ✅ Duct silencers: dissipative (broadband) vs reactive (tonal) — choose by source spectrum.
> - ✅ Enclosure performance depends on leakage, absorption lining, and panel TL.
> - ✅ Always state the assumed incidence (normal, random, field) when quoting mass-law equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control at Receiver
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control at Receiver

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Control at the Receiver"?

**Control at the Receiver** is the third and final tier in the classic noise control hierarchy, deployed at the *point of human exposure* when noise cannot be sufficiently reduced at the source or along the transmission path. It encompasses all personal, administrative, and spatial measures used to **protect the listener's hearing and comfort** from residual acoustic energy that has bypassed earlier control stages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Receiver control refers to measures employed at the location of the person or sensitive equipment to minimize the adverse effects of noise, including personal hearing protection, administrative controls, and the acoustic design of occupied spaces."

## 1.2 Position in the Noise Control Hierarchy

The engineering hierarchy of noise control follows a strict order of preference:

1. **Source Control** (most preferred – cheapest long-term)
2. **Path Control** (barriers, enclosures, absorbers)
3. **Receiver Control** (last resort – protects the person, not the noise)

Receiver control is considered a *mitigation strategy*, not an *elimination strategy*. It accepts that the noise exists and acts on the **exposed individual** to reduce the dose reaching the ear.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Rainy Commute**
> Imagine you must walk to work in heavy rain.
> - *Source control* = stopping the rain clouds (impossible!)
> - *Path control* = building a covered walkway (expensive, slow)
> - **Receiver control** = simply wearing a raincoat and carrying an umbrella.
>
> The raincoat (earplug/earmuff) does not stop the rain, but it keeps *you* dry. Similarly, hearing protection does not silence the machine, but it keeps the cochlear hair cells safe.

## 1.4 Why Receiver Control is Mandatory in Industry

Even with excellent engineering controls, certain occupational environments (aviation, mining, construction, heavy forging) cannot achieve sub-85 dBA levels everywhere. Statutory bodies like **OSHA (USA)**, **NIOSH (USA)**, and the **Factories Act (India)** therefore mandate receiver-side interventions through **Hearing Conservation Programs (HCPs)**.

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics Used at the Receiver End

- **Noise Reduction Rating (NRR)** – single-number attenuation of a Hearing Protection Device (HPD), in **decibels (dB)**.
- **Real-Ear Attenuation at Threshold (REAT)** – the gold-standard subjective measurement of HPD effectiveness.
- **Time-Weighted Average (TWA)** – the equivalent continuous sound level normalized to an 8-hour workday.
- **Noise Dose (%)** – cumulative exposure expressed as a percentage of the permissible daily criterion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two governing exchange rates in occupational noise are:
> - **3 dB exchange rate** (ISO/NIOSH) – dose doubles every 3 dB increase (energy-doubling).
> - **5 dB exchange rate** (OSHA) – dose doubles every 5 dB increase (more lenient).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Receiver Control

### 2.1.1 Personal Hearing Protection (PHP / HPD)
Devices worn by individuals that acoustically attenuate noise before it reaches the tympanic membrane.

**Categories of HPDs:**

| Type | Description | Typical NRR | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Earplugs (foam/preformed)** | Inserted into the ear canal | 25 – 35 dB | Long shifts, hot environments |
| **Semi-insert earplugs** | Cap over canal, no deep insertion | 15 – 25 dB | Intermittent exposure |
| **Earmuffs (circum-aural)** | Cups sealing around the ear | 20 – 30 dB | Quick donning/doffing |
| **Helmet-mounted muffs** | Attached to safety helmet | 20 – 30 dB | Construction, mining |
| **Active noise reduction (ANR)** | Electronics + passive muffs | 25 – 40 dB | Aviation, military |

### 2.1.2 Administrative Controls
- **Job rotation** – limit continuous exposure duration.
- **Shift scheduling** – split 8-hour TWA by rest periods in low-noise zones.
- **Posted noise maps** – mark high-noise zones and restrict access.
- **Training & signage** – mandatory PPE enforcement.

### 2.1.3 Workplace & Architectural Receiver Design
- **Acoustic enclosures** (booths, cabins) around operators.
- **Absorbent treatment** of walls/ceiling in control rooms.
- **Buffer zones** (distance separation from machinery).
- **Quiet rooms** for hearing recovery during breaks.

## 2.2 Measurement Standards for HPD Attenuation

The **Real-Ear Attenuation at Threshold (REAT)** test (per **ANSI/ASA S3.19-1974** and **ISO 4869**) involves:

1. Measuring subject's hearing threshold *without* the HPD (open ear).
2. Measuring the same subject's threshold *with* the HPD occluded.
3. Subtracting: $\text{REAT}(f) = T_{\text{open}}(f) - T_{\text{occluded}}(f)$.
4. Averaging across octave bands 125 Hz – 8000 Hz to derive the **NRR**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Noise Dose (%, OSHA 5 dB) | $D = 100 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | % | $C_i$ = time at level $i$, $T_i$ = OSHA reference duration |
| 2 | OSHA Reference Duration | $T_i = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L_i - 90)/5}}$ | hours | Criterion level 90 dBA, 5 dB exchange |
| 3 | NIOSH Reference Duration | $T_i = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L_i - 85)/3}}$ | hours | Criterion level 85 dBA, 3 dB exchange |
| 4 | Time-Weighted Average (TWA, OSHA) | $\text{TWA} = 16.61 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{100}\right) + 90$ | dBA | Where $D$ is in % |
| 5 | TWA from multiple levels | $\text{TWA} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{T_0}\sum_{i} t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10}\right]$ | dBA | $T_0 = 8$ h |
| 6 | OSHA Protected Exposure | $L_{\text{prot}} = L_{\text{meas}} - \text{NRR} - 7$ | dBA | Subtract 7 dB to convert NRR (C-weighted) to A-weighted field use |
| 7 | NIOSH Protected Exposure | $L_{\text{prot}} = L_{\text{meas}} - (\text{NRR} - 7) - 2$ | dBA | Adjusts for spectral assumptions |
| 8 | REAT (per frequency) | $\text{REAT}(f) = T_{\text{open}}(f) - T_{\text{occluded}}(f)$ | dB | Measured at octave/⅓-octave bands |
| 9 | Single-Number NRR (simplified) | $\text{NRR} \approx \overline{\text{REAT}} - 7$ | dB | "7 dB" is a spectral safety derating |
| 10 | Double Hearing Protector Rule | $L_{\text{prot,total}} = L_{\text{meas}} - 5 - (\text{NRR}_1 + \text{NRR}_2 - 5)$ | dBA | 5 dB derate when dual protection used |
| 11 | Daily Exposure (EU Directive) | $L_{EX,8h} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{T_0}\sum t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10}\right]$ | dBA | Identical to OSHA TWA definition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constant **"−7 dB"** in OSHA formula (6) is **not a safety margin** — it converts a *C-weighted* NRR to an *A-weighted field* derating because laboratory NRR is measured in dBC and the 90 → 80 dBC correction plus 3 dB spectral padding equals 7 dB.

## 2.4 Engineering & Occupational Utility

| Sector | Typical Receiver Control Strategy |
|---|---|
| Aviation | ANR headsets (NRR ≈ 30 dB) for cockpit crew |
| Mining | Dual protection (plugs + muffs) due to 100+ dBA levels |
| Manufacturing | Earmuffs at spot-tool stations; booths around CNC operators |
| Construction | Helmet-mounted muffs (compatible with hard hats) |
| Hospitals | Quiet zones in ICUs; HPDs for MRI staff |
| Offices | Acoustic panels; distance from HVAC noise sources |

## 2.5 Criteria for Selection of HPDs (KTU Standard)

A good HPD must satisfy:

- **Adequate attenuation** – reduces exposure below 85 dBA (action level) but not below ~70 dBA (over-protection causes isolation/safety hazards).
- **Comfort** – worn for full shift without irritation.
- **Stability** – stays in place during movement.
- **Compatibility** – works with other PPE (helmets, glasses, respirators).
- **Hygiene** – easy to clean / disposable options.
- **Cost-effectiveness** – affordable for the workforce size.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Target Minus 5" Rule**: Derate the HPD's laboratory NRR by 50%, then ensure the *protected* level lands at or below the 85 dBA action level, but ideally no more than 5 dB below ambient to maintain auditory situational awareness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: OSHA Noise Dose from Exposure Schedule

**Problem Setup (typical KTU-style):**
A worker is exposed to the following noise levels during an 8-hour shift:

| Interval | Duration (h) | Level (dBA) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 95 |
| 2 | 1.5 | 100 |
| 3 | 1 | 88 |
| 4 | 3.5 | 80 (below criterion) |

**Criterion level** $L_c = 90$ dBA, **Exchange rate** $q = 5$ dB, **Threshold level** $L_t = 80$ dBA (OSHA).

### Step 1: Identify contributing intervals
Only levels $\geq 80$ dBA contribute to the dose.

$$
\text{Contributing intervals: } 95, 100, 88 \text{ dBA}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the OSHA reference duration $T_i$ for each level
The OSHA reference duration is the time allowed at level $L_i$ that equals 100% dose for the criterion:

$$
T_i = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L_i - 90)/5}}
$$

Apply to each interval:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{95} &= \dfrac{8}{2^{(95-90)/5}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{1.0}} = \dfrac{8}{2} = 4 \text{ h} \\[4pt]
T_{100} &= \dfrac{8}{2^{(100-90)/5}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{2.0}} = \dfrac{8}{4} = 2 \text{ h} \\[4pt]
T_{88} &= \dfrac{8}{2^{(88-90)/5}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{-0.4}} = 8 \cdot 2^{0.4} = 8 \cdot 1.3195 \approx 10.556 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute fractional contributions $C_i / T_i$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{C_{95}}{T_{95}} &= \dfrac{2}{4} = 0.500 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{C_{100}}{T_{100}} &= \dfrac{1.5}{2} = 0.750 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{C_{88}}{T_{88}} &= \dfrac{1}{10.556} \approx 0.0947
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Sum and express as percentage

$$
D\% = 100 \times (0.500 + 0.750 + 0.0947) = 100 \times 1.3447 = 134.47 \%
$$

### Step 5: Compute the TWA

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TWA} &= 16.61 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{100}\right) + 90 \\[4pt]
&= 16.61 \log_{10}(1.3447) + 90 \\[4pt]
&= 16.61 \times 0.1287 + 90 \\[4pt]
&\approx 2.138 + 90 = 92.14 \text{ dBA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A dose of 134.5% and TWA of 92.1 dBA both **exceed** the OSHA PEL of 100% / 90 dBA. The worker has overdosed by ~34% and requires immediate engineering/administrative control.

## 3.2 Derivation: Required NRR for Compliance

**Problem:** A worker spends 8 hours in a steady 100 dBA environment. What minimum NRR earplug is required so that the *protected* level falls to 85 dBA (NIOSH REL)?

### Step 1: Define target protected level

$$
L_{\text{target}} = 85 \text{ dBA}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the OSHA derating equation (assuming OSHA formula will be used)

$$
L_{\text{prot}} = L_{\text{meas}} - \text{NRR} - 7
$$

### Step 3: Solve for NRR

$$
\begin{aligned}
85 &= 100 - \text{NRR} - 7 \\[4pt]
\text{NRR} &= 100 - 7 - 85 = 8 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply the "Target Minus 5 / 50% derate" real-world rule

Because field derating halves the NRR (real-world attenuation ≈ 50% of NRR):

$$
\text{NRR}_{\text{required,realistic}} = 2 \times (L_{\text{meas}} - L_{\text{target}} - 7) = 2 \times 8 = 16 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the **higher of the two** values should be selected. For 100 dBA exposure aiming for 85 dBA, an NRR of **at least 16 dB** is recommended.

## 3.3 Derivation: Dual Hearing Protector Combined NRR

When both earplugs and earmuffs are worn, the *combined* attenuation is *not* additive. The OSHA rule is:

$$
\text{NRR}_{\text{combined}} = \text{NRR}_{\text{highest}} + 5
$$

**Example:** Earplugs NRR = 27 dB; Earmuffs NRR = 25 dB.

$$
\text{NRR}_{\text{combined}} = 27 + 5 = 32 \text{ dB}
$$

**Protected level** in a 105 dBA environment:

$$
L_{\text{prot}} = 105 - 32 - 7 = 66 \text{ dBA}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Over-protection below ~70 dBA can impair warning-signal detection, communication, and situational awareness — a critical safety hazard. Always compute the protected level before issuing dual HPDs.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of HPD Types

| Parameter | Foam Earplug | Preformed Earplug | Earmuff | ANR Headset |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NRR (typ.) | 32 dB | 27 dB | 26 dB | 30 dB |
| Low-freq. attenuation | Poor | Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| High-freq. attenuation | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| Comfort (long wear) | Moderate | High | High | High |
| Replaceable | Daily | 1–3 mo | 1–3 yr | 3–5 yr |
| Cost per user | ₹5–20 | ₹50–200 | ₹500–1500 | ₹5000–50000 |
| Hygiene risk | High | Moderate | Low | Low |
| Compatibility | Excellent | Excellent | Conflicts with glasses | Self-contained |

## 3.5 Time-Weighted Average — General Form Derivation

The TWA is the steady A-weighted level that, if present for the full 8-hour reference period, would deliver the same acoustic energy as the variable schedule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy}_{8\text{h}} &= \sum_{i} p_0^2 \cdot 10^{L_i/10} \cdot t_i \\[4pt]
L_{EX,8h} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{T_0}\sum_{i} t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10}\right], \quad T_0 = 8 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **EU ISO 1999** formulation; the OSHA TWA equals $L_{EX,8h}$ mathematically.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Noise Control Hierarchy with Receiver Focus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Industrial Noise Source"] --> B{"Source Control"}
    B -- "Vibration damping, mufflers, quieter machines" --> C["Reduced Emission"]
    C --> D{"Path Control"}
    D -- "Barriers, enclosures, absorbers" --> E["Attenuated Path"]
    E --> F{"Receiver Control"}
    F -- "HPDs, admin controls, booths" --> G["Protected Listener"]
    G --> H["TWA less than 85 dBA"]
    G --> I["Hearing Conservation Program Active"]

    P1["Earplugs"] --> F
    P2["Earmuffs"] --> F
    P3["ANR Headsets"] --> F
    P4["Administrative Rotation"] --> F
    P5["Acoustic Booths"] --> F
    P6["Quiet Zones"] --> F

    style A fill:#FFD1D1,stroke:#900000
    style B fill:#FFE9B0,stroke:#806000
    style D fill:#FFF4B0,stroke:#807000
    style F fill:#D1E8FF,stroke:#003D80
    style G fill:#D1FFD1,stroke:#006020
```

## 4.2 Hearing Conservation Program — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1["Noise Survey"] --> N2["Identify Exposed Workers"]
    N2 --> N3["Audiometric Baseline Test"]
    N3 --> N4["Issue HPDs with NRR matching exposure"]
    N4 --> N5["Training and Motivation"]
    N5 --> N6["Annual Audiometric Retest"]
    N6 --> N7{"Standard Threshold Shift Detected?"}
    N7 -- Yes --> N8["Re-evaluate controls and reissue HPDs"]
    N7 -- No --> N9["Continue routine monitoring"]
    N8 --> N5
    N9 --> N6

    style N1 fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#003D80
    style N4 fill:#FFF0D0,stroke:#806000
    style N7 fill:#FFD0D0,stroke:#900000
    style N9 fill:#D0FFD0,stroke:#006020
```

## 4.3 HPD Selection Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Measure workplace L_eq in dBA"] --> S2{"Exceeds 85 dBA?"}
    S2 -- No --> S3["No HPD needed — record assessment"]
    S2 -- Yes --> S4["Compute required NRR using OSHA formula"]
    S4 --> S5{"Required NRR greater than 30 dB?"}
    S5 -- No --> S6["Single HPD: Earmuff or preformed plug"]
    S5 -- Yes --> S7["Dual protection: Plug + Muff"]
    S6 --> S8["Check comfort and compatibility"]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9["Train worker on correct insertion / sealing"]
    S9 --> S10["Field-verify attenuation with personal dosimetry"]

    style S1 fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#003D80
    style S5 fill:#FFE0A0,stroke:#806000
    style S7 fill:#FFD0D0,stroke:#900000
    style S10 fill:#D0FFD0,stroke:#006020
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Personal Noise Dosimeter Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["Microphone at worker's ear"] --> M2["Pre-amplifier and A-weighting filter"]
    M2 --> M3["RMS Detector with Exponential Averaging"]
    M3 --> M4["Exchange-Rate Integrator 3 dB or 5 dB"]
    M4 --> M5["Dose Memory Register percent"]
    M4 --> M6["TWA Calculator dBA"]
    M4 --> M7["Threshold Discriminator excludes below 80 dBA"]
    M5 --> M8["LCD Display and Data Logger"]
    M6 --> M8
    M7 --> M4

    style M1 fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#003D80
    style M4 fill:#FFE0A0,stroke:#806000
    style M8 fill:#D0FFD0,stroke:#006020
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Comparison of Exchange Rate Effects on Permissible Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Level dBA"] --> A2["OSHA 5 dB Exchange"]
    A1 --> A3["NIOSH 3 dB Exchange"]
    A2 --> A4["Permissible Hours: 8 / 2 to the power L-90 over 5"]
    A3 --> A5["Permissible Hours: 8 / 2 to the power L-85 over 3"]
    A4 --> A6["TWA Report"]
    A5 --> A6

    style A2 fill:#FFE5B0,stroke:#804000
    style A3 fill:#D6E5FF,stroke:#003580
    style A6 fill:#D0FFD0,stroke:#006020
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **List the three main categories of receiver control measures. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Personal Hearing Protection Devices (HPDs)** – e.g., foam earplugs, earmuffs, ANR headsets. *(1 mark)*
2. **Administrative Controls** – e.g., job rotation, posted noise maps, restricting access to high-noise zones. *(1 mark)*
3. **Architectural / Workplace Design Controls** – e.g., operator booths, acoustic enclosures, absorbent-lined control rooms. *(1 mark)*

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define Noise Reduction Rating (NRR). What is the OSHA derating formula relating NRR to protected A-weighted exposure?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **NRR** is a single-number laboratory rating (in dB) expressing the sound attenuation capability of an HPD, measured by the REAT method per ANSI S3.19. *(1 mark)*
- OSHA field-use derating formula: $L_{\text{prot}} = L_{\text{meas}} - \text{NRR} - 7$. *(2 marks)*
- The "−7 dB" corrects the C-weighted lab measurement to a derated A-weighted field estimate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a)** A factory worker is exposed to the following levels in an 8-hour shift:
> - 2 h at 95 dBA
> - 1.5 h at 100 dBA
> - 1 h at 88 dBA
> - Rest of the time at 78 dBA
>
> Using OSHA criteria (criterion 90 dBA, exchange rate 5 dB, threshold 80 dBA), determine:
> (i) The noise dose in percent, and
> (ii) The 8-hour Time-Weighted Average (TWA).
>
> **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** With reference to Hearing Protection Devices (HPDs):
> (i) Classify the major types of HPDs and state their typical NRR ranges.
> (ii) Discuss the criteria for selecting a suitable HPD for an industrial worker.
>
> **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Identify contributing intervals** (those $\geq 80$ dBA): 95, 100, 88 dBA. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 2: Reference durations $T_i$ for OSHA criterion:**

$$
T_i = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L_i-90)/5}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{95} &= 8 / 2^{(95-90)/5} = 8/2 = 4 \text{ h} \\[3pt]
T_{100} &= 8 / 2^{(100-90)/5} = 8/4 = 2 \text{ h} \\[3pt]
T_{88} &= 8 / 2^{(88-90)/5} = 8 / 2^{-0.4} = 8 \times 1.3195 \approx 10.556 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for correct evaluation)*

**Step 3: Compute fractional contributions $C_i/T_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{95}/T_{95} &= 2/4 = 0.500 \\[3pt]
C_{100}/T_{100} &= 1.5/2 = 0.750 \\[3pt]
C_{88}/T_{88} &= 1/10.556 \approx 0.0947
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4: Total dose:**

$$
D\% = 100 \times (0.500 + 0.750 + 0.0947) = 134.47\%
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5: TWA:**

$$
\text{TWA} = 16.61 \log_{10}(1.3447) + 90 = 16.61 \times 0.1287 + 90 \approx 92.14 \text{ dBA}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Conclusion:** Dose of **134.5%** and TWA of **92.1 dBA** — exceeds the OSHA PEL; corrective action is mandatory. *(1 mark)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Classification of HPDs (3.5 marks):**

| Type | Description | NRR Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Earplugs (foam)** | Disposable, inserted into ear canal | 25 – 35 dB |
| **Earplugs (preformed)** | Re-usable, flanged | 20 – 30 dB |
| **Semi-insert earplugs** | Caps that seal at canal entrance | 15 – 25 dB |
| **Earmuffs (circum-aural)** | Cups over the entire ear | 20 – 30 dB |
| **Helmet-mounted muffs** | Attached to hard hat | 20 – 30 dB |
| **ANR headsets** | Active + passive hybrid | 25 – 40 dB |

**(ii) Selection Criteria (3.5 marks):**

1. **Adequate attenuation** — derate NRR by 50%; target protected level 70–85 dBA. *(1 mark)*
2. **Comfort & wearability** — must be tolerated for full shift. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Compatibility** — works with helmet, glasses, respirator. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Communication needs** — must allow warning-signal perception. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Hygiene & maintenance** — disposable vs. cleanable. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Cost & worker training** — affordable for population; training on insertion technique. *(0.5 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** A worker is exposed to a steady 100 dBA for 8 hours. Using the OSHA derating formula, determine:
> (i) The minimum NRR of an earplug to reduce exposure to 85 dBA.
> (ii) The minimum NRR if dual protection (earplug + earmuff) is used and the dual rule applies.
> **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Write short notes on:
> (i) Real-Ear Attenuation at Threshold (REAT) measurement procedure.
> (ii) Components of an effective Hearing Conservation Program (HCP).
> **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**(i) Single HPD requirement (3.5 marks):**

OSHA derating: $L_{\text{prot}} = L_{\text{meas}} - \text{NRR} - 7$.

$$
85 = 100 - \text{NRR} - 7 \Rightarrow \text{NRR} = 8 \text{ dB}
$$

Applying 50% field derate (Target-Minus-5/real-world rule):

$$
\text{NRR}_{\text{recommended}} = 2 \times 8 = 16 \text{ dB}
$$

> **A plug with NRR ≥ 16 dB is the minimum acceptable.** *(3.5 marks)*

**(ii) Dual protection requirement (3.5 marks):**

OSHA combined rule: $\text{NRR}_{\text{combined}} = \text{NRR}_{\text{highest}} + 5$.

Solving for the highest individual NRR $N$:

$$
85 = 100 - (N + 5) - 7 \Rightarrow N = 3 \text{ dB}
$$

> Practically, dual protection is only used when a *single* HPD cannot deliver sufficient attenuation. Since most plugs/muffs exceed 25 dB, dual protection is selected for **extreme exposures** (e.g., >105 dBA), not for routine 100 dBA work. *(3.5 marks)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) REAT Procedure (3.5 marks):**
1. Recruit ≥ 10 otologically normal subjects. *(0.5)*
2. Measure each subject's hearing threshold in octave bands 125 Hz – 8 kHz *without* HPD. *(1.0)*
3. Re-measure thresholds *with* HPD fitted by trained personnel. *(1.0)*
4. $\text{REAT}(f) = T_{\text{open}}(f) - T_{\text{occluded}}(f)$ for each band. *(0.5)*
5. Compute the mean REAT across subjects and adjust by −7 dB spectral derate to obtain the published NRR. *(0.5)*

**(ii) HCP Components (3.5 marks):**
1. **Noise survey** to identify exposures ≥ 85 dBA. *(0.5)*
2. **Audiometric testing** — baseline + annual. *(0.5)*
3. **HPD provision and enforcement.** *(0.5)*
4. **Worker training and education.** *(0.5)*
5. **Record keeping** for at least the worker's tenure. *(0.5)*
6. **Standard Threshold Shift (STS) follow-up** — re-issue PPE and re-audition. *(0.5)*
7. **Program evaluation and continuous improvement.** *(0.5)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the "−7 dB" derate** in the OSHA NRR formula — this costs 1 full mark almost every exam. Always write: $L_{\text{prot}} = L_{\text{meas}} - \text{NRR} - 7$.
> 2. **Mixing up 3 dB vs 5 dB exchange rates** — NIOSH uses 3 dB, OSHA uses 5 dB. The criterion level also differs (85 dBA vs 90 dBA).
> 3. **Adding NRRs arithmetically for dual protectors** — the correct OSHA rule is $N_{\text{combined}} = N_{\text{highest}} + 5$, **not** $N_1 + N_2$.
> 4. **Including sub-threshold levels (<80 dBA OSHA, <75 dBA NIOSH)** in the dose — these are excluded; examiners deduct for including them.
> 5. **Skipping the "−50% derate" in real-world application** — laboratory NRR is optimistic; field attenuation ≈ ½ NRR.
> 6. **Computing TWA using arithmetic mean of dB levels** — wrong! TWA is a *log-energy* average.
> 7. **Forgetting units** in the answer (dBA, %, hours). Always annotate.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Control at the Receiver"**
> - Receiver control is the **last** tier in the noise control hierarchy (Source → Path → **Receiver**). *(1)*
> - Three pillars: **HPDs**, **Administrative controls**, **Architectural/workplace design**. *(2)*
> - **HPD types**: earplugs, semi-inserts, earmuffs, helmet-mounted, ANR headsets. *(3)*
> - **NRR** is the single-number attenuation; OSHA derating: $L_{\text{prot}} = L - \text{NRR} - 7$. *(4)*
> - **NIOSH derating**: $L_{\text{prot}} = L - (\text{NRR} - 7) - 2$. *(5)*
> - Field attenuation ≈ **50% of lab NRR** (real-world derate). *(6)*
> - Dual protector combined NRR = $N_{\text{highest}} + 5$ (OSHA). *(7)*
> - **REAT** = open-ear threshold − occluded threshold (per ANSI/ASA S3.19 / ISO 4869). *(8)*
> - **OSHA** criterion: 90 dBA, 5 dB exchange, 80 dBA threshold, 8 h TWA. *(9)*
> - **NIOSH/ISO** criterion: 85 dBA, 3 dB exchange, 80 dBA threshold, 8 h TWA. *(10)*
> - Noise dose formula: $D\% = 100 \sum (C_i / T_i)$. *(11)*
> - TWA from dose: $\text{TWA} = 16.61 \log_{10}(D/100) + 90$ (OSHA). *(12)*
> - TWA from levels: $\text{TWA} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{8}\sum t_i 10^{L_i/10}\right]$. *(13)*
> - Action level: **85 dBA** (TWA) triggers HCP enrollment. *(14)*
> - PEL (OSHA): **90 dBA** TWA; REL (NIOSH): **85 dBA** TWA. *(15)*
> - HCP components: noise survey, audiometry, HPDs, training, records, STS follow-up. *(16)*
> - Selection criteria: attenuation, comfort, compatibility, hygiene, cost, communication. *(17)*
> - **Target-Minus-5 Rule**: protected level should be ~70 dBA minimum (no over-protection). *(18)*
> - Active Noise Reduction (ANR) is best for **low-frequency** noise (aviation, military). *(19)*
> - Foam earplugs must be **rolled, inserted, and held** until expanded for full NRR. *(20)*
> - Hearing Conservation Programs are **legally mandated** above the action level in most jurisdictions. *(21)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causes of Noise and Noise Control in Pumps, Compressors, Fans, Electric Motor, Cooling Towers, Acoustic Enclosures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Causes of Noise and Noise Control in Pumps, Compressors, Fans, Electric Motor, Cooling Towers, Acoustic Enclosures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 4 — INDUSTRIAL NOISE SOURCES & ENGINEERING CONTROL

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PEMET752):** This module deals with the identification, quantification, and engineering mitigation of noise generated by **rotodynamic and positive-displacement machinery**, which form the backbone of HVAC, process, and power plants. Mastery of this topic is critical for Plant Engineers, MEP Consultants, and Industrial Acoustics Designers.

---

## 1.1 What is Industrial Equipment Noise?

### Formal KTU Definition
> **Equipment Noise** is the unwanted acoustic energy radiated by industrial machinery (pumps, compressors, fans, motors, and cooling towers) as a byproduct of fluid–structure interaction, mechanical impact, electromagnetic excitation, or thermal effects. It is quantified in **dB re 10⁻¹² W (Sound Power Level, Lw)** or **dB re 20 µPa (Sound Pressure Level, Lp)** as per **ISO 3744** and **ISO 9614**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a busy **restaurant kitchen**: the exhaust hood (fan) hums, the dishwasher pump gurgles, the refrigerator compressor clicks on and off, and the ice machine vibrates. Each appliance makes a *distinct* sound because each uses energy differently — air pushed, liquid forced, magnetic fields cycling. Industrial noise control is the science of turning this chaotic "kitchen" into a quiet, well-engineered environment — by either silencing the source, blocking the path, or absorbing the energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Metric:** The **Sound Power Level (Lw)** is the *intrinsic* noise property of a machine — independent of distance and room acoustics. It is the ONLY valid way to compare machinery noise (not Sound Pressure Level, which depends on where you measure).

### The Four Fundamental Mechanisms of Equipment Noise

| # | Mechanism | Physical Origin | Typical Equipment |
|---|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Aerodynamic / Hydrodynamic** | Turbulence, vortex shedding, cavitation | Fans, compressors, pumps |
| 2 | **Mechanical / Impact** | Bearings, gears, piston slap, imbalance | Motors, reciprocating pumps |
| 3 | **Electromagnetic** | Magnetostriction, slot harmonics, torque ripple | Electric motors, transformers |
| 4 | **Combustion / Process** | Pulsating gas release, valve chatter | Internal combustion engines, gas compressors |

---

## 1.2 Pumps — Sources and Controls

### Centrifugal Pumps
**Causes of Noise:**
- **Cavitation noise:** Vapor bubble collapse near the impeller eye.
- **Hydrodynamic noise:** Turbulent flow and impeller–volute interaction (Blade Passage Frequency tone).
- **Mechanical noise:** Bearing wear, shaft misalignment, loose coupling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Blade Passage Frequency (BPF):** $f_{BPF} = N \times n / 60$ Hz, where N = number of impeller vanes, n = shaft rpm.

**Control Measures:**
- Mount on **inertia blocks** (concrete plinth) of mass ≥ 3× pump mass.
- Use **flexible couplings** (neoprene, rubber element).
- Install **vibration isolators** (spring or elastomeric pads) with static deflection ≥ 25 mm.
- Maintain suction NPSH to **avoid cavitation**.
- Apply **acoustic enclosure** (STC ≥ 30).

### Reciprocating Pumps
**Causes:** Piston slap, valve slam, liquid pulsation (2× running frequency).
**Control:** **Pulsation dampeners** (bladder or diaphragm type), suction/discharge **silencers**, rigid common-mount base.

---

## 1.3 Compressors — Sources and Controls

### Reciprocating Compressors (Loudest Type: 95–110 dBA)
- **Valve noise** (dominant): Plate flutter and impact at discharge.
- **Cylinder/piston noise:** Combustion knock (gas) or hydraulic slap (air).
- **Pulsation:** Strong odd harmonics of running frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discharge Pulse Frequency:** $f_p = \frac{n \times N_c}{60}$ where N_c = number of cylinders, n = rpm.

### Centrifugal Compressors
- **Rotor–stator interaction:** BPF tone.
- **Surge noise:** Random low-frequency oscillation.
- **Turbulence broadband noise.**

### Screw / Rotary Compressors
- Rotor meshing frequency: $f_m = Z \times n / 60$.

**Universal Compressor Control Measures:**
1. **Inlet silencer** (reactive or absorptive) at air intake.
2. **Discharge silencer / pulsation dampener.**
3. **Acoustic enclosure** with ventilation (acoustic-lined duct).
4. **Pipe lagging** with mass-loaded vinyl (≥ 5 kg/m²).

---

## 1.4 Fans — Sources and Controls

**Sources:**
- **Rotational noise (BPF):** Discrete tones at $f = N \times n/60$.
- **Vortex shedding / tip turbulence:** Broadband "whoosh."
- **Flow separation noise.**
- **Duct breakout noise** downstream of fans.

**Control Hierarchy:**
1. **Select low-noise fans** (backward-curved/curved-blade better than forward-curved).
2. **Operate near BEP** (Best Efficiency Point) to minimize turbulence.
3. **Inlet and discharge silencers** (dissipative type for broadband, reactive for tonal).
4. **Flexible canvas ducts** between fan and rigid ductwork.
5. **Vibration isolation** springs — natural frequency < 0.4 × fan rpm/60.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard:** For HVAC, fan noise is the *dominant* indoor noise source — often 60–75 dBA in AHU rooms.

---

## 1.5 Electric Motors — Sources and Controls

**Sources:**
- **Magnetic noise (dominant):** Magnetostriction, slot harmonics — tones at $2f_s$, $4f_s$, rotor slot passing frequency.
- **Mechanical noise:** Bearing race frequencies (BPFI, BPFO), imbalance.
- **Cooling fan noise** (built-in impeller).

**Control:**
- Use **inverter-duty motors** with insulated bearings to suppress shaft currents.
- **Soft starters / VFDs** to avoid inrush magnetic transients.
- **Resilient mounts** + flexible conduits.
- **Tighten stator laminations** (reduces magnetostriction).

---

## 1.6 Cooling Towers — Sources and Controls

**Sources:**
- **Water splash noise** (dominant, broadband, 500 Hz–4 kHz).
- **Fan noise** (BPF + broadband).
- **Gearbox / drive noise.**
- **Motor noise.**

**Control:**
- **Drift eliminators** (cellular type) — reduce splash by > 50%.
- **Low-noise fans** (wide-chord blades, reduced tip speed).
- **Attenuation splitters** at air intake and discharge.
- **Sound attenuator stacks** (vertical lined ducts on fan discharge).
- **Vibration isolation** of fan stack from structure.

---

## 1.7 Acoustic Enclosures — The Engineering Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** An acoustic enclosure is a sealed or semi-sealed barrier (single, double, or triple-wall construction) surrounding a noise source, designed to achieve a target **Insertion Loss (IL)** by combining mass, absorption, damping, and vibration isolation.

**Components:**
1. **Outer skin:** Steel sheet (1.6–3.2 mm), lined.
2. **Acoustic absorption:** Mineral wool / glass fiber (25–100 mm, density 30–80 kg/m³).
3. **Inner perforated liner:** 0.8 mm perforated sheet, 25–30 % open area.
4. **Seals / Gaskets:** Neoprene gaskets at door seams (leakage control!).
5. **Vibration isolation:** Springs at the equipment mounting points.

> [!WARNING]
> **Leakage Rule (Sabine's Leakage Formula):** A 1 % open area in a seal can reduce enclosure IL by **up to 10 dB**. Proper gasket design is non-negotiable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

---

## 2.1 The Noise Generation Mechanism (Aerodynamic)

For fans, compressors, and pumps, **Lighthill's Acoustic Analogy** provides the foundational framework. The sound power radiated by a turbulent fluid is proportional to the **eighth power of flow velocity** (for a dipole source) or **fifth power** (monopole).

> [!NOTE]
> **Lighthill's Law (simplified, free-field, for a compact source):** $W \propto \rho_0 \, U^8 \, D^2$ for a dipole-dominated source. This is why reducing fan tip speed by just 20 % can cut noise by ~5–6 dB.

---

## 2.2 Noise Control Hierarchy (KTU Standard)

Always apply controls in this priority order:

1. **Source Control:** Quietest machine selection, smooth operation, proper installation.
2. **Path Control:** Enclosures, barriers, silencers, lagging.
3. **Receiver Control:** PPE (personal protective equipment), hearing zones.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable** for KTU PEMET752 under Module 4. Memorize with units.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Sound Power Level (in situ from SPL) | $L_W = L_p + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{S_0}\right)$ | $L_W$ in dB re 10⁻¹² W, $S$ in m², $S_0 = 1$ m² |
| 2 | Sound Pressure Level at distance | $L_p(r) = L_W - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11$ | r in m, free field |
| 3 | Blade Passage Frequency (Fan/Impeller) | $f_{BPF} = \dfrac{N \cdot n}{60}$ | N = blades, n = rpm |
| 4 | Pulsation Frequency (Recip. Compressor) | $f_p = \dfrac{n \cdot N_c}{60}$ | N_c = cylinders, n = rpm |
| 5 | Mass Law (Single Panel Transmission Loss) | $TL = 20\log_{10}(m_f) + 47$ | $m_f$ = surface density in kg/m² |
| 6 | Mass Law (dB re 500 Hz) | $TL = 20\log_{10}(m_f \cdot f) - 47$ | $m_f$ in kg/m², f in Hz |
| 7 | Insertion Loss | $IL = L_{p,\text{before}} - L_{p,\text{after}}$ | All in dB |
| 8 | Transmission Loss — Composite Wall | $TL_{\text{total}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\tau_1}+\dfrac{1}{\tau_2}-\dots\right)$ | $\tau$ = transmission coefficient |
| 9 | Mass–Air–Mass Resonance Frequency | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{S}{m_1 m_2}}$ | $S$ = air stiffness, $m_1,m_2$ in kg/m² |
| 10 | Coincidence Frequency (Plate) | $f_c = \dfrac{c^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{m_f}{D}}$ | D = bending stiffness (N·m) |
| 11 | Sound Power of Cavitation Noise | $W_{cav} \propto \Delta p^{2}$ | $\Delta p$ = pressure drop across impeller |
| 12 | Sound Power due to Rotor–Stator Interaction | $W \propto \left(\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}\right)^2$ | $\Delta V/V$ = velocity fluctuation ratio |
| 13 | Reduction of Fan Noise by Speed | $\Delta L_W \approx 50\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$ | Affinity law for noise |
| 14 | Sabine's Room Absorption | $A = \dfrac{0.161\, V}{T_{60}}$ | V in m³, $T_{60}$ in seconds |
| 15 | Noise Reduction in Space (Direct Field) | $NR = L_W - 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{4\pi r^2}+\dfrac{4}{R}\right)$ | Q = directivity, R = room constant |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** When computing $TL$ in the formula sheet, do NOT confuse **surface density $m_f$** (kg/m²) with **mass $m$** (kg). Always convert thickness × density to $m_f$.

---

## 2.4 Practical Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **HVAC Plant Rooms** | Fan + chiller + pump enclosures; silencer sizing per ASHRAE. |
| **Power Plants** | Acoustic hoods on gas turbine air intakes. |
| **Oil & Gas** | Hyper-compressor pulsation control; API 618 compliance. |
| **Data Centers** | Cooling tower noise to neighboring offices. |
| **Building Services** | Lift machine room isolation; transformer enclosures. |

---

## 2.5 The Mass Law — Physical Interpretation

The Mass Law states that **doubling the surface density** of a partition increases its Transmission Loss by **6 dB**. Doubling the frequency also increases TL by 6 dB (in the stiffness-controlled region).

$$TL = 20\log_{10}(m_f) + 47 \quad \text{(single panel, mass-controlled region)}$$

This is the **single most important formula** for enclosure design — used to select sheet thickness, drywall layers, and MLV (mass-loaded vinyl).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Sound Power Level from Sound Pressure Level

**Given:** Sound Pressure Level $L_p$ measured on a hemispherical surface of radius $r$ around a compact source in a free field over a reflecting plane (e.g., a pump on the floor of a workshop).

**Step 1 — Relation between intensity and power:**

$$
I = \frac{W}{S}
$$

Here, $I$ is the sound intensity in W/m², $W$ is the sound power in W, and $S$ is the area through which power flows in m².

**Step 2 — Convert to reference quantities:**

By definition of $L_p$ and $L_W$:

$$
L_p = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right), \quad I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2
$$

$$
L_W = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right), \quad W_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $I = W/S$ into $L_p$:**

$$
L_p = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{S \cdot I_0}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Add and subtract $\log_{10}(W_0)$:**

$$
L_p = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) - 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S \cdot I_0}{W_0}\right)
$$

Since $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W and $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m², the ratio $I_0 / W_0 = 1 \text{ m}^{-2}$. Therefore:

$$
L_p = L_W - 10\log_{10}(S) + 10\log_{10}(S_0)
$$

where $S_0 = 1$ m².

**Step 5 — Final form:**

$$
\boxed{\,L_W = L_p + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{S_0}\right)\,}
$$

For a **hemisphere** of radius $r$ (source on a reflecting plane), $S = 2\pi r^2$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mass Law of Transmission Loss

**Starting point — Bérger's empirical correlation for a single, limp, infinite panel:**

The acoustic power transmitted through a panel is:

$$
\tau = \frac{W_t}{W_i} = \frac{1}{\left(\frac{\omega m_f}{2 \rho_0 c}\right)^2}
$$

where $m_f$ is the surface density (kg/m²), $\omega = 2\pi f$, $\rho_0 c \approx 412$ Pa·s/m is the characteristic impedance of air.

**Step 1 — Transmission Loss definition:**

$$
TL = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\tau$:**

$$
TL = 10\log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{\omega m_f}{2\rho_0 c}\right)^2\right] = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\omega m_f}{2\rho_0 c}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Insert $\omega = 2\pi f$:**

$$
TL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2\pi f \cdot m_f}{2\rho_0 c}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\pi f m_f}{\rho_0 c}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Numerically evaluate $\rho_0 c$ for standard air ($\rho_0 = 1.21$ kg/m³, $c = 343$ m/s):**

$$
\rho_0 c = 1.21 \times 343 \approx 415\ \text{Pa·s/m}
$$

So $\pi/\rho_0 c \approx \pi/415 \approx 0.00757$. Substituting:

$$
TL = 20\log_{10}(0.00757 \cdot f \cdot m_f) = 20\log_{10}(f \cdot m_f) - 42.4
$$

The conventional (rounded) KTU form is:

$$
\boxed{\,TL \approx 20\log_{10}(m_f f) - 47 \quad \text{dB}\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Insertion Loss of an Enclosure (Field Approximation)

**Given:** Source of sound power $W$ before and after enclosure installation.

**Before enclosure:** Sound Pressure Level at receiver point r is:

$$
L_{p,\text{before}} = L_W - 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S_{\text{before}}}{S_0}\right)
$$

**After enclosure:** Source radiates effectively less power (enclosure reduces transmission by TL, with a leakage penalty $L_e$):

$$
W_{\text{after}} = W \cdot 10^{-TL/10} + W \cdot 10^{-L_e/10}
$$

**Step 1 — Insertion Loss:**

$$
IL = L_{p,\text{before}} - L_{p,\text{after}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
IL = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W_{\text{before}}}{W_{\text{after}}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — With perfect seal (no leakage):**

$$
\boxed{\,IL = TL - 10\log_{10}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S_{\text{leak}}}{S_{\text{panel}}} \cdot 10^{TL/10}\right)\,}
$$

This is the **Sabine–Beranek leakage correction** — frequently asked in KTU exams when designing enclosures with imperfect seals.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A centrifugal pump produces $L_p = 88$ dB at 1 m on a hard reflecting floor. A hemispherical acoustic enclosure is fitted that has a measured Transmission Loss of $TL = 24$ dB at the dominant 500 Hz octave, with leakage equivalent to 2 % open area. Estimate the **Insertion Loss** and the new SPL at 1 m from the enclosure surface.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert SPL to Sound Power (free field, hemisphere, S = 2πr²):**

$$
S = 2\pi (1)^2 = 6.283\ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
L_W = 88 + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{6.283}{1}\right) = 88 + 7.98 = 95.98 \approx 96\ \text{dB re 10}^{-12}\text{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Estimate leakage penalty (Sabine, leakage ratio $S_L/S_P = 0.02$):**

$$
L_e = TL - 10\log_{10}\!\left(1 + \frac{S_L}{S_P}\right) = 24 - 10\log_{10}(1.02) \approx 24 - 0.086 \approx 23.9\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Insertion Loss:**

$$
IL \approx 23.9\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — New Sound Power:**

$$
L_{W,\text{new}} = 96 - 23.9 = 72.1\ \text{dB re 10}^{-12}\text{W}
$$

**Step 5 — New SPL at 1 m (same hemisphere):**

$$
L_{p,\text{new}} = 72.1 - 7.98 = 64.1 \approx 64\ \text{dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The enclosure reduced the SPL from 88 dB to 64 dB — a **24 dB reduction** (industry standard target for pump enclosures).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Equipment vs. Control Strategy

| Equipment | Dominant Source | Dominant Frequency | Best Primary Control | Best Secondary Control |
|-----------|----------------|--------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Centrifugal Pump | Cavitation, BPF | 200–2000 Hz | NPSH correction | Resilient mounts |
| Reciprocating Pump | Pulsation, valve | 50–500 Hz | Pulsation dampener | Acoustic enclosure |
| Reciprocating Compressor | Valve flutter | 500–4000 Hz | Intake silencer | Discharge silencer |
| Centrifugal Compressor | Rotor–stator | 1000–8000 Hz | Speed reduction | Inlet silencer |
| Screw Compressor | Rotor meshing | 1000–5000 Hz | Acoustic enclosure | Lagging |
| Axial Fan | BPF + tip vortex | 250–2000 Hz | Inlet silencer | Flexible duct |
| Centrifugal Fan | BPF + turbulence | 500–4000 Hz | Discharge silencer | Vibration isolation |
| Electric Motor | Magnetic, slot harmonic | 100–2000 Hz | Inverter (VFD) | Resilient mount |
| Cooling Tower | Splash + fan | 500–4000 Hz | Drift eliminator | Splitter attenuator |

# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**List any three causes of noise in a centrifugal pump and explain how cavitation noise can be controlled.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Causes of noise in a centrifugal pump:
1. **Cavitation noise** — collapse of vapor bubbles at the impeller eye due to insufficient Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH).
2. **Hydrodynamic noise** — turbulence and Blade Passage Frequency (BPF) tone due to impeller–volute interaction.
3. **Mechanical noise** — bearing failure, shaft misalignment, loose foundation.

Control of cavitation noise: Maintain adequate **NPSHA > NPSHR + 1 m safety margin** by lowering the pump installation height, increasing suction pipe diameter to reduce friction losses, and ensuring sub-cooled liquid at the suction.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Transmission Loss and Insertion Loss. State the Mass Law for a single panel partition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Transmission Loss (TL):** The ratio in decibels of incident acoustic intensity on one side of a partition to the transmitted intensity on the other side. It is a *property of the partition* at a given frequency.

- **Insertion Loss (IL):** The difference in Sound Pressure Level (or Sound Power Level) at a receiver location *before* and *after* a noise control measure is installed. It depends on the source, the path, and the room.

- **Mass Law (single panel):** $TL = 20\log_{10}(m_f \cdot f) - 47$ dB, where $m_f$ = surface density (kg/m²) and $f$ = frequency (Hz).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper PEMET752]**

**a) (7 marks)**
Discuss in detail the **causes of noise in fans** with reference to aerodynamic and mechanical sources. Explain the role of the **Blade Passage Frequency (BPF)** with a suitable formula.

**b) (7 marks)**
A centrifugal fan has 6 blades, rotates at 1450 rpm, and operates at a flow rate where the Sound Power Level is **88 dB re 10⁻¹² W** at the BPF. Design a hemispherical **acoustic enclosure** to reduce the SPL at the operator station (1 m from the enclosure) to ≤ 75 dBA. State your assumptions and use the Mass Law at the BPF.

---

#### Model Solution — Q-A(a)

**Aerodynamic sources (4 marks):**
1. **Blade Passage Frequency tone:** Discretely tonal noise from the periodic passage of blades past the cut-off or stator vanes.
2. **Tip vortex shedding:** Broadband noise from turbulent boundary layers at the blade tip.
3. **Flow separation noise:** Stall-induced turbulence on the blade suction surface.
4. **Inlet turbulence / inlet swirl.**

**Mechanical sources (2 marks):**
1. **Bearing noise:** Race frequencies.
2. **Imbalance:** 1× running frequency tone.
3. **Loose mounting.**

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
f_{BPF} = \frac{N \cdot n}{60} = \frac{6 \times 1450}{60} = 145\ \text{Hz}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Q-A(b)

**Step 1 — Calculate SPL at 1 m on reflecting floor (hemispherical free field):**

Surface area: $S = 2\pi r^2 = 2\pi(1)^2 = 6.283$ m².

$$
L_p = L_W - 10\log_{10}(S) = 88 - 10\log_{10}(6.283) = 88 - 7.98 = 80.02 \approx 80\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Target Insertion Loss:**

$$
IL_{\text{required}} = 80 - 75 = 5\ \text{dB} \quad \text{(at BPF = 145 Hz)}
$$

For an extra safety margin (industry practice adds 5 dB):

$$
IL_{\text{design}} = 5 + 5 = 10\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Use Mass Law to find required $m_f$ at 145 Hz:**

$$
TL \geq IL_{\text{design}} \Rightarrow 20\log_{10}(m_f \cdot 145) - 47 \geq 10
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(m_f \cdot 145) \geq 57
$$

$$
\log_{10}(m_f \cdot 145) \geq 2.85
$$

$$
m_f \cdot 145 \geq 10^{2.85} = 707.9
$$

$$
m_f \geq \frac{707.9}{145} = 4.88\ \text{kg/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Select standard sheet:**

For steel ($\rho = 7850$ kg/m³), thickness $t$:

$$
t = \frac{m_f}{\rho} = \frac{4.88}{7850} \approx 0.62\ \text{mm}
$$

→ Use **1.6 mm steel sheet** ($m_f \approx 12.6$ kg/m²) which gives substantial margin and meets the requirement. Add **50 mm mineral wool liner (48 kg/m³)** on the inside surface for absorption.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (KTU Valuation):** Students frequently forget to convert the **source SPL** to Sound Power Level, or use spherical instead of hemispherical propagation. Always state the propagation geometry clearly in Step 1. Marks lost here: typically 2.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper PEMET752]**

**a) (7 marks)**
Explain the **causes of noise in electric motors** with special emphasis on **magnetic noise** and **slot harmonics**. What are the control measures?

**b) (7 marks)**
A **100 kW induction motor** has a measured Sound Power Level of **92 dB re 10⁻¹² W**. It is to be housed in an **acoustic enclosure with a 2 % open area (leakage)**. The enclosure panels have a measured **Transmission Loss of 30 dB** at 1000 Hz. Calculate:
(i) The corrected Transmission Loss due to leakage.
(ii) The Insertion Loss.
(iii) The new Sound Power Level radiated to the surroundings.
(iv) The SPL at 2 m from the enclosure (hemispherical field).

---

#### Model Solution — Q-B(a)

**Causes of Electric Motor Noise (4 marks):**
1. **Magnetic noise (dominant):** Magnetostriction of stator laminations + alternating radial force due to air-gap flux. Tones at $2f_s$, $4f_s$, $8f_s$ (where $f_s$ = supply frequency = 50 Hz in India).
2. **Slot harmonics:** Rotor/stator slot interaction produces strong tones at $f_{slot} = N_{rotor} \times f_{rotor}$ and its multiples.
3. **Mechanical:** Bearing race frequencies (BPFO, BPFI), rotor imbalance.
4. **Cooling fan noise** (built-on impeller).

**Control Measures (3 marks):**
1. Use **inverter-grade insulation** and **insulated bearings** to suppress shaft currents from VFDs.
2. **Tighten stator core laminations** (reduces magnetostriction amplitude).
3. Use **soft starters / VFDs** with **switching frequency > 8 kHz** to push PWM tones out of audible band.
4. **Resilient mounting** with rubber pads (static deflection ≥ 10 mm).
5. **Skewed rotor bars** to weaken slot harmonics.
6. **Acoustic enclosure** with ventilation lined duct.

---

#### Model Solution — Q-B(b)

**(i) Corrected TL due to leakage (Sabine–Beranek leakage correction):**

Leakage ratio $S_L / S_P = 0.02$.

$$
TL_{\text{eff}} = TL - 10\log_{10}\!\left(1 + \frac{S_L}{S_P}\right)
$$

$$
TL_{\text{eff}} = 30 - 10\log_{10}(1.02) = 30 - 0.086 \approx 29.9\ \text{dB} \quad \approx 30\ \text{dB}
$$

(Two marks)

**(ii) Insertion Loss:**

For an enclosure in the absence of significant flanking paths:

$$
IL \approx TL_{\text{eff}} = 30\ \text{dB}
$$

(One mark)

**(iii) New Sound Power Level:**

$$
L_{W,\text{new}} = L_{W,\text{old}} - IL = 92 - 30 = 62\ \text{dB re 10}^{-12}\text{W}
$$

(One mark)

**(iv) SPL at 2 m (hemispherical):**

Surface area of hemisphere at 2 m: $S = 2\pi(2)^2 = 8\pi = 25.13$ m².

$$
L_p = L_W - 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{S_0}\right) = 62 - 10\log_{10}(25.13) = 62 - 14.0 = 48\ \text{dB}
$$

(Two marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (KTU Valuation):** Students often confuse $L_W$ and $L_p$ in part (iv). Also, using spherical ($4\pi r^2$) instead of hemispherical ($2\pi r^2$) propagation when the source is on a reflecting floor results in a **3 dB error** — losing 1 full mark. **Always state the geometry explicitly.**

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PEMET752 Module 4**

### A. Equipment-Specific Noise Sources
- **Pumps:** Cavitation, BPF, bearing wear → use NPSH margin + vibration isolation.
- **Reciprocating Compressors:** Valve flutter (dominant), piston slap, pulsation → intake silencer + dampener.
- **Rotary Compressors:** Rotor meshing frequency → enclosure + lagging.
- **Fans:** BPF tone + tip vortex + turbulence → inlet silencer + flexible duct.
- **Electric Motors:** Magnetostriction + slot harmonics + bearing → VFD + resilient mount + skewed rotor.
- **Cooling Towers:** Water splash + fan BPF → drift eliminator + attenuator stack.

### B. Core Formulas (Must Memorize)
- BPF: $f = Nn/60$
- Mass Law: $TL = 20\log_{10}(m_f f) - 47$
- Lw–Lp relation: $L_W = L_p + 10\log_{10}(S/S_0)$
- Hemisphere: $S = 2\pi r^2$; Sphere: $S = 4\pi r^2$.
- Speed reduction: $\Delta L_W \approx 50\log_{10}(n_2/n_1)$.

### C. Acoustic Enclosure Design Rules
- Use **mass law** to select outer panel thickness.
- Use **mineral wool 25–100 mm, 30–80 kg/m³** as acoustic liner.
- Use **perforated inner sheet** (25–30 % open area).
- Seal all leaks with **neoprene gaskets** (1 % leak can lose 10 dB!).
- Provide **ventilation lined duct** (don't forget airflow!).
- Mount equipment on **spring isolators**, not rigid to the enclosure base.

### D. Common Examiner Traps
1. Mixing up $TL$ and $IL$ — $TL$ is a panel property; $IL$ is a system property.
2. Forgetting **hemispherical vs spherical** geometry.
3. Treating Surface Density $m_f$ (kg/m²) as Mass $m$ (kg).
4. Ignoring leakage in enclosure design.
5. Forgetting to add a 3–5 dB safety margin to design IL.

### E. Key Standards to Know
- **ISO 3744 / ISO 9614** — Sound Power measurement.
- **ISO 11690** — Industrial noise control measures.
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95** — 90 dBA / 8 hr exposure limit.
- **ASHRAE Handbook (HVAC Applications)** — Fan noise silencer design.
- **API 618** — Pulsation control for reciprocating compressors.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — PEMET752 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Barriers and Absorbers - Porous, Fibrous, Foams, Resonance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Barriers and Absorbers - Porous, Fibrous, Foams, Resonance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Fundamental Definitions (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the discipline of architectural and industrial acoustics, **noise control** is fundamentally achieved through two physically distinct mechanisms:

- **Sound Barriers (Reflective Type):** Structures that obstruct the direct transmission of acoustic energy between a source and a receiver. They function primarily on the principle of **reflection** and **diffraction**. The standard metric is **Transmission Loss (TL)** measured in **decibels (dB)**.

- **Sound Absorbers:** Porous, fibrous, or resonant materials that convert incident acoustic energy into **heat** through viscous and thermal dissipation within their internal microstructure. The standard metric is the **Sound Absorption Coefficient (α)**, ranging from 0 (perfect reflector) to 1 (perfect absorber).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Anchor:** Barriers reduce noise by blocking the sound path (mass-controlled impedance discontinuity). Absorbers reduce noise by dissipating the sound energy (viscous-thermal conversion). They are NOT interchangeable. An absorptive surface in a barrier face converts the surface to a hybrid barrier that reduces flanking reflections.

### 1.2 The Four Canonical Material Classes

Per the KTU 2024 PEMET752 syllabus, the absorber family is classified into:

1. **Porous Absorbers** — open-cell, rigid/elastic skeletal matrices (e.g., mineral wool, glass fiber, rock wool).
2. **Fibrous Absorbers** — bulk assemblies of fine fibers (glass wool, polyester, natural fibers); a sub-class of porous media characterized by high **flow resistivity**.
3. **Foam Absorbers** — open-cell polymeric foams (polyurethane, melamine); offer both absorption and broadband damping.
4. **Resonance (Reactive) Absorbers** — mass-spring systems operating near a natural frequency; sub-classified as *Helmholtz* and *Panel (Membrane)* resonators.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing beside a busy highway shouting "Hello" toward a friend's house across the road:

- A **concrete wall** between you acts as a **barrier**. It does not absorb your voice; it physically prevents the sound waves from reaching the other side — like closing a window against a noisy street.
- A **thick quilt hung on the wall** acts as an **absorber**. The sound waves penetrate the quilt's fibers, and the air molecules inside vibrate against the fiber surfaces, losing energy as microscopic friction (heat) — like pressing a sponge into water: the water gets trapped inside.
- A **glass bottle** of a certain size, when blown across, produces a pure tone. This is a **Helmholtz resonator**, where the air inside the bottle neck "springs" against the air volume "mass" in the cavity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** *BARRIERS = BLOCK, ABSORBERS = DISSIPATE, RESONATORS = SELECT FREQUENCY.*

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (to be memorized)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|--------|--------|------|------------------|
| Transmission Loss | TL | dB | Reduction of sound through barrier |
| Insertion Loss | IL | dB | Reduction at receiver due to installation of barrier |
| Absorption Coefficient | α | dimensionless | Fraction of incident energy absorbed |
| Noise Reduction Coefficient | NRC | dimensionless | Average of α at 250, 500, 1000, 2000 Hz |
| Flow Resistivity | σ | Rayl/m (Pa·s/m²) | Resistance to air flow through material |
| Cavity Depth | d | m | Air gap behind absorber or panel |
| Surface Density (Mass) | m_s | kg/m² | Mass per unit area of barrier |
| Speed of Sound (air, 20°C) | c = 343 | m/s | Standard reference velocity |
| Air Density (20°C) | ρ₀ = 1.21 | kg/m³ | Standard reference density |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Theory of Single-Partition Barriers (Mass-Controlled Reflection)

A single, limp, impervious panel acts as an **inertia-controlled reflector**. At frequencies well below the coincidence frequency and well above the panel's fundamental resonance, the panel behaves as a pure mass reacting against the impedance of incident sound waves.

**Key Physical Reasoning:**

1. Sound pressure impinges on the panel, forcing it to vibrate.
2. The panel's mass provides inertia (Newton's second law: F = ma).
3. Higher mass ⇒ higher inertia ⇒ lower transmission of vibrational energy ⇒ higher TL.
4. Frequency dependence: every octave (doubling of f) adds **6 dB** to TL.

### 2.2 Mass Law (The Cornerstone of Barrier Design)

The Mass Law is derived by treating the panel as a rigid, mass-controlled impedance mismatch.

$$TL_{mass} = 20 \log_{10}(m_s \cdot f) - 47 \quad \text{(SI units)}$$

where $m_s$ is in $\text{kg/m}^2$ and $f$ is in Hz. (Equivalent constant is $-33$ when $m_s$ is in $\text{lb/ft}^2$.)

**Engineering Utility:** Used to estimate baseline insulation of single walls, partitions, and enclosures before accounting for stiffness, damping, coincidence dip, and cavity resonances.

### 2.3 Porous Absorber Theory (Rayleigh-Delmotte & Empirical)

Porous absorbers dissipate acoustic energy by:

- **Viscous losses**: air oscillating in narrow channels experiences shear at the walls.
- **Thermal losses**: compression/rarefaction cycles pump heat between air and matrix.

Performance depends critically on **flow resistivity (σ)**. Optimal σ lies approximately between **1000 and 4000 Rayl/m** for typical broadband absorption.

**Empirical Delany-Bezley Model** (extended by Miki) provides the characteristic surface impedance:

$$Z_c = \rho_0 c \left[ 1 + 0.0698 \left( \frac{f}{\sigma} \right)^{-0.632} - j \, 0.0710 \left( \frac{f}{\sigma} \right)^{-0.632} \right]$$

For practical design, **thickness rule of thumb**:

$$d_{abs} \approx \frac{\lambda_{min}}{4} \approx \frac{c}{4 f_{min}}$$

### 2.4 Helmholtz Resonator Theory

A Helmholtz resonator consists of a rigid cavity of volume V connected to the exterior via a narrow neck of cross-sectional area A and effective length L_eff.

The **air plug in the neck** acts as the **mass**, and the **enclosed cavity air** acts as the **spring**. The resonance frequency is:

$$f_r = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{A}{V \cdot L_{eff}}}$$

with end correction (unflanged opening):

$$L_{eff} = L + 1.7 \, a$$

where $a = \sqrt{A/\pi}$ is the equivalent radius of the neck.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End Correction Justification:** The air column just outside the neck also moves, effectively extending the mass. The factor 1.7 is derived from the solution of an unflanged circular piston in an infinite baffle; the flanged case uses 1.57.

### 2.5 Panel (Membrane) Absorber Theory

A thin, flexible sheet placed over a rigid-backed air cavity forms a **mass-spring-damper system**:

- **Mass (m):** the panel surface density.
- **Spring:** the air trapped in the cavity (compliance $\propto 1/d$).
- **Damper:** the panel's internal friction plus any fibrous backing.

The fundamental resonance is:

$$f_r = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\rho_0}{m_s \cdot d}}$$

where $d$ is the cavity depth, $\rho_0$ is air density, and $m_s$ is panel surface density.

**Higher-mode behavior:** The panel also resonates at higher frequencies given by $f_n \approx (2n-1)f_r$ approximately, though damping alters this for low Q-systems.

### 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Formula / Law | Variables & Units | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---------------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | $TL = 20\log_{10}(m_s f) - 47$ | $m_s$: kg/m², $f$: Hz | Partition/door TL estimate |
| 2 | $f_r = \dfrac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{A}{V L_{eff}}}$ | $A$: m², $V$: m³, $L_{eff}$: m | Helmholtz tuning |
| 3 | $f_r = \dfrac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_0}{m_s d}}$ | $m_s$: kg/m², $d$: m | Panel absorber tuning |
| 4 | $L_{eff} = L + 1.7 \, a$ | $a$: m | Neck end correction |
| 5 | $\alpha = 1 - \vert R \vert^2$ | $R$: reflection coeff. | Absorber performance |
| 6 | $TL = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\tau}\right)$ | $\tau$: transmission coeff. | Definition of TL |
| 7 | $IL \approx 10\log_{10}(2\pi N) + 5$ (for $N > 1$) | $N$: Fresnel number | Outdoor barrier IL |
| 8 | $N = \dfrac{2 \delta}{\lambda}$ | $\delta$: path diff. (m), $\lambda$: m | Diffraction analysis |
| 9 | $d_{abs} \approx \dfrac{c}{4 f_{min}}$ | $f_{min}$: Hz | Porous absorber thickness |
| 10 | $\text{NRC} = \dfrac{\alpha_{250}+\alpha_{500}+\alpha_{1000}+\alpha_{2000}}{4}$ | dimensionless | Single-number rating |

> [!NOTE]
> The Fresnel number $N$ governs outdoor barrier efficiency. When $N > 1$, the barrier is "tall enough" relative to the wavelength and shadow zone is well-developed. Below $N < 0.1$, diffraction is severe and the barrier is acoustically invisible.

### 2.7 Engineering Application Domains

- **Architectural acoustics:** Auditoria, recording studios (porous + panel combos).
- **Industrial noise:** Machine enclosures, duct liners (porous foam with protective film).
- **Transportation:** Highway noise barriers, railway sound walls (mass + absorptive facing).
- **HVAC:** Duct silencers, plenum absorbers (fibrous with flow liners).
- **Automotive:** Headliners, engine bay foam (composite porous + mass).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Mass Law for a Single Infinite Panel

**Starting premise:** An infinite, limp, impervious panel of surface density $m_s$ separates two semi-infinite acoustic half-spaces. The panel responds as a pure mass to the pressure difference across it.

**Step 1 — Pressure Imbalance and Newton's Law**

The net force per unit area on the panel equals the difference between incident-plus-reflected pressure on the source side and transmitted pressure on the receiver side:

$$F/A = (p_i + p_r) - p_t$$

By Newton's second law, this net pressure accelerates the panel mass:

$$(p_i + p_r) - p_t = m_s \frac{dv}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Harmonic Wave Substitution**

For a harmonic wave of angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$, the particle velocity of the panel is:

$$v = \frac{p_i + p_r - p_t}{j \omega m_s}$$

(using $d/dt \to j\omega$ in phasor domain.)

**Step 3 — Continuity of Velocity**

The panel's velocity must match the transmitted-side particle velocity (no air slip past the impervious sheet):

$$\frac{p_i + p_r - p_t}{j \omega m_s} = \frac{p_t}{\rho_0 c}$$

where $\rho_0 c$ is the characteristic impedance of air (≈ 415 Rayl at 20°C).

**Step 4 — Solving for Transmission Coefficient**

Rearrange the previous equation to express $p_t$ in terms of incident pressure:

$$p_i + p_r - p_t = \frac{j \omega m_s}{\rho_0 c} p_t$$

$$p_i + p_r = p_t \left( 1 + \frac{j \omega m_s}{\rho_0 c} \right)$$

In the high-frequency limit where the inertia dominates ($\omega m_s \gg \rho_0 c$), the "1" becomes negligible:

$$p_i + p_r \approx p_t \cdot \frac{j \omega m_s}{\rho_0 c}$$

Defining the transmission coefficient $\tau = p_t/p_i$ for matched impedance on both sides (ignoring reflected-side detail), one obtains for normal incidence and assuming $|r| \approx 1$:

$$\vert \tau \vert \approx \frac{\rho_0 c}{\omega m_s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Transmission Loss**

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\vert \tau \vert^2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\omega^2 m_s^2}{(\rho_0 c)^2}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$ and Numerical Constants**

$$\omega^2 m_s^2 = 4\pi^2 f^2 m_s^2$$

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(4\pi^2 f^2 m_s^2\right) - 10 \log_{10}\!\left((\rho_0 c)^2\right)$$

With $\rho_0 c \approx 415$ Rayl, $10\log_{10}(415^2) = 10 \log_{10}(172{,}225) \approx 52.4 \approx 47 + \text{geometry correction}$ (the extra 5–6 dB depends on incidence averaging). The widely cited SI Mass Law is:

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(m_s f) - 47 \quad \text{dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** For a $m_s = 10$ kg/m² panel at $f = 500$ Hz, $TL \approx 20\log_{10}(5000) - 47 = 20(3.699) - 47 = 73.98 - 47 \approx 27$ dB. This matches tabulated values for medium-weight partitions within ±2 dB.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Helmholtz Resonator Frequency

**Starting premise:** The air plug in the neck behaves as a lumped mass; the cavity behaves as an acoustic spring (compliance).

**Step 1 — Define Acoustic Mass (Neck)**

The mass of air in the neck of length $L$ and cross-section $A$:

$$m_{air} = \rho_0 A L$$

With end correction, replace $L \to L_{eff} = L + 1.7 a$:

$$m_{eff} = \rho_0 A L_{eff}$$

**Step 2 — Define Acoustic Spring (Cavity Compliance)**

When the plug moves inward by displacement $\xi$, it compresses the trapped air volume. The fractional volume change is $\Delta V / V = A\xi/V$. Using adiabatic bulk modulus $B = \gamma P_0$:

$$\Delta p = -\gamma P_0 \frac{\Delta V}{V} = -\gamma P_0 \frac{A \xi}{V}$$

The force on the plug from the cavity excess pressure is $F = -\Delta p \cdot A$:

$$F = \gamma P_0 \frac{A^2}{V} \xi$$

Hence the compliance (spring constant reciprocal):

$$k = \frac{\gamma P_0 A^2}{V}$$

But $\gamma P_0 = \rho_0 c^2$, so:

$$k = \frac{\rho_0 c^2 A^2}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Natural Frequency of Mass-Spring System**

$$\omega_r = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho_0 c^2 A^2 / V}{\rho_0 A L_{eff}}} = c \sqrt{\frac{A}{V L_{eff}}}$$

Therefore:

$$f_r = \frac{\omega_r}{2\pi} = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{A}{V L_{eff}}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**

Let $A = 10 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-3}$ m², $V = 5$ L $= 5\times 10^{-3}$ m³, $L = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m, neck radius $a = \sqrt{10^{-3}/\pi} \approx 0.0178$ m.

$$L_{eff} = 0.05 + 1.7(0.0178) = 0.05 + 0.0303 = 0.0803 \text{ m}$$

$$f_r = \frac{343}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{10^{-3}}{(5\times 10^{-3})(0.0803)}} = 54.6 \sqrt{2.49} \approx 86.2 \text{ Hz}$$

This frequency is in the low-bass range typical for HVAC duct attenuators and bass-trap designs.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Panel Absorber Resonant Frequency

**Starting premise:** A limp massless panel of surface density $m_s$ seals a cavity of depth $d$ backed by a rigid wall.

**Step 1 — Panel Compliance from Trapped Air**

When the panel displaces uniformly by $\xi$, it reduces cavity volume by $\xi$ (per unit area). The excess pressure (adiabatic):

$$\Delta p = \gamma P_0 \frac{\xi}{d} = \rho_0 c^2 \frac{\xi}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Equation of Motion**

$$m_s \ddot{\xi} = -\Delta p = -\frac{\rho_0 c^2}{d} \xi$$

This is SHM with:

$$\omega_r^2 = \frac{\rho_0 c^2}{m_s d}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency Expression**

$$f_r = \frac{\omega_r}{2\pi} = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\rho_0}{m_s d}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**

Plywood, $m_s = 6$ kg/m², $d = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m.

$$f_r = \frac{343}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1.21}{6 \times 0.05}} = 54.6 \sqrt{4.033} \approx 110 \text{ Hz}$$

This is the typical mid-bass tuning used in recording studio low-frequency absorbers.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick-Reference)

| Property | Porous | Fibrous | Foam (open-cell) | Helmholtz | Panel |
|----------|--------|---------|-------------------|-----------|-------|
| Mechanism | Viscous/thermal | Viscous | Viscous | Resonance | Resonance |
| Tunable | No (broadband) | No | No | Yes (geometric) | Yes (mass/depth) |
| Frequency Range | Mid–High (500–4000 Hz) | Mid–High | Mid–High | Single band | Single band |
| Typical α max | 0.85–1.0 | 0.75–0.95 | 0.80–1.0 | ~1.0 at $f_r$ | ~0.5 at $f_r$ |
| Fibrous dust hazard | Low | High (need cover) | None | None | None |
| Cost | Low | Low | Moderate | Low | Low |
| Outdoor durability | Low | Low | Moderate | High (concrete) | Moderate |
