## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Distinguish clearly between a sound barrier and a sound absorber. State one engineering application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A sound **barrier** is a dense, impervious structure that reflects or blocks the transmission of acoustic energy through the principle of mass-controlled impedance mismatch. Its performance is quantified by **Transmission Loss (TL)** in decibels. Example: a 100 mm brick wall acting as a partition between a generator room and an office.

A sound **absorber** is a porous, fibrous, or resonant material that converts incident acoustic energy into heat via viscous and thermal dissipation, without reflection. Its performance is quantified by the **sound absorption coefficient (α)**. Example: glass-wool panels lining the walls of an anechoic chamber.

Barriers = BLOCK (mass-based reflection). Absorbers = DISSIPATE (energy conversion). *(3 marks: 1.5 for definition of each, 1 for distinguishing principle.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is a Helmholtz resonator? Write the expression for its resonance frequency and explain the role of end correction.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Helmholtz resonator** is an acoustic reactive device consisting of a rigid-walled cavity of volume $V$ connected to the surrounding air through a narrow neck of cross-sectional area $A$ and physical length $L$. The air plug in the neck behaves as a **lumped acoustic mass**, while the air in the cavity acts as an **acoustic spring**.

The resonant frequency is:

$$f_r = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{A}{V \, L_{eff}}}$$

The **end correction** accounts for the additional air mass just outside the neck that also moves in phase with the plug. For an unflanged circular neck of equivalent radius $a$:

$$L_{eff} = L + 1.7\, a$$

Ignoring the end correction under-predicts $L_{eff}$, leading to over-prediction of $f_r$. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for end-correction rationale.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]

**(a)** Derive the Mass Law for transmission loss of a single limp, impervious partition. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single partition of surface density 15 kg/m² separates two rooms. Calculate the transmission loss at 500 Hz and at 1000 Hz using the SI Mass Law. Comment on the trend. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Panel is infinite, limp, and perfectly rigid (no bending stiffness).
2. Panel is impervious (no air leakage).
3. Frequency is well above panel resonance and well below coincidence.
4. Normal incidence; no flanking paths.

**Derivation (as in Section 3.1):**

Pressure difference drives panel inertia:

$$(p_i + p_r) - p_t = m_s \frac{dv}{dt}$$

For harmonic excitation, $v = j\omega \xi$:

$$(p_i + p_r) - p_t = j\omega m_s v$$

Continuity of velocity across the panel:

$$\frac{(p_i + p_r) - p_t}{j\omega m_s} = \frac{p_t}{\rho_0 c}$$

In the high-frequency inertia-dominated limit, with $p_i \approx -p_r$ for a nearly rigid reflector, solving gives:

$$\vert \tau \vert \approx \frac{\rho_0 c}{\omega m_s}$$

Therefore:

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\vert \tau \vert^2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(m_s f) - 47 \text{ dB}$$

(7 marks: 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation, 2 for final expression and units.)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $m_s = 15$ kg/m².

**At $f_1 = 500$ Hz:**

$$TL_1 = 20 \log_{10}(15 \times 500) - 47 = 20 \log_{10}(7500) - 47$$

$$\log_{10}(7500) = 3.875$$

$$TL_1 = 20(3.875) - 47 = 77.5 - 47 = 30.5 \text{ dB}$$

**At $f_2 = 1000$ Hz:**

$$TL_2 = 20 \log_{10}(15 \times 1000) - 47 = 20 \log_{10}(15000) - 47$$

$$\log_{10}(15000) = 4.176$$

$$TL_2 = 20(4.176) - 47 = 83.52 - 47 = 36.52 \text{ dB}$$

**Comment on trend:**

$$TL_2 - TL_1 = 36.52 - 30.5 = 6.02 \text{ dB} \approx 6 \text{ dB}$$

This confirms the **6 dB-per-octave rule** of the Mass Law. Doubling the frequency exactly increases TL by 6 dB. *(7 marks: 2 each for the two calculations, 1 for log math, 2 for trend comment.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a porous absorber. Discuss the role of flow resistivity and the rule of thumb for selecting absorber thickness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 mm thick fibrous absorber is proposed for controlling reverberation in a 1000 m³ hall. Recommend whether this is adequate for a target minimum frequency of 200 Hz, and justify with the quarter-wavelength criterion. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Working principle:** When sound waves impinge on a porous absorber, the air molecules within the open pores are forced to oscillate. The viscous shear between moving air and the static fiber/skeleton walls converts acoustic energy into heat (viscous dissipation). Additionally, the cyclic compression and rarefaction of the trapped air transfers heat to the matrix (thermal dissipation). The combined effect produces an effective complex acoustic impedance that absorbs energy.

**Role of flow resistivity (σ):** Flow resistivity is the resistance to steady air flow per unit thickness, measured in Rayl/m (Pa·s/m²). It governs the trade-off:
- If σ is **too low** (< 500 Rayl/m): material is acoustically transparent; little energy is dissipated.
- If σ is **too high** (> 5000 Rayl/m): material reflects sound because impedance mismatch is large.
- **Optimal range:** 1000–4000 Rayl/m for broadband absorption.

**Thickness rule of thumb:** To absorb sound at frequency $f_{min}$ with good efficiency:

$$d \geq \frac{\lambda_{min}}{4} = \frac{c}{4 f_{min}}$$

This ensures the reflected wave from the rigid backing arrives back at the surface 180° out of phase, destructively interfering with the surface-reflected component. *(7 marks: 3 for principle, 2 for flow resistivity, 2 for thickness rule.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $d_{proposed} = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m, $f_{min} = 200$ Hz, $c = 343$ m/s.

**Quarter-wavelength thickness required:**

$$d_{req} = \frac{c}{4 f_{min}} = \frac{343}{4 \times 200} = \frac{343}{800} = 0.42875 \text{ m} \approx 429 \text{ mm}$$

**Comparison:**

$$d_{req} = 429 \text{ mm} \gg d_{proposed} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** The proposed 50 mm absorber is **inadequate** for a target minimum frequency of 200 Hz. A 50 mm thick absorber will provide good absorption roughly above:

$$f_{min,eff} \approx \frac{c}{4 d} = \frac{343}{4 \times 0.05} = 1715 \text{ Hz}$$

Therefore, this absorber is suitable only for frequencies above approximately 1.7 kHz. For 200 Hz control, either:
1. Increase absorber thickness to ≥ 430 mm, OR
2. Use a **panel resonator** tuned to 200 Hz, OR
3. Use a **Helmholtz resonator array** tuned to 200 Hz.

*(7 marks: 2 for calculation of $d_{req}$, 2 for comparison, 2 for recommendation, 1 for alternatives.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing TL and IL:** TL is intrinsic to a partition; IL is the field-measured reduction including diffraction and flanking. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are conflated.
> 2. **Forgetting end correction in Helmholtz formula:** Using $L$ instead of $L_{eff}$ gives a frequency error of 15–25%. Always state the end correction explicitly.
> 3. **Sign error in mass law log:** The constant is $-47$ (SI) or $-33$ (English). Wrong sign = 0 marks for the formula even if derivation is correct.
> 4. **Treating porous and fibrous as identical:** They are NOT — fibrous specifically refers to bulk fibrous assemblies; porous is a broader class. Examiners look for this distinction.
> 5. **Ignoring incidence angle:** Mass Law applies to normal incidence; random incidence adds ~3–5 dB. State the assumption.
> 6. **Using NRC = α:** NRC is the AVERAGE of four specific octave-band values, NOT a general absorption coefficient.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Two noise-control paradigms:** Blocking (barriers, TL) vs. Dissipating (absorbers, α).
- ✅ **Four absorber families:** Porous, Fibrous, Foam, Resonant (Helmholtz + Panel).
- ✅ **Mass Law:** $TL = 20\log_{10}(m_s f) - 47$ dB (SI); +6 dB per octave, +6 dB per doubling of mass.
- ✅ **Helmholtz resonance:** $f_r = \dfrac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{A}{V L_{eff}}}$; air plug = mass, cavity = spring.
- ✅ **End correction:** $L_{eff} = L + 1.7 a$ (unflanged) — air just outside the neck participates in motion.
- ✅ **Panel resonance:** $f_r = \dfrac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_0}{m_s d}}$; panel = mass, trapped air = spring.
- ✅ **Porous thickness rule:** $d \geq \dfrac{c}{4 f_{min}}$ for quarter-wave absorber design.
- ✅ **Flow resistivity optimal range:** 1000–4000 Rayl/m for broadband absorbers.
- ✅ **Barrier Insertion Loss (Kurze-Anderson):** $IL \approx 10\log_{10}(2\pi N) + 5$ dB for $N > 1$.
- ✅ **Fresnel number:** $N = 2\delta/\lambda$; governs barrier shadow zone strength.
- ✅ **NRC:** Average of α at 250, 500, 1000, 2000 Hz — single-number rating.
- ✅ **6 dB/octave trend:** Doubling frequency OR doubling mass each adds 6 dB to TL (Mass Law).
- ✅ **Limitation:** Mass Law assumes limp, infinite panel — actual partitions show coincidence dip and stiffness-controlled resonance at extremes.
- ✅ **Hybrid strategy:** Best acoustic treatment uses broadband porous absorber PLUS tuned resonators for low-frequency absorption that porous alone cannot achieve.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acoustic Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET752 - ACOUSTICS AND NOISE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Acoustic Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Acoustic Filter:** An acoustic filter is a passive or active device, structure, or network designed to modify the frequency spectrum of an incident sound wave by selectively **transmitting** certain frequency bands with minimal loss while **attenuating** (reflecting or absorbing) others. In the KTU 2024 framework, acoustic filters are classified by their frequency selectivity into **low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, and band-stop** types, mirroring the language of electrical filter theory but operating on acoustic impedance and particle velocity fields.

The acoustic filter is mathematically defined by its **Insertion Loss (IL)** or **Transmission Loss (TL)** as a function of frequency $f$:

$$IL(f) = 10 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{P_{\text{incident}}(f)}{P_{\text{transmitted}}(f)} \right] \quad [\text{dB}]$$

where $P_{\text{incident}}$ and $P_{\text{transmitted}}$ are the acoustic power measured downstream with and without the filter inserted, respectively.

### 1.2 Classification of Acoustic Filters

| Filter Type | Frequency Behaviour | Typical Acoustic Realization |
|---|---|---|
| **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | Passes $f < f_c$, attenuates $f > f_c$ | Expansion chamber silencer |
| **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** | Passes $f > f_c$, attenuates $f < f_c$ | Side-branch resonator (inverted geometry) |
| **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** | Passes $f_1 < f < f_2$ | Coupled expansion-resonator network |
| **Band-Stop Filter (BSF)** | Attenuates $f_1 < f < f_2$ | Helmholtz resonator, quarter-wave stub |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"A Coffee Filter for Sound":** Imagine pouring a mixture of fine sand and large pebbles through a kitchen sieve. The sieve does not change the *amount* of material — it changes the *spectrum* of what gets through. An acoustic filter behaves identically with sound: a wall made of porous foam passes low-frequency rumble (the "fine sand") but soaks up high-frequency hiss (the "pebbles"). Conversely, a hollow bottle with a narrow neck (Helmholtz resonator) acts like a **frequency-selective trap** — it absorbs almost nothing *except* a very specific whistle pitch, just as a tuned wine glass vibrates only when you sing its exact resonant note.

### 1.4 Why Acoustic Filters Matter in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> Acoustic filters are the silent workhorses of modern engineering. They are embedded in:
> - **Automotive exhausts** (mufflers): the expansion chamber is a classic low-pass filter that suppresses the high-frequency combustion "pop" while letting low-frequency engine drone pass (or be tuned to suppress drone peaks).
> - **HVAC ductwork**: plenum chambers lined with fiberglass attenuate blower tonal noise.
> - **Anechoic test chambers**: modular wedge arrays form broad-band absorptive filters.
> - **Hearing protection devices**: tuned resonator arrays embedded in ear defenders notch-out specific industrial frequencies (e.g., 4 kHz nail-gun spike).
> - **Active noise control headsets**: feedforward ANC is, in essence, an *active adaptive filter* implemented in DSP.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The standard speed of sound in air at 20 °C is **c ≈ 343 m/s** (more precisely 343.2 m/s). All acoustic filter formulas in this module are derived assuming **plane-wave propagation** in a rigid-walled duct of cross-section $S_1$, with the filter element creating a localized change in cross-section or impedance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Physical Mechanisms of Acoustic Filtering

Acoustic filters achieve their frequency-selective behaviour through exactly three physical mechanisms, and *every* filter is some combination of these:

1. **Reactive (Reflective / Dissipationless) Filtering**
   - Uses discontinuities in acoustic impedance (area changes, side branches, cavities).
   - Energy is *reflected* back toward the source; no energy is absorbed.
   - Frequency-selective because impedance mismatch depends on $\omega$ via the wave number $k = \omega / c$.
   - Examples: **expansion chamber, Helmholtz resonator, side-branch stub, quarter-wave tube**.

2. **Dissipative (Absorptive) Filtering**
   - Uses porous/fibrous materials (fiberglass, mineral wool, foam) whose complex acoustic impedance has a real (resistive) part.
   - Energy is *converted to heat* by viscous and thermal losses in the pore network.
   - Example: **lined duct silencer**.

3. **Active Filtering**
   - Uses microphones, DSP, and loudspeakers to generate an *anti-noise* signal that destructively interferes with the unwanted component.
   - Example: **feedforward/feedback ANC headsets**.

### 2.2 Operational Principle of the Expansion Chamber (Low-Pass Filter)

The expansion chamber is a simple, hollow cylindrical cavity inserted in-line in a duct. It works as a **low-pass acoustic filter** for the following intuitive reason:

- **Low frequencies** ($\lambda \gg L$): the cavity appears as a simple *area discontinuity*. The wave sees a sudden enlargement then a sudden contraction. The forward and backward waves partially cancel by simple geometric area-jump reflection, and most energy passes through.
- **High frequencies** ($\lambda \ll L$): the cavity now behaves as a **resonant tube**. Standing-wave modes form inside, and at specific frequencies the cavity acts as a near-perfect reflector (anti-transmission peaks).

The result is a low-pass-like envelope with a deep **null** at the first anti-resonance.

### 2.3 Operational Principle of the Helmholtz Resonator (Band-Stop Filter)

A Helmholtz resonator is a cavity of volume $V$ connected to the main duct by a narrow neck of length $L$ and cross-section $S$. The air mass in the neck behaves as a *lumped* mass $m = \rho_0 S L'$, and the air in the cavity behaves as a *lumped* spring of compliance $C = V / (\rho_0 c^2)$.

This is the acoustic analogue of a mass-on-a-spring, with resonant angular frequency:

$$\omega_r = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m \cdot C}} = c \sqrt{\frac{S}{V \cdot L'}}$$

Near $\omega_r$, the neck air oscillates violently in and out of the cavity, drawing energy from the duct mode. Hence the resonator *absorbs* (in a dissipative case) or *reflects* (in a purely reactive case) energy in a narrow band around $f_r = \omega_r / (2\pi)$ — making it a **band-stop filter**.

### 2.4 Operational Principle of the Side-Branch Resonator (Quarter-Wave / Band-Stop)

A side branch is a tube of length $L$ closed at the far end, attached perpendicular to the main duct. A plane wave in the duct drives the air column of the side branch. When the side branch length equals an odd multiple of a quarter wavelength ($L = \lambda/4, 3\lambda/4, \dots$), the closed end is a velocity node and the mouth is a velocity antinode — a **pressure node** appears at the mouth.

> A pressure node at the mouth is a near-zero acoustic impedance to the main duct → the side branch *short-circuits* the duct pressure, reflecting almost all the incident wave back. This produces sharp **transmission-loss peaks** at $f_n = (2n-1)c / (4L)$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-critical** formula set. Memorize the symbols and the assumptions (rigid walls, plane wave, no mean flow).

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acoustic wave number | $k = \omega / c = 2\pi f / c$ | rad/m |
| 2 | Wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | m |
| 3 | Characteristic impedance of air | $Z_0 = \rho_0 c \approx 413$ (rayl) at 20 °C | Pa·s/m |
| 4 | Expansion-chamber area ratio | $m = S_2 / S_1$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | **TL of simple expansion chamber** | $TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\Big[ 1 + \tfrac{m^2}{4} \sin^2(kL) \Big]$ | dB |
| 6 | Max TL of expansion chamber (at $kL = \pi/2$) | $TL_{\max} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( 1 + m^2/4 \right)$ | dB |
| 7 | **Helmholtz resonant frequency** | $f_r = \dfrac{c}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{S}{V L'}}$ | Hz |
| 8 | End correction (unflanged neck) | $L' = L + 0.85 d$ | m |
| 9 | End correction (flanged neck, $L \approx 0$) | $L' \approx 1.46 d$ | m |
| 10 | Max TL of single Helmholtz resonator | $TL_{\max} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[ 1 + \dfrac{1}{4}\!\left(\dfrac{S c}{2\pi f_r V}\right)^{\!2}\right]$ | dB |
| 11 | Side-branch (closed) resonant frequencies | $f_n = (2n-1)\,c/(4L)$ | Hz |
| 12 | Quarter-wave stub condition | $L = \lambda/4$ at the notched frequency | m |
| 13 | Transmission coefficient magnitude | $\vert T \vert = \sqrt{P_{\text{trans}}/P_{\text{inc}}}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Reflection coefficient | $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | Insertion loss | $IL = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{without}}/P_{\text{with}})$ | dB |
| 16 | Two-chamber expansion TL (decoupled) | $TL_{\text{total}} = TL_1 + TL_2$ (approx., if chambers are non-interacting) | dB |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility — Real-World Significance

> [!TIP]
> - **Industrial exhaust mufflers** are expansion chambers chosen for $m$ so that $TL_{\max}$ exceeds the regulatory noise limit at the dominant tone.
> - **Reverberation control in auditoria** uses porous (dissipative) broadband filters to tame mid–high frequencies.
> - **Subsonic wind-tunnel silencing** uses dissipative filters (because reactive filters are ineffective at very low $kL$).
> - **Transformer hum (100/120 Hz) suppression** uses **active adaptive filters** because the source is tonal and predictable.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transmission Loss of a Simple Expansion Chamber (Low-Pass Filter)

Consider a rigid duct of cross-section $S_1$ with a sudden expansion to $S_2$ over a length $L$, then a sudden contraction back to $S_1$. The filter is the region of cross-section $S_2$.

**Step 1 — Set up the transfer-matrix (four-pole) model.**

For a wave-guide section of cross-section $S_2$, length $L$, the acoustic transfer matrix relating $(p_1, U_1)$ at the inlet to $(p_2, U_2)$ at the outlet is, in the plane-wave approximation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} p_1 \\ U_1 \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix} \cos(kL) & j\,Z_2 \sin(kL) \\ j\,\dfrac{\sin(kL)}{Z_2} & \cos(kL) \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} p_2 \\ U_2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $Z_2 = \rho_0 c / S_2$ is the characteristic impedance *per unit area* of the expanded section, and $k = \omega / c$.

**Step 2 — Apply the continuity and pressure boundary conditions at the two area jumps.**

At the inlet jump ($S_1 \to S_2$):
- Pressure continuity: $p_{\text{duct}} = p_{\text{chamber, inlet}}$
- Volume-velocity continuity: $U_1 = U_{\text{chamber, inlet}}$

These are already embedded in the matrix.

**Step 3 — Terminate the chamber into a matched load $Z_L = Z_1 = \rho_0 c / S_1$.**

Set $p_2 = Z_1 U_2$. Substitute into the transfer matrix.

**Step 4 — Compute the input impedance seen at $S_1$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= \cos(kL) \, p_2 + j Z_2 \sin(kL) \, U_2 \\
U_1 &= j\,\frac{\sin(kL)}{Z_2} \, p_2 + \cos(kL) \, U_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $p_2 = Z_1 U_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= U_2 \!\left[ Z_1 \cos(kL) + j Z_2 \sin(kL) \right] \\
U_1 &= U_2 \!\left[ j\,\dfrac{Z_1 \sin(kL)}{Z_2} + \cos(kL) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the input impedance at the duct mouth is:

$$Z_{\text{in}} = \frac{p_1}{U_1} = \frac{Z_1 \cos(kL) + j Z_2 \sin(kL)}{\cos(kL) + j\,(Z_1/Z_2)\sin(kL)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the transmission coefficient $T$.**

For a wave incident on the chamber from a source of internal impedance $Z_1$, the transmission coefficient into the matched load $Z_1$ is:

$$T = \frac{2 Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_{\text{in}}}$$

Substituting $Z_{\text{in}}$ and simplifying (using $Z_2 / Z_1 = S_1 / S_2 = 1/m$):

**Step 6 — Simplify using the substitution $Z_2 = Z_1 / m$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{in}} &= \frac{Z_1 \cos(kL) + j\,(Z_1/m)\sin(kL)}{\cos(kL) + j\,m \sin(kL)} \\[4pt]
&= Z_1 \cdot \frac{\cos(kL) + j\,(1/m)\sin(kL)}{\cos(kL) + j\,m \sin(kL)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{2 Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_1 \cdot \dfrac{\cos(kL) + j\,(1/m)\sin(kL)}{\cos(kL) + j\,m \sin(kL)}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2\,[\cos(kL) + j\,m\sin(kL)]}{[\cos(kL) + j\,m\sin(kL)] + [\cos(kL) + j\,(1/m)\sin(kL)]} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2\,[\cos(kL) + j\,m\sin(kL)]}{2\cos(kL) + j\,(m + 1/m)\sin(kL)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the magnitude-squared $\vert T \vert^2$.**

Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate, and use $\cos^2(kL) + \sin^2(kL) = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert T \vert^2 &= \frac{4\,[\cos^2(kL) + m^2 \sin^2(kL)]}{4\cos^2(kL) + (m + 1/m)^2 \sin^2(kL)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $(m + 1/m)^2 = m^2 + 2 + 1/m^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert T \vert^2 &= \frac{4\,[\cos^2(kL) + m^2 \sin^2(kL)]}{4\cos^2(kL) + (m^2 + 2 + 1/m^2)\sin^2(kL)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4\cos^2(kL) + 4 m^2 \sin^2(kL)}{4\cos^2(kL) + m^2 \sin^2(kL) + 2 \sin^2(kL) + (1/m^2)\sin^2(kL)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $4\cos^2 = 4 - 4\sin^2$ in the denominator, the denominator becomes $4 + (m^2 + 2 + 1/m^2 - 4)\sin^2(kL) = 4 + (m - 1/m)^2 \sin^2(kL)$. After algebraic simplification (a standard textbook result, e.g., Beranek & Vér), one obtains the celebrated **low-pass filter formula**:

$$
\boxed{\,TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[ 1 + \dfrac{m^2}{4} \sin^2(kL) \right]\,}
$$

**Step 8 — Identify the low-pass behaviour.**

- As $kL \to 0$ (low frequency): $\sin(kL) \to 0$, so $TL \to 0$ → sound passes.
- At $kL = \pi/2$ (first anti-resonance): $\sin(kL) = 1$, so $TL = 10 \log_{10}(1 + m^2/4)$ → **maximum attenuation**.
- At $kL = \pi$: $\sin(kL) = 0$ again → sound re-emerges (a "pass-band lobe" at higher frequencies). This shows the expansion chamber is *not* a perfect low-pass filter — it has alternating pass/stop bands, characteristic of all finite-length reactive filters.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resonant Frequency of a Helmholtz Resonator (Band-Stop Filter)

The lumped-element model treats the neck air as a rigid mass and the cavity air as a spring.

**Step 1 — Mass of air in the neck.**

$$m = \rho_0 \, S \, L'$$

where $L' = L + \Delta L$ is the **effective length** including the end correction $\Delta L$.

**Step 2 — Acoustic compliance of the cavity.**

The cavity of volume $V$ behaves as a spring with compliance $C = V / (\rho_0 c^2)$ — this follows from the adiabatic relation $pV^{\gamma} = \text{const}$, linearized to give $\Delta p / p_0 = -\gamma \Delta V / V$, and noting that $c^2 = \gamma p_0 / \rho_0$.

**Step 3 — Resonance condition of a mass–spring system.**

For a mass $m$ on a spring of compliance $C$:

$$\omega_r = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m C}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\rho_0 S L' \cdot V / (\rho_0 c^2)}} = c \sqrt{\frac{S}{V L'}}$$

Dividing by $2\pi$:

$$
\boxed{\,f_r = \dfrac{c}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{S}{V L'}}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — End-correction derivation (sketch).**

The air just outside the neck mouth has to move too. Solving the velocity-potential for an unflanged circular piston of radius $a = d/2$ in an infinite baffle gives an *added mass length* $\Delta L = (8/3\pi) a \approx 0.85\,a = 0.425\,d$. When two such corrections are summed (one inside, one outside, and the inside one being smaller), the standard result is:

$$L' = L + 0.85\,d \quad \text{(unflanged circular neck, $L > d$)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transmission-Zero Condition of a Closed Side-Branch Stub

A closed-end side branch of length $L$ attached to a main duct acts as a **band-stop filter** (notch). We derive the *transmission-zero* frequencies — the frequencies at which no energy passes.

**Step 1 — Input impedance of a closed tube.**

For a tube of length $L$ closed at the far end, the input impedance at the mouth is:

$$Z_{\text{stub}} = -j\,Z_{\text{stub,0}} \cot(kL)$$

where $Z_{\text{stub,0}} = \rho_0 c / S_{\text{stub}}$ is the characteristic impedance of the stub.

**Step 2 — Connection to the main duct.**

At the junction (assuming small side-branch area $S_{\text{stub}} \ll S_{\text{duct}}$), the boundary condition is pressure continuity and volume-velocity summation:

$$p_{\text{duct}} = p_{\text{stub, mouth}}, \quad U_{\text{duct}} = U_{\text{through}} + U_{\text{into stub}}$$

**Step 3 — Transmission-zero condition.**

Transmission through the duct is *zero* when the side branch *short-circuits* the pressure at the junction, i.e., when $Z_{\text{stub}} = 0$. From Step 1, this requires:

$$\cot(kL) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; kL = \frac{\pi}{2},\; \frac{3\pi}{2},\; \frac{5\pi}{2},\; \dots$$

i.e.,

$$
\boxed{\,L = \dfrac{(2n-1)\,\lambda}{4}, \quad f_n = \dfrac{(2n-1)\,c}{4L}\,}
$$

This is the famous **quarter-wave stub notch filter** result.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Expansion Chamber (Reactive LPF) | Helmholtz Resonator (Reactive BSF) | Dissipative Lined Duct (Absorptive) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Filtering type | Low-pass | Band-stop (narrow) | Broad-band |
| Physical mechanism | Impedance mismatch (area jump) | Lumped mass–spring resonance | Viscous + thermal losses in pores |
| Energy fate | Reflected | Reflected (or absorbed if damped) | Converted to heat |
| Bandwidth | Wide (alternating pass/stop) | Very narrow ($\Delta f / f \sim 0.1$) | Continuous from mid to high $f$ |
| Frequency dependence | $\sin^2(kL)$ | Resonant at $f_r$ | Increases with $f$ |
| Typical use | Engine exhaust | HVAC tonal noise, transformer hum | Duct lining, anechoic wedges |
| Affected by mean flow | Moderate | High | Low |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Acoustic Filter Functional Architecture

The diagram below shows the taxonomy of acoustic filters and the *flow of sound energy* through a generic filter. Acoustic energy entering the system is either **transmitted**, **reflected**, or **absorbed** — and the relative fractions depend on frequency.

```mermaid

    src["SOUND SOURCE"]:::src --> inc["INCIDENT WAVE P_inc"]
    inc --> filt{"ACOUSTIC FILTER"}:::filt

    filt --> refl["REFLECTED P_ref"]
    filt --> trans["TRANSMITTED P_trans"]:::out
    filt --> abs["ABSORBED P_abs"]:::loss

    refl -.-> back["Back to source"]
    abs -.-> heat["Heat in porous material or viscous losses"]

    filt -.->|"f < f_c (low-pass)"| passA["LOW BAND PASSES"]:::out
    filt -.->|"f > f_c (low-pass)"| stopA["HIGH BAND ATTENUATED"]:::loss

    subgraph CLASS["CLASSIFICATION BY MECHANISM"]
        r1["REACTIVE: expansion chamber, side-branch, Helmholtz"]
        d1["DISSIPATIVE: porous liner, fiberglass, foam"]

    end

```
